Machines arranged in a **U-shape** so that the **operator can handle multiple machines**. Maximizes operator efficiency and supports JIT/Heijunka. Highly favored in Toyota Production System.

**Type 3 — L-Shaped or Cluster Layout:**
Machines grouped in a non-linear shape to **fit building constraints** or to support **mixed families**. Useful when a cell processes two related part families.

**Applications:**

- **In-line:** Shafts, pins (linear process)
- **U-shaped:** Engine component subassemblies
- **Cluster:** Sheet-metal families with welding + paint

---

#### Question B
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1, Q2]`

**(a) With a step-by-step example, explain the Rank Order Clustering (ROC) algorithm for cell formation. State any two limitations of ROC.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Compare Cellular Manufacturing with Job Shop and Continuous Flow layouts on at least six parameters.** (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

##### Part (a) — ROC Algorithm with Example (7 Marks)

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full worked example. The student should present:

1. **Algorithm statement** (1 Mark) — ROC is an iterative procedure that reorders rows and columns of a binary machine-part matrix by computing their **binary-weighted decimal values** and sorting in decreasing order.

2. **Iteration steps** (4 Marks) — Compute row weights, sort, recompute column weights, sort, repeat until convergence.

3. **Final cell structure** (1 Mark) — Identify the diagonal blocks as the manufacturing cells.

4. **Two limitations of ROC** (1 Mark):
   - May converge to a **local optimum** rather than the global best arrangement.
   - Treats all operations as **equally important**; ignores processing times, volumes, and bottleneck loads.
   - Cannot handle **bottleneck or duplicate machines** without extension.

##### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Job Shop | Cellular Manufacturing | Continuous Flow |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Layout Type | Functional | Grouped (Family-based) | Product-based line |
| Part Variety | Very high | Moderate (1 family) | Very low |
| Volume per part | Low | Medium | High |
| Lead Time | Long | Moderate to short | Very short |
| WIP Inventory | High | Low | Very low |
| Routing Flexibility | Excellent | Limited to cell | Very low |
| Equipment Utilization | Lower | Moderate | Highest |
| Operator Skill | High (multi-skill) | Multi-skill within cell | Single-task |
| Capital Investment | Moderate | Moderate | Very high |
| Suitability | Job orders | Batch / mass-custom | Mass production |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In ROC, do NOT compute column weights before sorting rows.** Many students get this order wrong. The correct cycle is: (1) Sort Rows → (2) Reorder Matrix → (3) Sort Columns → (4) Reorder Matrix → (5) Check Convergence → Repeat.
> 2. **Do not forget to define "1" and "0" in the matrix** — a 1 means "part uses the machine" and 0 means "does not use". Examiners specifically check for this.
> 3. **In GT definitions, never write "GT is grouping of similar machines"** — that is a major conceptual error. The correct definition groups **similar parts**, which then form part families, and the machines serving those families form cells.
> 4. **Opitz code digits 1–3 = form, 4–5 = internal features, 6–9 = process.** Mixing this up costs a full 2-mark definition.
> 5. **For grouping efficiency, write the formula clearly: $\eta = 1 - e/(m \cdot n)$** and substitute numeric values. Skipping substitution loses a mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — GT and Cellular Manufacturing:**

- **Group Technology (GT)** groups **similar parts** into **part families** for efficient manufacturing.
- **Cellular Manufacturing (CM)** is the physical layout strategy that materializes GT.
- The **three pillars of GT** are: **Classification & Coding**, **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)**, and **Cell Formation**.
- **Opitz Code** = 9 digits: **Form (1–3)**, **Internal Features (4–5)**, **Process (6–9)**.
- **PFA (Burbidge, 1963)** uses the machine-part incidence matrix to visually identify diagonal blocks.
- **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** uses **binary-weighted decimal values** to iteratively reorder rows and columns.
- **Bond Energy (BE_ij)** = $\sum_k n_{ijk}(m - n_{ijk})$ — used in the Bond Energy algorithm.
- **Grouping Efficiency** $\eta = 1 - e/(m \cdot n)$ — higher is better; >85% is excellent.
- **Exceptional elements (e)** are the 1s lying **off the diagonal** — they represent **inter-cell movement** and are undesirable.
- **Cell layouts** include: **In-line**, **U-shaped** (Toyota-style), **L-shaped / cluster**.
- **U-shaped cells** allow **one operator to attend multiple machines** — foundation of **JIT**.
- **GT benefits** include reduced lead time, lower WIP, simplified planning, and improved quality.
- **GT limitations** include initial coding effort, balancing sub-cells, and bottleneck machines.
- **Comparison with Flow Line:** GT/CM lies **between job shop and flow line** on the layout spectrum — "**best of both worlds**" for **high-mix low-volume** production.
- **Industry 4.0 link:** GT forms the **pre-processing layer** for **digital twins** and **cyber-physical cells** in modern smart factories.
- **Memorize the formula** $\eta = 1 - e/(m \cdot n)$ and the **ROC steps** — together worth **at least 5–6 marks** in any KTU 14-mark question.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexible Manufacturing Systems - Features of FMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flexible Manufacturing Systems - Features of FMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is a highly automated, computer-controlled group technology-based production system that integrates a set of computer numerically controlled (CNC) machine tools, an automated material handling system (AMH), and a centralized hierarchical computer control network to simultaneously process a family of parts (or part variants) in random or mixed order, within a defined capacity range, with minimal manual intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> An FMS is a manufacturing system that combines the high productivity of a transfer line with the flexibility of a job shop, capable of producing a variety of parts in low-to-medium batch sizes (typically **20 to 1000 units/part**) with minimal reconfiguration time.

**Key Standard Metrics Associated with FMS:**
- **Flexibility Index (Fᵢ):** A measure of the system's ability to switch between part types without major retooling.
- **Production Lead Time:** Typically reduced to **25% to 50%** of conventional job-shop systems.
- **Work-in-Process (WIP) Inventory:** Reduced by **60% to 80%** compared to conventional batch production.
- **Machine Utilization:** Target benchmark of **75% to 90%** under optimal scheduling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Modern Cloud Kitchen**
> Imagine a modern cloud kitchen (e.g., Swiggy/DoorDash central kitchen) instead of a traditional restaurant. A traditional restaurant has fixed menus and one chef handling one dish at a time. A cloud kitchen, however, has **multiple cooking stations (CNC machines)**, a **conveyor belt system (automated material handling)** that ferries raw ingredients between stations, and a **centralized digital dashboard (computer control)** that receives orders dynamically and routes ingredients accordingly. If an order for Biryani, Pizza, and Tandoori comes simultaneously, the dashboard automatically assigns each dish to the best-suited station, tracks cooking time, and dispatches them — all without manual coordination.
> 
> This is exactly how an FMS functions: it dynamically routes **pallets with different part types** to the right **CNC workstations** using **AGVs or conveyors**, with a **centralized DNC/MDC computer** orchestrating the entire flow.

## 1.3 Why FMS? — The Industrial Justification

| Parameter | Conventional Job Shop | Transfer Line | **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Batch Size | Low | High (mass production) | **Low to Medium (20–1000)** |
| Product Variety | Very High | Very Low | **Medium to High (Family-based)** |
| Changeover Time | High | Very High | **Low (Minutes)** |
| WIP Inventory | Very High | Low | **Low (Automated storage)** |
| Manpower | High | Low | **Very Low (Unattended operation)** |
| Capital Investment | Low | Very High | **High (Justified for mid-volume variety)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** FMS bridges the gap between a **Job Shop** (high variety, low volume) and a **Transfer Line** (low variety, high volume) — often called the **"Middle Ground of Manufacturing Strategy."**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of an FMS

An FMS is not a single machine — it is an **integrated cyber-physical system** with four tightly coupled sub-systems. Let us break them down logically.

### A. Workstations (CNC Machine Tools)
- The "heart" of the FMS where actual value addition occurs.
- Typically **CNC machining centres (VMCs, HMCs), turning centres, or grinding machines**.
- Each machine is equipped with a **pallet changer**, **tool magazine (capacity 30–200 tools)**, and a **part-programmable controller**.
- Workstations are laid out in a closed loop, open loop, or ladder layout depending on part routing logic.

### B. Automated Material Handling System (AMH)
- The "circulatory system" that ferries work-pallets between workstations, load/unload stations, and the storage system.
- Three principal technologies used:
  1. **Conveyor Systems** — fixed path, high throughput, low flexibility.
  2. **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** — battery-powered driverless carts following magnetic/optical tape paths; very flexible routing.
  3. **Industrial Robots (6-axis)** — used for palletizing, machine loading, and inter-machine transfer in cell-based FMS.

### C. Centralized Computer Control (The "Brain")
- A **hierarchical control architecture** with three layers:
  1. **Shop Floor Level:** Machine Control Units (MCU) — each CNC has its own controller (e.g., FANUC, Siemens SINUMERIK).
  2. **Cell Level:** Cell Controller (or DNC — Direct Numerical Control host) — downloads NC part programs, monitors machine status, coordinates pallets.
  3. **Factory Level:** Host Computer / Manufacturing Management System (MMS) — handles production scheduling, order release, and reporting.
- Communication protocols: **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)**, **Ethernet/IP**, **PROFINET**, or **OPC-UA** (modern standard).

### D. Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)
- A high-density, computer-controlled warehouse integrated with the FMS.
- Stores raw blanks, in-process parts, finished parts, fixtures, and tooling.
- Reduces factory floor footprint and enables **unattended overnight production**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Without the AMH, the machines would sit idle waiting for an operator. Without the centralized control, the AMH would not know *where* to go. Without AS/RS, the FMS cannot sustain **lights-out (unattended) manufacturing**. Hence, all four sub-systems are *necessary and sufficient* for FMS operation.

## 2.3 KTU Concept Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **exam-ready summary** of every FMS feature you must know for a KTU 2024 question on this topic. Memorize the definitions and the corresponding KTU keyword for each.

| S.No. | Feature | Definition (KTU Standard Wording) | Engineering Purpose / Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** | Each workstation is a CNC machine with stored part programs. | Enables automatic machining of varied geometries without manual setup. |
| 2 | **Automated Material Handling (AMH)** | Use of conveyors, AGVs, or robots to move parts between stations. | Eliminates manual transport; supports 24/7 unattended operation. |
| 3 | **Centralized Computer Control** | Hierarchical DNC/MDC system controlling machines, AMH, and tools. | Coordinates routing, scheduling, and real-time decision making. |
| 4 | **Automated Storage & Retrieval (AS/RS)** | Computerized buffer for raw, in-process, and finished parts. | Reduces WIP, saves floor space, supports lights-out production. |
| 5 | **Tooling Flexibility** | Centralized tool magazine + Automatic Tool Changer (ATC). | Allows the machine to execute multiple operations (drill, mill, tap, bore) in one setup. |
| 6 | **Routing Flexibility** | Any pallet can be routed to any workstation via AMH. | Permits parallel processing and load balancing across machines. |
| 7 | **Modular / Expandable Design** | System designed with plug-and-play stations and standardized interfaces. | Future capacity expansion by adding new workstations without redesign. |
| 8 | **Quick Changeover (SMED support)** | Fixtures and tooling designed for rapid swap (Single Minute Exchange of Die philosophy). | Reduces setup time, enables small-batch economics. |
| 9 | **Integrated Quality Control** | In-line probing, CMM, or vision systems within the FMS loop. | First-piece and in-process inspection without breaking the automated flow. |
| 10 | **Pallet Pool / Random Mix Production** | A buffer of mixed pallets of different part types circulating in the system. | Enables **"build-to-order"** with mixed-model sequencing. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of FMS

- **Aerospace Industry:** Machining of structural airframe components (aluminum ribs, spars) in family batches.
- **Automotive Tier-1 Suppliers:** Production of gearbox housings, differential cases, and cylinder heads.
- **Defense Manufacturing:** Limited batch production of critical parts requiring strict traceability.
- **Medical Implants:** Customized orthopedic implants (knee, hip) where geometry varies per patient.
- **General Engineering:** Batch production of hydraulic valve bodies, pump casings, and diesel engine blocks.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "Discuss the features of FMS," always structure your answer under **four heads — Hardware (Machines + AMH), Software (Control), Support (Storage + Tooling), and Strategy (Flexibility types).** Examiners reward this systematic structure with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical & Classification Matrix

## 3.1 The Eight Recognized Types of Flexibility in an FMS (KTU High-Yield)

The word **"Flexible"** in FMS is not a single attribute — it is a composite of **eight distinct flexibilities**, classified by **Sethi & Sethi (1991)** and adopted as a KTU board standard. Let us derive each from first principles, since this is a frequently asked 7-mark sub-question.

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Flexibility Classification

**Step 1 — Define "Flexibility" formally.**

Flexibility, $F$, in a manufacturing context is defined as the *capability of a system to respond to changes* in product, demand, or process without major disruption to the production flow.

$$F = \frac{\text{Number of distinct states the system can achieve}}{\text{Time required to transition between states}}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose $F$ by the nature of the change.**

The numerator (number of achievable states) depends on *what* is changing. This leads to a natural classification:

$$\mathcal{F} = \{ F_{\text{machine}}, F_{\text{product}}, F_{\text{routing}}, F_{\text{volume}}, F_{\text{expansion}}, F_{\text{operation}}, F_{\text{process}}, F_{\text{programming}} \}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret each component.**

| Flexibility Type | Definition | Worked Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Flexibility** | Ability of a single machine to produce a range of part types. | A VMC switching from drilling to milling without hardware change. |
| **Product Flexibility** | Ability to introduce *new* products without major system redesign. | Adding a new part number to the FMS schedule. |
| **Routing Flexibility** | Ability to produce a part via *multiple alternate paths*. | A gearbox housing machined on CNC-1 *or* CNC-2 depending on load. |
| **Volume Flexibility** | Ability to operate profitably at different output volumes. | Producing 50 units/month vs. 500 units/month on the same FMS. |
| **Expansion Flexibility** | Ability to add new machines, tools, or workstations modularly. | Plugging in a 5th VMC into the existing loop. |
| **Operation Flexibility** | Ability to perform a wide range of operations on a part. | Drilling, tapping, boring, milling all in one clamping. |
| **Process Flexibility** | Ability to produce a part via *different process plans*. | A part machined by turning-then-milling OR milling-then-turning. |
| **Programming Flexibility** | Ability to easily modify part programs off-line. | CAM software generating NC code without stopping machines. |

**Step 4 — Combine into a Flexibility Index.**

A practical composite **Flexibility Index** is computed as a weighted sum:

$$F_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{8} w_{j} \cdot F_{j}$$

where $w_{j}$ is the strategic weight (with $\sum w_{j} = 1$) assigned by the plant manager, and $F_{j}$ is the normalized score (0 to 1) of the $j$-th flexibility type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** A perfectly rigid transfer line has $F_{i} = 0$. A fully flexible job shop has $F_{i} \to 1$. A well-designed FMS targets $F_{i} \geq 0.7$.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison: FMS vs. Dedicated vs. Job Shop

Let us quantitatively justify why FMS is preferred for **mid-volume, mid-variety** production.

| Parameter | Dedicated Transfer Line (DTL) | **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** | Job Shop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variety ($V$) | 1 part (very low) | **10 – 100 part family (medium)** | 100s of unrelated parts (very high) |
| Volume ($Q$) | $> 10^{5}$ | **$20 - 10^{4}$** | $< 50$ |
| Lead Time ($LT$) | Very Low | **Low (50% of job shop)** | High |
| Capital per machine ($C$) | Very High | **High** | Low |
| Break-even Batch Size | Large | **Small to medium** | Trivial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** An FMS is economically justified when the cumulative part family volume falls in the **$20$ to $1000$ units/part** range, and the part geometry family is amenable to **Group Technology (GT) classification.**

## 3.3 Operational Sequence in an FMS (Step-by-Step Process Flow)

Let us trace a typical part's journey through the FMS, marking every control decision.

1. **Order Release:** The Host Computer (MMS layer) receives a production order for a part family.
2. **Pallet Allocation:** A work-pallet with a fixture is retrieved from the AS/RS by the cell controller.
3. **Workpiece Loading:** At a Load/Unload station, the operator mounts the raw blank on the fixture. (Some FMSs use **robotic loading** for full automation.)
4. **Pallet Identification:** A **barcode/RFID tag** on the pallet is scanned; the cell controller identifies the part and downloads the correct NC program to the assigned CNC.
5. **Material Handling:** The AGV (or conveyor) routes the pallet to the first workstation in its routing sequence.
6. **Machining:** The CNC executes the part program with **in-process probing** for dimensional feedback.
7. **Inter-Operation Transfer:** After completion, the AMH routes the pallet to the next workstation (e.g., a turning centre).
8. **Routing Decision:** The cell controller checks **real-time machine availability** and may *re-route* the pallet to an alternate machine if the primary is busy.
9. **Final Inspection:** The finished part is moved to a **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** station or a vision-inspection cell.
10. **Storage / Shipment:** Approved parts are stored in AS/RS awaiting dispatch; rejected parts are routed to a rework buffer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The decision at **Step 8** is what differentiates an FMS from a rigid transfer line. The cell controller performs **dynamic scheduling** in real time, maximizing system utilization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMS Architectural Block Diagram (Mermaid Functional Flow)

The diagram below maps the **block-level functional architecture** of an FMS, illustrating the data and material flow between all four sub-systems. (Mermaid compilation safeguards applied: alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase labels, no inline formatting inside node text.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[HOST COMPUTER MMS LAYER] --> A2[CELL CONTROLLER DNC LAYER]
    A2 --> A3[WORKSTATION 1 CNC VMC]
    A2 --> A4[WORKSTATION 2 CNC HMC]
    A2 --> A5[WORKSTATION 3 CNC TURNING CENTRE]
    A2 --> A6[WORKSTATION 4 CMM INSPECTION]
    
    A7[AS RS AUTOMATED STORAGE] --> A8[LOAD UNLOAD STATION]
    A8 --> A9[AMH CONVEYOR OR AGV NETWORK]
    A9 --> A3
    A9 --> A4
    A9 --> A5
    
    A3 --> A9
    A4 --> A9
    A5 --> A9
    A6 --> A10[FINISHED GOODS BUFFER]
    A10 --> A7
    
    A2 -.DOWNLOADS NC PROGRAM.-> A3
    A2 -.DOWNLOADS NC PROGRAM.-> A4
    A2 -.DOWNLOADS NC PROGRAM.-> A5
    A1 -.PRODUCTION SCHEDULE.-> A2
    A2 -.MACHINE STATUS TELEMETRY.-> A1
```

### Reading the Diagram
- **Solid arrows** = physical material flow (pallets, parts).
- **Dotted arrows** = information flow (NC code, telemetry, schedules).
- The **AMH** acts as a closed-loop bus connecting the storage, load station, and all four workstations in a *circulating* topology.
- The **Host → Cell Controller → Workstations** path is the **three-tier control hierarchy** (MAP/Ethernet stack).

## 4.2 Closed-Loop FMS Layout Schematic (Physical Topology)

The diagram below shows the **closed-loop layout** of an FMS shop floor — the most common KTU-referenced configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOOP[FMS CLOSED LOOP LAYOUT]
        direction LR
        L1[LOAD STATION]:::topo --> C1[CONVEYOR SEGMENT 1]:::topo
        C1 --> M1[WORKSTATION 1 CNC VMC]:::topo
        M1 --> C2[CONVEYOR SEGMENT 2]:::topo
        C2 --> M2[WORKSTATION 2 CNC HMC]:::topo
        M2 --> C3[CONVEYOR SEGMENT 3]:::topo
        C3 --> M3[WORKSTATION 3 TURNING CENTRE]:::topo
        M3 --> C4[CONVEYOR SEGMENT 4]:::topo
        C4 --> I1[INSPECTION CMM]:::topo
        I1 --> C5[CONVEYOR SEGMENT 5]:::topo
        C5 --> S1[AS RS STORAGE]:::topo
        S1 --> C6[CONVEYOR SEGMENT 6]:::topo
        C6 --> L1
    end
    
    classDef topo fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Draw a neat sketch of an FMS layout"*, you may reproduce the closed-loop topology above. The closed loop guarantees that *every* workstation is reachable from *every* other workstation via a known path — the geometric embodiment of **routing flexibility**.

## 4.3 Flexibility Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[FLEXIBILITY IN FMS]:::root --> F1[MACHINE FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F2[PRODUCT FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F3[ROUTING FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F4[VOLUME FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F5[EXPANSION FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F6[OPERATION FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F7[PROCESS FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    F --> F8[PROGRAMMING FLEXIBILITY]:::leaf
    
    classDef root fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#000000
    classDef leaf fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reference:**
> - **Part A:** Short answer, 3 marks each, 1–2 sentences.
> - **Part B:** Long answer, 14 marks each, with internal choice. Typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - All questions below are aligned to **CO1 / CO2 of OERUT612** and to **Bloom's Level: Understand (L2) and Apply (L3).**

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define FMS and list any four of its salient features.
**Model Answer:**
A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is a computer-controlled, integrated system of CNC machines, automated material handling, and centralized control that can produce a variety of parts in low-to-medium batch sizes with minimal manual intervention.

**Any four salient features:**
1. Computer-controlled CNC workstations with stored part programs.
2. Automated Material Handling (AMH) using conveyors or AGVs.
3. Hierarchical centralized computer control (Host → Cell → Machine).
4. Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS) for buffer and stock.
5. Routing flexibility enabling multiple alternate paths for each part.
6. Modular and expandable architecture.

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between **Routing Flexibility** and **Process Flexibility** in an FMS.
**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Routing Flexibility | Process Flexibility |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ability of a part to follow *multiple alternate machine sequences* to completion. | Ability to produce the same part using *different process plans or operations*. |
| What changes? | The **sequence of machines** (which workstation). | The **operations performed** (how the part is machined). |
| Example | A cylinder block routed to CNC-1 or CNC-2 depending on availability. | A part that can be made by *turning-then-milling* OR *milling-then-turning*. |
| Controlled by | Cell controller's real-time scheduler. | CAM system / process planner. |

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architectural components of an FMS** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With suitable examples, describe the **eight types of flexibility** that an FMS must possess to be classified as truly "flexible."

#### Model Solution — (a)

An FMS has **four principal architectural sub-systems**, integrated as follows:

1. **Workstations (CNC Machines):** A group of CNC machine tools (VMCs, HMCs, turning centres) equipped with automatic tool changers, pallet changers, and part-programmable controllers. *Function:* Perform the actual machining operations.
2. **Automated Material Handling System (AMH):** Conveyors, AGVs, or industrial robots that move work-pallets between stations without manual intervention. *Function:* Transport parts along programmed routes; supports unattended operation.
3. **Centralized Computer Control System:** A three-tier hierarchy — *Host Computer* (MMS for scheduling) → *Cell Controller* (DNC for coordination) → *Machine Control Unit* (CNC execution). *Function:* Real-time decision-making, NC program distribution, telemetry.
4. **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS):** Computerized warehouse storing raw blanks, WIP, finished parts, fixtures, and tooling. *Function:* Buffer for continuous flow, footprint reduction, lights-out support.

A supporting block diagram is as drawn in **Section 4.1** of these notes (Functional Flowchart). [Reference to be reproduced on the answer sheet.]

#### Model Solution — (b)

The eight flexibilities are:
1. **Machine Flexibility:** Single machine produces a range of parts (e.g., a VMC drills, mills, taps, bores in one setup).
2. **Product Flexibility:** New part numbers can be introduced without system redesign.
3. **Routing Flexibility:** A part can be produced via multiple machine sequences (e.g., gearbox housing on CNC-1 OR CNC-2).
4. **Volume Flexibility:** System operates economically at different output volumes (50 to 5000 units).
5. **Expansion Flexibility:** New machines can be added modularly to the loop.
6. **Operation Flexibility:** A single clamping supports multiple operations.
7. **Process Flexibility:** Alternate process plans achieve the same final geometry.
8. **Programming Flexibility:** NC programs can be modified off-line using CAM tools.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students write only **3 or 4 flexibilities** (routing, product, machine, volume) and stop. The KTU board's *expected* answer lists **all 8**. Writing fewer than 6 will cost you 3–4 marks. Memorize the full set with one example each.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** with a **Conventional Job Shop** and a **Dedicated Transfer Line (DTL)** on the basis of flexibility, batch size, and capital investment. In which production scenario is an FMS most justified?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **closed-loop layout** of an FMS. Explain why a closed loop is preferred over a linear layout for an FMS.

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Parameter | Job Shop | **FMS** | DTL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flexibility | Very High (any part) | **High (part family)** | Very Low (1 part) |
| Batch Size | Very Low ($< 50$) | **Medium ($20 - 10^{4}$)** | Very High ($> 10^{5}$) |
| Capital per Machine | Low | **High** | Very High |
| WIP Inventory | High | **Low** | Very Low |
| Lead Time | High | **Medium (Reduced)** | Very Low |
| Operator Skill | Very High | **Low (automated)** | Low |

**Economic Justification:** An FMS is most justified for **mid-volume, mid-variety production** of a **part family (Group Technology classification)** with batch sizes of **20 to 1000 units per part number**, where the part geometry shares a common manufacturing process plan. Example: aerospace structural components, automotive transmission housings.

#### Model Solution — (b)

*(Sketch — refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the closed-loop Mermaid block diagram. On the answer sheet, draw the same layout with: Load Station → Conveyor → M1 → M2 → M3 → Inspection → AS/RS → back to Load Station, in a closed loop.)*

**Why a Closed Loop is Preferred:**
1. **Multiple Access Paths:** Every workstation is reachable from every other station, enabling **routing flexibility**.
2. **No Deadlock:** Material flows continuously in one direction; no intersection conflicts.
3. **Redundancy:** If one workstation fails, the part can be *re-routed* via the loop to an alternate machine — a property a linear transfer line lacks.
4. **Balanced Utilization:** The cell controller can load-balance across machines in real time.
5. **Scalability:** A new machine can be inserted at any point in the loop without disturbing the existing flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** In the comparison question, students often confuse **"Flexibility"** with **"Productivity."** An FMS sacrifices *some* raw productivity (cycles per hour) compared to a DTL, but gains *enormous* flexibility. Examiners specifically award marks for this trade-off being stated explicitly. Skipping this trade-off costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this section 10 minutes before the exam for a quick last-mile recap.**

- **FMS = CNC + AMH + Computer Control + AS/RS.** All four are mandatory — missing any one disqualifies the system from being an FMS.
- **FMS is a "middle ground"** between a Job Shop and a Transfer Line — neither extreme flexibility nor extreme productivity.
- **Batch size sweet spot:** $20$ to $1000$ units per part number, with **Group Technology (GT)** part family classification.
- **Eight Flexibilities (must memorize):** Machine, Product, Routing, Volume, Expansion, Operation, Process, Programming.
- **Three-Tier Control Hierarchy:** Host Computer (MMS) → Cell Controller (DNC) → Machine Control Unit (CNC).
- **Closed-loop layout** is the geometric embodiment of routing flexibility.
- **AMH technologies** to remember: Conveyor, AGV, Industrial Robot — each with different flexibility/cost trade-offs.
- **AS/RS** is what enables *lights-out (unattended) manufacturing* — a hallmark of an FMS.
- **Flexibility Index** $F_{i} = \sum w_{j} F_{j}$ — target $F_{i} \geq 0.7$ for a well-designed FMS.
- **Communication protocols:** MAP (legacy), Ethernet/IP, PROFINET, OPC-UA (modern).
- **WIP Reduction:** 60–80% lower than a job shop; **Machine Utilization:** 75–90%.
- **Real-world applications** to quote in answers: Aerospace (airframe ribs), Automotive (gearbox housings), Medical (orthopedic implants), Defense (limited-batch critical parts).
- **Key exam keywords:** "Part Family," "Group Technology," "Mixed Model Production," "Lights-out Manufacturing," "Routing Flexibility," "Hierarchical Control."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Integrated Manufacturing - Need for AI and Expert Systems in CIM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Integrated Manufacturing - Need for AI and Expert Systems in CIM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** is the manufacturing approach in which the entire production process is orchestrated by an integrated network of computer systems, encompassing **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)**, **Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)**, **Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP)**, **Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP-II)**, **Shop Floor Control (SFC)**, and **Quality Control (CAQ)** subsystems, all linked via a common database and communication backbone.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, CIM is viewed as a **holistic philosophy** of automation rather than merely a sum of isolated automated machines. The central idea is the **"single source of truth"** — where the design data, process plan, scheduling data, and quality data are all derived from one unified digital model of the product and the enterprise.

### 1.2 Artificial Intelligence (AI) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is the branch of computer science concerned with developing systems that can perform tasks that, when executed by humans, require intelligence such as reasoning, learning, perception, and decision-making.

Within CIM, AI is not a replacement for traditional algorithms; it is a **decision-support layer** that handles unstructured, ambiguous, or knowledge-intensive problems that deterministic programs cannot solve efficiently.

### 1.3 Expert Systems — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> An **Expert System (ES)** is a computer program that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert in a narrow, well-defined domain by applying a structured **knowledge base** through an **inference engine** to produce expert-level conclusions and recommendations.

In CIM, expert systems are used to capture the **tacit knowledge** of experienced process planners, quality inspectors, and maintenance engineers and make it available 24/7 on the shop floor.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory Librarian"

Imagine a large, modern library (the factory) where thousands of books (machines) constantly need to be retrieved, reshelved, repaired, and organized.

- **CIM** is the **library building** — the integrated physical and digital infrastructure that houses all operations.
- **Databases and networks** are the **card catalog system** — instantly locating any item.
- **Traditional software (MRP, CAM)** is the **routine clerical staff** — they follow fixed rules perfectly, but cannot handle novel situations.
- **AI** is the **head librarian** — someone who can reason, learn, and handle unusual requests.
- **Expert System** is a **specialist consultant librarian** — say, an expert in repairing 19th-century manuscripts. This consultant has been trained on all the problems previous experts solved, and can apply that knowledge to a new damaged manuscript instantly.

Just as the consultant librarian saves the library from having to wait for a rare human expert every time something unusual happens, an expert system in CIM saves a factory from depending on the rare human expert for every non-routine decision.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in CIM (KTU 2024 Highlights)

- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** typically targeted at **≥ 85%** in world-class CIM.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** **hours** between successive failures.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** **hours** required to restore a system.
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio:** times per year inventory is replenished.
- **Cycle Time:** time from raw material input to finished product output.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does CIM Need AI? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

Traditional automation (PLCs, NC machines, robots) excels at **structured, repetitive, deterministic** tasks. However, modern manufacturing throws up many problems that are **semi-structured or unstructured**:

1. **Diagnosis of complex machine faults** — symptoms are noisy, multiple faults may coexist.
2. **Process planning for new parts** — there is no single correct plan; trade-offs are needed.
3. **Scheduling under disruptions** — breakdowns, rush orders, tool wear.
4. **Quality interpretation** — pattern recognition on SPC charts, visual inspection.
5. **Predictive maintenance** — forecasting failures from historical sensor data.

These tasks require **reasoning under uncertainty**, **learning from experience**, and **heuristic rule application** — exactly the strengths of AI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Takeaway:** The four primary needs for AI in CIM are: (1) **Intelligent Decision Making**, (2) **Knowledge Preservation**, (3) **Adaptive Control**, and (4) **Pattern Recognition**.

### 2.2 Why Does CIM Need Expert Systems Specifically?

| # | Problem in CIM | Why an Expert System Solves It |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rare human expertise (retirement, attrition) | Captures rules in a permanent knowledge base |
| 2 | Inconsistent decisions across shifts | Provides uniform, repeatable expert-level advice |
| 3 | 24/7 availability required | ES never sleeps, never tires |
| 4 | Hazardous diagnostics (e.g., nuclear plant valves) | ES can advise in environments unsafe for humans |
| 5 | Complex multi-factor troubleshooting | Inference engine can chain dozens of rules rapidly |

### 2.3 Components of an Expert System — Step-by-Step Logic

An expert system in CIM typically contains:

1. **Knowledge Base (KB):** Stores domain facts and **IF–THEN rules** (e.g., `IF spindle_vibration > 5mm/s AND temperature > 80°C THEN bearing_failure_likely`).
2. **Inference Engine (IE):** The "reasoning brain" that applies rules to working memory using **forward chaining** (data → conclusion) or **backward chaining** (hypothesis → evidence).
3. **Working Memory (WM):** Holds current facts about the problem.
4. **User Interface (UI):** Allows the operator to input symptoms and receive advice, including **explanation facilities** (`WHY` and `HOW`).
5. **Knowledge Acquisition Module:** The interface through which the human expert updates the KB.
6. **Explanation Subsystem:** Justifies conclusions to the user — critical for **operator trust** in CIM.

### 2.4 Architecture of CIM — Where AI/ES Fits

A typical CIM architecture (Kusiak's 6-level model) integrates:

- **Level 0 — Process:** Sensors, actuators.
- **Level 1 — Direct Control:** PLCs, DCS, CNC.
- **Level 2 — Supervisory:** SCADA, HMI.
- **Level 3 — Manufacturing Operations Management (MOM):** MES, scheduling, dispatching.
- **Level 4 — Business Planning:** ERP, MRP-II.
- **Level 5 — Enterprise/Strategic.**

**AI and Expert Systems typically operate at Levels 2 and 3**, providing intelligent supervisory control, fault diagnosis, scheduling optimization, and process planning.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Application in CIM |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Where A = Availability, P = Performance, Q = Quality (all decimal fractions) |
| 2 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Uptime fraction |
| 3 | Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{transport}$ | Total manufacturing lead time |
| 4 | Expert System Confidence (Certainty Factor) | $\text{CF}(h,e) = \text{MB}(h,e) - \text{MD}(h,e)$ | Where MB = Measure of Belief, MD = Measure of Disbelief, range $\vert CF \vert \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Bayesian Update (Expert Reasoning) | $P(h \vert e) = \dfrac{P(e \vert h) \cdot P(h)}{P(e)}$ | Updating belief in hypothesis h given evidence e |
| 6 | Manufacturing Lead Time | $\text{MLT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i + W_i)$ | Q = queue time, W = wait time per stage |
| 7 | Inventory Turnover | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Resource efficiency |
| 8 | Fuzzy Membership (for AI in CIM) | $\mu_A(x) \in [0,1]$ | Degree of belonging of input x to fuzzy set A |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Do not confuse **AI** (broad field) with **Expert Systems** (one specific branch of AI). Other branches like Neural Networks, Fuzzy Logic, and Genetic Algorithms are also AI but are NOT expert systems unless rule-based.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Siemens Amberg Electronics Plant (Germany):** ~75% automation, uses AI-based quality inspection achieving ~99.99885% quality — essentially zero defects.
- **General Electric Predix Platform:** Uses AI expert-system-style rule engines for **predictive maintenance** of jet engines and gas turbines.
- **Boeing Wire Harness Assembly:** Expert systems assist technicians in routing thousands of wires correctly per aircraft.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Computing OEE for a CIM Cell

**Problem Statement:** A CNC machining cell operates for **2 shifts × 8 hours = 16 hours/day** (Planned Production Time, PPT). During a 30-day month, the recorded data is:

- Downtime due to breakdowns: **18 hours**
- Downtime due to setup/changeover: **22 hours**
- Standard cycle time per part: **2 minutes/part**
- Actual parts produced: **9,500 parts**
- Defective parts: **150 parts**

**Find: Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE.**

---

#### Step 1: Compute Operating Time

$$
T_{op} = \text{PPT} - (\text{Breakdown} + \text{Setup}) = (16 \times 30) - (18 + 22)
$$

$$
T_{op} = 480 - 40 = 440 \text{ hours}
$$

This is the actual time the cell was available to produce good output.

---

#### Step 2: Compute Availability (A)

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{op}}{\text{PPT}} = \dfrac{440}{480} = 0.9167 \;\; (\approx 91.67\%)
$$

*Intuition:* The cell was available 91.67% of the planned time. The remaining 8.33% was lost to breakdowns and setups.

---

#### Step 3: Compute the Theoretical Maximum Production (Ideal Output)

Total operating time converted to minutes:

$$
T_{op\_min} = 440 \times 60 = 26{,}400 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Ideal Output} = \dfrac{T_{op\_min}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{26{,}400}{2} = 13{,}200 \text{ parts}
$$

*Intuition:* If the cell had run at standard cycle time with zero slowdowns, it would have produced 13,200 parts.

---

#### Step 4: Compute Performance (P)

$$
P = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Ideal Output}} = \dfrac{9{,}500}{13{,}200} = 0.7197 \;\; (\approx 71.97\%)
$$

*Intuition:* The cell achieved only 71.97% of its theoretical speed, indicating significant slow cycles, idling, or minor stoppages not classified as breakdowns.

---

#### Step 5: Compute Quality (Q)

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Actual Output}} = \dfrac{9{,}500 - 150}{9{,}500} = \dfrac{9{,}350}{9{,}500} = 0.9842 \;\; (\approx 98.42\%)
$$

*Intuition:* 98.42% of parts produced were first-quality.

---

#### Step 6: Compute Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9167 \times 0.7197 \times 0.9842
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9167 \times 0.7197 = 0.6598
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.6598 \times 0.9842 = 0.6494 \;\; (\approx 64.94\%)
$$

*Interpretation:* A world-class OEE is **≥ 85%**. This cell at 64.94% has significant room for AI-driven improvement: AI could analyze the slow-cycle causes, and an expert system could recommend the optimal setup parameters.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping CIM Problems to AI/ES Solutions

| CIM Problem | AI Technique Used | Specific ES Application Example | Expected Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle bearing failure diagnosis | Rule-based ES + Fuzzy Logic | `IF vibration>5 AND temp>80 THEN bearing_fault (CF=0.85)` | MTTR reduction 30-50% |
| Job-shop scheduling | Genetic Algorithm + Heuristic ES | Priority rules, bottleneck dispatching | 15-25% throughput increase |
| Visual inspection of weld defects | Neural Network (CNN) | Classify defects: crack / porosity / spatter | Defect detection >99% |
| Process parameter selection | Fuzzy Expert System | Input: material hardness, desired finish → Output: feed, speed, DOC | First-pass yield +20% |
| Predictive maintenance | ML + ES (hybrid) | Forecast RUL (Remaining Useful Life) | Unplanned downtime -40% |
| CAPP (process planning) | Rule-based ES | Select machining operations, tools, sequencing | Planning time -60% |
| Energy optimization | Reinforcement Learning | Schedule machines for off-peak power | Energy cost -10-25% |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Certainty Factor Calculation in an ES

A CIM expert system is diagnosing **tool wear** based on two pieces of evidence:

- Evidence $e_1$: Surface roughness increased. This raises belief in tool wear: $\text{MB}(h,e_1) = 0.7$, $\text{MD}(h,e_1) = 0.1$
- Evidence $e_2$: Coolant color darkened. $\text{MB}(h,e_2) = 0.5$, $\text{MD}(h,e_2) = 0.3$

**Step 1: Compute CF for $e_1$**

$$
\text{CF}_1 = \text{MB}_1 - \text{MD}_1 = 0.7 - 0.1 = 0.6
$$

**Step 2: Compute CF for $e_2$**

$$
\text{CF}_2 = \text{MB}_2 - \text{MD}_2 = 0.5 - 0.3 = 0.2
$$

**Step 3: Combine using the Certainty Factor Combination Formula (MYCIN-style)**

For two CFs of the same hypothesis, the combined CF is:

$$
\text{CF}_{comb} = \text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2 \cdot (1 - \text{CF}_1)
$$

$$
\text{CF}_{comb} = 0.6 + 0.2 \cdot (1 - 0.6)
$$

$$
\text{CF}_{comb} = 0.6 + 0.2 \cdot 0.4 = 0.6 + 0.08 = 0.68
$$

**Interpretation:** The expert system concludes that tool wear is the hypothesis with a certainty of **0.68 (68%)**, which is high enough to recommend immediate tool change.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a CIM System with Integrated AI/ES

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["Level 5 - Enterprise Strategic Planning"] --> L4
    L4["Level 4 - Business Planning ERP and MRP II"] --> L3
    L3["Level 3 - Manufacturing Operations MES and Scheduling"] --> L2
    L2["Level 2 - Supervisory Control SCADA and HMI"] --> L1
    L1["Level 1 - Direct Control PLCs CNC and Robots"] --> L0
    L0["Level 0 - Physical Process Sensors and Actuators"]

    AIE1["AI Engine - Neural Networks and Fuzzy Logic"] -.-> L2
    AIE2["Expert System Module"] -.-> L3
    AIE3["Predictive Maintenance AI"] -.-> L1
    AIE4["Computer Aided Process Planning CAPP"] -.-> L3

    DB[("Common CIM Database - Single Source of Truth")] --- L2
    DB --- L3
    DB --- L4
    DB --- AIE1
    DB --- AIE2
    DB --- AIE3
    DB --- AIE4
```

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Manufacturing Expert System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER["Factory Operator / Engineer"] --> UI["User Interface - HMI Console"]
    UI --> WM["Working Memory - Current Facts and Symptoms"]
    KB[("Knowledge Base - IF THEN Rules and Heuristics")] --> IE
    IE["Inference Engine - Forward and Backward Chaining"] --> WM
    IE --> EXPL["Explanation Subsystem - WHY and HOW Justifier"]
    EXPL --> UI
    UI --> USER
    KA["Knowledge Acquisition Module"] --> KB
    EXPERT["Domain Expert - Process Planner / Maintenance Engineer"] --> KA
    SENSORS["Shop Floor Sensors and PLC Tags"] --> WM
    IE --> REC["Recommendation Output - Action Advice"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: AI-Assisted Decision in CIM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Acquire Data from Shop Floor Sensors"] --> S2
    S2["Step 2: Preprocess and Normalize Sensor Streams"] --> S3
    S3["Step 3: Pattern Recognition via Neural Network or Fuzzy Classifier"] --> S4
    S4{"Step 4: Is Pattern Recognized as Known Fault?"}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5["Step 5: Trigger Expert System Rule Engine"]
    S4 -- No --> S6["Step 6: Log as Unknown - Trigger Learning Module"]
    S5 --> S7["Step 7: Forward Chain Rules to Determine Root Cause"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Compute Certainty Factor of Diagnosis"]
    S8 --> S9{"Step 9: CF above Threshold?"}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10["Step 10: Recommend Corrective Action to Operator"]
    S9 -- No --> S11["Step 11: Escalate to Human Expert"]
    S6 --> S12["Step 12: Update Knowledge Base after Human Validation"]
    S12 --> S5
    S10 --> S13["Step 13: Log Outcome for Continuous Improvement"]
    S11 --> S13
    S13 --> S1
```

### 4.4 KTU 2024 Block-Level Mapping — CIM Subsystems and AI/ES Role

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAD["CAD - Computer Aided Design"] --> CAPP["CAPP - Process Planning AI and ES used here"]
    CAPP --> CAM["CAM - Computer Aided Manufacturing"]
    CAM --> SFC["SFC - Shop Floor Control AI scheduling used here"]
    SFC --> CAQ["CAQ - Computer Aided Quality AI vision and ES diagnosis"]
    CAQ --> MRP["MRP II - Resource Planning"]
    MRP --> ERP["ERP - Enterprise Resource Planning"]
    CAPP -.-> AIFB["AI and Expert System Layer"]
    SFC -.-> AIFB
    CAQ -.-> AIFB
    AIFB -.-> KB2[("Shared Knowledge Base and Database")]
    CAD -.-> KB2
    CAM -.-> KB2
    MRP -.-> KB2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Computer Integrated Manufacturing. List any **four** functional subsystems of CIM.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) is a manufacturing philosophy in which the entire production operation — from product design to dispatch — is controlled and integrated through computer systems, with all subsystems sharing a common database for seamless data flow.

**Four Functional Subsystems (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **CAD** – Computer-Aided Design for product modelling.
2. **CAM** – Computer-Aided Manufacturing for NC programming and machine control.
3. **CAPP** – Computer-Aided Process Planning for route and operation sheet generation.
4. **CAQ** – Computer-Aided Quality Assurance for inspection and statistical process control.

*(Alternative valid subsystems: MRP-II, SFC, MES, ERP.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State **any four** reasons why Artificial Intelligence is needed in CIM.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Intelligent Decision Making:** AI handles unstructured decisions (e.g., fault diagnosis) that cannot be reduced to a fixed algorithm.
2. **Knowledge Preservation:** Expert systems capture the know-how of retiring experts, preventing loss of tribal knowledge.
3. **Adaptive Control:** AI enables machines to adjust parameters in real time (e.g., fuzzy controllers in CNC).
4. **Pattern Recognition:** Neural networks detect subtle quality defects, vibrations, or tool-wear signatures invisible to threshold-based systems.

*(Valid alternatives: Predictive maintenance, scheduling optimization, vision-based inspection, natural-language shop-floor reporting.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model – 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an Expert System** as used in CIM. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of **Inference Engine** and **Certainty Factors** in handling uncertain manufacturing data. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Expert System Architecture (7 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: A well-labelled diagram is worth **3 marks**; explanation of components is worth **4 marks**.

An expert system in CIM consists of the following interconnected modules:

| Component | Function | Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Base (KB)** | Stores domain rules in IF–THEN form, facts, and heuristics. | 1 |
| **Inference Engine (IE)** | Applies rules to working memory via forward or backward chaining. | 1 |
| **Working Memory (WM)** | Holds current problem facts and intermediate conclusions. | 0.5 |
| **User Interface (UI)** | Bidirectional communication with operator. | 0.5 |
| **Explanation Facility** | Answers `WHY` and `HOW` to build operator trust. | 0.5 |
| **Knowledge Acquisition Module** | Lets the human expert update the KB without programmer help. | 0.5 |
| **Block Diagram** | Mermaid or hand-drawn showing KB ↔ IE ↔ WM ↔ UI. | 3 |

**Application in CIM:** A typical use is **machine-fault diagnosis** — operators enter observed symptoms (vibration, noise, temperature) and the ES returns probable causes ranked by certainty.

---

**Part (b) — Inference Engine and Certainty Factors (7 Marks):**

**Inference Engine (3.5 Marks):**

The inference engine is the "reasoning brain" of the ES. It uses two primary strategies:

- **Forward Chaining (Data-Driven):** Starts from known facts and applies rules until a goal is reached.
  - *CIM Example:* Sensors report `spindle_temp = 85°C` and `vibration = 6 mm/s`. Rules fire → conclusion: `bearing_fault (CF=0.7)`.

- **Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven):** Starts from a hypothesis and seeks evidence to support or refute it.
  - *CIM Example:* Hypothesis `coolant_pump_failure`. Engine queries operator: "Is there a pressure drop?" "Is there a leak?"

The engine uses a **conflict resolution strategy** to decide which rule to fire when multiple rules are eligible (e.g., recency, specificity, priority).

**Certainty Factors (3.5 Marks):**

In real CIM, sensor data is noisy and expert rules are heuristic. Certainty Factors (CF) — popularized by the MYCIN system — express the **degree of belief** in a hypothesis.

$$
\text{CF}(h,e) = \text{MB}(h,e) - \text{MD}(h,e)
$$

Where:
- $\text{MB}$ = Measure of Belief (how much evidence supports hypothesis $h$).
- $\text{MD}$ = Measure of Disbelief (how much evidence refutes $h$).
- $\text{CF} \in [-1, +1]$.

**Combination of two independent pieces of evidence:**

$$
\text{CF}_{comb} = \text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2 \cdot (1 - \text{CF}_1)
$$

*Numerical Example (already derived in Section 3.3):* Two pieces of evidence give $\text{CF}_1 = 0.6$ and $\text{CF}_2 = 0.2$, yielding $\text{CF}_{comb} = 0.68$ — strong enough to recommend tool change.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse MB and MD, OR use Bayesian probability formulas (which require prior probabilities) instead of CF formulas (which are heuristic and do not). Read the question carefully — KTU 2024 questions specifically distinguish between **probabilistic reasoning** and **CF-based reasoning**.

---

**Q3B. [Internal Choice – 14 Marks]**

**(a)** What is **CIM**? Explain the **Kusiak's layered architecture** of CIM with a neat diagram. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **need for AI and Expert Systems in CIM**, with **three real-world industrial examples**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — CIM and Kusiak's Architecture (7 Marks):**

CIM integrates all factory functions through a hierarchical computer network. Kusiak's model (1990) defines **6 levels**:

| Level | Name | Function | AI/ES Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Process | Sensors, actuators | Data acquisition |
| **1** | Direct Control | PLC, CNC, Robot | Closed-loop control |
| **2** | Supervisory | SCADA, HMI | Real-time monitoring; **ES for fault diagnosis** |
| **3** | MOM | MES, scheduling | **AI for scheduling optimization** |
| **4** | Business Planning | MRP-II, ERP | Forecasting |
| **5** | Enterprise | Strategic planning | Long-term capacity AI models |

*(Block diagram worth 2 marks, level-wise explanation 5 marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — Need for AI/ES in CIM (7 Marks — 2.33 per example):**

1. **Predictive Maintenance in a Steel Rolling Mill (2.33 Marks):**
   - Traditional: Run-to-failure or fixed-time maintenance → costly breakdowns.
   - AI/ES Solution: Vibration, temperature, and acoustic sensors feed an ES with rules such as `IF bearing_temp > 90°C AND vibration_rms > 4.5 mm/s THEN alert: replace bearing within 48 hours`.
   - Outcome: 30-40% reduction in unplanned downtime.

2. **Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) in a Job Shop (2.33 Marks):**
   - Traditional: Manual planning by a few senior process planners — bottleneck, inconsistent.
   - AI/ES Solution: Variant or generative CAPP using an ES that selects machines, tools, fixtures, and operations based on part features.
   - Outcome: Planning time reduced by ~60%, plans become consistent.

3. **Vision-Based Quality Inspection in Automotive Painting (2.33 Marks):**
   - Traditional: Human inspectors miss micro-defects; inconsistent across shifts.
   - AI/ES Solution: Convolutional Neural Network classifies paint defects; expert system rules determine whether to rework, touch-up, or scrap.
   - Outcome: Defect detection rate exceeds 99% and is uniform 24/7.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Q3B:**
> 1. Listing AI applications **without naming real industrial systems** — examiner awards 0.5/example for specificity.
> 2. Drawing CIM architecture as a single block (0.5 marks) instead of the **layered pyramid** (2 marks).
> 3. Not distinguishing between **AI** and **Expert Systems** — Q3B(a) tests this distinction; lose 1 mark if confused.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **CIM** = Total integration of factory functions through computers, with a **common database** as the backbone.
- **AI in CIM** is needed for **unstructured decisions**: fault diagnosis, scheduling, vision, process planning, predictive maintenance.
- **Expert System** is a rule-based AI system with three core parts: **Knowledge Base + Inference Engine + User Interface**, plus explanation and acquisition modules.
- **Inference Engine** uses **forward chaining** (data → conclusion) or **backward chaining** (hypothesis → evidence), with **conflict resolution** strategies.
- **Certainty Factor** $\text{CF} = \text{MB} - \text{MD}$, range $[-1, +1]$; combines via $\text{CF}_{comb} = \text{CF}_1 + \text{CF}_2(1 - \text{CF}_1)$.
- **Kusiak's CIM model** has **6 levels (0-5)**; AI/ES typically operate at **Levels 2 and 3**.
- **OEE = A × P × Q**; world-class benchmark **≥ 85%**.
- **AI ≠ Expert Systems**: AI is the umbrella (NN, Fuzzy, GA, ES); ES is the **rule-based reasoning** branch.
- **Real-world CIM AI examples**: Siemens Amberg, GE Predix, Boeing wire harness ES, automotive paint-vision systems.
- For KTU 2024 14-mark answers: always include a **labelled diagram (2-3 marks)** and **three concrete examples (5-6 marks)** plus a **summary table (1-2 marks)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Assembly System - Flexible Assembly Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUTUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Automated Assembly System - Flexible Assembly Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Automated Assembly System (AAS)

An **Automated Assembly System (AAS)** is a fully integrated, mechanized manufacturing cell in which discrete component parts are automatically fed, oriented, transported, joined, inspected, and discharged to form a complete, functional sub-assembly or finished product with **no direct human intervention** in the joining or inspection operation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an AAS is classified as a "Transfer-Line-equivalent for discrete products" — analogous to a flow line in machining, but applied to assembly tasks such as snap-fits, screwing, welding, riveting, adhesive bonding, and press-fitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An *automated* assembly system must execute at least **two** of the following operations automatically: (1) parts feeding, (2) orientation, (3) joining, (4) inspection. If only one is automated, it is a *semi-automated* workstation, not a true AAS.

### 1.2 Flexible Assembly Automation (FAA)

**Flexible Assembly Automation (FAA)** is a subset of AAS in which the system is **programmable, reconfigurable, and capable of producing a defined family of product variants** (different sizes, colors, models) on the same physical line with **minimal changeover time** and **no hard-tooling replacement**.

The flexibility is delivered through:
- **Programmable Industrial Robots** (SCARA, 6-axis articulated, Cartesian gantries)
- **Vision-guided part recognition**
- **Programmable fixtures and grippers**
- **Software-driven sequence control** (PLC + SCADA / supervisory computer)
- **Quick-change tooling (QCT)** and standardized end-effectors

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Modular Kitchen Factory**
> Imagine a kitchen factory that today assembles a 2-burner gas stove, tomorrow a 4-burner model, and next month a glass-top induction cooktop. The conveyor, robots, and inspection cameras remain the **same physical hardware** — only the **software recipe (program), gripper fingers, and feeder bowl settings** change. That is **Flexible Assembly Automation**. A *dedicated* assembly line, in contrast, can only build *one* model — change the product and you must rebuild the line.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Benchmarks)

The following performance indices are universally used to grade an FAA:

- **Cycle Time ($T_c$):** Time between two successive completed units at the system output. Units: **seconds/unit**.
- **Production Rate ($R_p$):** Units produced per unit time. $R_p = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$. Units: **units/hour**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Average uptime between failures. **MTBF ≥ 200 hours** is the KTU benchmark for a "reliable" FAA.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Average downtime per failure. **MTTR ≤ 30 minutes** is the benchmark.
- **Availability ($A$):** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$. Industrial target: **A ≥ 95%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Flexibility in an FAA (KTU 5-Mark Favorite)

The literature (Groover, 2015 — primary KTU reference) defines **four** distinct flexibilities. The student is expected to know each:

1. **Machine Flexibility** — The system can produce a new product variant without major hardware modification.
2. **Product Flexibility** — The system can switch between different products in the same family without retooling (changeover < 10% of cycle time).
3. **Routing Flexibility** — Multiple alternative paths exist for a part to travel between workstations; if one station fails, production can be rerouted.
4. **Volume Flexibility** — Production rate can be varied up or down without proportional cost penalty.

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Automation in Assembly

Assembly systems are classified by the **degree of automation** (Groover's six-level hierarchy, KTU Module 1 syllabus):

| Level | System Type | Worker Role | Typical Use |
|:-----:|:------------|:------------|:------------|
| 1 | Manual Assembly | Performs all tasks | Low-volume, custom |
| 2 | Fixed-Automation Assembly | Feeds parts only | High-volume, single product |
| 3 | **Flexible (Programmable) Assembly** | Monitors | Mixed-model medium volume |
| 4 | **Integrated Flexible Assembly** | Supervises via HMI | High-mix low-to-medium volume |
| 5 | Adaptive / Intelligent Assembly | Strategic decisions | R&D prototyping |
| 6 | Fully Autonomous Assembly | None | Lights-out manufacturing |

### 2.3 Why and How Each FAA Component Works

- **Why industrial robots?** Because they are *reprogrammable in 3D space*, providing the 6 degrees-of-freedom needed for arbitrary insertion trajectories.
- **Why vision systems?** Because a flexible system cannot use mechanical jigs for *every* part; cameras identify variant and orientation in real-time.
- **Why programmable fixtures?** To support multiple product geometries on the same nest.
- **Why PLC + SCADA?** The PLC handles deterministic real-time I/O (millisecond response), while SCADA handles recipe management, data logging, and OEE dashboards.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize the units and the symbol definitions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $N_{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_c} \right\rceil$ | Minimum number of workstations | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot T_c} \times 100\%$ | Line balancing efficiency | % |
| 3 | $\beta = 1 - \eta$ | Balancing delay / idle fraction | fraction |
| 4 | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{T_c} \times 3600$ | Hourly production rate | units/hr |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System availability | fraction |
| 6 | $T_{u} = A \cdot T_{s}$ | Effective uptime in a shift | hours |
| 7 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_0 + C_v \cdot N + C_{\text{tooling}}$ | Total annual cost | ₹/year |
| 8 | $N_B = \dfrac{C_2 - C_0}{C_1 - C_0}$ | Break-even analysis (flexible vs dedicated) | units/year |

**Symbol glossary:**
- $t_i$ = task time for the $i$-th assembly operation
- $T_c$ = desired cycle time
- $N$ = actual number of stations assigned
- $N_{\min}$ = theoretical minimum (upper rounded)
- $\eta$ = efficiency (0 to 1, or expressed as %)
- $R_p$ = production rate
- $A$ = availability (0 to 1)
- $N_B$ = break-even annual production volume

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

FAA is the backbone of:
- **Automotive final assembly** (Tesla, Tata, Toyota — mixed model lines)
- **Consumer electronics** (Apple iPhone — 7 variants on one line)
- **Medical devices** (Syringe assembly, IV-set assembly)
- **PCB population and module assembly**
- **White goods** (washing machines, refrigerators)

A typical FAA cell achieves **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) of 75–85%**, against a 60% OEE in dedicated lines, due to the higher availability and performance recovery options.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Workstations

Let us rigorously derive Equation (1) of the cheat sheet.

**Given:**
- A product requires $n$ distinct assembly tasks, $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$, with execution times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ (seconds).
- The desired market output requires a cycle time $T_c$ (seconds).

**Step 1 — Total work content.**
The total time to complete *one* unit, ignoring parallelism, is the arithmetic sum:

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i
$$

This represents the cumulative human/robot effort *if the tasks were executed sequentially by a single operator*.

**Step 2 — Physical lower bound on stations.**
A single workstation cannot be assigned more than $T_c$ seconds of work, otherwise the cycle time is violated. Therefore, the *minimum integer* number of workstations needed to hold $T_{\text{sum}}$ seconds of work within $T_c$-second buckets is:

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{T_{\text{sum}}}{T_c} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Ceiling operation.**
Since the number of stations is discrete (you cannot install 3.4 stations — only 3 or 4), we apply the **ceiling function**:

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_c} \right\rceil
$$

The ceiling is mandatory — a non-integer result is rounded **up**, never down, because rounding down would violate the cycle time constraint.

### 3.2 Derivation: Line Balancing Efficiency

Once $N$ stations are *actually* assigned (with $N \geq N_{\min}$), the line is balanced when each station's load $L_k$ satisfies $L_k \leq T_c$.

The **balancing efficiency** is the ratio of productive work to available work-time across all stations:

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{\text{sum}}}{N \cdot T_c}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta(\%) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{N \cdot T_c} \times 100
$$

The **balancing delay** (idle time fraction) is the complement:

$$
\beta = 1 - \eta
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Exam Problem)

**Problem statement:**
An electronic module requires 8 assembly operations with the following times (in seconds):

$$
T = \{20, 25, 30, 15, 22, 28, 18, 24\}
$$

The marketing demand mandates a cycle time of **45 seconds**. Compute:
(a) the minimum number of stations,
(b) one valid line balance,
(c) the balancing efficiency,
(d) the balancing delay.

**Step (a) — Total work content:**

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 20 + 25 + 30 + 15 + 22 + 28 + 18 + 24
$$

Adding term by term:

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 45 + 30 + 15 + 22 + 28 + 18 + 24
$$

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 75 + 15 + 22 + 28 + 18 + 24
$$

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 90 + 22 + 28 + 18 + 24
$$

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 112 + 28 + 18 + 24
$$

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 140 + 18 + 24
$$

$$
T_{\text{sum}} = 158 + 24 = 182 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step (a) — Apply ceiling formula:**

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{182}{45} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.044 \rceil = 5 \text{ stations}
$$

**Note on rounding down:** A naive student might write $N_{\min} = 4$. This is **wrong**, because 4 stations would only allow $4 \times 45 = 180$ seconds of work, which is **less** than the 182-second requirement. The 2-second deficit would cause a cycle-time violation on every unit — a critical marking error.

**Step (b) — Construct a valid balance:**
A simple greedy (Longest Processing Time first) allocation, respecting the 45-second cap:

| Station | Tasks Assigned | Total Time (s) | Idle Time (s) |
|:-------:|:---------------|:--------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | 25 + 20 | 45 | 0 |
| 2 | 30 + 15 | 45 | 0 |
| 3 | 22 + 18 | 40 | 5 |
| 4 | 28 | 28 | 17 |
| 5 | 24 | 24 | 21 |
| **Total** | **8 tasks** | **182** | **43** |

**Step (c) — Balancing efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{182}{5 \times 45} \times 100 = \frac{182}{225} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = 0.80889 \times 100 = 80.89\%
$$

**Step (d) — Balancing delay:**

$$
\beta = 1 - 0.80889 = 0.19111 = 19.11\%
$$

### 3.4 Production Rate and Availability Example

Using the above balanced line with an **availability A = 0.95** (typical FAA) and a **shift length of 8 hours**:

**Effective cycle output per hour:**

$$
R_p = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{45} = 80 \text{ units/hr (theoretical)}
$$

**Effective operational hours in one shift:**

$$
T_u = A \times T_s = 0.95 \times 8 = 7.6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Realized production per shift:**

$$
Q_{\text{shift}} = R_p \times T_u = 80 \times 7.6 = 608 \text{ units/shift}
$$

**OEE (assuming a quality rate of 99%):**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality} = 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 0.99 = 0.9405 = 94.05\%
$$

This is a **world-class** OEE, justifying the FAA investment in a high-mix, medium-volume scenario.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Flexible Assembly System

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the *functional architecture* of a typical FAA cell, showing all data, material, and control flows between subsystems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUP[SUPERVISORY COMPUTER SCADA HMI] --> PLC[PLC MASTER CONTROLLER]
    PLC --> CONV[CONVEYOR AND INDEXING TABLE]
    PLC --> ROB1[ROBOT 1 SCARA PICK AND PLACE]
    PLC --> ROB2[ROBOT 2 SIX AXIS ASSEMBLY]
    PLC --> VISION[VISION SYSTEM INSPECTION]
    PLC --> FIX[PROGRAMMABLE FIXTURE]
    PLC --> DISP[DISPATCH CONVEYOR OUT]
    FEED[PARTS FEEDER BOWL AND HOPPER] --> CONV
    CONV --> FIX
    FIX --> ROB1
    ROB1 --> ROB2
    ROB2 --> VISION
    VISION --> DISP
    VISION --> PLC
    SAF[SAFETY PLC E STOP LIGHT CURTAIN] --> PLC
    PLC --> SAF
    REC[RECIPE DATABASE SERVER] --> SUP
    SUP --> REC
    MES[SHOP FLOOR MES ERP LINK] --> SUP
    SUP --> MES
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagram

**Material flow (solid black arrows in the production line):**

Parts Feeder → Conveyor → Programmable Fixture → Robot 1 (Pick-and-Place) → Robot 2 (Insertion / Joining) → Vision Inspection → Dispatch Conveyor.

**Control flow (PLC at the center):**
The PLC is the deterministic brain, executing the recipe (sequence of operations) downloaded from the SCADA supervisory computer. Every actuator (conveyor, robots, fixture) is triggered by the PLC and returns status feedback.

**Vision feedback loop:**
The vision system does not just inspect — it *closes the feedback loop* by reporting positional corrections to the PLC, which then re-orients Robot 1's next pick. This is what makes the system *adaptive*.

**Recipe / MES integration:**
The Recipe Database Server stores product-family programs; when the operator changes the SKU on the HMI, the SCADA pushes the new program to the PLC in < 5 seconds — the essence of **product flexibility**.

**Safety overlay:**
The Safety PLC is an independent, hard-wired controller that monitors E-stops, light curtains, and door interlocks. It is a regulatory requirement under ISO 13849 and must remain functional even if the main PLC fails.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*Define Flexible Assembly System. List any four of its characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**
A Flexible Assembly System (FAS) is an automated assembly system capable of producing a variety of product variants within a defined family on the same physical line, by altering the program, grippers, and feeder settings — without major retooling.

Four characteristics:
1. **Programmability** — operations are controlled by software recipes.
2. **Quick changeover** — product-to-product switchover typically < 10% of cycle time.
3. **Multi-product capability** — handles a defined product family.
4. **Integrated inspection** — vision / sensor-based quality checks in-line.
5. *Any other valid point: routing flexibility, self-diagnostics, modular tooling.*

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for four characteristics — ½ mark each.)**

---

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Differentiate between a dedicated assembly system and a flexible assembly system.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Dedicated Assembly System | Flexible Assembly System |
|:----------|:--------------------------|:-------------------------|
| Product variety | Single product | Family of products |
| Changeover | Major — line rebuild | Software recipe change |
| Initial investment | High | Very high |
| Unit cost at high volume | Lowest | Moderate |
| Volume flexibility | Poor | High |
| Typical use | Mass production (e.g., light bulbs) | Mixed-model (e.g., smartphones) |

**(3 marks: 1 mark per correct difference across any three parameters.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> • **Don't** define only the *first* type of flexibility and stop. KTU rubric awards 1–2 marks per flexibility type, so all four MUST be listed.
> • **Don't** forget to convert cycle time to *seconds* before applying the formula. Marks are routinely lost on unit mismatches.
> • **Don't** round $N_{\min}$ *down*. Always use the ceiling function; rounding down is a cycle-time violation and an automatic 1-mark penalty.
> • **Don't** skip the Mermaid / block diagram in design questions. A textual answer without a diagram loses a guaranteed 2–3 marks.
> • **Don't** confuse *balancing efficiency* with *availability*. They are different OEE components.

---

#### Question B — Option A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*Explain the different types of flexibility in an automated assembly system. Discuss how an industrial robot contributes to each type.*

**Model Solution:**

The four types of flexibility (Groover, 2015) are:

1. **Machine Flexibility** — The ability of the system to produce a *new* product variant with minimal setup change. *Robot's role:* A 6-axis articulated robot can be reprogrammed for a new insertion trajectory in minutes via the teach pendant; no new jig is required.

2. **Product Flexibility** — The ability to switch between defined products quickly. *Robot's role:* Quick-Change Tool (QCT) end-effectors allow gripper swap in < 30 seconds; the robot then executes the new product's program from the recipe database.

3. **Routing Flexibility** — Multiple paths for the workpart through the system. *Robot's role:* The robot can pick from *either* of two conveyors (a primary and a bypass) depending on which downstream station is free, preventing line blockage.

4. **Volume Flexibility** — Scaling output up or down without proportional cost. *Robot's role:* The robot's speed and acceleration profiles are software-tunable; reducing conveyor speed directly scales the throughput without mechanical alteration.

**(7 marks: 1.5 per type + 0.5 for synthesis = 7. Mark allocation per type: 0.5 for definition, 0.75 for robot's role, 0.25 for example.)**

---

**(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Flexible Assembly System. List any four hardware components.*

**Model Solution:**

A flexible assembly system is organized as a **centralized PLC-controlled cell** with the following subsystems (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the full architecture):

1. **Parts Feeding Subsystem** — Vibratory bowl feeders, hopper elevators, magazine loaders. *Purpose:* Deliver oriented parts to the pick-point.
2. **Material Handling Subsystem** — Servo-conveyor, indexing table, pallet shuttle. *Purpose:* Move the workpart between stations at the prescribed cycle time.
3. **Robotic Assembly Subsystem** — Industrial robot (SCARA for pick-and-place, 6-axis for insertion). *Purpose:* Execute joining operations.
4. **Programmable Fixturing Subsystem** — Servo-driven clamps on a reconfigurable nest. *Purpose:* Hold different product variants.
5. **Vision and Inspection Subsystem** — Industrial cameras, smart sensors. *Purpose:* Verify correct placement and final quality.
6. **Control and Supervisory Subsystem** — PLC + SCADA + MES link. *Purpose:* Real-time control, recipe management, traceability.
7. **Safety Subsystem** — Safety PLC, light curtains, E-stops. *Purpose:* Operator protection per ISO 13849.

**(7 marks: 3 marks for the diagram, 4 marks — 1 mark per each of the four components explained.)**

---

#### Question B — Option B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Option A*

**(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
*Discuss the various components of a flexible automated assembly system with their functions.*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Component | Function |
|:-:|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | **Industrial Robot (SCARA / 6-axis)** | Executes pick-and-place and insertion operations; reprogrammable for multiple variants. |
