**Goal:** Show that MTBF = 1/λ for the exponential model.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of mean (expected) time to failure:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt
$$

*Logic:* The mean of a continuous random variable T is the integral of `t × probability density function`.

**Step 2 — Express the pdf f(t) in terms of R(t):**

$$
f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$

*Logic:* The pdf is the negative derivative of the CDF (1 − R(t)). Differentiating `e^(−λt)` gives `−λ·e^(−λt)`, and the negative sign makes it positive.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the mean integral:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Solve by integration by parts.** Let u = t, dv = λ·e^(−λt)·dt.

Then du = dt, and v = −e^(−λt).

$$
\text{MTBF} = \left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

*Logic:* Integration by parts formula: ∫u·dv = uv − ∫v·du.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first term:**

$$
\left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0
$$

*Logic:* As t → ∞, e^(−λt) decays faster than t grows (L'Hôpital's rule confirms this), so the product → 0. At t = 0, the product is 0.

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second term:**

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

*Logic:* Standard integral of e^(−λt).

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

This is why failure rate and mean time between failures are reciprocals under the exponential model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Availability A = MTBF ÷ (MTBF + MTTR)

**Context:** This is arguably the most important formula for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. It is the steady-state fraction of time a repairable system is operational.

**Step 1 — Define the steady-state cycle:**
A repairable system alternates between two states: **UP** (functioning) and **DOWN** (being repaired). In the long run, the average cycle length is:

$$
T_{cycle} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

*Logic:* The cycle consists of one operational period (MTBF) plus one repair period (MTTR). Over many cycles, these averages hold.

**Step 2 — Define Availability as the fraction of time spent UP:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{Time UP}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

*Logic:* Availability is a ratio of useful time to total time. In the long run, the fraction of time UP is exactly the ratio of UP duration to cycle duration.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> **Question:** A packaging machine has MTBF = 2,000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate its (a) Availability, (b) Annual downtime in hours (assuming 24×7 operation), and (c) The minimum MTBF required to achieve 99% availability if MTTR remains 8 hours.

**Solution:**

**(a) Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008} = 0.9960 = 99.60\%
$$

**(b) Annual downtime:**
Total hours per year = 24 × 365 = **8,760 hours**

$$
\text{Downtime} = 8760 \times (1 - 0.9960) = 8760 \times 0.004 = 35.04 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**(c) Required MTBF for 99% availability:**

$$
0.99 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + 8}
$$

Solving:

$$
0.99 \times (\text{MTBF} + 8) = \text{MTBF}
$$

$$
0.99 \cdot \text{MTBF} + 7.92 = \text{MTBF}
$$

$$
7.92 = \text{MTBF} - 0.99 \cdot \text{MTBF} = 0.01 \cdot \text{MTBF}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{7.92}{0.01} = 792 \text{ hours}
$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* If the company wants 99% uptime, it must improve the MTBF from 2,000 hours to **at least 792 hours under this formula** — wait, that's a *reduction*. The flaw in the question: 99% < 99.6%, so MTBF 2,000 already exceeds the requirement. To go *above* 99.6% (e.g., 99.9%), one would need to *reduce* MTTR, not increase MTBF. The lesson: **higher availability is achieved primarily by reducing MTTR**, because MTTR appears in both numerator and denominator additively.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Trade-offs

| Criterion | Electric Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator | Hydraulic Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Electricity | Compressed air | Pressurized oil |
| **Force/Stroke** | Medium (up to ~50 kN) | Low (up to ~25 kN) | Very High (>100 kN) |
| **Speed** | Variable, precise | Very fast | Moderate, smooth |
| **Precision** | Excellent (servo) | Moderate | High |
| **Cost (CAPEX)** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Cost (OPEX)** | Low | High (compressed air = energy waste) | High (oil, filtration) |
| **Cleanliness** | Clean | Clean (some oil aerosol) | Risk of oil leak |
| **Maintenance** | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Best Use** | Robotics, CNC, packaging | Material handling, light assembly | Presses, injection moulding, heavy machinery |
| **Safety in Hazardous Areas** | Requires Ex-proof enclosure | Intrinsically safe (no spark) | Requires fire-resistant fluid |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Automation Design

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of design considerations** in an industrial automation project — from concept to commissioning. This replaces a physical drawing that would normally show a P&ID or control loop, and instead shows the *engineering decision flow*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Project Inception: New Automation Need]) --> PA[Process Analysis: PFD, P&ID, Process Variables]
    PA --> FS{Feasibility Study}
    FS -->|Technically Viable| ECON[Economic Analysis: ROI, Payback, NPV]
    FS -->|Not Viable| REJECT([Project Rejected / Re-scoped])
    ECON -->|Justified| AL[Automation Level Selection: L0-L4 per ISA-95]
    AL --> CS[Component Selection: Sensors, Actuators, Controllers]
    CS --> SA[Safety and Risk Assessment: ISO 13849, IEC 61508, SIL/PL]
    SA --> INT[Integration: Wiring, Communication, Protocols]
    INT --> FAT[Factory Acceptance Test FAT]
    FAT -->|Passed| SAT[Site Acceptance Test SAT]
    FAT -->|Failed| CS
    SAT -->|Passed| HAND([Commissioning and Handover])
    SAT -->|Failed| INT
    HAND --> REVIEW[30-60-90 Day Performance Review]
    REVIEW --> DONE([System in Production with Continuous Improvement])
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- Each rectangular block is a **design stage**.
- The diamond `FS` is a **decision gate** — only approved projects proceed.
- Dashed-circular nodes are **terminal states** (rejection, completion).
- The feedback loop from `FAT` to `CS` and from `SAT` to `INT` represents the **iterative refinement** that is a hallmark of good engineering design.

## 4.2 The ISA-95 Automation Pyramid — Functional Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L4["LAYER 4 - ENTERPRISE"]
        ERP[ERP System: SAP, Oracle - Long Term Planning]
    end
    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 - MANUFACTURING OPERATIONS"]
        MES[MES: Scheduling, Genealogy, OEE Tracking]
    end
    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 - SUPERVISORY"]
        SCADA[SCADA and HMI: Operator Interface, Alarms, Trends]
    end
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 - BASIC CONTROL"]
        PLC[PLC and DCS Controllers: PID, Ladder, SFC]
    end
    subgraph L0["LAYER 0 - FIELD DEVICES"]
        SENSORS[Sensors: Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level]
        ACTUATORS[Actuators: Motors, Valves, Cylinders]
    end
    ERP <--> MES
    MES <--> SCADA
    SCADA <--> PLC
    PLC <--> SENSORS
    PLC <--> ACTUATORS
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- Information flows **upward** (field data → enterprise) and commands flow **downward** (business decisions → field actuation).
- Each layer has distinct **latency requirements**: Layer 0–1 in **milliseconds**; Layer 4 in **hours/days**.
- **Designers must choose the right layers** to avoid over-engineering or under-engineering.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Automate vs. When NOT to Automate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the process repetitive and high volume?}
    Q1 -->|No| MANUAL[Keep Manual: Custom or Low-Volume Work]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does the process involve hazards to humans?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| AUTOSAFE[Automate for Safety: Robotics, Remote Handling]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Can automation deliver payback within 5 years?}
    Q3 -->|No| SEMI[Semi-Automate: Operator Assists Machine]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Are the required sensors and actuators mature in market?}
    Q4 -->|No| WAIT[Wait for Technology to Mature: Re-evaluate in 2-3 Years]
    Q4 -->|Yes| FULLAUTO[Proceed with Full Automation]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is a **classical decision tree** taught in every Industrial Engineering textbook.
- It captures the wisdom of the **"FRESP" pillars** in flowchart form.
- KTU may ask students to draw or interpret this tree in viva or Part A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**"List and briefly explain any THREE key design considerations in industrial automation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Functional Suitability (1 mark):** The automation system must be capable of executing the required process within specified tolerances for speed, accuracy, and repeatability. A mismatch between process demand and system capability is the leading cause of automation failure.
2. **Economic Viability (1 mark):** The total lifecycle cost (CAPEX + OPEX) must yield a positive ROI within the organizationally acceptable payback period, typically **2 to 5 years**. The designer must also account for hidden costs like training, spares, and software licensing.
3. **Maintainability (1 mark):** The system must be designed for easy diagnosis and repair — measured by **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**. Features like modular I/O, standard connectors, and clear labelling drastically reduce downtime.

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Tip:** If asked for "FIVE" considerations, add **Safety (ISO 13849, IEC 61508)** and **Scalability/Flexibility (open protocols, modular architecture)** to the above three.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"Define OEE. Mention its three components and the world-class benchmark value."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a composite metric that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, expressed as a percentage. *(1 mark)*
- The three multiplicative components are: **Availability**, **Performance**, and **Quality**. *(1 mark)*
- **Formula:** OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality *(0.5 marks)*
- **World-class benchmark** as defined by **SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International)** and industry consensus is **85%**. Typical Indian manufacturing plants operate at **40–60%**. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **five pillars (FRESP) of design considerations** in industrial automation. Discuss how each pillar influences the final system design with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Functionality (1 mark):** The system must execute the intended process correctly. *Example:* A pick-and-place robot must achieve positional repeatability of ±0.02 mm for a PCB assembly task; failure to meet this renders the system non-functional.
2. **Reliability (1.5 marks):** Measured by MTBF, the system should operate without failure for an acceptable period. *Example:* A CNC spindle rated for MTBF ≥ 20,000 hours ensures at least 2 years of two-shift operation before major overhaul.
3. **Economics (1.5 marks):** ROI and payback must be positive. *Example:* A ₹50 lakh welding robot replacing 4 welders at ₹30,000/month each yields annual savings of ₹14.4 lakh, giving a payback of 3.5 years — within the typical 5-year limit.
4. **Safety (1.5 marks):** Must comply with ISO 13849 / IEC 61508. *Example:* A hydraulic press must have a **light curtain + two-hand control + emergency stop** to achieve **PL d / SIL 2**.
5. **Maintainability (1.5 marks):** Measured by MTTR; modular design reduces repair time. *Example:* Using **modular PLC racks** allows swapping a faulty I/O card in 5 minutes vs. 45 minutes for hard-wired systems.

---

**Q. (b)** A conveyor system has MTBF = 5,000 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. The plant operates **two 8-hour shifts, 300 days per year**.
   - (i) Calculate the **Availability**.
   - (ii) Calculate the **Annual Downtime in hours**.
   - (iii) If the maintenance team targets **99.5% availability** without changing the failure rate, what is the **maximum allowable MTTR**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Availability (2 marks):**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 10} = \frac{5000}{5010} = 0.99800
$$

Therefore, **A = 99.80%**.

**(ii) Annual Downtime (2 marks):**

Total operating hours per year = 2 × 8 × 300 = **4,800 hours/year**.

$$
\text{Downtime} = 4800 \times (1 - 0.99800) = 4800 \times 0.002 = 9.6 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**(iii) Required MTTR for 99.5% (3 marks):**

$$
0.995 = \frac{5000}{5000 + \text{MTTR}_{new}}
$$

$$
0.995 \times (5000 + \text{MTTR}_{new}) = 5000
$$

$$
4975 + 0.995 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{new} = 5000
$$

$$
0.995 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{new} = 25
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTR}_{new} = \frac{25}{0.995} \approx 25.13 \text{ hours}}
$$

*Engineering Note:* Since the current MTTR (10 h) is **already less than** 25.13 h, the system already exceeds 99.5% availability. The target is achievable without changes. (Validates design.) To reach 99.9% availability, the team would need to reduce MTTR to 5.0 hours.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With the aid of a **labeled block diagram**, explain the **five levels of the ISA-95 automation pyramid**. Discuss the function and typical equipment used at each level. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** standard defines a **five-level hierarchy** that integrates enterprise and plant-floor systems:

1. **Level 4 — Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) (1.5 marks):** Handles long-term business planning — finance, procurement, supply chain. *Typical systems:* SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics. *Latency:* hours to days.
2. **Level 3 — Manufacturing Operations Management (MES) (1.5 marks):** Bridges ERP and plant floor. Handles scheduling, batch tracking, genealogy, OEE reporting. *Typical systems:* Siemens Opcenter, Rockwell FactoryTalk ProductionCentre. *Latency:* minutes to hours.
3. **Level 2 — Supervisory (SCADA / HMI) (1.5 marks):** Provides the operator's window into the process. Trends, alarms, recipes. *Typical systems:* WinCC, Ignition, Wonderware. *Latency:* seconds.
4. **Level 1 — Basic Control (PLC / DCS) (1.5 marks):** Real-time control logic. PID loops, sequencing, interlocking. *Typical systems:* Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Honeywell Experion. *Latency:* 10–100 ms.
5. **Level 0 — Field Devices (1 mark):** The physical interface — sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, level, proximity) and actuators (motors, valves, cylinders, drives). *Latency:* <10 ms.

*Block Diagram:* Refer to the **Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2** above. (Compulsory for full marks.)

---

**Q. (b)** A factory is considering automating a manual welding process.
- **Current manual cost:** 4 welders × ₹35,000/month × 12 months = **₹16.8 lakh/year**
- **Manual scrap cost:** 6% of production (₹4 lakh/year wasted material)
- **Robot cost (CAPEX):** **₹60 lakh** (including installation, tooling, training)
- **Robot operating cost (OPEX):** **₹4 lakh/year** (power, spares, programming)
- **Robot-induced scrap rate:** 1% (₹0.67 lakh/year)
- **Discount rate:** 10% per annum
- **Useful life:** 10 years

**Calculate:**
   - (i) Net annual savings
   - (ii) Simple payback period
   - (iii) Comment on whether the investment is justified. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Net Annual Savings (2.5 marks):**

Labor savings = ₹16.8 lakh
Scrap savings = ₹4.00 − ₹0.67 = ₹3.33 lakh
Less: Robot OPEX = ₹4.00 lakh

$$
\text{Net Annual Savings} = 16.8 + 3.33 - 4.00 = \boxed{₹16.13 \text{ lakh/year}}
$$

**(ii) Simple Payback Period (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{CAPEX}}{\text{Net Annual Savings}} = \frac{60}{16.13} = 3.72 \text{ years}
$$

**(iii) Justification (2.5 marks):**
- The payback period of **3.72 years** is well within the industry-acceptable 5-year limit. ✅
- Over the 10-year life, the total undiscounted savings = 16.13 × 10 = **₹161.3 lakh**, vastly exceeding the ₹60 lakh CAPEX.
- Even at a 10% discount rate, the **Net Present Value (NPV)** is positive (a rough present-value factor over 10 years at 10% gives an annuity factor of ~6.14, so PV of savings ≈ 16.13 × 6.14 = ₹99.04 lakh; NPV ≈ 99.04 − 60 = **₹39 lakh positive**).
- Additional **non-monetary benefits** (improved safety, consistent weld quality, 24×7 operation, reduced operator fatigue) further strengthen the case.
- **Verdict:** The investment is **strongly justified**. The factory should proceed with the automation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF (–2 marks):** MTTF applies to **non-repairable** items (a light bulb). MTBF applies to **repairable** systems (a motor). Using them interchangeably is a frequent error.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount rate in NPV (–2 marks):** A 14-mark question often requires NPV computation. Students compute simple sum of cash flows and miss the time value of money.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers (–0.5 to –1 mark):** MTBF must be in *hours*, ROI in *%*, payback in *years*. Bare numbers without units are penalized.
> 4. **Confusing OEE components (–1 mark):** Students often list OEE as "Availability + Performance + Quality" (additive). It is **multiplicative**.
> 5. **Not referencing standards in safety answers (–1 mark):** A safety discussion without mentioning **ISO 13849, IEC 61508, or PL/SIL** is incomplete. KTU examiners reward standard citations.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram in hierarchy questions (–2 marks):** A 7-mark question on ISA-95 *explicitly* demands a "labeled block diagram." A text-only answer caps at ~5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Principles and Design Considerations**

- ☐ The **five pillars** of design are **Functionality, Reliability, Economics, Safety, Maintainability (FRESP)**.
- ☐ Every automation project passes through **six stages**: Process Analysis → Feasibility → Level Selection → Component Selection → Safety Assessment → Validation.
- ☐ **The ISA-95 Pyramid** has **5 levels** (L0 Field → L4 ERP); latency increases as you go up.
- ☐ **OEE = A × P × Q**; world-class benchmark is **85%**.
- ☐ **Reliability R(t) = e^(−λt)**; **MTBF = 1/λ** for exponential (constant failure rate) systems.
- ☐ **Availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**; higher availability is achieved mainly by **lowering MTTR**.
- ☐ **Series reliability = product of individual reliabilities**; **Parallel reliability = 1 − product of unreliabilities** (redundancy).
- ☐ **ROI and Payback** are the two minimum economic metrics; **NPV** is preferred for multi-year projects.
- ☐ **Safety standards to remember:** ISO 12100 (general), ISO 13849 (mechanical, PL a–e), IEC 61508 (E/E/PE, SIL 1–4), IEC 62061 (machinery safety), OSHA / Indian Factories Act (regulatory).
- ☐ **Communication protocols to know:** Modbus RTU/TCP, PROFINET, EtherCAT, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA (the modern IIoT standard).
- ☐ **Open vs. proprietary** — always prefer open standards to avoid vendor lock-in.
- ☐ **Redundancy** (parallel) is the standard technique to boost system reliability beyond what a single component can offer.
- ☐ **Design for Maintainability** — modular I/O, standard connectors, colour-coded wiring, clear labelling, and remote diagnostics all reduce MTTR.
- ☐ **Energy efficiency** is increasingly a design metric — VFDs, regenerative drives, and sleep modes cut OPEX.
- ☐ **Cybersecurity** is the newest pillar; IEC 62443 governs industrial network security and is now a KTU 2024 syllabus expectation.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 — Principles and Design Considerations. Students are advised to practise at least 3 numerical problems on ROI/Payback/Availability/MTBF and 2 descriptive answers on FRESP/ISA-95 before the University Examination.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Transport Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Transport Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Transport System (MTS)** is a coordinated arrangement of mechanical, electromechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, and computer-controlled equipment used to move, store, protect, and control the flow of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods within a manufacturing plant, warehouse, or distribution center.

According to the **Kulweic–Hackman Material Handling Wheel**, material handling is broadly classified into **10 functional categories** (Transport, Quantity/Size, Time, Place, Position, Information, Environment, Ergonomics, Control, and Unit Load). In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, MTS is treated as the backbone of any automated or semi-automated production line.

### 1.1.1 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Favourite)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material flow rate | $Q$ | items/hr or kg/hr | Volume of material moved per unit time |
| Conveyor belt speed | $v$ | m/s | Linear velocity of belt |
| Throughput | $T$ | tons/day | Net useful material delivered |
| Utilization factor | $\eta_u$ | % | Actual operating time / Available time |
| Availability | $A$ | % | MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) × 100 |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Corridor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. Patients (raw materials) are continuously admitted at the emergency entrance (receiving dock), pushed on stretchers (conveyors) through corridors, lifted by elevators (vertical lifts) to the operating theatre (workstation), and finally wheeled out into the recovery ward (finished goods). The **lift attendant, stretcher, and elevator** are all *material transport equipment*. Without them, even the best surgeons (machines) are useless.

The **goal** of any MTS is to deliver the right material, in the right quantity, in the right position, at the right time, in the right condition, and at the right cost — the **6R Principle** of material handling.

## 1.3 Why Industrial Automation Needs MTS

- **Bottleneck elimination** between workstations.
- **Labour cost reduction** (up to 40–60% in repetitive transport tasks).
- **Damage reduction** in fragile or hazardous goods.
- **Synchronization** with Just-In-Time (JIT) and lean production.
- **Integration** with PLC, SCADA, and Industry 4.0 IoT layers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Material Transport Systems

Material Transport Systems are classified along **three primary axes**: (i) Mode of operation, (ii) Path of motion, and (iii) Energy source.

### 2.1.1 Based on Mode of Operation

| Type | Description | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous** | Uninterrupted flow at constant rate | Belt conveyors, screw conveyors, pneumatic pipelines |
| **Intermittent (Discrete)** | Stop-and-go, load-by-load | Forklifts, AGVs, cranes, hoists, industrial trucks |

### 2.1.2 Based on Path of Motion

- **Fixed Path** — Conveyors, monorails, chain tracks.
- **Variable (Free) Path** — AGVs, mobile robots, forklifts, trucks.

### 2.1.3 Based on Energy / Power Source

- **Electric** — Motor-driven conveyors, electric hoists.
- **Pneumatic** — Air-vacuum tubes, pneumatic conveyors.
- **Hydraulic** — Hydraulic lifts, dump trucks.
- **Gravitational** — Chutes, gravity roller conveyors.
- **Manual / Human** — Hand trolleys, pallet jacks.

## 2.2 The 10 Principles of Material Handling (KTU Direct Exam Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This framework, originally proposed by the **College-Industry Council on Material Handling Education (CICMHE)**, is frequently asked as a **2-mark short question**.

1. **Orientation Principle** — Plan the operation as a system for cost reduction.
2. **Planning Principle** — Plan before purchasing equipment.
3. **Flow Principle** — Use flow diagrams to integrate handling with production.
4. **Simplification Principle** — Eliminate unnecessary motion.
5. **Gravity Principle** — Utilize gravity where possible.
6. **Space Utilization Principle** — Use cubic space efficiently.
7. **Unit Size Principle** — Increase unit load size.
8. **Mechanization Principle** — Mechanize handling operations.
9. **Equipment Utilization Principle** — Use equipment for full capacity.
10. **Automation Principle** — Automate to reduce labour and improve control.

## 2.3 Selection Criteria for MTS

A transport system is selected based on the following decision matrix:

- **Load characteristics** — weight, size, shape, fragility, hazard class.
- **Path geometry** — straight, curved, vertical lift, multi-floor.
- **Distance** — short (<30 m) vs long-haul (>100 m).
- **Speed requirement** — throughput targets.
- **Environmental** — temperature, dust, cleanliness (e.g., pharma/food).
- **Cost** — CAPEX vs OPEX, payback period.
- **Compatibility** with PLC, SCADA, MES, ERP layers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{m}{t}$ | Material flow rate | kg/s or items/hr |
| 2 | $Q_b = v \cdot w \cdot \rho$ | Volumetric flow on belt conveyor ($w$=width, $\rho$=bulk density) | kg/s |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{T_e \cdot v}{1000}$ | Belt conveyor drive power | kW |
| 4 | $T_e = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4$ | Effective belt tension (sum of tensions) | N |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Drive efficiency | % |
| 6 | $T_{cycle} = T_{load} + T_{travel} + T_{unload} + T_{idle}$ | AGV cycle time | s |
| 7 | $N_{AGV} = \dfrac{\text{Total transport demand}}{Q_{AGV} \cdot \eta_u}$ | Number of AGVs required | dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_{lift} = \dfrac{h}{t_{cycle}}$ | Hoist/lift velocity | m/s |
| 9 | $\Delta P = \rho_{air} \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(Pneumatic)}$ | Pressure drop in pneumatic system | Pa |
| 10 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Throughput}}{\text{Designed capacity}} \times 100$ | Realized throughput ratio | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, examiners **love** questions that ask the student to compute belt power, AGV fleet size, or lift motor sizing. Memorize formulas 3, 6, and 7 — they appear in **>70%** of past Module-2 questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines** (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors plants) use overhead conveyors and skid-return systems.
- **E-commerce warehouses** (Amazon, Flipkart) deploy Kiva-style AGV fleets.
- **Bulk material industries** (cement, steel, mining) use high-capacity belt conveyors — a single 1.6 m wide belt can transport **20,000 tons/hour** of coal.
- **Food & Pharma** use stainless-steel hygienic conveyors integrated with CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems.
- **Hospitals & Airports** deploy automated guided carts and tube-based pneumatic networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Belt Conveyor Throughput

The instantaneous mass flow rate on a belt conveyor depends on the cross-sectional area of the loaded material, its bulk density, and the belt's linear velocity.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a differential element of length $dx$ on the belt. The volume of material on this element is:

$$dV = A \cdot dx$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the material load (m²). For a troughed belt, the cross-section approximates a segment of a circle.

**Step 2:** The differential mass on this element is:

$$dm = \rho \cdot dV = \rho \cdot A \cdot dx$$

where $\rho$ is the bulk density of the material (kg/m³).

**Step 3:** The mass flow rate is the mass passing a fixed point per unit time. As the belt moves with linear velocity $v$ (m/s), in time $dt$ the belt advances $dx = v \cdot dt$.

$$Q = \frac{dm}{dt} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot dt}{dt} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$

**Step 4:** For a flat belt of width $w$ carrying a uniform layer of thickness $h$, $A = w \cdot h$. Therefore:

$$Q = \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

**Step 5:** Converting to a more practical unit (tons per hour) using the conversion factor 1 ton = 1000 kg and 1 hr = 3600 s:

$$Q_{tph} = \frac{3600 \cdot \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v}{1000} = 3.6 \cdot \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Belt Conveyor Drive Power

The drive power of a belt conveyor is the product of the effective belt tension and belt velocity, divided by mechanical efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The effective tension $T_e$ is the difference between the tight-side tension $T_1$ and slack-side tension $T_2$:

$$T_e = T_1 - T_2$$

**Step 2:** The total resistance to motion in a horizontal conveyor (no lift) comprises four major components:
- $T_1$ — Friction in the carrying idlers.
- $T_2$ — Friction in the return idlers.
- $T_3$ — Indentation resistance of the belt.
- $T_4$ — Skewing and material acceleration resistance.

$$T_e = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4$$

**Step 3:** The work done per second (i.e., power) by the drive is:

$$P = T_e \cdot v \quad \text{(W, if SI units)}$$

**Step 4:** To account for drive-train losses (gearbox, coupling, motor), the shaft power must be divided by the overall drive efficiency $\eta$:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{T_e \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU exam:** If lift is involved (inclined conveyor), add a vertical lift term $T_5 = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \cdot L$ to the effective tension, where $\theta$ is the inclination angle and $L$ is the belt length.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — AGV Fleet Size Calculation

In a typical flexible manufacturing system, multiple AGVs operate concurrently to satisfy a fixed transport demand.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Let the total transport demand be $D$ (kg/hr or trips/hr). Let each AGV deliver $Q_{AGV}$ per trip and complete $N_{trips}$ trips per hour.

$$Q_{AGV/hr} = Q_{AGV} \cdot N_{trips}$$

**Step 2:** The number of trips per hour per AGV is the inverse of the cycle time:

$$N_{trips} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$Q_{AGV/hr} = Q_{AGV} \cdot \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 4:** The total demand must be met, considering utilization $\eta_u$:

$$N_{AGV} = \frac{D}{Q_{AGV/hr} \cdot \eta_u} = \frac{D \cdot T_{cycle}}{3600 \cdot Q_{AGV} \cdot \eta_u}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

> **Problem:** A belt conveyor is 50 m long, runs horizontally, and transports sand with bulk density $\rho = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The belt is 0.8 m wide, carrying a uniform 50 mm thick layer at 1.5 m/s. The drive efficiency is 0.85. Calculate (a) the mass flow rate in tph, and (b) the motor power required if the effective tension is 1200 N.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Mass flow rate:**

$$Q_{tph} = 3.6 \cdot \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v$$

$$Q_{tph} = 3.6 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1.5$$

$$Q_{tph} = 3.6 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.06 = 324 \text{ tph}$$

**Part (b) — Motor power:**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{T_e \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta} = \frac{1200 \cdot 1.5}{1000 \cdot 0.85}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{1800}{850} \approx 2.12 \text{ kW}$$

A **3 kW standard motor** is selected (with 30% safety margin as per KTU industrial standards).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Belt Conveyor | AGV | Pneumatic Tube | Industrial Robot |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Path** | Fixed | Free | Fixed | Free |
| **Load Type** | Bulk, packaged | Unit loads | Small parts | Varied |
| **Throughput** | Very High | Medium | Low | Low-Medium |
| **Flexibility** | Low | High | Low | Very High |
| **Initial Cost** | Medium | High | Medium | High |
| **Energy Use** | Low | Medium | High (compression) | High |
| **Best For** | Bulk material | Assembly plants | Pharma/Hospitals | Machining cells |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MTS Architecture in an Automated Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Raw Material Receiving Dock] --> B1[Inbound Conveyor Belt System]
    B1 --> C1[Automated Storage and Retrieval System ASRS]
    C1 --> D1[PLC Controlled Sorting Station]
    D1 --> E1[AGV Fleet Manager]
    E1 --> F1[Workstation 1 Assembly]
    E1 --> F2[Workstation 2 Machining]
    E1 --> F3[Workstation 3 Inspection]
    F1 --> G1[Outbound Pneumatic Conveyor]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Finished Goods Warehouse]
    H1 --> I1[Dispatch and Shipping Dock]
    J1[SCADA HMI Supervisor] -.-> B1
    J1 -.-> C1
    J1 -.-> D1
    J1 -.-> E1
    J1 -.-> G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Types of Material Transport Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MTS[Material Transport Systems] --> M1[Fixed Path]
    MTS --> M2[Variable Free Path]
    MTS --> M3[Vertical Lift Systems]
    M1 --> S1[Belt Conveyor]
    M1 --> S2[Roller Conveyor]
    M1 --> S3[Chain Conveyor]
    M1 --> S4[Screw Conveyor]
    M1 --> S5[Pneumatic Pipeline]
    M2 --> S6[Automated Guided Vehicle AGV]
    M2 --> S7[Mobile Robot AMR]
    M2 --> S8[Forklift Truck]
    M2 --> S9[Automated Cart]
    M3 --> S10[Electric Hoist]
    M3 --> S11[Hydraulic Lift]
    M3 --> S12[Crane Overhead]
    M3 --> S13[Scissor Lift Platform]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — AGV Decision Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Idle at Charging Station] --> P2[Receive Job from Fleet Manager]
    P2 --> P3[Navigate to Pickup Point]
    P3 --> P4{Load Confirmed?}
    P4 -->|No| P3
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Plan Path Avoiding Obstacles]
    P5 --> P6[Travel to Drop off Point]
    P6 --> P7[Unload and Confirm]
    P7 --> P8{Return to Charge?}
    P8 -->|Yes| P1
    P8 -->|No| P2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Conveyor Drive System (KTU Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[AC Induction Motor] --> M2[Gear Reducer Coupling]
    M2 --> M3[Drive Pulley Head]
    M3 --> M4[Belt Loop with Carrying Idlers]
    M4 --> M5[Tail Pulley Take Up]
    M5 --> M6[Return Idlers]
    M6 --> M3
    M7[Tension Sensor] -.-> M3
    M8[Speed Encoder] -.-> M3
    M9[PLC Controller] -.-> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU viva voce, students are often asked to "draw and explain the components of a belt conveyor." The above block diagram is the expected answer structure. Use **labeled arrows**, not just lines, to show **power flow** (solid) and **signal flow** (dotted).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define Material Handling. List any **four** principles of material handling.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Material handling refers to the movement, protection, storage, and control of materials throughout the manufacturing and distribution process. The four principles are:
> (i) **Planning Principle** — Plan all handling activities before purchasing equipment.
> (ii) **Gravity Principle** — Use gravity to reduce energy consumption.
> (iii) **Space Utilization Principle** — Use the cubic volume of the workspace efficiently.
> (iv) **Automation Principle** — Automate to reduce manual effort and improve consistency.
> *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **continuous** and **intermittent** material transport systems with one example each.
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Parameter | Continuous MTS | Intermittent MTS |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Flow** | Uninterrupted | Stop-and-go |
> | **Speed** | Constant | Variable |
> | **Example** | Belt conveyor | Forklift truck |
> | **Best for** | Bulk solids | Discrete unit loads |
> *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **MODULE 2 — Q3. Question A [14 Marks]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the various types of material transport systems used in modern automated industries. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and suitable applications. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* A horizontal belt conveyor 80 m long carries iron ore of bulk density 2000 kg/m³. The belt is 1.0 m wide and the ore layer is uniformly 75 mm thick. The belt speed is 1.8 m/s. Calculate:
> (i) The mass flow rate in tph.
> (ii) The motor power required if the effective tension is 2500 N and drive efficiency is 0.88.
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Types of MTS:**

The major categories are:
1. **Conveyors** — Belt, roller, chain, screw, overhead.
   - *Advantages:* High capacity, low labour, continuous flow.
   - *Limitations:* Fixed path, high installation cost.
   - *Application:* Mining, automotive assembly lines.

2. **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGV)** — Battery-powered, sensor-guided carts.
   - *Advantages:* Flexible path, programmable routes.
   - *Limitations:* Moderate speed, high upfront cost.
   - *Application:* Warehousing (Amazon Kiva), FMS cells.

3. **Cranes and Hoists** — Overhead travelling, gantry, jib cranes.
   - *Advantages:* Vertical and horizontal reach.
   - *Limitations:* Limited to a single load at a time.
   - *Application:* Steel plants, ports, heavy engineering.

4. **Pneumatic Conveyors** — Air-suspended particles in pipelines.
   - *Advantages:* Sealed, hygienic, handles toxic material.
   - *Limitations:* High energy for compression, particle size limited.
   - *Application:* Cement, flour, plastic pellets, pharma powders.

5. **Industrial Robots** — Articulated arms for pick-and-place.
   - *Advantages:* High flexibility, multi-axis motion.
   - *Limitations:* High cost, limited payload.
   - *Application:* Press-shop automation, palletizing.

*(7 marks for the descriptive part)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

$$Q_{tph} = 3.6 \cdot \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v = 3.6 \cdot 2000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.075 \cdot 1.8$$

$$Q_{tph} = 3.6 \cdot 270 = 972 \text{ tph}$$

**(ii) Motor power:**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{T_e \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta} = \frac{2500 \cdot 1.8}{1000 \cdot 0.88}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{4500}{880} \approx 5.11 \text{ kW}$$

A standard **5.5 kW or 7.5 kW motor** is selected. *(7 marks for the numerical part)*

---

> **MODULE 2 — Q3. Question B [14 Marks]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** and its role in Industry 4.0. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* An AGV has a cycle time of 240 s. The transport demand is 180 trips per hour. Each trip carries 500 kg. If the AGV utilization is 80%, calculate the number of AGVs required. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — AS/RS Explanation:**

An **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** is a computer-controlled system that automatically places and retrieves loads from defined storage locations. Major components are:

1. **Storage Racks** — High-bay vertical racks, often 20–40 m tall.
2. **Storage/Retrieval (S/R) Machine** — Crane-like device that moves on rails between aisles.
3. **Conveyor Infeed/Outfeed** — Connects S/R machine to production lines.
4. **Pickup/Delivery Stations** — End points for operators or AGVs.
5. **WMS Software** — Warehouse Management System running inventory and order logic.
6. **Sensors and Encoders** — Barcode readers, RFID, position encoders.

**Role in Industry 4.0:** AS/RS acts as the **physical–digital interface** between IoT-enabled inventory tracking (RFID + cloud WMS) and the manufacturing execution system (MES). Real-time stock visibility, predictive maintenance via vibration sensors, and AI-driven slotting make AS/RS the **nerve centre** of smart warehouses.

*(7 marks for the descriptive part)*

**Part (b) — AGV Fleet Calculation:**

Step 1: Effective trips per AGV per hour:

$$N_{trips} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{240} = 15 \text{ trips/hr}$$

Step 2: Net effective capacity per AGV with utilization:

$$Q_{eff} = 15 \cdot 0.80 = 12 \text{ trips/hr}$$

Step 3: Number of AGVs needed:

$$N_{AGV} = \frac{D}{Q_{eff}} = \frac{180}{12} = 15 \text{ AGVs}$$

*(7 marks for the numerical part)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Most students forget to convert tph or write `kg/s` instead of `tph`. Always show unit conversion step.
> 2. **Missing efficiency term** — In motor power calculations, omitting the 1000 and η factor is a guaranteed **−1 mark** deduction.
> 3. **Confusing effective tension** — Students often write $T_e = T_1 - T_2$ but forget that in long conveyors, the additional $T_3$ (indentation) and $T_4$ (skewing) terms cannot be ignored.
> 4. **AGV integer rounding** — Always round **UP** the number of AGVs. 14.3 → 15, not 14.
> 5. **Diagram missing labels** — Block diagrams without labels fetch **zero marks** for the "neat diagram" component.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** MTS = movement + storage + protection + control of materials.
- ✅ **6R Principle:** Right material, quantity, position, time, condition, cost.
- ✅ **Classification axes:** Mode (continuous/intermittent), Path (fixed/free), Energy (electric/pneumatic/hydraulic/gravity).
- ✅ **10 Principles** — remember all 10 (top exam question).
- ✅ **Belt Throughput Formula:** $Q = 3.6 \cdot \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v$ (in tph).
- ✅ **Motor Power Formula:** $P = T_e \cdot v / (1000 \cdot \eta)$.
- ✅ **AGV Fleet Size:** Round **up** to nearest integer.
- ✅ **AS/RS** components: Racks, S/R machine, conveyors, WMS, sensors.
- ✅ **Conveyor types:** Belt (bulk), Roller (boxes), Chain (pallets), Screw (powders), Pneumatic (light bulk).
- ✅ **AGV vs AMR:** AGV follows fixed magnetic/wire paths; AMR uses SLAM-based free navigation.
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 link:** MTS integrates with IoT, RFID, WMS, MES, and digital twins.
- ✅ **Selection matrix:** load + path + distance + speed + environment + cost.
- ✅ **Energy efficiency tip:** Always prefer gravity-fed systems where possible (Principle 5).
- ✅ **Safety standards:** OSHA, ISO 3691, and Indian Factory Act apply to all MTS installations.
- ✅ **Unit vigilance:** tph ≠ kg/s ≠ items/hr — read the question carefully.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Storage Systems — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage Systems** in industrial automation refer to the integrated set of physical infrastructure, automated mechanisms, control systems, and information management tools used to **receive, hold, buffer, retrieve, and dispatch** raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), sub-assemblies, finished goods, and tools within a manufacturing or distribution environment.

In the context of the **KTU OERUT612 syllabus**, Storage Systems form a critical bridge between the *inbound logistics* and the *production/processing subsystems* of a flexible manufacturing system (FMS) or computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) architecture. The modern view of storage in automation is **not merely a passive "warehouse"** — it is an **active, real-time, sensor-driven subsystem** that supports just-in-time (JIT) production, kitting operations, and order fulfillment.

## 1.2 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board-Favored Terms)

The following metrics are universally expected in KTU answer scripts:

- **SKU (Stock Keeping Unit)** — Unique identifier for each storable item.
- **WIP (Work-In-Progress)** — Semi-finished inventory between process stages.
- **FIFO / LIFO** — First-In-First-Out / Last-In-First-Out material flow discipline.
- **Pick Face** — The accessible location from which an operator/robot picks an item.
- **Throughput** — Units moved per unit time (e.g., **pallets/hour** or **bins/hour**).
- **Storage Density** — Volume of inventory per unit floor area (m³/m²).
- **Retrieval Time (T<sub>R</sub>)** — Average time to fetch one unit from storage.
- **Cycle Time (T<sub>C</sub>)** — Time to complete one full storage-retrieval cycle.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Library of a Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a Storage System as a **highly automated library inside a factory**.
> - The **books** = raw materials, components, or finished products.
> - The **librarian (human or robot)** = the Automated Storage & Retrieval System (AS/RS) crane or shuttle.
> - The **catalogue/index system** = the Warehouse Management Software (WMS) / Manufacturing Execution System (MES).
> - The **borrowing desk** = the pick face / conveyor output.
> - The **stacks/shelves** = racks, bins, carousels, or silos.
>
> Just as a modern library uses **barcodes, robotic arms, and sorted return slots**, an automated storage system uses **RFID, sensors, PLCs, and shuttles** to maintain order, accuracy, and speed.

## 1.4 Functional Role in the Automation Hierarchy (CIM Context)

The Storage System sits on the **shop-floor execution layer** of the CIM pyramid:

| CIM Layer | Storage System's Interaction |
|---|---|
| **Level 4 — ERP** | Receives demand forecasts & dispatch schedules. |
| **Level 3 — MES** | Updates WIP status, triggers kitting. |
| **Level 2 — SCADA / PLC** | Controls cranes, conveyors, stacker motors. |
| **Level 1 — Sensors/Actuators** | Photo-eyes, encoders, load cells, RFID antennas. |
| **Level 0 — Physical Process** | The racks, bins, pallets, and parts themselves. |

## 1.5 High-Yield Classification of Storage Systems (Board Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following taxonomy. Memorizing this classification tree secures full marks on any 3-mark definitional question.

1. **Bulk Storage Systems** — Silos, hoppers, tanks (for liquids, grains, powders).
2. **Unit-Load Storage Systems** — Pallet racks, drive-in racks, drive-through racks.
3. **Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)** — Crane-based, shuttle-based.
4. **Mini-Load AS/RS** — For small parts / totes (< 250 kg).
5. **Carousel Storage** — Horizontal Carousel, Vertical Carousel.
6. **Vertical Lift Modules (VLM)** — Enclosed column with extractor tray.
7. **Automated Bin Storage** — Stackable totes in multi-level frames.
8. **Tool Storage / Tool Cribs** — CNC tool management with RFID.
9. **In-Process Buffer Storage** — Inter-stage conveyors, accumulation tables.
10. **Cold Storage / Hazardous Storage** — Specialized climate/ATEX zones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an AS/RS — The Heart of Modern Storage

The Automated Storage and Retrieval System is the **most commonly examined topic** under this module. Its operation can be broken down into seven sequential steps:

- **Step 1 — Order Receipt:** The WMS issues a *put-away* or *retrieval* order referencing a SKU and target location (row, column, level).
- **Step 2 — Path Planning:** The S/R (Storage/Retrieval) crane's controller calculates the optimal **Chebyshev distance** to the target bin.
- **Step 3 — Crane Travel:** The crane moves along the **aisle (X-axis)** and the **hoist (Z-axis)** simultaneously, often with the **telescopic fork (Y-axis)** extending last.
- **Step 4 — Bin Engagement:** The fork/load-handling device extracts or inserts the bin/pallet. Load cells verify the mass.
- **Step 5 — Verification:** Barcode / RFID read confirms the correct unit. Mismatches trigger an alarm and re-routing.
- **Step 6 — Transport & Delivery:** Crane returns the bin to either storage (put-away) or the pick station (retrieval).
- **Step 7 — Database Update:** The WMS logs the transaction, updates bin status (OCCUPIED / EMPTY / RESERVED), and re-optimizes the next command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AS/RS matters in Industry 4.0:** It eliminates manual search time, supports **24/7 lights-out operation**, and reduces warehouse labor costs by up to **70%** while improving inventory accuracy from ~95% (manual) to **> 99.99%**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are the **highest-yield formulas** for the KTU 2024 Scheme examination on Storage Systems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of cylindrical storage tank | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | $r$ = radius (m), $h$ = height (m), $V$ in m³ | Used for **vertical bulk storage** |
| 2 | Volume of spherical storage tank | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ | $r$ in m, $V$ in m³ | LPG / cryogenic storage |
| 3 | Surface area of cylindrical tank (lateral) | $A_{L} = 2\pi r h$ | All SI units | Excludes top & bottom |
| 4 | Total surface area of cylindrical tank | $A_{T} = 2\pi r (h + r)$ | All SI units | Includes both end caps |
| 5 | Storage Density | $\rho_{s} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{u}}{A_{floor}}$ | $N$ = no. of units, $V_u$ = unit volume, $A_{floor}$ in m² | Key for space utilization |
| 6 | AS/RS Chebyshev travel distance | $D = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert, \vert \Delta z \vert)$ | $D$ in m | Crane moves **all axes simultaneously** |
| 7 | AS/RS single-command cycle time | $T_{SC} = \dfrac{D}{V_{c}} + 2 t_{f}$ | $V_c$ = crane velocity, $t_f$ = fork extension time | Storage OR retrieval only |
| 8 | AS/RS dual-command cycle time | $T_{DC} = \dfrac{D_{1}}{V_{c}} + \dfrac{D_{2}}{V_{c}} + 4 t_{f}$ | $D_1, D_2$ = distances for combined store+retrieve | Higher throughput per trip |
| 9 | Conveyor throughput | $Q = \dfrac{v}{L_{p}}$ | $v$ = belt speed (m/s), $L_p$ = pitch between parts (m), $Q$ = parts/s | Used in in-process buffers |
| 10 | FIFO queue waiting time (Little's Law) | $L = \lambda W$ | $L$ = items in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = waiting time | Storage as a queuing system |
| 11 | Effective storage volume (with rack void) | $V_{eff} = \eta \cdot V_{gross}$ | $\eta$ = fill efficiency (0 < η ≤ 1) | Typically **0.70 – 0.85** |
| 12 | Pallet throughput | $\eta_{p} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot n}{T_{C}}$ | $n$ = units per cycle, $T_C$ in s | Pallet positions per hour |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students frequently write $\vert \Delta x \vert$ with the **vertical pipe `|` character** inside a markdown table, which **breaks the table rendering**. Always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as shown in row 6 above.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** FIFO carousels ensure drug expiry compliance.
- **Automotive paint shops:** AS/RS buffer storage between paint line and assembly.
- **E-commerce fulfillment:** VLMs and shuttle-based systems enable **30-minute order-to-dispatch** SLAs.
- **Chemical plants:** Spherical pressure vessels minimize surface area / volume ratio (less heat loss).
- **Cold chain logistics:** Insulated bulk silos store grain, cement, or LNG.

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis — Choosing the Right Storage Type

| Storage Type | Typical Capacity | Best Use Case | Automation Level | Floor Space Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pallet Rack (static) | 500 – 1500 kg/bay | Slow-moving SKUs | Low (forklift) | Medium |
| Drive-In / Drive-Through | 1000+ kg/pallet | High-density, low-SKU | Medium | High |
| Unit-Load AS/RS | 1000 kg/pallet | Fast-moving, 24/7 | **Very High** | Very High |
| Mini-Load AS/RS | 50 – 250 kg/bin | Small parts, kitting | **Very High** | High |
| Horizontal Carousel | 500 – 1000 kg/m bay | Order picking | Medium | Medium |
| Vertical Carousel | 500 – 800 kg/m height | Tools, parts | Medium | High |
| Vertical Lift Module | 50 – 250 kg/tray | Slow-movers, secure storage | **High** | **Very High** |
| Bulk Silo / Tank | 10³ – 10⁶ litres | Liquids, grains, powders | Sensor-driven only | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **fully worked out** for KTU's 14-mark analytical questions. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly; no logical gap is left for the student to fill.

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal AS/RS Storage Location Using the "Turnover-Based" Policy

> **Problem Context:** An AS/RS has dimensions 30 m (length) × 8 m (height) × 2 m (aisle width). The crane can travel at 2 m/s horizontally and 1 m/s vertically. A new SKU has a daily turnover of 50 units. Determine the **optimal I/O point coordinate** that minimizes expected single-command retrieval time.

### Step 1 — Define the system geometry

Let the input/output (I/O) point be fixed at one end of the aisle at coordinate $(0, 0)$. A generic bin is located at $(x, z)$ where $0 \leq x \leq 30$ and $0 \leq z \leq 8$.

### Step 2 — Express the Chebyshev travel time

Because the crane moves both axes simultaneously, the time to reach $(x, z)$ is governed by the **slower of the two axes**:

$$T(x,z) = \max\left( \dfrac{x}{V_x}, \dfrac{z}{V_z} \right) + t_f$$

Substituting numerical values, $V_x = 2$ m/s and $V_z = 1$ m/s:

$$T(x,z) = \max\left( \dfrac{x}{2}, \dfrac{z}{1} \right) + t_f$$

### Step 3 — Identify the critical pivot

The pivot point occurs when both axis times are equal:

$$\dfrac{x_{p}}{2} = \dfrac{z_{p}}{1}$$

$$x_{p} = 2 z_{p}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition

The maximum values are $x_{max} = 30$ m and $z_{max} = 8$ m. If we substitute $z_{max}$:

$$x_{p} = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ m}$$

Since $x_p = 16$ m $\leq x_{max} = 30$ m, the **pivot is reachable** within the rack.

### Step 5 — Conclusion

The travel time is dominated by the **vertical axis** for bins located in the region $x \leq 16$ m, and by the **horizontal axis** for bins with $x > 16$ m. The **fastest-moving SKU (50 units/day)** should be stored **closest to the I/O point** at the pivot zone, ideally at coordinates $(4, 2)$ — within easy reach of both axes.

**Engineering interpretation:** The AS/RS control software uses this principle to perform **"slotting optimization"** — a 14-mark KTU favorite.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Storage Tank Capacity for a Chemical Plant

> **Problem Context:** A chemical plant requires a **vertical cylindrical storage tank** with a height of 10 m and diameter of 6 m. Calculate (a) the storage volume in litres, (b) the lateral surface area in m², and (c) the **mass of water** it can hold (assume water density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³).

### Step 1 — Extract given data

$$h = 10 \text{ m}, \quad d = 6 \text{ m} \Rightarrow r = 3 \text{ m}, \quad \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Compute the volume using $V = \pi r^{2} h$

$$V = \pi \times (3)^{2} \times 10$$

$$V = \pi \times 9 \times 10 = 90\pi$$

$$V = 282.74 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Convert to litres

$$V = 282.74 \text{ m}^3 \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3$$

$$V = 2{,}82{,}743 \text{ L}$$

### Step 4 — Lateral surface area using $A_L = 2\pi r h$

$$A_L = 2 \times \pi \times 3 \times 10 = 60\pi$$

$$A_L = 188.50 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 5 — Mass of water using $m = \rho V$

$$m = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 282.74 \text{ m}^3$$

$$m = 2.827 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering summary table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | $V$ | 282.74 | m³ |
| Volume in litres | $V$ | 2,82,743 | L |
| Lateral surface area | $A_L$ | 188.50 | m² |
| Mass of water | $m$ | 2.827 × 10⁵ | kg |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Conveyor Throughput for a Bottling Line In-Process Buffer

> **Problem Context:** A conveyor in a bottling line runs at $v = 1.5$ m/s. Bottles are spaced at a pitch of $L_p = 0.4$ m. Determine (a) the throughput in bottles/second, (b) the number of bottles on a 25 m conveyor section.

### Step 1 — Apply $Q = v / L_p$

$$Q = \dfrac{1.5}{0.4} = 3.75 \text{ bottles/s}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to bottles/hour

$$Q_{h} = 3.75 \times 3600 = 13{,}500 \text{ bottles/hour}$$

### Step 3 — Bottles on 25 m buffer

$$N = \dfrac{L_{conv}}{L_p} = \dfrac{25}{0.4} = 62.5$$

Rounding down to the nearest whole bottle (the conveyor cannot hold a partial unit):

$$N = 62 \text{ bottles}$$

This is a **classic KTU 7-mark sub-question**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Storage Equation

| Exam Phrasing | Correct Equation | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| "Volume of a vertical cylindrical tank" | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | Using diameter $d$ instead of radius $r$. |
| "Travel time of AS/RS crane" | $T = D/V_c + t_f$ | Forgetting the fork extension time $t_f$. |
| "Throughput of a conveyor buffer" | $Q = v / L_p$ | Forgetting to convert per-second to per-hour. |
| "Surface area of a tank" | $A = 2\pi r h$ (lateral) | Including end caps when not asked. |
| "Mass of stored liquid" | $m = \rho V$ | Using $g$ in a mass calculation (mass ≠ weight). |
| "FIFO waiting time" | $L = \lambda W$ | Misnaming this as Little's Law and swapping variables. |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define an Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS). List any two types of AS/RS.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)** is a computer-controlled system that automates the storage and retrieval of items from defined storage locations, using cranes, conveyors, shuttles, or carousels under the supervision of a Warehouse Management System (WMS).
Two types are:
1. **Unit-Load AS/RS** — handles full pallets (typically 500 – 1500 kg).
2. **Mini-Load AS/RS** — handles totes/bins of small parts (typically ≤ 250 kg).
*(Acceptable alternatives: Man-on-board AS/RS, Shuttle-based AS/RS, Carousel-based.)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: What is FIFO in storage systems? Why is it critical in pharmaceutical warehouses?**

**Model Answer:**
**FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** is a material flow discipline in which the **oldest stock** is dispatched or consumed **first**, ensuring chronological rotation of inventory.
It is **critical in pharmaceutical warehouses** because drugs and vaccines have **expiry dates** — issuing older batches first prevents **product wastage**, regulatory non-compliance, and potential patient harm from expired medications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. The most common pattern is **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the functional architecture of an **AS/RS**. Discuss the role of the WMS and the PLC. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Answer:**

An AS/RS consists of four major subsystems:

1. **Storage Structure** — Racks arranged in rows and columns; each bin address is a tuple (Aisle, Row, Column, Level).
2. **Storage/Retrieval (S/R) Crane** — Moves on rails along the aisle (X-axis), the hoist raises/lowers (Z-axis), and a telescopic fork (Y-axis) extracts the bin.
3. **Conveyor Subsystem** — Transfers bins between the crane and the input/output (I/O) pick station.
4. **Control Subsystem** — Comprises the WMS (Level 3) and PLC (Level 2).

**Role of the WMS:**
- Maintains the **bin-to-SKU mapping** database.
- Issues **put-away and retrieval orders** to the PLC.
- Optimizes **slotting** of fast-moving SKUs near the I/O point.
- Generates **inventory reports** for ERP integration.

**Role of the PLC:**
- Executes **real-time motion control** of the crane (acceleration, deceleration, position control via encoders).
- Handles **I/O logic** for fork extend/retract, door interlocks, and emergency stop.
- Communicates with **photo-eyes, load cells, and RFID readers** for safety and verification.

The block diagram (refer Section 4.1) shows the complete interconnections: WMS → PLC → Crane → Rack → Pick Station, with sensors feeding back to the PLC.

**Q: (b)** A cylindrical storage tank has a **diameter of 4 m** and a **height of 12 m**. Calculate (i) its storage capacity in cubic metres, (ii) its lateral surface area, and (iii) the **mass of industrial oil** it can hold (oil density = 920 kg/m³). **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 4$ m $\Rightarrow r = 2$ m; $h = 12$ m; $\rho_{oil} = 920$ kg/m³.

**(i) Volume:**
$$V = \pi r^{2} h = \pi \times (2)^{2} \times 12 = 48\pi \approx 150.80 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Lateral surface area:**
$$A_L = 2 \pi r h = 2 \times \pi \times 2 \times 12 = 48\pi \approx 150.80 \text{ m}^2$$

**(iii) Mass of oil:**
$$m = \rho \cdot V = 920 \times 150.80 \approx 1.387 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg}$$

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Volume $V$ | 150.80 | m³ |
| Lateral area $A_L$ | 150.80 | m² |
| Oil mass $m$ | 1.387 × 10⁵ | kg |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Compare **Unit-Load AS/RS**, **Mini-Load AS/RS**, and **Vertical Lift Module (VLM)** in terms of capacity, application, and floor space efficiency. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Unit-Load AS/RS | Mini-Load AS/RS | VLM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical Load** | 500 – 1500 kg/pallet | 50 – 250 kg/bin | 50 – 250 kg/tray |
| **Item Size** | Full pallets | Totes, bins, cartons | Trays, small boxes |
| **Application** | Finished goods, raw material reserve | Spare parts, kitting, e-commerce | Slow-movers, secure tools, archives |
| **Storage Height** | Up to 40 m | Up to 12 m | Up to 10 m |
| **Aisle Width** | Narrow (1.5 m) | Narrow (1 m) | **No aisle needed** (enclosed) |
| **Floor Space Efficiency** | High | High | **Very High** (up to 85% reduction) |
| **Cycle Time** | 30 – 90 s | 15 – 45 s | 10 – 30 s |
| **Cost** | High | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Operator Access** | None (fully automated) | None | Single-window manual access |

**Engineering conclusion:** VLM is best for **secure, slow-moving** SKUs; Mini-Load AS/RS suits **high-throughput** small-parts picking; Unit-Load AS/RS is the **heavy backbone** of FMCG and automotive distribution centers.

**Q: (b)** Derive the **Chebyshev travel distance** for an AS/RS crane. A crane in a 25 m × 7 m aisle moves at $V_x = 1.8$ m/s horizontally and $V_z = 0.9$ m/s vertically, with a fork time $t_f = 4$ s. A bin is at $(x, z) = (10, 5)$ m and the I/O point is at $(0, 0)$. Calculate the **single-command cycle time**. **[(7 Marks)]**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** Since the crane moves both axes simultaneously, it cannot arrive at the target before the slower axis completes its travel. The travel distance is therefore the **maximum of the two axis travel times**, and the corresponding **Chebyshev distance** is:

$$D = \max(\vert x - x_{IO} \vert, \vert z - z_{IO} \vert)$$

$$T_{travel} = \max\left( \dfrac{\vert \Delta x \vert}{V_x}, \dfrac{\vert \Delta z \vert}{V_z} \right)$$

For a **single-command cycle** (one storage OR one retrieval), the cycle time is:

$$T_{SC} = T_{travel} + 2 t_f$$

(The factor 2 accounts for fork extend + retract.)

**Calculation:**

$$\dfrac{\vert \Delta x \vert}{V_x} = \dfrac{10}{1.8} = 5.56 \text{ s}$$

$$\dfrac{\vert \Delta z \vert}{V_z} = \dfrac{5}{0.9} = 5.56 \text{ s}$$

The two axis times are **equal** — this is the **pivot point** of the system.

$$T_{travel} = \max(5.56, 5.56) = 5.56 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{SC} = 5.56 + 2 \times 4 = 5.56 + 8 = 13.56 \text{ s}$$

**Single-command cycle time = 13.56 s.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $t_f$:** Many students write only the travel time and lose **2 – 3 marks**.
> 2. **Using Euclidean distance:** The crane does **not** travel in a straight diagonal line — it moves both axes in parallel. Always use the **Chebyshev (max) metric**.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2** on $t_f$ (both extend AND retract).
> 4. **Mixing up single-command vs dual-command cycle** — read the question carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **last-30-seconds revision sheet** before entering the KTU exam hall.

- ✅ **Storage Systems** = integrated hardware + software for buffering inventory in a CIM plant.
- ✅ **AS/RS** is the **most-automated** storage type — crane moves on X, Y, Z axes under PLC + WMS control.
- ✅ **FIFO** = oldest item out first; **LIFO** = newest item out first. **Pharma and food → FIFO mandatory.**
- ✅ **Volume of cylinder** $V = \pi r^{2} h$. **Sphere** $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$. **Watch the radius vs diameter trap.**
- ✅ **Chebyshev distance** uses **MAX of axis times**, not Euclidean distance — both axes move simultaneously.
- ✅ **Single-command cycle** = one trip (store OR retrieve) = $T_{travel} + 2 t_f$.
- ✅ **Dual-command cycle** = combined store + retrieve in one trip = $T_{travel,1} + T_{travel,2} + 4 t_f$.
- ✅ **Conveyor throughput** $Q = v / L_p$. Convert to per-hour by multiplying by 3600.
- ✅ **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ — relates queue length, arrival rate, and wait time.
- ✅ **VLM** is the best solution for **secure, low-throughput, space-constrained** environments.
- ✅ **Mini-Load AS/RS** = totes ≤ 250 kg; **Unit-Load AS/RS** = pallets up to 1500 kg.
- ✅ **Slotting optimization** = fast-moving SKUs near the I/O point — **standard 7-mark KTU sub-question**.
- ✅ **Sensors in storage:** photo-eyes (presence), encoders (position), load cells (mass), RFID (identity).
- ✅ **CIM pyramid integration:** ERP (L4) → MES (L3) → WMS/PLC (L2) → Sensors (L1) → Physical storage (L0).
- ✅ **Fill efficiency** $\eta$ for a typical AS/RS is **0.70 – 0.85** — the rest is aisle + structural void.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Always state SI units; convert m³ to litres (× 1000) when the question demands.

---
