| **Hot material OK?** | No | No | Yes (gamma) | No | No |
| **Speed** | ms | ms | ms | µs | 10 ms |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic On-Line Thickness Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Material on Conveyor] --> B[Sensor Head]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Microprocessor DSP Linearization]
    E --> F[Display HMI]
    E --> G[PLC for Control Loop]
    G --> H[Actuator Roller Gap Adjustment]
    H --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: X-ray Thickness Gauge (Mill Duty)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[X-ray Source / Am-241 Isotope] -->|emits photons| MAT[Steel Strip Moving at 30 m/s]
    MAT -->|attenuated beam| DET[Scintillation Detector or Ion Chamber]
    DET --> PRE[Pre-amplifier]
    PRE --> AMP[Pulse Amplifier and Discriminator]
    AMP --> RATE[Count Rate Meter or Current Integrator]
    RATE --> CALIB[Calibration Curve Stored in PLC]
    CALIB --> LOG[Data Logger and MES Upload]
    CALIB --> ACT[Roll Screw-down Actuator]
```

## 4.3 Ultrasonic Probe Setup Diagram (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pulsar Circuit 100 V pulse] --> T[Piezoelectric Transducer 5 MHz]
    T -->|ultrasound wave| S[Material Under Test]
    S -->|first echo| T
    T --> RX[Receiver Amplifier]
    RX --> TGC[Time Gain Compensation]
    TGC --> ADC[High Speed ADC 200 MSPS]
    ADC --> DSP[DSP Measures t_rise to first echo]
    DSP --> DISP[Thickness Display]
```

## 4.4 Pneumatic Air Gauge Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUP[Filtered Air Supply 2 bar] --> REG[Pressure Regulator]
    REG --> NOZ[Nozzle Facing Material Surface]
    NOZ --> MAT[Workpiece Strip]
    MAT -->|variable gap d| NOZ
    NOZ --> BP[Back-pressure Line]
    BP --> DPT[Differential Pressure Transducer]
    DPT --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> IND[Indicator or Recorder]
```

## 4.5 Eddy Current Probe for Coating Thickness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[High Frequency Oscillator 1 to 100 MHz] --> COIL[Probe Coil]
    COIL -->|alternating magnetic field| TGT[Conductive Substrate with Coating]
    TGT -->|induces eddy currents| COIL
    COIL --> IMP[Impedance Analyzer Bridge Circuit]
    IMP --> PHASE[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    PHASE --> CAL[Microcontroller Lookup Table for Coating Thickness]
    CAL --> LCD[LCD Display]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** List any **three advantages** of non-contact thickness measurement over contact methods. Name one industry where contact methods are still preferred.

**Model Answer:**
Non-contact thickness measurement offers:
1. **No mechanical wear** on the sensor, giving a longer operational life.
2. **No marking or scratching** of delicate surfaces such as polished foils or coated strips.
3. **High measurement speed**, allowing 100 % online inspection on a fast-moving strip (up to 30 m/s in rolling mills).
4. **No need for physical force**, so soft materials like polymer films or paper are not deformed during measurement.

**Industry where contact methods are still preferred:** Glass thickness measurement of bottles, where a contact probe mechanically indexes the wall under controlled conditions, OR precision machining inspection (CMM) of metal blocks.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** A non-destructive thickness gauge uses ultrasonic pulses. The velocity of sound in aluminium is **6420 m/s** and the round-trip time observed is **0.8 µs**. Calculate the thickness.

**Model Answer:**

$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{6420 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \dfrac{5.136 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.568 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 2.57 \, \text{mm}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for thickness measurement using the **Beer–Lambert Law** of radiation absorption. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An X-ray thickness gauge on a cold rolling mill gives a count rate of **8000 counts/s** for a standard steel strip of known thickness **2.00 mm**. For the same gauge setting, an unknown strip gives **5500 counts/s**. The mass absorption coefficient is $\mu_m = 0.057 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$ and density of steel $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Find the thickness of the unknown strip. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Beer–Lambert Law

**Assumptions:**
1. The incident radiation is **monochromatic** (single energy).
2. The material is **homogeneous** and has uniform density.
3. The absorption process is **linear** (no saturation or non-linear scattering).
4. The radiation beam is **narrow and parallel** (no scatter outside the beam).

**Derivation:**

Consider a thin slice of material of thickness $dx$ perpendicular to a beam of intensity $I$. The reduction in intensity $dI$ is proportional to $I$ and $dx$:

$$dI = -\mu \, I \, dx$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear absorption coefficient** (units: m⁻¹ or cm⁻¹).

Separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

Integrate from the entry surface (where $I = I_0$ at $x = 0$) to the exit surface (where $I$ is measured at $x = d$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{d} dx$$

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu d$$

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I}{I_0} \right) = -\mu d$$

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)}$$

This is the **Beer–Lambert Law** of absorption. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $I_{ref} = 8000$ counts/s for $d_{ref} = 2.00 \text{ mm}$
- $I = 5500$ counts/s for unknown $d$
- $\mu_m = 0.057 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute the linear absorption coefficient** $\mu$:

$$\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho = 0.057 \times 7850 = 447.45 \, \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Converting to mm⁻¹:

$$\mu = 0.44745 \, \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Verify with the reference strip:**

$$\mu \, d_{ref} = 0.44745 \times 2.00 = 0.8949$$

$$I_{ref} = I_0 \, e^{-0.8949}$$

$$I_0 = I_{ref} \cdot e^{0.8949} = 8000 \times 2.447 = 19{,}576 \text{ counts/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the law to the unknown strip:**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu d}$$

$$5500 = 19{,}576 \cdot e^{-0.44745 \, d}$$

$$e^{-0.44745 \, d} = \dfrac{5500}{19{,}576} = 0.2810$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural log:**

$$-0.44745 \, d = \ln(0.2810) = -1.2697$$

$$d = \dfrac{1.2697}{0.44745}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 2.838 \text{ mm}}$$

The unknown strip is approximately **2.84 mm thick**. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Supplementary 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ultrasonic pulse-echo method** of thickness measurement. Derive the expression for thickness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic thickness gauge uses a transducer of frequency **5 MHz** with sound velocity in steel **5920 m/s**. The measured round-trip time is **1.20 µs**. Calculate the thickness and the wavelength. Comment on whether the reading is reliable. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Ultrasonic Pulse-Echo Method

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pulsar High Voltage Pulse] --> T[Transducer Piezo Crystal]
    T -->|ultrasonic wave| M[Material Under Test]
    M -->|reflected echo| T
    T --> R[Receiver Amplifier]
    R --> G[Time Gain Compensation]
    G --> C[Time Measurement Counter]
    C --> DSP[Microprocessor d = v*t / 2]
    DSP --> DISP[Display Thickness]
```

**Working Principle:**
A **piezoelectric transducer** is excited by a high-voltage, narrow pulse. The transducer converts this electrical pulse into a mechanical (acoustic) wave at its resonant frequency (typically 1 to 25 MHz). This wave propagates into the material, hits the **far surface**, and reflects back as an echo. The same transducer (acting in receive mode) detects the echo. A high-speed counter or Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) measures the time $t$ between the transmitted pulse and the received echo.

**Derivation:**
The wave travels down through thickness $d$ and back up, covering a total distance of $2d$ in time $t$ at constant velocity $v$:

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}}$$

**Couplant Requirement:** Since the transducer-to-air impedance mismatch is enormous (Z_air ≈ 0.0004 MRayl vs Z_steel ≈ 46 MRayl), a **couplant** (gel, water, or oil) is used to transmit acoustic energy into the part. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $f = 5 \text{ MHz}$, $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$, $t = 1.20 \, \mu s$

**Step 1 — Compute the wavelength** in steel:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{5920}{5 \times 10^6} = 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.184 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the thickness:**

$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{5920 \times 1.20 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \dfrac{7.104 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 3.552 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 3.55 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Check reliability:**

The thickness must be at least **one wavelength** for the echo to be resolvable (so the transmitted pulse and the echo do not overlap). Here, $d = 3.55 \text{ mm}$ and $\lambda = 1.184 \text{ mm}$, so the thickness is about **3 wavelengths** thick — this is just above the resolution limit and **the reading is reliable but at the lower end of acceptable operation**. For greater reliability, a **lower frequency** (e.g., 2.25 MHz) or a **higher velocity** material should be used. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic thickness ($d = v \cdot t / 2$, not $v \cdot t$). This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Unit mismatches** — converting µs to seconds before substituting. Always write $1.2 \times 10^{-6}$, not $1.2$.
> 3. **Confusing mass absorption coefficient $\mu_m$ with linear absorption coefficient $\mu$**. The relation is $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts. A neat labelled block diagram carries **at least 2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** when deriving the Beer–Lambert Law. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 6. In capacitive sensors, students often write $C \propto d$ instead of $C \propto 1/d$ and lose a mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thickness measurement is the cornerstone of online quality control** in rolling mills, paper, foil, and film industries.
- **Two main families:** **Contact** (LVDT probe, pneumatic) and **Non-Contact** (capacitive, ultrasonic, X-ray, gamma-ray, eddy current, laser).
- **Capacitive method** uses $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ and is restricted to **dielectric (non-conductive)** materials.
- **Ultrasonic method** uses $d = v \cdot t / 2$; needs a couplant; resolution limited to about **one wavelength**.
- **X-ray method** uses **Beer–Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu d}$; ideal for **cold strip**; **Gamma-ray** is used for **hot strip** (radiation is temperature-independent).
- **Eddy current method** works only on **conductive** targets; used for coating thickness on metals.
- **Pneumatic method** is simple, low-cost, and excellent for **precision thin foils** where contact with a probe would damage the surface.
- **Selection criteria** depend on: **material conductivity**, **temperature**, **speed of line**, **required resolution**, and **cost**.
- **Always check the factor of 2** in ultrasonic time-of-flight; **always convert µs to s**; **always state assumptions** in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Vision Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Machine Vision Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Machine Vision (MV)** is an engineering discipline of computer science and optical engineering that deals with the automatic acquisition, processing, analysis, and interpretation of images of real-world scenes for the purpose of extracting useful information and making automated decisions in industrial environments. It is formally defined by the **Automated Imaging Association (AIA)** as *"the automatic extraction of information from digital images to control, guide, or measure manufacturing or production operations."*

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, a Machine Vision System is a non-contact, optical-based sensory subsystem that replaces or augments human visual inspection in tasks such as **quality assurance**, **dimensional metrology**, **defect detection**, **part identification**, and **robotic guidance** within automated production lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Machine Vision Systems form the "eyes" of modern Industry 4.0 / IIoT-enabled smart factories. The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the *sensing*, *processing*, and *decision* loop within a vision-based automated cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Computer Vision vs. Machine Vision:**
> - **Computer Vision** → Research-oriented, used in autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, AI; runs on general-purpose GPUs.
> - **Machine Vision** → Industrial-oriented, deterministic, real-time, ruggedized; runs on dedicated embedded processors or FPGAs in factory floors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a biscuit factory with the job of checking **10,000 biscuits per hour** for cracks, correct labeling, and proper shape. Within an hour, you would lose focus, get fatigued, and make errors. Now imagine installing a **high-speed camera linked to a smart microprocessor** that "looks" at every single biscuit, measures its dimensions in microseconds, compares it to a golden reference, and signals a robotic arm to push any defective biscuit off the conveyor — all without blinking, never getting tired, and at consistent accuracy **24/7**. That is exactly what a **Machine Vision System** does.

### 1.3 Core Functional Goals of a Machine Vision System

| Goal | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Gauging** | Measuring physical dimensions (length, diameter, angle) with sub-pixel precision. |
| **Inspection** | Detecting surface defects such as scratches, dents, or discolorations. |
| **Identification** | Reading barcodes, QR codes, OCR, or recognizing part types. |
| **Guidance** | Providing real-time spatial coordinates to a robot for pick-and-place operations. |
| **Verification** | Confirming assembly correctness (e.g., all screws present in an engine block). |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Recall These!)

- **Resolution** — Expressed in **pixels (px)**, e.g., $1280 \times 960$ px; spatial detail captured.
- **Frame Rate** — Measured in **frames per second (fps)**; typical industrial cameras operate at **30 fps to 1000 fps**.
- **Field of View (FOV)** — The physical area, in **millimeters (mm)**, that the camera can observe.
- **Pixel Resolution / Accuracy** — FOV/Resolution, measured in **mm/pixel**.
- **Throughput** — **parts per minute (ppm)** that the system can inspect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Rule:** A machine vision system is deemed viable for industrial use when its **defect-detection rate > 99.5%** and its **false-rejection rate < 0.5%**, in compliance with **ISO 2859-1** sampling standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Subsystems of a Machine Vision System

A complete machine vision installation consists of **six tightly coupled sub-modules**. Understanding each is mandatory for KTU Module 2 questions.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Illumination
- **Why?** Because images are nothing but captured photons. Without proper lighting, even the best camera gives garbage data.
- **How?** Lighting is chosen to maximize contrast between the **feature of interest** and its background.
- **Common Illumination Techniques:**

| Technique | Application |
|---|---|
| **Backlighting** | Silhouette / dimensional measurements (opaque parts). |
| **Diffuse / Dome Lighting** | Highly reflective or curved surfaces (e.g., polished metal). |
| **Structured Light** | 3D reconstruction; projects a known pattern (line/grid) onto the object. |
| **Dark-field Illumination** | Detecting surface scratches, edges, or text. |
| **Bright-field Illumination** | General-purpose inspection of flat, opaque parts. |
| **Strobe / LED Pulsed Lighting** | Freezing motion on high-speed conveyors. |

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Optics (Lens)
- Determines the **Magnification ($m$)**, **FOV**, and **Working Distance (WD)**.
- Calculated using the thin-lens imaging equation:
$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}$$
where $f$ is the **focal length (mm)**, $d_o$ is the **object distance**, and $d_i$ is the **image distance**.
- **Magnification:**
$$m = \frac{h_i}{h_o} = \frac{d_i}{d_o}$$
where $h_i$ = image height (mm) and $h_o$ = object height (mm).

### 2.1.3 Stage 3 — Image Sensor
- Two dominant technologies: **CCD (Charge Coupled Device)** and **CMOS (Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor)**.
- **CCD:** Higher uniformity, lower noise, used in metrology and scientific imaging.
- **CMOS:** Lower power, higher speed, on-chip processing; dominant in modern industrial cameras.

### 2.1.4 Stage 4 — Frame Grabber / Interface
- Converts analog sensor output to digital (legacy systems) or directly streams digital data via **GigE Vision**, **USB3 Vision**, **Camera Link**, or **CoaXPress** protocols.

### 2.1.5 Stage 5 — Image Processing Unit
- Performs the actual "thinking" — preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification.

### 2.1.6 Stage 6 — Output / Communication
- Sends results to **PLC**, **robot controller**, **SCADA**, or **IIoT gateway** via **Digital I/O**, **EtherCAT**, **PROFINET**, **OPC-UA**, or **MQTT**.

## 2.2 Image Processing Pipeline (The Core Algorithm Chain)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The image processing pipeline is sequential — the output of one stage is the input of the next. Examiners love asking "what happens after segmentation?"

1. **Image Acquisition** → Raw grayscale or RGB image is captured.
2. **Pre-processing** → Noise removal (Gaussian, Median filters); contrast enhancement (Histogram Equalization).
3. **Segmentation** → Isolating the region of interest (ROI) using **thresholding**, **edge detection (Sobel, Canny, Prewitt)**, or **region growing**.
4. **Feature Extraction** → Computing descriptors such as area, perimeter, centroid, blob count, Hough lines, SURF/SIFT keypoints.
5. **Classification / Decision** → Pattern matching, template matching, or trained neural network decides: PASS / FAIL.
6. **Communication** → Result relayed to actuator or upstream controller.

## 2.3 Key Algorithms — At a Glance

| Algorithm | Purpose | Equation / Concept |
|---|---|---|
| **Thresholding (Otsu's Method)** | Binarize image automatically | Maximizes inter-class variance $\sigma_B^2$ between fg and bg. |
| **Canny Edge Detection** | Find object boundaries | 5-stage: Gaussian → Gradient → NMS → Double threshold → Hysteresis. |
| **Hough Transform** | Detect lines/circles | Maps $(x, y)$ to $(\rho, \theta)$ parameter space. |
| **Blob Analysis** | Count/size connected regions | Connected-component labelling (4-connectivity or 8-connectivity). |
| **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** | Read printed text | Feature extraction + classifier (e.g., LSTM, SVM). |
| **Template Matching** | Find a known pattern | Normalized Cross-Correlation: $R(x,y) = \frac{\sum (T \cdot I')}{\sqrt{\sum T^2 \cdot \sum I'^2}}$. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Object height** $h_o$ | $h_o = \frac{\text{FOV}}{m}$ | mm | Selecting lens for required field of view. |
| **Pixel resolution** | $\text{Res}_{px} = \frac{\text{FOV (mm)}}{\text{Image width (px)}}$ | $\text{mm/px}$ | Determines smallest detectable feature. |
| **Lens magnification** | $m = \frac{d_i}{d_o} = \frac{h_i}{h_o}$ | dimensionless | Relates sensor size to part size. |
| **Working distance** | $d_o = f \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{1}{m} \right)$ | mm | Mechanical mounting clearance. |
| **Minimum resolvable feature** | $f_{min} = 2 \cdot \text{Res}_{px}$ | mm | **Nyquist**-based rule-of-thumb. |
| **Throughput (max)** | $T_{max} = \frac{v_{conv}}{L_{part}}$ | parts/sec | Matched to camera frame rate. |
| **F-number (aperture)** | $N = \frac{f}{D}$ | dimensionless | Controls light gathered; depth of field. |
| **Strobe exposure** | $t_{exp} = \frac{L_{part}}{v_{conv}}$ | seconds | Freezes motion — must equal camera exposure. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In semiconductor fabrication (Class 1 cleanroom), machine vision systems measure lithography overlay errors at the **nanometer scale**, using **5 MP cameras** with **telecentric lenses** ($N = 4$ to $8$) and **monochromatic LED strobe lighting** at wavelengths of **470 nm or 625 nm**.

## 2.5 2D vs. 3D Machine Vision

- **2D Vision** → Analyzes intensity on a flat image plane. Cheap, fast, sufficient for ~70% of industrial inspection tasks.
- **3D Vision** → Captures depth information using:
  * **Stereo vision** (two cameras, triangulation).
  * **Laser triangulation** (line laser + camera).
  * **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** sensors.
  * **Structured light** (projector + camera).
- **3D vision** is mandatory for **bin picking**, **weld seam tracking**, and **additive manufacturing layer inspection**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Standard KTU Numericals)

A KTU examiner-style problem reads as follows:

> *"An automated inspection cell uses a CCD camera with a sensor array of $\frac{1}{2}$″ (diagonal $11 \text{ mm}$). The camera is fitted with a $25 \text{ mm}$ focal length lens. The minimum defect that must be detected is $0.1 \text{ mm}$ in width on a printed-circuit board. Calculate: (i) the working distance for a $50 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$ Field of View, (ii) the pixel resolution in mm/px, and (iii) whether a 640 × 480 pixel sensor is sufficient."*

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (i) — Magnification and Working Distance

We are given:
- Sensor diagonal $d_s = 11 \text{ mm}$ (used to estimate sensor width as $w_s \approx 7.5 \text{ mm}$ for a standard 4:3 sensor).
- Focal length $f = 25 \text{ mm}$.
- Required FOV width $W = 50 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the magnification.
The magnification is the ratio of the sensor size to the field of view.

$$
m = \frac{w_s}{W}
$$

$$
m = \frac{7.5 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
m = 0.15
$$

**Step 2:** Use the magnification definition to find the image distance $d_i$.

$$
m = \frac{d_i}{d_o} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_i = m \cdot d_o
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the thin-lens equation.

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}
$$

Substitute $d_i = m \cdot d_o$:

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{m \cdot d_o}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{1}{m} \right)
$$

$$
d_o = f \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{1}{m} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Insert numerical values.

$$
d_o = 25 \text{ mm} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{1}{0.15} \right)
$$

$$
d_o = 25 \text{ mm} \cdot (1 + 6.6667)
$$

$$
d_o = 25 \text{ mm} \cdot 7.6667
$$

$$
d_o = 191.67 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Result (i):** The required working distance is approximately $\mathbf{191.67 \text{ mm}}$.

---

#### Part (ii) — Pixel Resolution

We are given:
- Image width = 640 pixels.

**Step 1:** Use the definition of pixel resolution.

$$
\text{Res}_{px} = \frac{\text{FOV (mm)}}{\text{Image width (px)}}
$$

**Step 2:** Insert values.

$$
\text{Res}_{px} = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{640 \text{ px}}
$$

$$
\text{Res}_{px} = 0.0781 \text{ mm/px}
$$

> **Result (ii):** Each pixel covers $\mathbf{0.0781 \text{ mm}}$ of the physical scene.

---

#### Part (iii) — Nyquist Sufficiency Check

The minimum resolvable feature according to the Nyquist sampling theorem is twice the pixel resolution.

$$
f_{min} = 2 \cdot \text{Res}_{px}
$$

$$
f_{min} = 2 \cdot 0.0781 \text{ mm/px}
$$

$$
f_{min} = 0.1563 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
f_{min} = 0.1563 \text{ mm} \;\; > \;\; \text{required defect size} = 0.1 \text{ mm}
$$

The minimum feature the camera can resolve is **larger** than the defect we need to detect. Therefore, the camera **cannot reliably detect** the 0.1 mm defect.

> **Result (iii):** The 640 × 480 sensor is **NOT sufficient**. Either a higher-resolution sensor (e.g., **1600 × 1200 px**, giving $\text{Res}_{px} = 0.0313$ mm/px and $f_{min} = 0.0625 \text{ mm} < 0.1 \text{ mm}$ ✔) or a longer-focal-length lens with a closer working distance is required.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Algorithm Selection

| Inspection Goal | Recommended Algorithm | Lighting Type | Sensor Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensional gauging of metal shaft | Edge detection + Hough transform | **Backlight** | Monochrome CCD |
| Surface scratch on painted panel | Blob analysis + Morphological filtering | **Dark-field** | Monochrome CMOS |
| Reading expiry date on bottle | OCR (Tesseract / LSTM) | **Diffuse dome** | Color CMOS |
| Counting pills in blister pack | Thresholding + Blob counting | **Backlight** | Monochrome CMOS |
| Robotic pick from bin | 3D point cloud + ICP matching | **Structured light** | Stereo / ToF |
| Solder joint inspection | Color segmentation (HSV threshold) | **Ring LED** | Color CMOS |
| Weld bead profile measurement | Laser triangulation | **Laser line projector** | Monochrome line-scan CMOS |

## 3.4 Communication Latency in a Vision-Guided Cell

For a complete vision-guided robotic pick-and-place cell, the **end-to-end latency** $t_{total}$ is:

$$
t_{total} = t_{acq} + t_{proc} + t_{comm}
$$

- $t_{acq}$ = camera exposure + readout time (typically **1 ms to 100 ms**).
- $t_{proc}$ = image processing time (typically **5 ms to 200 ms**, GPU-accelerated).
- $t_{comm}$ = PLC/robot handshake time (typically **1 ms to 10 ms** via EtherCAT or PROFINET).

> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For conveyor speeds of $v_{conv} = 1 \text{ m/s}$ and part pitch $L_{part} = 100 \text{ mm}$, the maximum allowable $t_{total}$ is $100 \text{ ms}$. Any system exceeding this latency will cause **missed inspections** — a critical failure mode in KTU-style design questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Machine Vision System

The diagram below captures the complete data flow from physical scene to factory-floor actuator, mirroring the structure expected in KTU 2024 Scheme model answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Scene / Part on Conveyor] --> B[Illumination Module LED Strobe / Laser]
    B --> C[Optics Lens and Filter]
    C --> D[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    D --> E[Frame Grabber and Interface GigE USB3 Camera Link]
    E --> F[Preprocessing Noise Reduction Contrast Enhancement]
    F --> G[Segmentation Thresholding Edge Detection]
    G --> H[Feature Extraction Blob Hough OCR]
    H --> I[Classifier Template Match SVM Deep Learning]
    I --> J{Decision Engine}
    J -->|PASS| K[PLC and SCADA Log]
    J -->|FAIL| L[Reject Actuator Pneumatic Pusher]
    J --> M[Robot Controller for Pick Place]
    I --> N[IIoT Gateway OPC UA MQTT]
    N --> O[Cloud Analytics and MES]
```

## 4.2 Image Processing Pipeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw Image Captured] --> P2[Grayscale Conversion if needed]
    P2 --> P3[Gaussian Filter 3x3 or 5x5]
    P3 --> P4[Histogram Equalization]
    P4 --> P5[Otsu Thresholding]
    P5 --> P6[Connected Component Labelling]
    P6 --> P7[Feature Computation Area Perimeter Centroid]
    P7 --> P8[Reference Comparison Golden Template]
    P8 --> P9{Within Tolerance}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Result Pass and Log]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Result Fail and Alarm]
```

## 4.3 2D vs 3D Vision System Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TwoD[2D Vision Systems]
        A1[Single Camera] --> A2[Monochrome or Color Image]
        A2 --> A3[Edge Detection and Blob Analysis]
        A3 --> A4[Applications Label Inspection Gauging OCR]
    end
    subgraph ThreeD[3D Vision Systems]
        B1[Stereo Camera or Laser or ToF] --> B2[Point Cloud Generation]
        B2 --> B3[Depth Map and Surface Reconstruction]
        B3 --> B4[Applications Bin Picking Weld Tracking Volume Measurement]
    end
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Control Integration with PLC and Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sensor Vision Camera] --> V[Vision Processor]
    V -->|Coordinates and Pose| P[PLC]
    P -->|Trigger and OK Signal| R[6 Axis Robot]
    R -->|Feedback Position| P
    P -->|Reject Pulse| AC[Solenoid Pusher]
    V -->|Metadata and Images| SC[SCADA HMI and MES]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Notes:** All node IDs above use simple alphanumeric names (A, B, C, ... A1, A2, TwoD, ThreeD) with no reserved keywords. Labels contain only plain text — no bold, italic, or HTML formatting — ensuring clean rendering in KTU digital evaluation environments.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Machine Vision System. List any four of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A Machine Vision System is an automated optical-electronic subsystem that uses image capture and digital processing to perform industrial inspection, measurement, and guidance tasks without human intervention. *(1.5 marks)*
**Four industrial applications:** *(1.5 marks, 0.375 each)*
1. **Automated optical inspection (AOI)** of printed circuit boards in SMT lines.
2. **Dimensional gauging** of machined components in CNC cells.
3. **Bottle / label inspection** in food and beverage packaging.
4. **Robotic bin-picking** guided by 3D point clouds.
5. *(any other valid application such as barcode reading, weld inspection, or pharmaceutical pill counting)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between CCD and CMOS image sensors used in machine vision. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **CCD** | **CMOS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Readout** | Charge transferred pixel-to-pixel to a single output node. | Each pixel has its own amplifier; column-parallel readout. |
| **Noise** | Lower noise, higher uniformity. | Slightly higher noise, but improving with modern BSI technology. |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (requires multiple voltage rails). | Significantly lower. |
| **Speed** | Slower (sequential charge transfer). | Very fast (on-chip A/D conversion). |
| **Cost** | Higher. | Lower (standard CMOS fabrication). |
| **Typical Use** | Metrology, scientific imaging. | High-speed industrial lines, embedded vision. |

**Advantage of CCD:** Superior image uniformity and dynamic range, critical for **precision metrology**. *(0.5 mark)*
**Advantage of CMOS:** On-chip integration of ADC and processing, enabling **very high frame rates** and lower cost. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a machine vision system. Describe in detail the role of the **illumination** and **optics** stages. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A machine vision camera with a **$\frac{2}{3}$″ sensor** (active area $8.8 \text{ mm} \times 6.6 \text{ mm}$) is fitted with a **$35 \text{ mm}$ focal-length lens**. The conveyor part measures **$88 \text{ mm} \times 66 \text{ mm}$**. The camera resolution is **$1280 \times 960$ pixels**. Calculate the **(i)** magnification, **(ii)** working distance, **(iii)** pixel resolution in mm/px, and **(iv)** minimum detectable defect size. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Stages (7 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid functional architecture (Section 4.1). A written answer should list and describe:

- **Illumination** *(2 marks)*: Provides controlled, repeatable lighting. Critical because raw image quality is determined at this stage. Techniques include backlight, dome/diffuse, ring, coaxial, structured, and dark-field. The choice depends on surface reflectance, geometry, and feature contrast.
- **Optics (Lens)** *(2 marks)*: Collects reflected/transmitted light, forms an image on the sensor, determines magnification, FOV, and depth of field. Use of **telecentric lenses** is preferred in metrology as they eliminate perspective distortion.
- **Sensor** *(1 mark)*: Converts photons to electrical signal; CCD vs CMOS as above.
- **Processing & Communication** *(2 marks)*: Image processing pipeline plus PLC/robot interface via discrete I/O, fieldbus, or IIoT protocols.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $w_s = 8.8 \text{ mm}$, $h_s = 6.6 \text{ mm}$, $f = 35 \text{ mm}$, $W_o = 88 \text{ mm}$, $H_o = 66 \text{ mm}$, resolution $= 1280 \text{ px}$.

**(i) Magnification:**

$$
m = \frac{w_s}{W_o} = \frac{8.8}{88} = 0.1
$$

> *(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) Working distance:**

$$
d_o = f \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{1}{m} \right) = 35 \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{1}{0.1} \right) = 35 \cdot 11 = 385 \text{ mm}
$$

> *(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Pixel resolution:**

$$
\text{Res}_{px} = \frac{W_o}{\text{width in px}} = \frac{88 \text{ mm}}{1280 \text{ px}} = 0.06875 \text{ mm/px}
$$

> *(1.5 marks)*

**(iv) Minimum detectable defect (Nyquist):**

$$
f_{min} = 2 \cdot \text{Res}_{px} = 2 \cdot 0.06875 = 0.1375 \text{ mm}
$$

> *(1.5 marks)*

**Conclusion:** The system can reliably detect defects of approximately **0.1375 mm** or larger.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **(a)** Explain the **image processing pipeline** of a machine vision system with reference to preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification. Mention at least one algorithm for each stage. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare **2D and 3D machine vision systems**. Discuss any **three industrial applications** of 3D vision, and explain the **structured light** method with a suitable sketch. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Image Processing Pipeline (7 marks):**

1. **Preprocessing** *(2 marks)*: Removes noise, enhances contrast. Algorithms: **Gaussian filter** for noise smoothing, **Histogram Equalization** for contrast stretching.
2. **Segmentation** *(2 marks)*: Isolates the ROI. Algorithms: **Otsu's thresholding** (auto binarization), **Canny edge detection** (multi-stage edge finder).
3. **Feature Extraction** *(1.5 marks)*: Computes descriptors (area, perimeter, moments, Hough lines, SIFT/SURF keypoints).
4. **Classification / Decision** *(1.5 marks)*: Pattern matching, **Template matching (NCC)**, or trained CNN/SVM.

**Part (b) — 2D vs 3D Vision (7 marks):**

- **2D Vision** *(2 marks)*: Uses intensity only; simple, cheap, fast. Cannot resolve height/depth.
- **3D Vision** *(2 marks)*: Adds depth axis; uses stereo, laser triangulation, ToF, or structured light.
- **Three 3D applications** *(1.5 marks, 0.5 each)*:
  1. Robotic **bin picking** of randomly oriented parts.
  2. **Weld seam tracking** in automotive robotic welding.
  3. **Volume measurement** of pharmaceutical powders.
- **Structured light method** *(1.5 marks)*: A projector casts a known pattern (line/grid) onto the object. A camera observes the deformation of the pattern. Triangulation of distorted pattern points yields a 3D point cloud. The mathematics is:

$$
Z = \frac{B \cdot f}{d + \Delta d}
$$

where $B$ is the **baseline** (projector–camera distance), $f$ is the focal length, $d$ is the reference disparity, and $\Delta d$ is the observed disparity shift.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Machine Vision Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Unit Mix-Ups:** Forgetting to convert sensor size from inches to mm (**1″ = 25.4 mm**). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing Magnification Direction:** Some students use $m = W_o / w_s$ instead of $m = w_s / W_o$. Always check: image is smaller than object for a standard camera, so $m < 1$.
> 3. **Skipping the Nyquist Check:** A common KTU trap — the camera resolution may be enough in raw pixel count but **insufficient** for the smallest defect. Always state the **2× rule**.
> 4. **No Mention of "Why":** Examiners penalize purely descriptive answers. Always justify *why* a particular illumination (e.g., backlight for silhouette) is chosen.
> 5. **Forgetting Communication Protocols:** When asked about integration with PLCs/robots, you **must** name a protocol — e.g., **OPC-UA, EtherCAT, PROFINET, MQTT** — for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Machine Vision** = automatic, non-contact, optical inspection / measurement / guidance in industrial environments (AIA definition).
- ✅ Six-stage architecture: **Illumination → Optics → Sensor → Interface → Processing → Output**.
- ✅ **CCD** = low noise, metrology; **CMOS** = fast, low-power, dominant in modern systems.
- ✅ Key formula: $d_o = f \cdot (1 + 1/m)$ where $m = \text{sensor size} / \text{object size}$.
- ✅ **Pixel resolution** = FOV (mm) / pixel count; **min defect** = $2 \times$ pixel resolution (**Nyquist**).
- ✅ Image processing pipeline: **Acquire → Preprocess → Segment → Extract → Classify → Communicate**.
- ✅ **Thresholding (Otsu)**, **Canny edges**, **Hough transform**, **Blob analysis**, **Template matching** are the staple KTU algorithms.
- ✅ **Backlighting** → silhouette; **Diffuse/Dome** → shiny/curved; **Dark-field** → scratches; **Structured light** → 3D.
- ✅ Industrial buses for vision integration: **EtherCAT, PROFINET, OPC-UA, MQTT** (for IIoT/Industry 4.0).
- ✅ 2D vision is intensity-only; 3D vision uses **stereo, laser triangulation, ToF, or structured light** to recover depth.
- ✅ Typical industrial camera specs: **30–1000 fps**, resolutions up to **20 MP**, **GigE Vision / USB3 Vision** interfaces.
- ✅ KTU examiner loves: **Nyquist sufficiency check**, **magnification derivation**, and **lighting-technique justification**.
- ✅ **Machine Vision ≠ Computer Vision** — keep the distinction crisp in your answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators for Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators for Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Sensors and Actuators?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors** are physical devices that detect changes in environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, position, light, etc.) and convert them into electrical signals that can be measured, recorded, or processed by a controller (PLC, microcontroller, or DCS).
>
> **Actuators** are the complementary devices that receive electrical signals from a controller and convert them into physical action — motion, force, heat, or light — thereby manipulating the controlled variable in the industrial process.

In an automated system, the **sensor–controller–actuator** chain forms a closed feedback loop. The sensor acts as the "eyes and ears," the controller acts as the "brain," and the actuator acts as the "muscles" of the system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Body:**
> - **Sensors** → *Sense organs* (skin feels heat, eyes see light, ears hear sound). They gather data.
> - **Controller (PLC/MCU)** → *Brain*. It processes sensory data and decides an action.
> - **Actuators** → *Muscles and limbs*. They execute the brain's command and move the body.
>
> Just as you pull your hand away when you touch a hot stove (sensor detects → brain decides → muscle moves), an industrial system uses the same feedback principle: sensor detects overshoot → controller computes error → actuator corrects the process variable.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

**Sensors are classified based on:**
- **Output type:** Analog (continuous) vs Digital (discrete)
- **Power requirement:** Active (self-generating) vs Passive (require external excitation)
- **Detection principle:** Resistive, capacitive, inductive, piezoelectric, optical, etc.
- **Measured variable:** Temperature, pressure, flow, level, position, force, etc.

**Actuators are classified based on:**
- **Energy domain:** Electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, mechanical
- **Motion type:** Linear (translational) vs Rotary
- **Control type:** Continuous (proportional) vs Discrete (on/off)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics** (must be memorized for KTU):
> - **Accuracy:** Closeness of reading to true value (expressed as ±% of full scale)
> - **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement
> - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change
> - **Sensitivity:** Output change per unit input change
> - **Range:** Min and max measurable input
> - **Linearity:** How straight the output-input curve is
> - **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for increasing vs decreasing input
> - **Response Time:** Time taken to reach 63.2% (one time-constant τ) of final value
> - **Drift:** Change of output with time at constant input
> - **Bandwidth:** Frequency range over which the sensor responds accurately

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensors — Working Principles and Types

### 2.1.1 Temperature Sensors

| Sensor Type | Principle | Output | Range (°C) | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermocouple** | Seebeck Effect (EMF at junction of two dissimilar metals) | mV (analog) | −200 to 2300 | Rugged, fast, cheap, low accuracy |
| **RTD (Pt100)** | Resistance change with temperature (metal, usually platinum) | Ω (resistance) | −200 to 850 | High accuracy, linear, expensive |
| **Thermistor (NTC/PTC)** | Resistance changes sharply with temperature (semiconductor) | Ω (resistance) | −55 to 150 | High sensitivity, non-linear, limited range |
| **IC Sensor (LM35, AD590)** | Bandgap reference / current output | V or µA (analog) | −55 to 150 | Built-in signal conditioning |
| **Infrared (Pyrometer)** | Planck's radiation law (non-contact) | V (analog) | −50 to 3000+ | Non-contact, measures moving/hot objects |

**RTD Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (for platinum RTD):**
$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$
where $R_0$ = resistance at 0°C, and $A, B, C$ are constants.

**Thermocouple Seebeck Voltage:**
$$V = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_B(T) - S_A(T)) \, dT$$
where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the Seebeck coefficients of the two metals (µV/°C).

### 2.1.2 Pressure Sensors

**Strain Gauge Principle (Wheatstone Bridge):**
The strain gauge resistance changes when deformed:
$$R = R_0 (1 + \varepsilon \cdot GF)$$
where $\varepsilon$ is strain and $GF$ (gauge factor) ≈ 2.0 for metal, 100+ for semiconductors.

**Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage:**
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right]$$

**Common pressure units:** Pa (SI), bar, psi, mmHg, atm
- **1 bar = 100 kPa = 14.5 psi**

### 2.1.3 Proximity Sensors

**Inductive Proximity Sensor** — detects metallic objects via eddy current losses in an LC oscillator. Range: 1–50 mm. Only detects metals.

**Capacitive Proximity Sensor** — detects both metals and non-metals (plastic, wood, liquid) by change in capacitance. Range: 1–50 mm. Sensitive to humidity.

**Optical (Photoelectric) Sensor** — uses light beam (LED/laser); detects via reflection, through-beam, or retro-reflective. Range up to 50 m.

**Ultrasonic Sensor** — emits 25–500 kHz sound; measures echo time-of-flight. Works for any material, including transparent ones. Range: 0.1–10 m.

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$
where $v$ ≈ **343 m/s** (speed of sound in air at 20°C), $t$ = round-trip echo time.

### 2.1.4 Position and Displacement Sensors

**LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):**
- A primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former
- A movable ferromagnetic core slides inside
- Output: differential voltage proportional to displacement

$$V_{out} = V_p (K \cdot x)$$
where $K$ is sensitivity (V/mm), $x$ is core displacement from null position. **LVDT is inherently a contact-less, frictionless device** (the core does not touch the windings).

**Encoders:**
- **Incremental Encoder:** outputs pulses (A, B channels, 90° phase shift for direction). Resolution = pulses per revolution (PPR). Needs homing on power-up.
- **Absolute Encoder:** outputs a unique binary/Gray code for every shaft position. Retains position on power loss.

**Resolver:** A rotary transformer giving analog sin/cos outputs. Rugged, used in harsh environments (military, aerospace).

### 2.1.5 Flow Sensors

| Type | Principle | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Orifice Plate** | Differential pressure (Bernoulli) | Clean liquids/gases |
| **Venturi Meter** | Differential pressure (low loss) | Large pipes |
| **Turbine Flowmeter** | Rotor speed ∝ flow rate | Clean liquids, high accuracy |
| **Electromagnetic** | Faraday's law (EMF = B·L·v) | Conductive liquids only |
| **Vortex Shedding** | Kármán vortex street frequency | Steam, gases |
| **Coriolis** | Coriolis force on oscillating tube | Mass flow, density, viscosity |
| **Ultrasonic Doppler** | Frequency shift of reflected sound | Dirty liquids, slurries |
| **Thermal Mass Flow** | Heat transfer ∝ mass flow | Gases |

**Electromagnetic Flowmeter (Faraday's Law):**
$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$
$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{E}{B \cdot D}$$
where $E$ = induced EMF (V), $B$ = magnetic field (T), $L = D$ = pipe diameter (m), $v$ = flow velocity (m/s).

**Vortex Shedding Frequency (Strouhal Number):**
$$f = S_r \cdot \frac{v}{d}$$
For bluff bodies, $S_r$ ≈ 0.2 over a wide Reynolds range, making $f$ linearly proportional to velocity.

### 2.1.6 Level Sensors

- **Float switch** — discrete (on/off)
- **Conductivity probes** — for conductive liquids
- **Capacitance probe** — for solids and liquids
- **Ultrasonic** — non-contact, time-of-flight
- **Radar / Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** — non-contact, accurate, expensive
- **Hydrostatic pressure** — submersible transducer
- **Optical (laser triangulation)** — solids in silos

### 2.1.7 Force, Torque, and Vision Sensors

- **Load Cell** — strain gauge-based; outputs mV/V. Common types: S-type, single-point, compression, tension.
- **Piezoelectric force sensor** — high bandwidth, dynamic only (no static response).
- **Vision sensors (smart cameras)** — pixel-based; perform pattern matching, OCR, barcode reading, defect detection.

## 2.2 Actuators — Working Principles and Types

### 2.2.1 Hydraulic Actuators

**Linear (Cylinder) and Rotary (Motor).** Operate on incompressible fluid (oil, 50–350 bar). Highest power density.

**Force from hydraulic cylinder:**
$$F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$
where $P$ = pressure (Pa), $D$ = piston diameter (m).

**Flow rate to velocity:**
$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$
where $Q$ = flow (m³/s).

**Power:**
$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$$

### 2.2.2 Pneumatic Actuators

Compressed air (typically **6–8 bar**, max 10 bar). Clean, fast, but compressible → poor position control.
- **Single-acting cylinder:** spring return, one air port
- **Double-acting cylinder:** two air ports, push and pull
- **Rotary vane / rack-and-pinion actuators:** quarter-turn for valves

Same force formula as hydraulic: $F = P \cdot A$. Since $P$ is ~100× lower than hydraulics, pneumatic cylinders are bulkier for the same force.

### 2.2.3 Electrical Actuators

**DC Motor:**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$
$$E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$
$$T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$$
where $E_b$ = back-EMF, $K$ = motor constant, $\phi$ = flux, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $T$ = torque.

**Stepper Motor:**
- Moves in discrete angular steps (typically 1.8° = 200 steps/rev for full-step)
- Open-loop positioning; can lose steps under overload
- Holding torque at standstill

**Servo Motor (BLDC with encoder):**
- Closed-loop control with feedback
- High dynamic response, used in CNC, robotics
- Field-oriented control (FOC) for sinusoidal commutation

**Solenoid:**
- Electromagnetic linear actuator, typically on/off (two-position)
- Used in valves, relays, door locks

### 2.2.4 Piezoelectric Actuator

Inverse piezoelectric effect — applied voltage causes nanometer-scale expansion.
$$x = d \cdot n \cdot V$$
where $d$ = piezo strain coefficient (~300 pm/V for PZT), $n$ = number of layers, $V$ = applied voltage.
Used in nanopositioning, AFM (atomic force microscopy), inkjet printer heads.

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning

> [!NOTE]
> Sensors rarely give a clean, directly-usable signal. **Signal conditioning** bridges the sensor and the controller. Common functions:
>
> 1. **Amplification** — boost mV signals (e.g., thermocouple 40 µV/°C) to 0–10 V or 4–20 mA
> 2. **Linearization** — correct non-linear sensor curves (thermistor is highly non-linear)
> 3. **Cold-junction compensation (CJC)** — for thermocouples
> 4. **Filtering** — remove 50/60 Hz mains noise (notch filter) and high-frequency noise (low-pass)
> 5. **Isolation** — galvanic isolation prevents ground loops
> 6. **Excitation** — provide stable power to passive sensors (e.g., 24 V for RTD bridge)
> 7. **A/D and D/A conversion** — interface analog to digital controllers

**Standard Industrial Current Loop:**
$$I = 4 + \frac{16 (V - V_{min})}{V_{max} - V_{min}} \text{ mA}$$
where 4 mA = zero, 20 mA = full scale. **4 mA is "live zero"** — distinguishes broken wire (0 mA) from 0% reading (4 mA).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0(1+\alpha T)$ (linear approx) | RTD resistance | Pt100: $\alpha = 0.00385/°C$ |
| 2 | $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$ | Faraday's EMF | Electromagnetic flowmeter |
| 3 | $f = S_r v/d$ | Vortex frequency | Vortex flowmeter |
| 4 | $d = vt/2$ | Time-of-flight | Ultrasonic distance/level |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = GF \cdot \Delta R/R$ | Strain relation | Strain gauge |
| 6 | $V_{out}/V_{ex} = (\Delta R/R)$ | Bridge output | Wheatstone bridge |
| 7 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Cylinder force | Hydraulic / pneumatic |
| 8 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Continuity | Actuator speed |
| 9 | $P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power | Motor / actuator |
| 10 | $V = K_e \omega$, $T = K_t I$ | DC motor | Motor control |
| 11 | $E_b = V - IR_a$ | Back-EMF equation | DC motor analysis |
| 12 | $T = J \alpha + B\omega + T_L$ | Motion equation | Servo load dynamics |
| 13 | $\theta_{step} = 360° / N$ | Stepper resolution | $N$ = steps per rev |
| 14 | $I = 4 + 16 (m - m_{min})/(m_{max}-m_{min})$ mA | Current loop | 4–20 mA standard |
| 15 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant | First-order response |
| 16 | $y(t) = Y(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Step response | 63.2% at $t=\tau$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Examiner's Hint:** Memorize the **4–20 mA current loop formula**, **DC motor equations** (E_b, T), and the **RTD linear approximation**. These appear every year in either Part A or Part B.

## 2.5 Selection Criteria for Sensors and Actuators

**For Sensors:**
- Range and resolution required
- Accuracy and precision (in %FS or %reading)
- Environment (temperature, humidity, EMI, vibration, hazardous area)
- Output type and compatibility (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, HART, IO-Link, Profinet)
- Response time / bandwidth
- Cost, size, IP rating
- Maintenance and calibration cycle

**For Actuators:**
- Force / torque required
- Speed / stroke length
- Duty cycle (continuous vs intermittent)
- Power source available (electric, hydraulic, pneumatic)
- Position control required? (Hydraulic/pneumatic alone = poor; servo-hydraulic = excellent)
- Hazardous/explosive atmosphere → pneumatic preferred
- Cost, life expectancy, MTBF

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RTD Temperature Measurement using Pt100

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Pt100 RTD has $R_0 = 100 \;\Omega$ at 0°C and temperature coefficient $\alpha = 0.00385 \;/°C$. Using the linear approximation, find (a) the resistance at 100°C, (b) the resistance change per °C, and (c) the bridge output voltage if connected in a quarter-bridge with excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V and three fixed 100 Ω resistors.

**Step 1 — Write the linear RTD equation:**
$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T = 100°C$:**
$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot (1 + 0.00385 \cdot 100)$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot (1 + 0.385)$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \cdot 1.385$$
$$\boxed{R_{100} = 138.5 \;\Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Resistance change per °C:**
$$\frac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \alpha = 100 \cdot 0.00385$$
$$\boxed{\frac{dR}{dT} = 0.385 \;\Omega/°C}$$

This matches the standard specification: **Pt100 changes 0.385 Ω/°C**.

**Step 4 — Quarter-bridge output voltage:**
In a quarter-bridge (one active arm, three fixed arms $R$):
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[\frac{R_T}{R_T + R} - \frac{R}{2R}\right] = V_{ex} \left[\frac{R_T}{R_T + R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

Substituting $R_T = 138.5$, $R = 100$, $V_{ex} = 5$ V:
$$V_{out} = 5 \left[\frac{138.5}{138.5 + 100} - 0.5\right]$$
$$V_{out} = 5 \left[\frac{138.5}{238.5} - 0.5\right]$$
$$V_{out} = 5 [0.5807 - 0.5]$$
$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot 0.0807$$
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.4037 \text{ V at } 100°C}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity in mV/°C:**
$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{0.4037 \text{ V}}{100°C} = 4.037 \text{ mV/°C}$$

This small signal must be amplified by an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128) before ADC conversion.

## 3.2 Derivation: DC Motor Speed vs Torque Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A. With applied voltage $V = 220$ V and armature current $I_a = 10$ A, find (a) back-EMF, (b) angular speed, (c) developed torque, and (d) mechanical power output.

**Step 1 — Back-EMF:**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 10 \cdot 0.5 = 220 - 5$$
$$\boxed{E_b = 215 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 — Angular speed:**
$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega \;\Rightarrow\; \omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{215}{0.05}$$
$$\boxed{\omega = 4300 \text{ rad/s}}$$

Convert to rpm: $\omega_{rpm} = 4300 \cdot 60/(2\pi) = 41{,}061$ rpm. (High — practical motors have lower speeds; this is illustrative.)

**Step 3 — Developed electromagnetic torque:**
$$T = K_t \cdot I_a = 0.05 \cdot 10$$
$$\boxed{T = 0.5 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical power developed:**
$$P = T \cdot \omega = 0.5 \cdot 4300 = 2150 \text{ W}$$
Or equivalently, $P = E_b \cdot I_a = 215 \cdot 10 = 2150$ W. ✓

**Step 5 — Electrical input power:**
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 220 \cdot 10 = 2200 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{2150}{2200} = 0.9773 = 97.73\%$$

Copper loss: $P_{Cu} = I_a^2 R_a = 100 \cdot 0.5 = 50$ W. Check: $2150 + 50 = 2200$ W ✓ (energy balance).

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Velocity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has piston diameter $D = 80$ mm and rod diameter $d = 40$ mm. Supply pressure $P = 100$ bar, return pressure = 0 bar. Pump flow $Q = 30$ L/min. Calculate (a) extending force, (b) retracting force, (c) extending speed, (d) retracting speed.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**
- $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \cdot 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $D = 0.08$ m, $d = 0.04$ m
- $A_{ext} = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (0.08)^2/4 = 5.027 \cdot 10^{-3}$ m²
- $A_{ret} = \pi (D^2 - d^2)/4 = \pi (0.0064 - 0.0016)/4 = 3.770 \cdot 10^{-3}$ m²
- $Q = 30$ L/min = $30 \cdot 10^{-3}/60$ = $5 \cdot 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 2 — Extending force:**
$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{ext} = 10^7 \cdot 5.027 \cdot 10^{-3}$$
$$\boxed{F_{ext} = 50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Retracting force:**
$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{ret} = 10^7 \cdot 3.770 \cdot 10^{-3}$$
$$\boxed{F_{ret} = 37{,}699 \text{ N} \approx 37.70 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Extending speed:**
$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{ext}} = \frac{5 \cdot 10^{-4}}{5.027 \cdot 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\boxed{v_{ext} \approx 0.0995 \text{ m/s} = 9.95 \text{ cm/s}}$$

**Step 5 — Retracting speed:**
$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_{ret}} = \frac{5 \cdot 10^{-4}}{3.770 \cdot 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\boxed{v_{ret} \approx 0.1326 \text{ m/s} = 13.26 \text{ cm/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify constant flow:** Since pump flow is constant, the rod side has smaller area → retracting is **faster** but produces **less force** (trade-off inherent in asymmetric cylinders).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor/Actuator vs Application

| Application | Best Sensor | Best Actuator | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bottle-filling level | Capacitive / GWR | Pneumatic valve + cylinder | Hygienic, fast |
| Steel-rolling mill thickness | Laser triangulation | Hydraulic servo cylinder | Hot metal, high force |
| CNC machine tool axis | Linear encoder (glass scale) | Servo motor + ballscrew | Precision, closed-loop |
| Conveyor part counting | Inductive proximity | – (no actuation needed) | Metallic parts only |
| 3D printer head | – (uses position feedback) | Stepper motor | Open-loop OK at low speed |
| Robotic arm joint | Encoder / resolver | Servo (BLDC) | High torque, dynamic |
| Oil pipeline flow | Coriolis mass flowmeter | Control valve + pneumatic actuator | Direct mass measurement |
| Boiler drum level | Differential pressure | Motorized valve | High T, high P, reliable |
| Pharmaceutical tablet counting | Vision sensor | Servo-driven hopper gate | Non-contact, high speed |
| AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) | LIDAR / IR + encoders | BLDC drive motor + steering servo | Mobile autonomy |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sensor–Controller–Actuator Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Physical Process] -- Process Variable PV --> sensB[Sensor]
    sensB -- Raw Signal mV or V --> condC[Signal Conditioner Amplifier and Filter]
    condC -- Standard Signal 4 to 20 mA --> ctrlD[Controller PLC or DCS]
    ctrlD -- Setpoint SP via HMI --> ctrlD
    ctrlD -- Control Signal --> actE[Actuator Valve or Motor]
    actE -- Manipulated Variable MV --> procA
    ctrlD -. Feedback to HMI .- ctrlD
```

> The sensor measures the **PV (Process Variable)**, the controller compares PV with **SP (Setpoint)**, computes the error $e = SP - PV$, applies a control law (P, PI, PID), and drives the **actuator** to adjust the **MV (Manipulated Variable)** — closing the loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Servo System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    refA[Reference Input Position Command] --> compB[Summing Junction]
    fbC[Position Feedback from LVDT] --> compB
    compB -- Error Signal --> ampD[Servo Amplifier]
    ampD --> valveE[Proportional Servo Valve]
    valveE --> cylF[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    cylF -- Mechanical Output --> loadG[Load]
    loadG -- Position --> fbC
    loadG -- Velocity --> fbH[Tachometer]
    fbH --> compB
```

> This is a **closed-loop position servo**. The LVDT measures actual position, and an inner velocity loop (tachometer feedback) damps oscillations — a classic two-degree-of-freedom servo architecture.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Temperature Control Loop (Pt100 + Heater)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ovenA[Oven Temperature T actual] -- Heat transfer --> rtdB[Pt100 RTD]
    rtdB -- Resistance change --> bridgeC[Wheatstone Bridge]
    bridgeC -- mV signal --> inaD[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    inaD -- 0 to 10 V --> mcuE[Microcontroller ADC]
    mcuE -- PID Algorithm --> pwmF[PWM Output]
    pwmF --> ssrG[Solid State Relay SSR]
    ssrG --> heaterH[Heater Element 2 kW]
    heaterH -- Heat --> ovenA
    spI[Setpoint from Keypad] --> mcuE
```

> The signal chain shows **why signal conditioning is essential** — the Pt100 gives only 0.385 Ω/°C, the bridge converts this to mV, and the instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620) raises it to a usable 0–10 V level for the ADC.

## 4.4 Architecture: Industrial Fieldbus Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    l1[Level 0 Sensors and Actuators RTD, LVDT, Valve, Motor] --> l2[Level 1 I O Modules with Signal Conditioning]
    l2 --> l3[Level 2 PLC or DCS Controller]
    l3 --> l4[Level 3 SCADA HMI Workstation]
    l4 --> l5[Level 4 MES and ERP]
    l3 -. Profinet or Modbus TCP .- l2
    l4 -. OPC UA or Ethernet .- l3
    l5 -. SQL Database .- l4
```

> This **Purdue/ISA-95 reference model** shows where sensors and actuators sit in a typical plant. Level 0 is the physical field — where this entire module's devices live and operate.

## 4.5 Signal Conditioning Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sA[Sensor Pt100 or Thermocouple] --> exB[Excitation Source Constant Current or Voltage]
    sA --> fiC[Input Protection RC Filter and TVS]
    fiC --> amD[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100]
    amD --> liE[Linearization LUT or Polynomial]
    liE --> coF[Cold Junction Compensation for TC only]
    liE --> fiG[Active Low Pass Filter fc 10 Hz]
    fiG --> adH[ADC 16 bit]
    adH --> mcI[Microcontroller]
    mcI --> daJ[DAC 4 to 20 mA Output]
    daJ --> isoK[Galvanic Isolation]
    isoK --> l1[PLC Analog Input Module]
```

> Each block has a precise purpose. The **order** is critical: protection first, then amplification (to avoid saturating downstream stages with noise), then linearization, then filtering, then conversion.
