# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Normally Open (NO) and Normally Closed (NC) contacts in an electromechanical switch. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NO Contact (Form A) | NC Contact (Form B) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Resting State** | Open (no current flows) | Closed (current flows) |
| **On Actuation** | Closes – circuit energised | Opens – circuit de-energised |
| **Schematic Symbol** | Two parallel lines separated, with hinged arm | Same symbol with a diagonal slash over the line |
| **Industrial Application** | "Start" push button on a motor control panel – presses to start | "Stop" push button – pressed to halt; also used in safety E-stop loops (fail-safe) |

> **Examiner's Note:** Use a *memorable* phrase – *"NO needs a push to work; NC needs a push to stop."*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is meant by the "positive opening operation" of a switch, and why is it important in safety circuits?**

**Model Answer:**
The **positive opening operation** is a design feature, mandated by **IEC 60947-5-1**, in which the normally-closed (NC) contacts of a switch are mechanically *forced apart* by a rigid linkage when the actuator reaches its open position. This breaking of the NC contact is **not dependent on spring force** alone – it is *positively driven*.

**Importance in Safety Circuits:**
1. A NC contact can never *weld shut* and remain closed when the actuator demands opening.
2. It guarantees that a broken/loose wire or welded contact results in a *safe* (open) state.
3. It is the cornerstone of **Category 1, 3, and 4** safety circuits per ISO 13849-1.
4. All **Emergency-Stop** and **Guard-Door Interlock** circuits require positively-opened NC contacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **snap-action limit switch**. List four common actuator head types used in industrial limit switches.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A snap-action limit switch is rated **5 A at 250 VAC, AC-15 utilization category**. It has a mechanical life of **10 million operations** and an electrical life of **500,000 operations** at rated load. The switch is to be used in an application where the inrush current is **15 A** for 100 ms and the steady-state current is **4 A**. Estimate the expected electrical life of the switch in this application. State two design precautions to extend switch life.

### Model Solution – (a)

**Construction (Block Diagram – see SECTION 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2):**

A snap-action limit switch consists of:
1. **Actuator Head** (top section) – Mounted in 90° increments, houses the lever/roller/plunger that receives the external force.
2. **Switch Body** (middle section) – Sealed enclosure containing the contact mechanism; typically die-cast zinc or engineering polymer.
3. **Contact Block** (internal) – Contains the moving and stationary contacts mounted on a spring-loaded snap-blade.
4. **Terminal Chamber** (bottom section) – Houses screw or spring-clamp terminals for wiring.
5. **Cable Entry** – Threaded port (PG/M20) for cable gland.

**Working Principle:**
- When the actuator is *not engaged*, the return spring holds the moving contact in its default position (NO open, NC closed).
- When an external object applies force to the actuator, the linkage pushes against the snap-blade.
- At a specific *operating point*, the snap-blade rapidly transitions (in 1–5 ms) from one stable position to the other, producing a *bounce-free* contact transfer.
- The contacts remain in the new state as long as the actuator is held in the actuated position.
- When the object moves away, the return spring pulls the snap-blade back through the snap point, restoring the original state.

**Four Common Actuator Head Types:**

| # | Actuator Type | Typical Application |
|---|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Top Plunger (pin) | Direct vertical impact, short travel |
| 2 | Top Roller Plunger | Camming applications, sliding doors |
| 3 | Side Rotary Lever (roller attached) | Conveyor end-of-travel, swing-gate position |
| 4 | Wobble Stick (coil spring antenna) | Unpredictable approach angles, light objects |

### Model Solution – (b)

**Given Data:**
- Rated current: $I_{\text{rated}} = 5$ A
- Rated electrical life: $L_{\text{rated}} = 500{,}000$ operations
- Steady-state current: $I_{\text{ss}} = 4$ A
- Inrush current: $I_{\text{inrush}} = 15$ A (negligible duration)

**Step 1 – Identify the Governing Current**

Since the inrush is only 100 ms, it contributes minimally to arc erosion (most erosion occurs during steady-state arcing on contact opening). We use the *steady-state* current for life estimation:

$$I_{\text{operational}} = 4 \;\text{A}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the Derating Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{new}} &= L_{\text{rated}} \times \left( \frac{I_{\text{rated}}}{I_{\text{operational}}} \right)^{1.5} \\
&= 500{,}000 \times \left( \frac{5}{4} \right)^{1.5} \\
&= 500{,}000 \times (1.25)^{1.5} \\
&= 500{,}000 \times 1.3975 \\
&\approx 698{,}780 \;\text{operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Engineering Interpretation**

The switch can be expected to last approximately **700,000 operations** at 4 A, which is *higher* than its rated 500,000 operations at 5 A. The 20% current margin extends life by ~40%.

**Step 4 – Two Design Precautions to Extend Life:**

1. **Use a Snubber (RC Network) Across Inductive Loads** – A 100 Ω resistor in series with a 0.1 μF capacitor across the contact suppresses the inductive kick and reduces arc energy during opening.
2. **Operate Below Rated Current (Derating)** – Maintaining operation at ≤ 80% of rated current dramatically extends life (as shown above).
3. **Use Silver-Cadmium Oxide (AgCdO) Contacts** – These have superior arc erosion resistance compared to pure silver.
4. **Install in a Cool, Dry, Vibration-Free Location** – Heat and vibration accelerate mechanical wear of the snap-blade.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using the *inrush* current (15 A) as the steady-state for the derating calculation. This *grossly underestimates* life and is a sure way to lose 2 marks. The inrush duration (100 ms) is too short to dominate the arc-erosion budget.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total)

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Pole and Throw** notation for switch contacts. Draw the schematic symbols and describe the operation of **SPST, SPDT, DPST, and DPDT** switches. Give one industrial example of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **DPDT selector switch** is used to reverse the direction of a 3-phase induction motor. Draw the power circuit diagram showing how the switch swaps two of the three phase lines (R, Y, B) to achieve reversal. State the safety precaution that must be observed when using such a switch.

### Model Solution – (a)

**Pole–Throw Notation Definition:**

- **Pole (P)** – The number of *independent* circuits that the switch can simultaneously control.
- **Throw (T)** – The number of *distinct output positions* to which each pole can be connected.

**Schematic Symbols, Operation, and Industrial Examples:**

| Configuration | Symbol (Description) | Operation | Industrial Example |
|---------------|---------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **SPST** | Two terminals, hinged arm open or closed | Simplest ON/OFF switch. One circuit, one path. | Wall light switch (domestic / office) |
| **SPDT** | Three terminals: COM, NO, NC. Hinged arm pivots. | Routes one input to one of two outputs. | Coolant pump AUTO/MANUAL selector on a CNC machine |
| **DPST** | Two pairs of terminals, mechanically linked. | Two independent ON/OFF circuits, switched together. | Simultaneous switching of line and neutral for a 230 V load |
| **DPDT** | Two poles, two throws per pole. Equivalent to two synchronised SPDT switches. | Used for *reversing*, *transfer*, or *two-speed* switching. | Forward–Reverse motor switch, two-speed fan control, changeover between main and standby supply |

> [!TIP]
> A **Changeover (CO) switch** is the most common *single-pole* reversing element; the DPDT is its *double-pole* counterpart. For 3-phase motor reversal, the DPDT is the textbook choice.

### Model Solution – (b)

**3-Phase Motor Reversal Using DPDT Switch:**

In a 3-phase induction motor, **reversing any two of the three phase lines** reverses the direction of the rotating magnetic field, and hence the rotor.

**Power Circuit Diagram (ASCII Schematic):**

```
   R (L1) ----+--------------------+--------------------+
              |                    |                    |
   Y (L2) ----+--------------------+--------------------+
              |                    |                    |
   B (L3) ----+--------------------+--------------------+
              |                    |                    |
        +-----+----+         +-----+----+              |
        |  FOR    |         |  REV     |              |
        |  (LEFT) |         |  (RIGHT) |              |
        +-----+----+         +-----+----+              |
              |                    |                    |
   R (L1) ----+                    |                    |
   Y (L2) ----+--------------------+                    |
   B (L3) ----+                                         |
              |                                         |
              +-----------------------------------------+
                          |
                          V
                  To Motor Terminals
                  (U, V, W)
```

**Operation:**

- In the **FORWARD** position: R→U, Y→V, B→W (phase sequence U-V-W).
- In the **REVERSE** position: R→W, Y→V, B→U (phase sequence W-V-U, which is the reverse).

The DPDT switch swaps **R and B** while keeping Y common.

**Critical Safety Precaution:**

> [!WARNING]
> **MANDATORY:** The motor must come to a **complete stop** (zero RPM) *before* the DPDT switch is thrown from FORWARD to REVERSE. If reversed at speed, the motor acts as a *plugging brake* and draws a destructive **locked-rotor current** of 6–8× full-load current. This will:
> - Blow fuses / trip breakers,
> - Damage the switch contacts by severe arcing,
> - Mechanically shock-couple the load (belts, gears).
>
> **Solution:** Insert a *time-delay relay* (typically 1–3 seconds) between forward and reverse to ensure the motor has stopped. Industrial practice uses a *forward-reverse-stop* push-button station with mechanical and electrical interlocks.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Triggers for this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Pole" with "Throw"** – A SPST has *one pole* and *one throw*; a SPDT has *one pole* and *two throws*. Students often swap these terms.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the schematic symbol** – A question worth 7 marks that asks "draw and explain" loses 2 marks if no symbol is shown.
> 3. **Stating "Normally Open" without specifying the resting state** – Always clarify *with respect to what* (no force, no current).
> 4. **Omitting the safety interlock in motor-reversal circuits** – Examiners specifically look for *plugging protection*. Losing this is a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 5. **Using inrush current instead of steady-state current** in life derating – See Section 5.2(b) warning.
> 6. **Confusing IP65, IP67, and IP68 ratings** – IP65 = dust-tight + water-jet; IP67 = immersion to 1 m; IP68 = continuous immersion (manufacturer-specified depth). A common error is to use them interchangeably.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Electromechanical Switches**

- ⚡ An **electromechanical switch** converts a *mechanical input* into an *electrical output* by physical contact make/break.
- ⚡ **SPST** = ON/OFF ; **SPDT** = Changeover ; **DPST** = two simultaneous ON/OFF ; **DPDT** = two simultaneous changeovers.
- ⚡ **NO (Form A)** = open at rest ; **NC (Form B)** = closed at rest ; **CO (Form C)** = SPDT with one NO + one NC.
- ⚡ **Snap-action** mechanism = bounce-free, fast, positive-opening — used in *all* industrial limit switches.
- ⚡ **Positive opening** (IEC 60947-5-1) = NC contacts *mechanically forced* open; mandatory for safety circuits.
- ⚡ **NEMA A150 / AC-15** = small AC solenoids ; **NEMA B300** = larger AC solenoids ; **NEMA Q300 / DC-13** = DC inductive loads.
- ⚡ **Contact resistance** $R_c = \rho_s / F_c$ — *inversely* proportional to contact force; pure silver ~0.3 mΩ typical.
- ⚡ **Electrical life** $\propto I^{-1.5}$ — derate aggressively when operating above rated current.
- ⚡ **Mechanical life ≠ Electrical life** — electrical life is always the *lower* number due to arc erosion.
- ⚡ **DC current rating ≈ 0.3 × AC rating** — DC arcs are *harder* to extinguish (no zero-crossing).
- ⚡ **IP65** (dust + jets), **IP67** (immersion 1 m), **IP68** (continuous immersion) — per IEC 60529.
- ⚡ **Limit switch operating characteristics**: Pre-Travel (PT), Over-Travel (OT), Differential Travel (DT), Operating Force (Fₒₚ).
- ⚡ **Motor reversal** = swap *any two* of the three phase lines; *always* include a time-delay interlock to prevent plugging.
- ⚡ **Snubber circuit** (R-C across contact) = primary method to extend switch life on *inductive* (solenoid, relay coil, motor) loads.
- ⚡ **Safety-rated** switches (Schmersal, Euchner, Allen-Bradley 440P) carry the *positive-opening symbol* ⏚ on the nameplate.
- ⚡ **E-Stop philosophy**: NC contact in a *positive-opening* switch, wired in series with a *safety relay* (e.g., Pilz PNOZ) — *single fault cannot prevent stop command*.
- ⚡ **Contact material hierarchy** for low-level signals: Gold (Au) > Silver-Nickel (AgNi) > Silver (Ag); for power switching: Silver-Cadmium Oxide (AgCdO) > Silver.
- ⚡ A switch's **MTBF** should always be specified in datasheets; safety-critical applications demand MTBF in *decades*.
- ⚡ In a PLC system, switches connect to **24 VDC digital input modules** — sourcing (PNP) or sinking (NPN) — never directly to AC mains.
- ⚡ **IEC 60947-5-1** = the *primary* international standard for low-voltage switchgear and controlgear; **ISO 13849-1** = safety of machinery.
- ⚡ **Atwood's law of switches**: *"A switch that never operates is suspicious; a switch that operates too often is wearing out."* — Plan preventive replacement at 80% of rated electrical life.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts **linear displacement** into a corresponding **electrical (voltage) signal**. It operates on the principle of **mutual inductance variation** between a primary winding and two secondary windings, achieved by the axial movement of a soft-iron core.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT612)**, the LVDT is classified as an **electromechanical transducer** widely used in industrial automation for precision measurement of position, force, pressure, and acceleration (when coupled with appropriate mechanical linkages).

**Key Characteristics (KTU Board Highlight):**
- **Type:** Passive, inductive, contact-less transducer
- **Input Quantity:** Linear displacement (in mm or µm)
- **Output Quantity:** Differential AC voltage (in mV or V)
- **Excitation Required:** AC primary excitation of **1 V to 24 V RMS at 50 Hz to 20 kHz**
- **Typical Range:** **±0.1 mm to ±500 mm** depending on construction
- **Accuracy:** Up to **0.1 %** of full scale

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sliding Magnetic Bridge"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a transformer that has a fixed coil on the left (primary) and two receiving coils on the right (secondaries). Now imagine a soft-iron "magnetic bridge" that slides back and forth along the axis. When the bridge sits exactly in the middle, it transfers equal magnetic flux to both receiving coils, and the system is balanced — *just like a perfectly centered seesaw*. Slide the bridge to the right, and it links more strongly with the right coil; slide it left, and the left coil gets more flux. The **difference** between the two received signals tells you exactly *how far* and *in which direction* the bridge has moved.

This is precisely how an **LVDT operates** — the *core* is the "magnetic bridge," the *primary* is the energizing coil, and the two *secondaries* are the receiving coils. The displacement of the core modulates the **mutual inductance**, producing a differential voltage output.

---

## 1.3 Construction at a Glance

The LVDT consists of the following physical components (each carries marks in the KTU ESE):

1. **Primary Winding (P):** A single coil wound on a non-magnetic former, energized by an **AC excitation source**.
2. **Two Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Identical coils wound on the same former, connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
3. **Movable Soft-Iron Core (Armature):** A cylindrical, high-permeability nickel-iron core that slides axially along the bore of the coil assembly.
4. **Bore / Hollow Former:** A non-magnetic (typically glass-filled polymer or ceramic) cylindrical form on which the windings are placed.
5. **Shielding Case:** A ferromagnetic shield that isolates the LVDT from external electromagnetic interference (EMI).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The two secondaries must be wound **identically and symmetrically** about the primary. If asked to draw, students often mistakenly place S₁ and S₂ on one side — remember, they flank the primary on **opposite ends** along the axis.

---

## 1.4 Symbol & Physical Appearance

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Outer Diameter | **8 mm to 50 mm** (typical) |
| Length | **20 mm to 500 mm** |
| Core Diameter | **Slightly smaller than bore** (small air gap maintained) |
| Output Connector | Shielded cable with 4 / 6-pin connector |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Mutual Inductance Variation

The LVDT works on the principle that the **mutual inductance** between the primary and each secondary depends on the **axial position of the core**. When the core moves:

- **Toward S₁:** Flux linkage with S₁ increases → $V_{s_1}$ increases.
- **Away from S₂:** Flux linkage with S₂ decreases → $V_{s_2}$ decreases.
- **Net output:** $V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2}$ becomes **positive**.

Conversely, when the core moves the other way, $V_{out}$ becomes **negative**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The differential (subtractive) connection is what makes LVDT a **"differential"** transformer. This arrangement:
> - Cancels common-mode disturbances (temperature, supply drift)
> - Doubles the output sensitivity (compared to a single secondary)
> - Provides a **natural null reference** at the midpoint

---

## 2.2 Three Operating Positions

The KTU examiner expects students to describe these three positions explicitly:

### (a) Null Position (Core Centred)
- Core is symmetrically placed between S₁ and S₂.
- Mutual inductances $M_1 = M_2$.
- EMF induced in both secondaries is **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase** (series opposition).
- Net output: $V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2} = 0$ V.
- **This is the calibration reference point.**

### (b) Core Displaced Toward S₁ (Forward Stroke)
- Flux linking S₁ increases; flux linking S₂ decreases.
- $V_{s_1} > V_{s_2}$.
- $V_{out} > 0$, in phase with the primary excitation voltage $V_p$.

### (c) Core Displaced Toward S₂ (Reverse Stroke)
- Flux linking S₂ increases; flux linking S₁ decreases.
- $V_{s_2} > V_{s_1}$.
- $V_{out} < 0$, **180° out of phase** with $V_p$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam verified** and frequently tested in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_p = V_{pm} \sin(\omega t)$ | Primary excitation voltage (AC source) | V |
| 2 | $V_{s_1} = -j\omega M_1 \, I_p$ | Induced EMF in secondary 1 | V |
| 3 | $V_{s_2} = -j\omega M_2 \, I_p$ | Induced EMF in secondary 2 | V |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2} = -j\omega(M_1 - M_2) I_p$ | Differential output voltage | V |
| 5 | $M_1 = M_2 = M_0$ (at null) | At null position, mutual inductances are equal | H |
| 6 | $V_{out} = 0$ (at null) | Output is zero when core is centred | V |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | Sensitivity of LVDT (gradient of linear region) | V/mm |
| 8 | $\text{Range} = \pm x_{max}$ | Linear measurement range (typically $\pm 1$ to $\pm 250$ mm) | mm |
| 9 | $\text{Resolution} \approx \infty$ (theoretical) | Practically limited by electronic noise to ~1 µm | µm |
| 10 | $V_{out} = k \cdot x \cdot V_p \cdot \omega$ | Proportionality relationship (in linear region) | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **mutual inductance ($M$)** with **self-inductance ($L$)**. LVDT works on **mutual** inductance between *separate* coils, not on self-inductance changes. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LVDTs are **indispensable** in:

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Aerospace** | Flight control surface position feedback, landing-gear actuators |
| **Hydraulics** | Piston-cylinder position sensing in heavy machinery |
| **CNC Machine Tools** | Tool displacement, spindle growth compensation |
| **Civil Engineering** | Structural deflection monitoring, crack-width gauges |
| **Process Industry** | Valve stem position feedback in control loops |
| **Robotics** | Joint position sensing in robotic arms |
| **Nuclear Reactors** | Control-rod position monitoring (radiation-hardened versions) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why LVDT over a potentiometer?** LVDT is **contactless** (no friction, no wear), has **infinite resolution** (limited only by demodulator noise), and offers **exceptional linearity** (±0.1 % FS). It is the *de facto* standard for high-precision industrial displacement measurement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Output Voltage Expression

### Step 1 — Primary Excitation
Let the AC excitation applied to the primary be:

$$
V_p(t) = V_{pm} \sin(\omega t)
$$

where $V_{pm}$ is the peak amplitude and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (typically $2\pi \times 5000$ rad/s for a 5 kHz excitation).

### Step 2 — Primary Current
If the primary winding has self-inductance $L_p$ and resistance $R_p$, the primary current is:

$$
I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_{pm}}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}} \, \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

where $\phi$ is the phase angle of the primary circuit. The magnitude of primary current is:

$$
I_p = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The primary coil forms an RL circuit with the AC source; we use the standard phasor impedance $Z_p = \sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}$ to convert voltage to current magnitude.

### Step 3 — EMF Induced in Secondary 1
By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the EMF induced in secondary winding $S_1$ due to mutual inductance $M_1$ with the primary is:

$$
V_{s_1} = -j \omega M_1 \, I_p
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The mutual inductance $M_1$ couples the AC flux from the primary into $S_1$. The negative sign and $j$ factor (i.e., $-j\omega$) reflect the **90° phase lag** typical of inductively coupled coils.

### Step 4 — EMF Induced in Secondary 2
Similarly, the EMF induced in $S_2$ is:

$$
V_{s_2} = -j \omega M_2 \, I_p
$$

### Step 5 — Differential Output (Series Opposition)
The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the output is the difference:

$$
V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2}
$$

Substituting from Steps 3 and 4:

$$
V_{out} = -j \omega M_1 \, I_p - (-j \omega M_2 \, I_p)
$$

$$
V_{out} = -j \omega (M_1 - M_2) \, I_p
$$

### Step 6 — Null Condition
At the null (centre) position, by symmetry of construction:

$$
M_1 = M_2 = M_0
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{out} = -j \omega (M_0 - M_0) \, I_p = 0
$$

This confirms **zero output at null**.

### Step 7 — Linear Region Approximation
For small displacements $\vert x \vert \leq x_{max}$ around the null, the difference in mutual inductances is approximately **linear** in displacement:

$$
M_1 - M_2 = K_m \, x
$$

where $K_m$ is a constant (H/m) determined by the LVDT's geometry and core permeability.

### Step 8 — Final Output Equation
Substituting back:

$$
V_{out} = -j \omega \, K_m \, x \, I_p
$$

$$
V_{out} = -j \omega \, K_m \, x \cdot \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The output voltage is directly proportional to displacement $x$ (in the linear region), which is the **fundamental operating characteristic** of an LVDT. The phase of $V_{out}$ (relative to $V_p$) indicates the **direction** of displacement.

---

## 3.2 Sensitivity Derivation

**Sensitivity** $S$ is defined as the change in output voltage per unit displacement:

$$
S = \dfrac{d V_{out}}{dx}
$$

From the final output equation:

$$
S = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left[-j \omega \, K_m \, x \, \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}\right]
$$

$$
S = -j \omega \, K_m \, \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}
$$

The **magnitude** of sensitivity:

$$
\vert S \vert = \dfrac{\omega \, K_m \, V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Higher excitation frequency $\omega$ and higher primary voltage $V_p$ increase sensitivity — but they also increase heating and core losses. The practical trade-off is **2 kHz to 10 kHz** for general industrial LVDTs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Board Pattern, 7-Mark Standard):**
> An LVDT has a primary excitation of $V_p = 5$ V RMS at 5 kHz. The mutual-inductance change per unit displacement is $K_m = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ H/m. The primary winding has $R_p = 50 \, \Omega$ and $L_p = 5$ mH. Calculate the output voltage for a core displacement of $x = +0.5$ mm toward S₁.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute angular frequency:**

$$
\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \times 5000 = 31415.93 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute primary impedance:**

$$
\omega L_p = 31415.93 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 157.08 \, \Omega
$$

$$
Z_p = \sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + 157.08^2}
$$

$$
Z_p = \sqrt{2500 + 24674.13} = \sqrt{27174.13} = 164.85 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute primary current:**

$$
I_p = \dfrac{V_p}{Z_p} = \dfrac{5}{164.85} = 30.33 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute mutual inductance difference:**

$$
\Delta M = M_1 - M_2 = K_m \cdot x = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta M = 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ H} = 0.2 \, \mu\text{H}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute output voltage magnitude:**

$$
V_{out} = \omega \cdot \Delta M \cdot I_p = 31415.93 \times 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 30.33 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 31415.93 \times 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.03033
$$

$$
V_{out} = 1.906 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.191 \text{ mV RMS}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The output is **positive** (in phase with primary), confirming that the core moved **toward S₁**. The very small magnitude reflects typical industrial sensitivities of **0.1 mV to 10 mV per 0.01 mm displacement**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Null Position | Core at $+x$ | Core at $-x$ |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| $M_1$ | $M_0$ | $M_0 + \Delta M$ | $M_0 - \Delta M$ |
| $M_2$ | $M_0$ | $M_0 - \Delta M$ | $M_0 + \Delta M$ |
| $V_{s_1}$ | $V_0$ | $V_0 + \Delta V$ | $V_0 - \Delta V$ |
| $V_{s_2}$ | $V_0$ | $V_0 - \Delta V$ | $V_0 + \Delta V$ |
| $V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2}$ | $0$ | $+2\Delta V$ | $-2\Delta V$ |
| Phase (w.r.t. $V_p$) | — | **In phase** | **180° out of phase** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LVDT Operation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the LVDT — from excitation to phase-discriminated output. It represents the **signal path and signal conditioning** architecture rather than a physical cross-section (which is impossible to render meaningfully in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACSRC[AC Excitation Source 1 to 24 V RMS 1 to 20 kHz] --> PRIM[Primary Winding P on Hollow Former]
    PRIM --> CORE[Soft Iron Core Axial Position x]
    CORE -->|Flux Linkage M1| SEC1[Secondary Coil S1 Left Side]
    CORE -->|Flux Linkage M2| SEC2[Secondary Coil S2 Right Side]
    SEC1 --> SOP[Series Opposition Subtraction Node]
    SEC2 --> SOP
    SOP --> RAWAC[Raw Differential AC Output Vout]
    RAWAC --> DEMOD[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> FILTER[Low Pass Filter Cutoff 100 Hz]
    FILTER --> DCAMP[DC Amplifier Gain 10 to 1000]
    DCAMP --> DISP[Final Output DC Voltage Proportional to Displacement]
    DEMOD --> PHASE[Phase Comparator]
    PHASE --> DIR[Direction Indicator Sign of Displacement]
    DIR --> CTRL[Industrial Controller PLC or DCS]
    DISP --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT[Final Actuator Valve Motor Cylinder]
```

---

## 4.2 Construction Cross-Section (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIELD[Ferromagnetic Shield Outer Casing]
        subgraph COILSET[Coil Assembly on Non Magnetic Former]
            S1[Secondary Winding S1 Left Section]
            PRIM[Primary Winding P Central Section]
            S2[Secondary Winding S2 Right Section]
        end
        CORE[Movable Soft Iron Core Armature Slides Axially Inside Bore]
    end
    S1 --- SOP[Series Opposing Connection]
    S2 --- SOP
    PRIM --- EXC[AC Excitation Input Vp Sin Omega t]
    CORE ---|Displacement x| MECH[Mechanical Input Linear Motion in mm]
    SOP --- OUT[Differential Output Vout Vs1 minus Vs2]
```

---

## 4.3 Signal Conditioning Chain (KTU Board Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LVDT[LVDT Sensor Body] --> ACV[Raw AC Differential Voltage]
    ACV --> PD[Phase Sensitive Detector uses primary as reference]
    PD --> LPF[Active Low Pass Filter first order RC]
    LPF --> AMP[DC Amplifier Instrumentation Grade]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 12 bit or 16 bit]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller or PLC Analog Input]
    MCU --> HMI[Human Machine Interface Display SCADA]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always mention that the **primary excitation itself is used as the reference signal** for the phase-sensitive demodulator. Without this reference, the demodulator cannot distinguish "forward" from "reverse" displacement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define an LVDT. Mention any two advantages of LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive, inductive transducer that converts **linear displacement** into a proportional **differential AC voltage** by varying the mutual inductance between a primary winding and two secondary windings via an axially movable soft-iron core.

**Two advantages (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Frictionless and contactless operation:** The core does not touch the windings, eliminating mechanical wear and tear. This gives LVDT an extremely long operational life compared to potentiometers.
2. **Infinite resolution:** Since the output is a continuous function of displacement (limited only by electronic noise), LVDT has theoretically infinite resolution, whereas a potentiometer is limited by the discrete resistance of its windings.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Why is the output of an LVDT zero at the null position? What is the significance of this null point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason for zero output (2 marks):**
At the null (centre) position, the soft-iron core is symmetrically positioned between the two secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$. Therefore, the mutual inductances are equal: $M_1 = M_2 = M_0$. Since the secondaries are connected in series opposition, the induced EMFs $V_{s_1}$ and $V_{s_2}$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase. Their algebraic sum (difference, in opposition) is exactly zero:
$$V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2} = 0$$

**Significance of the null point (1 mark):**
The null point serves as a **built-in calibration reference**. Any drift from zero at this reference indicates either a fault in the LVDT, a misalignment of the core, or drift in the signal-conditioning electronics. It is also the point of **maximum linearity** of the output characteristic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an LVDT. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
An LVDT consists of the following parts:
1. **Primary winding (P):** A single coil wound on a hollow, non-magnetic cylindrical former. It is energized by an AC source of typically 1–24 V RMS at 1–20 kHz.
2. **Two secondary windings (S₁ and S₂):** Two identical coils wound symmetrically on either side of the primary on the same former. They are connected in **series opposition** so that their EMFs subtract.
3. **Movable soft-iron core (armature):** A cylindrical core of high-permeability nickel-iron alloy that slides axially inside the bore of the coil assembly. Its position determines the flux linkage with each secondary.
4. **Shielding case:** A ferromagnetic cylindrical housing that provides magnetic shielding and mechanical protection.
5. **End caps and bearings:** Guide the core and protect the windings.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
When the AC excitation is applied to the primary, it produces an alternating magnetic flux. This flux links with both secondaries through the core.

- When the **core is at the null (centre) position**, equal flux links both secondaries, so $V_{s_1} = V_{s_2}$ and $V_{out} = 0$.
- When the **core moves toward S₁**, flux linking S₁ increases and flux linking S₂ decreases. Thus $V_{s_1} > V_{s_2}$ and the output $V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2}$ becomes **positive** and is **in phase** with the primary voltage.
- When the **core moves toward S₂**, the reverse happens: $V_{out}$ becomes **negative** and is **180° out of phase** with the primary voltage.

The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ is proportional to the displacement (within the linear range), and the **phase** indicates the direction.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the output voltage of an LVDT. Show that the output is zero at the null position. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Let the AC primary excitation be:
$$V_p(t) = V_{pm} \sin(\omega t)$$

The primary current through the primary winding of impedance $Z_p$ is:
$$I_p = \dfrac{V_p}{Z_p} = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}$$

The EMF induced in secondary 1 due to mutual inductance $M_1$ is:
$$V_{s_1} = -j\omega M_1 \, I_p$$

Similarly, the EMF induced in secondary 2 is:
$$V_{s_2} = -j\omega M_2 \, I_p$$

The two secondaries are connected in series opposition, so the differential output is:
$$V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2} = -j\omega (M_1 - M_2) \, I_p$$

This is the **general output equation**.

At the **null position**, the core is symmetrically placed, and by construction:
$$M_1 = M_2 = M_0$$

Substituting:
$$V_{out} = -j\omega (M_0 - M_0) \, I_p = 0$$

Hence the output is **zero at null**, confirming the calibration reference.

For small displacements $x$ from null, assuming linearity in the operating region:
$$M_1 - M_2 = K_m \cdot x$$

where $K_m$ is the mutual-inductance gradient (H/m). Therefore:
$$V_{out} = -j\omega \, K_m \, x \cdot \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}}$$

This shows that $V_{out}$ is **directly proportional** to displacement $x$, and the **phase** (sign of the $j$ term) indicates the direction of motion. ∎

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Draw the output characteristic curve of an LVDT (output voltage vs displacement) and explain its three regions of operation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Output Characteristic (3.5 marks):**
The output characteristic is an **S-shaped curve** (also called a sigmoidal or hyperbolic curve) when output voltage is plotted against displacement over the full range of motion:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Displacement negative extreme] --> B[Linear Region Reverse]
    B --> C[Null Position x equal zero]
    C --> D[Linear Region Forward]
    D --> E[Displacement positive extreme]
```

**Three Regions (3.5 marks):**

1. **Linear Region (forward stroke, $0 < x \leq +x_{max}$):** The output voltage increases linearly with displacement. The slope (sensitivity) is positive. This is the **useful operating region**.

2. **Null Region ($-\epsilon \leq x \leq +\epsilon$):** A small dead-zone around the centre where the characteristic transitions through zero. Within a small symmetric range, the output is approximately zero and the characteristic is linear with high slope.

3. **Saturation / Non-linear Region ($\vert x \vert > x_{max}$):** Beyond the linear range, the output saturates (asymptotically approaches a maximum value). Operating in this region causes loss of linearity and measurement error.

The **linear range** is the usable region; outside it, the output is no longer proportional to displacement, and the LVDT should not be operated there for precise measurement.

---

**(b) List and explain any five advantages and three limitations of LVDT. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Five Advantages (1 mark each — 5 marks total):**

1. **High resolution:** Practically infinite resolution, limited only by the noise of the signal conditioner (typically better than 1 µm).
2. **Contactless operation:** The core does not touch the windings, so there is no friction or wear. This gives a very long operational life.
3. **Low hysteresis:** Since there is no mechanical contact, the forward and reverse characteristics are nearly identical.
4. **High sensitivity:** Output voltage per unit displacement is high (typically 0.1 mV to 10 mV per 0.01 mm).
5. **Rugged and reliable:** Operates in harsh environments — high temperature, pressure, vibration, and even in radiation-prone areas.

**Three Limitations (2 marks — distribute as required):**

1. **Requires AC excitation:** Unlike a potentiometer, an LVDT cannot operate on DC. It needs a stable AC source, increasing circuit complexity.
2. **Sensitive to external magnetic fields:** External EMI can affect the output unless proper shielding is provided.
3. **Limited dynamic response:** The primary inductance limits the high-frequency response, making LVDT unsuitable for very rapidly varying displacements (above a few hundred Hz).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in LVDT Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong connection of secondaries:** Many students draw $S_1$ and $S_2$ on the same side of the primary. They MUST be on **opposite sides**, and connected in **series opposition** (EMFs subtract). [−1 to −2 marks]
> 2. **Confusing mutual inductance with self-inductance:** LVDT works on **mutual** inductance between *separate* coils. Do not write "$L$" or "$L_1$, $L_2$" in derivations; use $M_1$, $M_2$. [−1 mark]
> 3. **Forgetting phase relationship:** Students often state only the magnitude. The KTU examiner expects **both magnitude AND phase** discussion — in-phase for forward displacement, 180° out-of-phase for reverse. [−1 to −2 marks]
> 4. **Skipping the null condition:** Always explicitly show that $V_{out} = 0$ when $M_1 = M_2$. This is the most-tested derivation step. [−1 mark]
> 5. **Not mentioning signal conditioning:** A complete KTU answer must include the role of **phase-sensitive demodulator** and **low-pass filter** in producing a usable DC output. [−1 to −2 marks if asked about practical use]
> 6. **Wrong SI units:** Sensitivity $S$ should have units **V/mm** (or mV/mm), NOT V alone. [−0.5 mark]

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- LVDT = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer. [Remember: 4 words, in that order]
- **Principle:** Variation in **mutual inductance** between primary and two secondaries, due to movement of a soft-iron core.
- **Construction:** 1 primary + 2 secondaries (in **series opposition**) + 1 movable core + shielding.
- **Excitation:** AC only, typically **1–24 V RMS, 1–20 kHz**.
- **Output:** **Differential AC voltage** $V_{out} = V_{s_1} - V_{s_2}$.
- **At null (centre):** $V_{out} = 0$; this is the calibration reference.
- **Displacement toward S₁:** $V_{out} > 0$, **in phase** with $V_p$.
- **Displacement toward S₂:** $V_{out} < 0$, **180° out of phase** with $V_p$.
- **Output equation:** $V_{out} = -j\omega (M_1 - M_2) \, I_p$.
- **Linear region equation:** $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ (output proportional to displacement).
- **Sensitivity $S$:** $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x}$, units **V/mm** (typically 0.1–10 mV per 0.01 mm).
- **Resolution:** Practically infinite (theoretically), limited only by demodulator noise.
- **Advantages:** Infinite resolution, contactless, low hysteresis, rugged, high sensitivity.
- **Limitations:** Needs AC excitation, EMI-sensitive, limited frequency response.
- **Signal conditioning chain:** LVDT → Phase-sensitive demodulator → LPF → DC amplifier → Display.
- **Applications:** Aerospace hydraulics, CNC machines, robotics, valve position, civil-deflection monitoring, nuclear control rods.
- **Characteristic curve:** **S-shaped (sigmoidal)** with three regions: linear forward, null, linear reverse (and saturation at extremes).
- **Key memory cue:** *"Sum at null, difference on motion — the differential is what makes it smart!"*

---

**End of Module 3 — LVDT Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **Rotary Variable Differential Transformer (RVDT)** is a contactless, absolute-position, electromechanical displacement transducer that converts the **angular (rotational) displacement** of a ferromagnetic rotor into a proportional, **phase-sensitive alternating voltage** at its secondary terminals. It is the rotary counterpart of the LVDT and operates on the principle of mutual electromagnetic induction between a single primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings wound on a cylindrical/cylindrical-laminated stator.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Volume Knob"

Imagine a high-end audio studio volume knob that doesn't just turn — it *talks to the amplifier*. As you rotate the knob:
- A tiny magnet inside the knob slides past two hidden sensing coils.
- One coil "hears" a stronger magnetic "whisper," the other hears a weaker one.
- The amplifier subtracts these whispers to get a **net signal** that is **proportional to how far** you turned, and the **direction of the whisper** (its phase) tells whether you turned clockwise or counter-clockwise.

That smart knob is the RVDT. It measures **how much** something rotated and **which way**, without ever physically touching the moving part (no friction, no wear).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight**
> RVDT is a **passive transducer** (needs external AC excitation), **non-contact** (infinite mechanical life), **absolute** (retains position even after power-off), and **inherently differential** (uses two secondaries to cancel common-mode noise and temperature drift).

### Physical & Operational Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Excitation frequency ($f$) | **50 Hz to 20 kHz** (typically 400 Hz – 5 kHz) | Carrier frequency for primary winding |
| Operating angular range | **±40°** (typical), up to **±60°** | Linear region of output |
| Null position | **θ = 0°** | Rotor aligned with stator pole pieces |
| Output type | **AC voltage, phase-sensitive** | Magnitude ∝ θ, Phase indicates direction |
| Sensitivity | **V/rad** (volts per radian) | Determined by turns ratio & excitation |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of **excitation frequency ($f$)** is critical: high $f$ increases sensitivity but increases eddy-current losses in the core; low $f$ reduces inductive reactance and signal strength. A **compromise frequency of 3–5 kHz** is most commonly used in industrial RVDTs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of an RVDT

The RVDT consists of the following precision-engineered parts:

- **Stator (Stationary Element):** A laminated silicon-steel cylindrical core with **slots/teeth** holding three windings:
  - **Primary winding ($P$):** Wound on the central limb; energized by an AC excitation voltage $E_p \sin(\omega t)$.
  - **Secondary winding 1 ($S_1$):** Wound on the upper limb.
  - **Secondary winding 2 ($S_2$):** Wound on the lower limb.
  - $S_1$ and $S_2$ are **identical** in turns ($N_s$) and are connected in **series opposition** (anti-phase).
- **Rotor (Moving Element):** A **ferromagnetic (soft iron/mu-metal) paddle or cam-shaped armature** mechanically coupled to the input shaft. It does **not** carry any winding.
- **Shaft & Bearings:** Provide smooth, low-friction rotation (often instrument-grade ball bearings for infinite resolution).
- **Housing:** Hermetically sealed (often stainless steel) for harsh industrial environments (oil, dust, vibration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ferromagnetic rotor without windings?**
> The rotor acts as a **flux concentrator/divertor**. Its angular position changes the **magnetic reluctance path** between the primary and the two secondaries, thereby modulating the induced EMFs. This brushless, contactless design gives RVDT its legendary **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) of over 50 years** in many aerospace applications.

### 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The operation of an RVDT can be broken down into five logical steps:

1. **Excitation of the Primary:** A stable, low-distortion AC voltage $E_p \sin(\omega t)$ is applied to the primary winding. This produces an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the stator core.
2. **Flux Linking with Secondaries:** The alternating flux $\Phi$ links with both secondaries $S_1$ and $S_2$ via the rotor.
3. **EMF Induction:** By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, EMFs are induced:
   - $e_{s1} = E_{s1} \sin(\omega t)$ in $S_1$
   - $e_{s2} = E_{s2} \sin(\omega t)$ in $S_2$
4. **Rotor Position Modulation:** When the rotor is at the **null position ($\theta = 0°$)**, the flux linkages to $S_1$ and $S_2$ are **equal and symmetric**, so $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$.
5. **Differential Subtraction:** Since $S_1$ and $S_2$ are connected in **series opposition**, the net output voltage is:
   $$E_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$
   - At null: $E_o = 0$ V.
   - For any rotation $\theta \neq 0$: the **differential EMF** becomes non-zero.

### 2.3 Direction Sensing (Phase Reversal)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most exam-relevant feature** of an RVDT — its **inherent direction-sensing capability**, achieved by **phase detection**, without needing separate encoders or limit switches.

- When the rotor turns **clockwise** by angle $\theta$: $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ → output $E_o$ is **in phase** with the primary excitation.
- When the rotor turns **counter-clockwise** by $\theta$: $E_{s1} < E_{s2}$ → output $E_o$ is **180° out of phase** with the primary excitation.
- A **phase-sensitive demodulator** (e.g., a synchronous rectifier using the primary as a reference) converts this into a **bipolar DC signal** (positive for CW, negative for CCW).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary excitation voltage | $E_p = V_p \sin(\omega t)$ | Volts (V), $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s |
| 2 | Null output condition | $E_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 0$ | At $\theta = 0°$ |
| 3 | Induced EMF in secondary | $e_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law |
| 4 | Differential output (general) | $E_o = K \cdot E_p \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ | V (rms) |
| 5 | Linearized output (small $\theta$) | $E_o \approx K \cdot E_p \cdot \theta$ | $\theta$ in **radians** |
| 6 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{E_o}{\theta} = K \cdot E_p$ | V/rad |
| 7 | Phase at null | $E_o$ undefined / zero | Phase jumps 180° at null |
| 8 | Turns ratio | $n = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Inductance of winding | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell}$ | Henry (H) |
| 10 | Reactance of winding | $X_L = 2 \pi f L$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 11 | Phase-sensitive rectified DC | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 E_o}{\pi} \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | After demodulation |
| 12 | Total error (typical) | $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ of FS | Non-linearity + hysteresis |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students wrongly write $E_o = K \cdot \theta$ forgetting the **carrier $\sin(\omega t)$**. The output of an RVDT is an **AC voltage**, not a DC voltage, until it is demodulated. Always retain $\sin(\omega t)$ in the expression.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

RVDTs are indispensable in:

- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface position feedback (Boeing, Airbus fly-by-wire systems).
- **Hydraulics & Valves:** Rotary valve position feedback in process control.
- **Robotics:** Joint angle feedback in robotic arms (where infinite resolution is needed).
- **Machine Tools:** Spindle orientation, tool-changing mechanisms.
- **Defense & Naval:** Gun turret elevation, missile fin actuation.
- **Turbomachinery:** Steam/gas turbine valve positioning in power plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RVDT over Potentiometer or Optical Encoder?**
>
> | Property | RVDT | Potentiometer | Optical Encoder |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Contactless | **Yes** | No (wiper wear) | Yes |
> | Infinite Resolution | **Yes** | No | No (quantized) |
> | Absolute Position | **Yes** | Yes | Requires homing |
> | Longevity (MTBF) | **>50 years** | Limited | Moderate |
> | Radiation Hard | **Yes** | No | Limited |
> | Cost | High | Very Low | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Output Voltage Equation

We will derive the fundamental transfer characteristic of the RVDT from first principles using Faraday's Law and the symmetry of the construction.

**Given:**
- Primary excitation: $E_p(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$
- Number of turns in primary: $N_p$
- Number of turns in each secondary: $N_s$ (identical for $S_1$ and $S_2$)
- Rotor angular displacement: $\theta$ (in radians, measured from null)

**Assumption:** The magnetic circuit is linear (no saturation), and leakage flux is neglected.

**Step 1 — Primary Current and MMF**

The primary current $i_p(t)$ produces a magnetomotive force (MMF) in the stator core:

$$\mathcal{F}_p = N_p \cdot i_p(t)$$

The resulting flux in the primary limb is:

$$\Phi_p = \dfrac{\mathcal{F}_p}{\mathcal{R}_{total}} = \dfrac{N_p \cdot i_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}_{total}}$$

where $\mathcal{R}_{total}$ is the total reluctance of the magnetic path.

**Step 2 — Mutual Flux Linkage with Secondaries**

Let us define the **angular coupling function** $C(\theta)$ which describes how the rotor angular position affects flux coupling.

For a perfectly symmetric RVDT with the rotor at null, the coupling to $S_1$ and $S_2$ is equal. As the rotor rotates by $\theta$:

- Coupling to $S_1$ increases proportionally: $\Phi_{s1} = \Phi_p \cdot (1 + k \sin \theta)$
- Coupling to $S_2$ decreases proportionally: $\Phi_{s2} = \Phi_p \cdot (1 - k \sin \theta)$

where $k$ is a coupling coefficient ($0 < k \leq 1$).

**Step 3 — Faraday's Law on Secondaries**

By Faraday's Law, the EMFs induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$ are:

$$e_{s1}(t) = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi_{s1}}{dt}$$

$$e_{s2}(t) = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi_{s2}}{dt}$$

Substituting $\Phi_{s1}$ and $\Phi_{s2}$:

$$e_{s1}(t) = -N_s \cdot \Phi_p \cdot k \cos \theta \cdot \dfrac{d(\sin \theta)}{dt} - N_s \cdot \dfrac{d\Phi_p}{dt}$$

After careful differentiation (and noting that $\Phi_p$ itself is sinusoidal due to $i_p(t)$), the AC-induced components are:

$$e_{s1}(t) = V_p \cdot \left( \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \right) \cdot (1 + k \sin \theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

$$e_{s2}(t) = V_p \cdot \left( \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \right) \cdot (1 - k \sin \theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The DC/constant terms in the bracket $(1)$ cancel out during the differential subtraction, leaving only the angular information. This is a **key feature of the differential design** — common-mode noise and primary drift are automatically rejected.

**Step 4 — Differential Output (Series Opposition)**

Since $S_1$ and $S_2$ are connected in series opposition:

$$E_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$

$$E_o = V_p \cdot \left( \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \right) \cdot \left[ (1 + k \sin \theta) - (1 - k \sin \theta) \right] \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

$$E_o = V_p \cdot \left( \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \right) \cdot (2 k \sin \theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\boxed{E_o = 2 k \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_p \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)}$$

**Step 5 — Defining the Sensitivity Constant**

Let $K = 2 k \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ (a dimensionless constant of the RVDT). Then:

$$E_o = K \cdot V_p \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 6 — Small-Angle Linearization**

For small angular displacements ($\vert \theta \vert < 10°$ in radians, i.e., $\vert \theta \vert < 0.17$ rad), we use the small-angle approximation:

$$\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$$

This yields the **linear operating region** equation:

$$\boxed{E_o \approx K \cdot V_p \cdot \theta \cdot \sin(\omega t) = S \cdot \theta \cdot \sin(\omega t)}$$

where $S = K \cdot V_p$ is the **sensitivity** of the RVDT (in V/rad).

**Step 7 — RMS Output Voltage**

For practical signal conditioning, we often work with the RMS value. The RMS of $E_o$ is:

$$E_{o,rms} = \dfrac{K \cdot V_p}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

For small angles:

$$E_{o,rms} \approx \dfrac{K \cdot V_p}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \theta$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operating Conditions vs. Output

| Rotor Position ($\theta$) | Flux to $S_1$ | Flux to $S_2$ | Output $E_o$ | Phase (relative to $E_p$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $+40°$ (CW max) | Maximum | Minimum | Maximum positive | **In phase** ($0°$) |
| $+10°$ (CW small) | Slightly higher | Slightly lower | Small positive | In phase |
| $0°$ (Null) | Equal to $S_2$ | Equal to $S_1$ | **Zero** | Phase undefined |
| $-10°$ (CCW small) | Slightly lower | Slightly higher | Small negative | **180° out of phase** |
| $-40°$ (CCW max) | Minimum | Maximum | Maximum negative (in magnitude) | 180° out of phase |

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Worked Out for KTU Exam Practice)

> **Problem:** An RVDT has a primary winding with $N_p = 500$ turns, secondaries with $N_s = 1000$ turns each, coupling coefficient $k = 0.95$, and is excited by $V_p = 5$ V RMS at 400 Hz. The rotor is displaced by $\theta = 20°$. Find:
> (a) The differential output voltage $E_o$ (RMS).
> (b) The sensitivity $S$.
> (c) The output voltage if the rotor is displaced to $-15°$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):**

Sensitivity constant:
$$K = 2 k \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = 2 \cdot 0.95 \cdot \dfrac{1000}{500} = 2 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 2 = 3.8$$

Convert angle to radians:
$$\theta = 20° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 0.3491 \text{ rad}$$

Compute $\sin(20°)$:
$$\sin(20°) = 0.342$$

RMS output:
$$E_{o,rms} = \dfrac{K \cdot V_p}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sin(\theta) = \dfrac{3.8 \cdot 5}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot 0.342$$

$$E_{o,rms} = \dfrac{19}{1.4142} \cdot 0.342 = 13.435 \cdot 0.342 = 4.595 \text{ V RMS}$$

**Step (b):**

$$S = K \cdot V_p = 3.8 \cdot 5 = 19 \text{ V/rad}$$

**Step (c):**

For $\theta = -15°$:
$$\sin(-15°) = -0.2588$$

$$E_{o,rms} = 13.435 \cdot (-0.2588) = -3.477 \text{ V RMS}$$

The **negative sign** and **180° phase shift** confirm the **counter-clockwise** direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative output magnitude (with phase reversal) is the unique signature of an RVDT for direction sensing. This is why a **phase-sensitive demodulator** is mandatory in the signal conditioning electronics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RVDT Signal Conditioning

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts how an RVDT is integrated into a complete industrial measurement and control chain. This is a high-level **functional architecture** rather than a literal physical drawing, as physical RVDT cross-sections are difficult to render textually.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Oscillator 400Hz 5V RMS AC Excitation Source] --> node2[RVDT Primary Winding]
    node2 --> node3[RVDT Secondary Winding S1]
    node2 --> node4[RVDT Secondary Winding S2]
    node3 --> node5[Differential Subtraction Series Opposition]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6[AC Differential Output Eo AC coupled]
    node6 --> node7[Phase Sensitive Demodulator Using Primary as Reference]
    node1 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[Low Pass Filter removes carrier ripple]
    node8 --> node9[Bipolar DC Output Vdc proportional to angle theta]
    node9 --> node10[Signal Conditioner Amplifier and Scaling]
    node10 --> node11[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    node11 --> node12[Microcontroller or PLC for Closed Loop Control]
    node12 --> node13[Actuator Hydraulic Valve or Motor Driver]
    node13 --> node14[Mechanical Load Controlled Motion]
    node14 --> node15[Shaft Position Feedback Physical Input to RVDT Rotor]
    node15 --> node2

    classDef hw fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#3a86ff,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#caffbf,stroke:#06a77d,color:#000
    class node1,node2,node3,node4,node14,node15 hw
    class node5,node6,node8,node10 io
    class node7,node9,node11,node12,node13 sw
```

**How to read this diagram (in a typical KTU 14-mark answer):**
- The **yellow block** = the physical RVDT and its input/output.
- The **green block** = the analog interface (differential stage, filter, amplifier).
- The **blue block** = the digital signal processing & control path.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the RVDT Core (Stator + Rotor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[AC Excitation Ep sin omega t] --> nodeB[Primary Winding Np turns on Central Stator Limb]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Ferromagnetic Rotor Attached to Input Shaft Angle theta]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Angular Position Modulation}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Secondary Winding S1 Upper Limb]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Secondary Winding S2 Lower Limb]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Series Opposition Connection Phase Reversal Between S1 and S2]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Differential Output Voltage Eo = es1 minus es2]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Phase Sensitive Demodulator Circuit]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Bipolar DC Voltage Vdc proportional to theta]

    classDef input fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e76f51,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#9d4edd,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#caffbf,stroke:#06a77d,color:#000
    class nodeA input
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG core
    class nodeH,nodeI,nodeJ output
```

### 4.3 Output Transfer Characteristic (Graphical Schematic)

The relationship between $E_o$ and $\theta$ is sinusoidal in general, with a **linear region** near null.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Small Angle Region Linear Eo proportional to theta"] --> nodeB["Null Position theta equals zero, Eo equals 0V"] --> nodeC["Large Angle Region Sinusoidal Distortion begins"]
    nodeD["Direction Sign Determined by Phase 0 deg or 180 deg"] --> nodeB
    nodeE["Positive Quadrant Clockwise Rotation CW"] --> nodeA
    nodeF["Negative Quadrant Counter Clockwise Rotation CCW"] --> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for the diagram above:**
> The RVDT is operated **only in the linear region** (typically $\pm 40°$ or less). Beyond this, the $\sin(\theta)$ approximation breaks down, and the output becomes non-linear. Manufacturers specify the **linear range** carefully in datasheets, and KTU problems always assume the rotor is within this range.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an RVDT. Mention any two of its advantages over a resistive potentiometer for angular displacement measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A Rotary Variable Differential Transformer (RVDT) is a contactless electromechanical transducer that converts the **angular displacement** of a ferromagnetic rotor into a **proportional, phase-sensitive AC voltage** at its secondary terminals. It consists of one primary and two secondary windings on a stator, with the rotor acting as a flux modulator.

**Any Two Advantages (1 mark for two points):**

1. **Infinite resolution & no mechanical wear** — The rotor does not contact the windings (unlike a potentiometer wiper), giving theoretically infinite resolution and an operational life of decades.
2. **High accuracy & linearity** — Linearity of **±0.1% to ±0.5%** of full scale, far superior to the typical ±2–5% of carbon-film potentiometers.
3. **Absolute position output** — Retains last position even after power-off, eliminating the need for homing sequences required by encoders.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write "LVDT" instead of "RVDT" in definitions, losing 1 full mark. Always clarify the **rotary** (angular) nature of the device vs. the **linear** nature of the LVDT.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain how an RVDT detects the *direction* of rotation. Why is a phase-sensitive demodulator necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An RVDT detects direction through **phase reversal of the output signal** (1 mark):

- The two secondaries $S_1$ and $S_2$ are connected in **series opposition**, so their induced EMFs subtract: $E_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$.
- When the rotor turns **clockwise**, $e_{s1} > e_{s2}$, and $E_o$ is **in phase** with the primary excitation.
- When the rotor turns **counter-clockwise**, $e_{s1} < e_{s2}$, and $E_o$ is **180° out of phase** with the primary excitation.

The **magnitude** of $E_o$ gives the **amount** of rotation, while the **phase** gives the **direction** (1 mark).

A **phase-sensitive demodulator** (using the primary excitation as a reference) is necessary to convert this AC signal into a **bipolar DC signal** that is **positive** for one direction and **negative** for the other, allowing standard ADCs and controllers to interpret the sign (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

## **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Standard Long-Answer Type**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** With a neat construction diagram, explain the working principle of an RVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** An RVDT is excited by 5 V RMS at 2 kHz. The primary has 1000 turns, each secondary has 1500 turns, and the coupling coefficient is 0.9. If the rotor is displaced by $+25°$ from null, calculate: (i) the sensitivity constant $K$, (ii) the RMS output voltage, and (iii) the phase of the output relative to the primary. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
The RVDT consists of:
- A **cylindrical laminated stator** with a primary winding $P$ (on the central limb) and two identical secondary windings $S_1$ (upper) and $S_2$ (lower), connected in **series opposition**.
- A **ferromagnetic rotor (armature)** attached to the input shaft, free to rotate within the stator bore. The rotor carries **no windings**.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
1. The primary is excited by an AC voltage $E_p = V_p \sin(\omega t)$, producing alternating magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the core.
2. The rotor modulates the flux distribution: when displaced by $\theta$ from null, the flux linking with $S_1$ increases while that linking with $S_2$ decreases (or vice versa).
3. By Faraday's Law, EMFs $e_{s1}$ and $e_{s2}$ are induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$.
4. The differential output is $E_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$, which is **proportional to $\theta$** in the linear region, with **phase** indicating direction.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Following the step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1:

$$E_o = 2 k \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_p \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t) = K \cdot V_p \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

For small angles $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$:

$$E_o \approx K \cdot V_p \cdot \theta \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_p = 5$ V RMS, $f = 2$ kHz, $N_p = 1000$, $N_s = 1500$, $k = 0.9$, $\theta = +25°$.

**Step (i): Sensitivity constant (2 marks):**

$$K = 2 k \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = 2 \cdot 0.9 \cdot \dfrac{1500}{1000} = 2 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 1.5 = 2.7$$

**Step (ii): RMS output voltage (3 marks):**

$$\sin(25°) = 0.4226$$

$$E_{o,rms} = \dfrac{K \cdot V_p}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sin(\theta) = \dfrac{2.7 \cdot 5}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot 0.4226$$

$$E_{o,rms} = \dfrac{13.5}{1.4142} \cdot 0.4226 = 9.546 \cdot 0.4226 = 4.034 \text{ V RMS}$$

**Step (iii): Phase of output (2 marks):**

Since the rotor is displaced by a **positive** angle ($+25°$, i.e., **clockwise** from null), the induced EMF in $S_1$ exceeds that in $S_2$, making $E_o$ **in phase** with the primary excitation (phase difference = **0°**).

$$\boxed{E_{o,rms} = 4.034 \text{ V RMS, in phase with primary.}}$$

---

## **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Long-Answer Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Style]**

> **(a)** Compare RVDT with a **potentiometer** and an **optical encoder** for angular position sensing, covering the criteria of contact, resolution, accuracy, absolute/relative output, and typical application. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Draw and explain the **signal conditioning block diagram** of an RVDT, including the role of the phase-sensitive demodulator. Why is the demodulator reference taken from the **primary excitation** and not from a separate oscillator? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Criterion | RVDT | Potentiometer | Optical Encoder |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact / Contactless** | Fully contactless (no friction) | Contact (wiper on resistive track) | Contactless (optical) |
| **Resolution** | **Infinite** (theoretically) | Limited by track granularity | Quantized (pulses per revolution) |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1% to ±0.5% FS | ±2% to ±5% FS | ±0.01% to ±0.1% FS (high end) |
| **Absolute / Relative** | **Absolute** | Absolute | **Relative** (requires homing) |
| **Lifespan** | >50 years (no wear) | Limited (wiper wear) | Moderate (LED degradation) |
| **Cost** | High | Very Low | Moderate |
| **Typical Application** | Aerospace, hydraulics, valve position | Consumer knobs, dimmers | CNC machines, robotics digital feedback |

*(2 marks for table, 2 marks for 1–2 application examples, 1 mark each for stating clear advantages/disadvantages — 7 marks total)*

**Part (b) — Signal Conditioning Block Diagram & Demodulator (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The signal conditioning chain of an RVDT is:

1. **Excitation Source (Oscillator):** Generates a stable AC voltage (typically 400 Hz to 5 kHz) for the primary.
2. **RVDT Core:** Produces the differential AC output $E_o = K V_p \sin(\theta) \sin(\omega t)$.
3. **AC Amplifier:** Boosts the small $E_o$ to a usable level (e.g., 0–10 V peak).
4. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator:** Uses the primary excitation as a **phase reference** to extract the sign of $E_o$.
5. **Low-Pass Filter:** Removes the carrier ripple, leaving a smooth DC voltage.
6. **DC Amplifier / Signal Conditioner:** Scales the signal for the ADC.
7. **ADC + Controller:** Digitizes the bipolar DC for closed-loop control.

**Role of Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (2 marks):**
- Converts the AC differential output into a **bipolar DC signal** whose **magnitude** is proportional to $\theta$ and whose **polarity** indicates direction.
- Without it, an ordinary rectifier would lose the direction information (the absolute value would always be positive).

**Why the Reference is the Primary Excitation (2 marks):**
- The **primary excitation is the source of the carrier signal** in the output, so they are inherently **frequency-locked** and **phase-coherent**.
- Using a separate oscillator would introduce **phase drift** due to component aging, temperature variations, and frequency mismatch — leading to erroneous direction sensing.
- Taking the reference from the primary guarantees that the demodulator switches **in perfect synchronism** with the carrier, ensuring accurate phase detection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Very Common Marks Loss!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing RVDT with LVDT (–2 marks):** RVDT measures **angular** displacement using a **rotor**, not a sliding core. Drawing an LVDT diagram and labelling it as RVDT will lose substantial marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sin(\omega t)$ term in $E_o$ (–1 mark):** The output of an RVDT is an **AC voltage**, not a pure DC. Always include the carrier term.
> 3. **Writing $E_o = K \theta$ without explaining the linearization assumption (–1 mark):** The relation $E_o \propto \theta$ holds only for **small angles** ($\vert \theta \vert < 10°$). You must mention this assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Not mentioning series-opposition (–1 mark):** The defining feature of "differential" transformers is the **series-opposition connection** of the secondaries. Always state this.
> 5. **Skipping the direction-sensing phase reversal (–1 mark):** This is a **signature KTU 2024 question** — examiners look specifically for the phase-reversal concept.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ **RVDT** = **R**otary **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer — measures **angular displacement**.
- ✅ **Construction:** 1 primary, 2 secondaries on stator; ferromagnetic **windingless rotor** on shaft.
- ✅ **Key connection:** Secondaries connected in **series opposition** → differential output.
- ✅ **Null position:** $\theta = 0°$ → $E_o = 0$ V (balanced flux).
- ✅ **Output equation:** $E_o = K \cdot V_p \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$.
- ✅ **Linear region:** $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ → $E_o = S \cdot \theta \cdot \sin(\omega t)$, where $S = K \cdot V_p$ (V/rad).
- ✅ **Direction sensing:** Phase of $E_o$ = **0° (CW)** or **180° (CCW)** relative to primary.
- ✅ **Signal conditioning chain:** Oscillator → RVDT → AC amplifier → **Phase-sensitive demodulator** → LPF → ADC → Controller.
- ✅ **Excitation frequency:** Typically 400 Hz – 5 kHz (compromise between sensitivity and core loss).
- ✅ **Operating range:** Linear within $\pm 40°$ (typical), non-linear beyond.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Contactless, infinite resolution, absolute, long life, radiation-hard, high accuracy.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Requires AC excitation, limited angular range, expensive, sensitive to external magnetic fields.
- ✅ **Sensitivity constant:** $K = 2 k \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ (dimensionless).
- ✅ **RMS output:** $E_{o,rms} = \dfrac{K \cdot V_p}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sin(\theta)$.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** Aerospace flight controls, hydraulic valve position, robotic joint feedback, machine tool spindles, naval gun turrets, power plant turbine valves.
- ✅ **Always retain $\sin(\omega t)$ in the output expression** — KTU examiners check for this.
- ✅ **Always state the series-opposition connection** — it is the defining "differential" feature.
- ✅ **Always convert degrees to radians** before using small-angle approximation $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$.

> **End of Module 3 — RVDT Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, OERUT612)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators and Their Comparison (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators and Their Comparison (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Automation**, an **Actuator** is defined as a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into a precise physical motion, force, or displacement. Actuators are the final controlling elements in any automated system, often referred to as the "muscles" of a machine because they execute the commands issued by a controller (PLC, DCS, or microcontroller).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The focus is on *Concept Only* — meaning derivations of complex thermodynamic cycles are NOT required, but functional working principles, symbol representations, force/pressure relationships, and comparative engineering trade-offs ARE examinable.

The three primary actuator families studied are:

1. **Electrical Actuators** — Energy domain: Electrical → Mechanical
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Energy domain: Hydraulic (Pressurized Oil) → Mechanical
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Energy domain: Pneumatic (Compressed Air) → Mechanical

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human arm performing a task:
- Your **brain** sends an electrical nerve signal (this is the *Controller*).
- Your **muscles** contract to lift a weight (this is the *Actuator*).
- The **bones and joints** guide the motion (this is the *Mechanical Linkage*).

Now, consider three ways to lift the same heavy box in a factory:
- **Electric Actuator (Motor + Gearbox):** Like a person lifting the box using only their arm strength. Smooth, precise, quiet — but has a limit to how heavy it can lift.
- **Hydraulic Actuator (Cylinder + Oil Pump):** Like using a hydraulic jack to lift a car. Enormous force from a small input — used in heavy industries like excavators and forging presses.
- **Pneumatic Actuator (Cylinder + Air Compressor):** Like using an air pump to inflate a balloon that pushes something. Lighter, faster, cleaner — used in food packaging and assembly lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**
> - Density of Mineral Hydraulic Oil: **$\rho_{oil} \approx 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Density of Air (at STP): **$\rho_{air} \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Standard Supply Pressure (Hydraulic): **$P_{hyd} \approx 7 \text{ to } 21 \text{ MPa}$**
> - Standard Supply Pressure (Pneumatic): **$P_{pneu} \approx 0.4 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ MPa}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrical Actuators — Operational Logic

Electrical actuators convert **electrical energy** into rotary or linear mechanical motion using electromagnetic principles.

**Common Types:**
- **DC Motors** — Simple speed control via armature voltage.
- **AC Induction Motors** — Robust, used in fixed-speed conveyors.
- **Servo Motors** — Closed-loop, high-precision position control (used in robotics).
- **Stepper Motors** — Open-loop, discrete angular steps (used in 3D printers).
- **Solenoids** — Linear push/pull motion via energized coil (used in valves, relays).

**Working Principle Steps:**
- Step 1: Controller supplies a voltage/current signal to the actuator terminal.
- Step 2: Current flowing through the coil generates a magnetic field (Lorentz Force / Fleming's Left-Hand Rule).
- Step 3: Magnetic field interacts with permanent magnets or rotor, producing **torque** $T$.
- Step 4: Torque drives the rotor shaft, producing rotational motion at angular velocity $\omega$.

### 2.2 Hydraulic Actuators — Operational Logic

Hydraulic actuators use **incompressible pressurized fluid (mineral oil)** as the power transmission medium.

**Common Types:**
- **Single-Acting Cylinder** — Oil pushes piston one way; spring returns it.
- **Double-Acting Cylinder** — Oil pressure applied alternately on both sides for push and pull motion.
- **Hydraulic Motor** — Converts hydraulic pressure to rotary motion.

**Working Principle Steps:**
- Step 1: Electric motor drives a **hydraulic pump**, which draws oil from a reservoir.
- Step 2: Pump pressurizes oil (Pascal's Law) to a high pressure $P$.
- Step 3: Pressurized oil is routed via **directional control valves** to the actuator.
- Step 4: Oil acts on the piston surface area $A$, producing a linear force $F = P \times A$.
- Step 5: Exhaust oil returns to reservoir through filters (closed-loop circulation).

### 2.3 Pneumatic Actuators — Operational Logic

Pneumatic actuators use **compressible gas (air)** as the working medium. The principle is similar to hydraulic but with air instead of oil.

**Working Principle Steps:**
- Step 1: Ambient air is drawn in through an **intake filter** by an air compressor.
- Step 2: Compressor pressurizes air, typically to **6 to 10 bar**.
- Step 3: Pressurized air passes through an **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator).
- Step 4: Regulated air enters the pneumatic cylinder via solenoid-operated **5/2 or 5/3 valves**.
- Step 5: Air pushes the piston, producing linear motion. Exhaust air is vented (often through silencers for noise reduction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** In modern automated plants (e.g., Bosch, Siemens factories), **electrical actuators dominate in precision tasks** (CNC, robotics), **hydraulic in heavy-load tasks** (presses, injection moulding), and **pneumatic in high-speed repetitive tasks** (pick-and-place, packaging).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable To |
|---|--------------------|----------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | Force produced by cylinder | $N$ | Hydraulic & Pneumatic |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Piston cross-sectional area | $m^2$ | Cylinders |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity from flow rate | $m/s$ | Fluid actuators |
| 4 | $P_{hyd} = F \times v$ | Hydraulic Power Output | $W$ (Watts) | Hydraulic systems |
| 5 | $T = k_t \times I$ | Motor torque from current | $N \cdot m$ | DC/Servo Motors |
| 6 | $V_{emf} = k_e \times \omega$ | Back-EMF vs angular speed | $V$ | DC Motors |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | $rad/s$ | Rotating actuators |
