| 8 | $P_{elec} = T \times \omega$ | Mechanical power output | $W$ | Electrical motors |
| 9 | $P_{air} = \dfrac{P_{gauge} \times Q}{1000}$ | Pneumatic power | $kW$ | Pneumatic systems |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of actuator | % | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** Vertical pipe symbols (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value) must NEVER appear inside the markdown table cell. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` LaTeX command.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Force Produced by a Hydraulic / Pneumatic Cylinder

**Given:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d$. Supply pressure $P$ (gauge). Find the extension force and retraction force.

**Step 1 — Recall Pascal's Law:**
Pressure transmitted equally in all directions through an incompressible fluid at rest.

$$P = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Express the piston areas:**

Extension side (full piston face area):

$$A_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Retraction side (annular area since rod occupies one side):

$$A_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear forces:**

Extension force:

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{ext} = P \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Retraction force:

$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{ret} = P \times \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to practical engineering units:**
Often pressure is given in **bar** (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa) and diameter in **mm**. The formula becomes:

$$F (\text{in N}) = P (\text{bar}) \times A (\text{cm}^2) \times 10$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**
Let $P = 100$ bar, $D = 50$ mm, $d = 30$ mm.

First, compute areas in cm²:

$$A_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi (5)^2}{4} = 19.635 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi (5^2 - 3^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (16)}{4} = 12.566 \text{ cm}^2$$

Now apply the force formula:

$$F_{ext} = 100 \times 19.635 \times 10 = 19635 \text{ N} \approx 19.6 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{ret} = 100 \times 12.566 \times 10 = 12566 \text{ N} \approx 12.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result:**
The cylinder can push **19.6 kN** but can only pull **12.6 kN**. This asymmetric force profile is a key design consideration in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Output of an Actuator

**Step 1 — General Power Definition:**
Power is the rate of doing work.

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{W}{t} = F \times \dfrac{d}{t} = F \times v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hydraulic Variables:**
For a cylinder with flow rate $Q$ and pressure $P$:

$$P_{out} = F \times v = (P \times A) \times \dfrac{Q}{A} = P \times Q$$

This elegant cancellation shows hydraulic power depends ONLY on pressure and flow rate.

**Step 3 — Substitute Rotary Electrical Variables:**
For an electric motor with torque $T$ and angular speed $\omega$:

$$P_{out} = T \times \omega = T \times \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$

where $N$ is in RPM.

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification:**
A hydraulic cylinder with $P = 140$ bar and $Q = 20$ L/min.

Convert to SI units:

$$P = 140 \times 10^5 = 1.4 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Apply the formula:

$$P_{out} = 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 4666.67 \text{ W} \approx 4.67 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Electrical Actuator | Hydraulic Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Energy Source | Electricity | Pressurized Oil | Compressed Air |
| Medium | Electrons | Mineral Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Typical Pressure/Voltage | 12 V – 480 V AC | 70 – 210 bar | 4 – 10 bar |
| Force Output | Low to Medium | Very High (up to MN) | Low to Medium |
| Speed | High precision, variable | Medium, smooth | Very high, jerky end-stop |
| Positioning Accuracy | Excellent (closed-loop) | Excellent | Poor (compressibility) |
| Stiffness / Rigidity | Low (backlash issues) | Very High | Low (springiness) |
| Response Time | Fast (ms) | Medium (10s of ms) | Very Fast (a few ms) |
| Noise Level | Quiet | Moderate | Loud (needs silencer) |
| Cleanliness | Clean (no leakage) | Risk of oil leak | Cleanest (air leakage safe) |
| Initial Cost | Medium | High | Low |
| Maintenance Cost | Lowest | Highest (oil, seals) | Low |
| Typical Applications | CNC, Robotics, Conveyors | Presses, Excavators, Aircraft | Packaging, Pick-and-place |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "EPS = Cleanest to Dirtiest":** Electrical > Pneumatic > Servo-hydraulic in cleanliness grade. Pneumatic is preferred for food/pharma; Hydraulic is banned in those sectors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Actuator System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controller: PLC or DCS] --> B[Signal Conditioner]
    B --> C[Power Amplifier Stage]
    C --> D{Actuator Type Selection}
    D --> E1[Electrical Actuator: Motor or Solenoid]
    D --> E2[Hydraulic Actuator: Cylinder or Motor]
    D --> E3[Pneumatic Actuator: Cylinder or Motor]
    E1 --> F1[Mechanical Output: Linear or Rotary]
    E2 --> F2[Mechanical Output: Linear or Rotary]
    E3 --> F3[Mechanical Output: Linear or Rotary]
    F1 --> G[Feedback Sensor: Encoder or LVDT]
    F2 --> H[Feedback Sensor: Pressure Transducer]
    F3 --> I[Feedback Sensor: Limit Switch]
    G --> A
    H --> A
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Electrical Path]
        A1a[Electrical Energy] --> A1b[Motor or Coil] --> A1c[Mechanical Motion]
    end
    subgraph A2[Hydraulic Path]
        A2a[Electric Motor Pump] --> A2b[Pressurized Oil] --> A2c[Cylinder Force Output]
    end
    subgraph A3[Pneumatic Path]
        A3a[Air Compressor] --> A3b[Compressed Air via FRL] --> A3c[Piston Push or Pull]
    end
    A1c --> J[Industrial Process: Lifting, Pushing, Rotating]
    A2c --> J
    A3c --> J
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RES[Oil Reservoir Tank] --> PUMP[Hydraulic Pump driven by Electric Motor]
    PUMP --> FILT[In-line Filter]
    FILT --> PRV[Pressure Relief Valve set to 200 bar]
    PRV --> DCV[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3]
    DCV --> CYL[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL --> RET[Return Line via Filter]
    RET --> RES
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Pneumatic System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INTAKE[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> COMP[Air Compressor]
    COMP --> RCVR[Receiver Tank for storage]
    RCVR --> FRL[Filter Regulator Lubricator Unit]
    FRL --> SOLV[Solenoid Operated 5 by 2 Valve]
    SOLV --> PCYL[Pneumatic Cylinder Single or Double Acting]
    PCYL --> SIL[Exhaust Silencer]
    SIL --> OUT[Atmosphere]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students are expected to draw **labelled block diagrams** showing the source, control valve, actuator, and feedback path. The Mermaid diagrams above model the standard expected answer format.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an actuator. List the three main types of actuators used in industrial automation.**

**Model Answer:**
An actuator is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy) into a physical motion or force. It is the output element of an automated system that performs the actual mechanical work.

The three main types are:
- (i) **Electrical Actuator** — e.g., DC motor, stepper motor, solenoid.
- (ii) **Hydraulic Actuator** — e.g., hydraulic cylinder, hydraulic motor.
- (iii) **Pneumatic Actuator** — e.g., pneumatic cylinder, air motor.

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State two advantages and two disadvantages of pneumatic actuators compared to electrical actuators.**

**Model Answer:**
Advantages of pneumatic actuators:
- (i) Very fast response and high-speed operation (ideal for pick-and-place tasks).
- (ii) Clean operation with air as medium — suitable for food and pharmaceutical industries.

Disadvantages of pneumatic actuators:
- (i) Poor positioning accuracy due to compressibility of air.
- (ii) Cannot handle very high loads (limited force output compared to hydraulic).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a **hydraulic actuation system**. List its main components and state two industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. The hydraulic supply pressure is 150 bar. Calculate: (i) Extension force, (ii) Retraction force, (iii) If the flow rate supplied is 30 L/min, find the extension velocity of the piston. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A hydraulic actuation system works on **Pascal's Law** — pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions.

**Working Principle:**
1. An electric motor drives a **hydraulic pump**.
2. The pump draws oil from the reservoir and pressurizes it to the working pressure (typically 70–210 bar).
3. The pressurized oil is directed by a **directional control valve** (e.g., 4/3 solenoid-operated valve).
4. The oil enters the cylinder and acts on the piston area, producing a linear force $F = P \times A$.
5. The return oil from the cylinder passes through filters and returns to the reservoir.

**Main Components:**
- Reservoir tank, hydraulic pump, electric motor, filters, pressure relief valve, directional control valve, double-acting cylinder, pressure gauges, and return line.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
- (i) Hydraulic presses in metal forming and forging.
- (ii) Earth-moving equipment such as excavators and bulldozers.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $d = 45$ mm, $P = 150$ bar, $Q = 30$ L/min.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**
$D = 8$ cm, $d = 4.5$ cm, $A$ in cm², $P$ in bar.

**Step 2 — Compute areas:**

$$A_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (8)^2}{4} = 50.265 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (64 - 20.25)}{4} = 34.36 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute forces:**

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{ext} \times 10 = 150 \times 50.265 \times 10 = 75398 \text{ N} \approx 75.4 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{ret} \times 10 = 150 \times 34.36 \times 10 = 51540 \text{ N} \approx 51.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute extension velocity:**
Convert flow rate: $Q = 30$ L/min $= \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{60} = 500 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$

$$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{ext}} = \dfrac{500}{50.265} = 9.948 \text{ cm/s} \approx 0.0995 \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $F_{ext} = 75.4$ kN, $F_{ret} = 51.5$ kN, $v_{ext} \approx 0.0995$ m/s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **solenoid actuator** and a **stepper motor**. State two applications of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare electrical, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators based on: (i) Working medium, (ii) Force capability, (iii) Positioning accuracy, (iv) Maintenance cost, (v) Cleanliness, (vi) Typical applications, and (vii) Response time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Solenoid Actuator:**
A solenoid is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into linear mechanical motion using a magnetic field.
- When current flows through the coil, a strong magnetic field is produced.
- This magnetic field attracts a ferromagnetic plunger inside the coil.
- The plunger moves linearly, producing a push or pull motion.
- When current is removed, a spring returns the plunger to its original position.
- **Applications:** (i) Automotive starter motors, (ii) Solenoid valves for water/gas flow control.

**Stepper Motor:**
A stepper motor is a brushless DC motor that divides a full rotation into a number of equal steps.
- Each electrical pulse causes the rotor to rotate by a fixed angular displacement (e.g., 1.8°).
- Position is controlled by counting the number of pulses sent.
- Can operate in **open-loop** without feedback sensors.
- **Applications:** (i) 3D printers and CNC machines, (ii) Camera lens focusing mechanisms.

**Model Solution for Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Electrical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|-----------|------------|-----------|-----------|
| Working medium | Electricity (current/voltage) | Pressurized mineral oil | Compressed air |
| Force capability | Low to medium | Very high (up to MN) | Low to medium |
| Positioning accuracy | Excellent (with encoder) | Excellent | Poor |
| Maintenance cost | Lowest | Highest (oil change, seals) | Low |
| Cleanliness | Very clean | Can leak oil | Cleanest |
| Typical applications | Robotics, CNC, conveyors | Presses, excavators, aircraft | Packaging, food industry |
| Response time | Fast (ms range) | Medium (10s of ms) | Very fast (a few ms) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **forget to convert units** between bar, Pa, mm, and cm in hydraulic force problems. Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> - Many students confuse **Pascal's Law with Boyle's Law** — Pascal applies to incompressible fluids (hydraulic), Boyle applies to compressible gases (pneumatic).
> - In comparison answers, students list points **without grouping or tabular format**. KTU examiners reward a **structured tabular comparison** over paragraphs.
> - Avoid writing **"pneumatic is better than hydraulic"** — both have specific engineering domains where they excel. Use comparative language like "preferred for high force applications" or "preferred for clean environments".

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Actuator Definition:** Converts a control signal into mechanical motion — it is the "muscle" of any automated system.
- **Three Main Types:** Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic — classified by the **energy domain** they accept.
- **Electrical:** Best for **precision** tasks; uses motors, solenoids, and relays.
- **Hydraulic:** Best for **high force** tasks; works on **Pascal's Law** with incompressible oil at 70–210 bar.
- **Pneumatic:** Best for **high-speed, clean** tasks; uses compressed air at 4–10 bar.
- **Key Formula:** $F = P \times A$ for fluid actuators; $P = T \times \omega$ for electric motors.
- **Pressure Units:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa. Always convert before substituting in SI formulas.
- **Force Conversion Trick:** $F \text{ (N)} = P \text{ (bar)} \times A \text{ (cm}^2\text{)} \times 10$ — saves time in numericals.
- **Asymmetric Cylinder:** $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ because rod side has less effective area.
- **FRL Unit:** Filter-Regulator-Lubricator is mandatory in pneumatic circuits to ensure clean, regulated, lubricated air.
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Choose **Electrical** for precision, **Hydraulic** for power, **Pneumatic** for cleanliness and speed.
- **KTU Favorite Topics:** Block diagram of hydraulic/pneumatic system, force calculation, and tabular comparison — these three together cover ~80% of past paper marks on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples - Use of Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators in Industrial Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples - Use of Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators in Industrial Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Automation**, an **Actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy) into a physical motion — typically linear displacement, rotary motion, or oscillatory movement — in order to perform a designated task on a process or machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (High-Weight Concept):**
> An actuator is the *final control element* in an automation loop. It receives the low-power signal from a controller (PLC, microcontroller, DCS) and produces the high-power mechanical action required to drive the load. **Energy Conversion** is the core principle: Electrical → Mechanical, Hydraulic → Mechanical, or Pneumatic → Mechanical.

The three primary actuator families covered in KTU Module 3 are:

1. **Electrical Actuators** — Use electrical energy (DC/AC/stepper/servo motors, solenoids, relays).
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Use incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) under high pressure.
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Use compressible gas (typically filtered, lubricated air) at moderate pressures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscles of a Machine"

Imagine a human body. The **brain** is the controller (PLC/Computer), the **nerves** are the signal wires, and the **muscles** are the actuators. Just like a human can have different muscle types (biceps for pulling, heart for pumping, fingers for gripping), machines have different actuator types suited to specific tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Intuition Table**

| Human Body | Industrial Automation | Function |
|------------|----------------------|----------|
| Brain | PLC / Microcontroller | Decision-making |
| Nerves | Signal cables / Bus | Information transfer |
| Muscles | Actuators | Physical work output |
| Skeleton | Mechanical structure | Load support |

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

Every actuator in KTU exam questions is evaluated on these **standard metrics**:

- **Stroke Length (L)** — Maximum linear displacement in **mm** or **m**.
- **Force (F) / Thrust** — Push/pull capability, measured in **N (Newton)** or **kN**.
- **Torque (T)** — Rotary equivalent of force, measured in **N·m**.
- **Speed (v) / Angular Velocity (ω)** — Measured in **m/s** or **rad/s**.
- **Power (P)** — Rate of doing work, in **W (Watt)** or **kW**.
- **Pressure (p)** — Force per unit area, in **bar** or **Pa (Pascal)**.
- **Flow Rate (Q)** — Volume per unit time, in **L/min** or **m³/s**.

> [!TIP]
> **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa** (industry standard conversion for KTU numerical problems).

---

### 1.4 Industrial Examples — Quick Survey

| Application Domain | Actuator Used | Reason for Selection |
|--------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| CNC Machine Tool Feed | Servo Motor (Electrical) | Precise positioning, high repeatability |
| Injection Moulding Clamp | Hydraulic Cylinder | Extremely high clamping force |
| Pick-and-Place on Conveyor | Pneumatic Cylinder | Low cost, fast, clean operation |
| Car Assembly Welding Arm | Servo + Hydraulic Hybrid | High speed and high force |
| Bottling Plant Cap Tightening | Rotary Pneumatic Motor | Safe in explosive environments |
| Robotic Arm Joints | Servo Motor (Electrical) | High accuracy, easy control |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pneumatic Actuators — Working Principle

A **Pneumatic Actuator** uses compressed air (typically at **4 to 8 bar** industrial pressure) to produce linear or rotary motion.

**Working Logic (Step-by-step):**
- Compressed air from a compressor is stored in a reservoir.
- Air passes through an **FRL Unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) to remove moisture/dust and add oil mist.
- A **directional control valve (DCV)** (e.g., 5/2 or 3/2 valve) routes the air into either side of the cylinder.
- The pressure difference across the piston produces a net force, moving the piston.
- Exhaust air is vented to atmosphere.

### 2.2 Hydraulic Actuators — Working Principle

A **Hydraulic Actuator** uses pressurized oil (typically at **50 to 350 bar**). Because liquids are nearly incompressible, hydraulic systems deliver very high forces with excellent stiffness.

**Working Logic (Step-by-step):**
- Oil is drawn from a reservoir by a hydraulic pump.
- A **pressure relief valve** limits the maximum system pressure.
- A **directional control valve** (solenoid-operated) directs oil to the cap or rod end of the cylinder.
- The pressurized oil pushes the piston, generating thrust.
- Return oil flows back to the reservoir through a filter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** Pneumatic = compressible gas, low force, low cost. Hydraulic = incompressible liquid, very high force, higher cost.

### 2.3 Electrical Actuators — Working Principle

Electrical actuators convert electrical energy directly into mechanical motion. Common types are:

- **DC Motor** — Continuous rotary motion; speed controlled by armature voltage.
- **AC Induction Motor** — Robust, used for fixed-speed conveyor drives.
- **Stepper Motor** — Open-loop precise angular positioning (e.g., 1.8° per step).
- **Servo Motor (BLDC/PMSM)** — Closed-loop precise position/velocity control.
- **Solenoid** — Short-stroke linear push/pull action.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield topics. Master the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $F = p \cdot A$ | F=Force (N), p=Pressure (Pa), A=Piston area (m²) | Linear cylinder force |
| 2 | $F_{net} = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2$ | Effective thrust when both sides pressurized | Double-acting cylinder |
| 3 | $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | D = Bore diameter | Piston area |
| 4 | $\eta = \frac{F_{out} \cdot v}{p \cdot Q}$ | Mechanical / Volumetric efficiency | System performance |
| 5 | $P = p \cdot Q$ | P=Power (W), p=Pressure (Pa), Q=Flow (m³/s) | Hydraulic/Pneumatic power |
| 6 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | P=Power (W), T=Torque (N·m), ω=angular velocity (rad/s) | Motor shaft power |
| 7 | $T = F \cdot r$ | Torque from tangential force | Rotary actuator torque |
| 8 | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | Linear velocity at radius r | Conveyor/pulley motion |
| 9 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | Cylinder extension speed |
| 10 | $F_{ext} = \frac{2 \pi \eta_m T}{L_{lead}}$ | Ball-screw linear force from torque T | Electric linear actuator |
| 11 | $E = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2$ | Kinetic energy of rotating mass (J = inertia) | Braking/regen energy |
| 12 | $W = p \cdot \Delta V$ | Work done by expanding gas | Pneumatic cylinder stroke work |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**

- For a **double-acting cylinder** in pneumatic systems, the **rod-side area** is reduced:
$$A_{rod} = A_{piston} - A_{rod\,stem} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$
where **D** is bore diameter and **d** is rod diameter.

- For an **ideal motor**, electrical input power equals mechanical output power (100% efficiency):
$$V \cdot I = T \cdot \omega$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic actuators** dominate heavy industries: forging presses, ship steering gear, aircraft landing gear, mining shovels, injection moulding.
- **Pneumatic actuators** dominate FMCG (Fast Moving Consumer Goods) packaging, pharmaceuticals, and food — where cleanliness, safety (no sparks), and speed are critical.
- **Electrical actuators** dominate precision automation: robotics, semiconductor manufacturing, CNC, printing, and medical devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Double-Acting Pneumatic Cylinder Force

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter **D = 50 mm** and a piston rod diameter **d = 20 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar** and the back pressure (rod side exhaust resistance) is **0.5 bar**. Calculate:
(a) The extension (forward) thrust.
(b) The retraction (return) thrust.
(c) The work done in one full stroke of length **L = 200 mm**.

**Given Data:**

- $D = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.050 \, \text{m}$
- $d = 20 \, \text{mm} = 0.020 \, \text{m}$
- $p_1 = 6 \, \text{bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- $p_2 = 0.5 \, \text{bar} = 0.5 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- $L = 200 \, \text{mm} = 0.200 \, \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the piston areas.**

The full piston area (blank side):
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

The annular area (rod side):
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.050)^2 - (0.020)^2\right]$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0025 - 0.0004) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0021)$$
$$A_2 = 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

> *Textual Explanation:* The piston area is the circular cross-section of the bore. The rod-side area is smaller because part of the piston face is occupied by the rod stem.

**Step 2 — Compute extension thrust.**

During extension, full pressure $p_1$ acts on $A_1$ and back pressure $p_2$ acts on $A_2$:
$$F_{ext} = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2$$
$$F_{ext} = (6 \times 10^5)(1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.5 \times 10^5)(1.6493 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ext} = 1178.10 - 82.47$$
$$F_{ext} = 1095.63 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute retraction thrust.**

During retraction, the roles reverse — high pressure on $A_2$ and back pressure on $A_1$:
$$F_{ret} = p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_1$$
$$F_{ret} = (6 \times 10^5)(1.6493 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.5 \times 10^5)(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ret} = 989.60 - 98.18$$
$$F_{ret} = 891.42 \, \text{N}$$

> *Interpretation:* The cylinder pushes harder during extension (1095 N) than retraction (891 N) because the full bore area is exposed to pressure in extension.

**Step 4 — Compute work done in one full stroke.**

Work is the average of the two thrusts multiplied by the stroke length (a standard KTU simplification):
$$W = \frac{(F_{ext} + F_{ret})}{2} \cdot L$$
$$W = \frac{(1095.63 + 891.42)}{2} \times 0.200$$
$$W = 993.53 \times 0.200$$
$$W = 198.71 \, \text{J}$$

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Hydraulic Press Clamping Force

**Problem:** A hydraulic press cylinder has a bore **D = 100 mm** and operates at **200 bar**. The return spring pressure (counter-pressure) is **5 bar**. The mechanical efficiency of the press is **85%**. Find the actual clamping force.

**Given Data:**

- $D = 100 \, \text{mm} = 0.100 \, \text{m}$
- $p_{sup} = 200 \, \text{bar} = 200 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa}$
- $p_{ret} = 5 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- $\eta_m = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Theoretical (ideal) force.**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$
$$F_{ideal} = (p_{sup} - p_{ret}) \cdot A$$
$$F_{ideal} = (2 \times 10^7 - 5 \times 10^5)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ideal} = (1.95 \times 10^7)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ideal} = 153{,}153 \, \text{N} \approx 153.15 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Actual force after efficiency loss.**
$$F_{actual} = \eta_m \cdot F_{ideal}$$
$$F_{actual} = 0.85 \times 153.15 \, \text{kN}$$
$$F_{actual} = 130.18 \, \text{kN}$$

> *Practical insight:* A 100 mm bore hydraulic cylinder at 200 bar can clamp with over 13 tonnes of force — this is why hydraulics dominate heavy-duty clamping in injection moulding and forging.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — Electrical Servo Motor Drive Power

**Problem:** A conveyor belt driven by a servo motor must produce a linear pulling force of **F = 500 N** at the belt surface. The drive pulley diameter is **D = 200 mm** and the belt speed is **v = 1.5 m/s**. The motor has a mechanical efficiency of **90%**. Find:
(a) The torque required at the motor shaft.
(b) The angular velocity of the motor.
(c) The shaft power output and electrical input power.

**Step 1 — Torque at the pulley surface.**

Pulley radius:
$$r = \frac{D}{2} = 0.100 \, \text{m}$$
$$T_{pulley} = F \cdot r = 500 \times 0.100 = 50 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity of pulley (and motor, assuming 1:1 coupling).**
$$\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{1.5}{0.100} = 15 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Power output at the pulley.**
$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 50 \times 15 = 750 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical input power accounting for efficiency.**
$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_m} = \frac{750}{0.90} = 833.33 \, \text{W}$$

> *Textual bridge:* This 833 W is what the servo drive must draw from the DC bus. It illustrates why electrical actuators are sized by both **torque** AND **speed**, not just one.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

| Parameter | Electrical Actuator | Hydraulic Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Energy Source | Electricity (V × I) | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| Typical Working Pressure | — | **50–350 bar** | **4–8 bar** |
| Force Density (kN per kg) | Low to Medium | **Very High** | Medium |
| Speed Capability | Very High | Medium | High |
| Positioning Accuracy | **Excellent (sub-mm)** | Good (with servo valve) | Moderate (end-of-stroke) |
| Cost | Medium to High | High (pump, tank) | Low (only air needed) |
| Cleanliness | Clean | Risk of oil leak | Clean, dry air |
| Fire/Explosion Safety | Poor (sparks) | Poor (oil fire) | **Excellent** |
| Stiffness | Medium | **Very High** | Low (air compresses) |
| Typical Application | Robotics, CNC | Press, Steering, Moulding | Packaging, Bottling |
| Maintenance | Low | High (seals, filters) | Low to Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — General Industrial Actuator Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply or Compressor or Pump] --> B[Energy Conditioning Unit]
    B --> C[Controller PLC or Microcontroller]
    C --> D[Driver or Amplifier or Directional Valve]
    D --> E[ACTUATOR]
    E --> F[Mechanical Load or Machine Element]
    F --> G[Sensor Feedback Encoder or LVDT or Pressure]
    G --> C
```

> *Reading aid:* The dashed feedback arrow represents the closed-loop control. For open-loop devices like basic pneumatic cylinders or stepper motors, the feedback path may be absent.

### 4.2 Pneumatic Pick-and-Place System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SupplyChain["Compressed Air Supply Chain"]
        A1[Compressor 7 bar] --> A2[Receiver Tank]
        A2 --> A3[FRL Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    end

    subgraph ControlUnit["Valve and Control"]
        A3 --> A4[5 by 2 Solenoid DCV]
        PLC[PLC Controller 24V DC] --> A5[Solenoid Coil Driver]
        A5 --> A4
    end

    subgraph ActuatorBank["Actuator Mechanism"]
        A4 --> P1[Pneumatic Cylinder Vertical]
        A4 --> P2[Pneumatic Cylinder Horizontal]
        A4 --> P3[Pneumatic Gripper]
    end

    subgraph Feedback["Sensors"]
        S1[Reed Switch End of Stroke] --> PLC
        S2[Pressure Switch] --> PLC
        S3[Vacuum Sensor] --> PLC
    end

    P1 --> OBJ[Workpiece Pick and Place]
    P2 --> OBJ
    P3 --> OBJ
```

### 4.3 Hydraulic Press System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerPack["Hydraulic Power Pack"]
        T1[Oil Reservoir 100 L] --> P1[Variable Displacement Pump]
        P1 --> M1[Electric Drive Motor 15 kW]
        P1 --> RV1[Pressure Relief Valve 250 bar]
        RV1 --> T1
    end

    subgraph ValveBank["Valve Manifold"]
        P1 --> DCV1[4 by 3 Directional Control Valve Solenoid Operated]
        DCV1 --> CV1[Flow Control Valve for Speed Regulation]
    end

    subgraph Actuator["Press Cylinder"]
        CV1 --> CYL[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder Bore 100 mm]
        CYL --> PL[Platen and Die Assembly]
    end

    subgraph ControlLoop["Closed Loop Control"]
        PLC[PLC with HMI] --> DRV[Solenoid Driver Card]
        DRV --> DCV1
        PS1[Pressure Transducer] --> PLC
        LS1[Linear Position LVDT] --> PLC
    end
```

### 4.4 Electrical Servo-Driven Robot Arm (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CommandLayer["Command Layer"]
        HMI[Teach Pendant or HMI] --> TC[Tray Controller]
    end

    subgraph DriveLayer["Servo Drive Layer"]
        TC --> SD1[Servo Drive 1 for Joint 1 Base]
        TC --> SD2[Servo Drive 2 for Joint 2 Shoulder]
        TC --> SD3[Servo Drive 3 for Joint 3 Elbow]
        TC --> SD4[Servo Drive 4 for Joint 4 Wrist]
    end

    subgraph ActuatorLayer["Electric Actuator Layer"]
        SD1 --> M1[BLDC Servo Motor 1 with Harmonic Reducer]
        SD2 --> M2[BLDC Servo Motor 2 with Harmonic Reducer]
        SD3 --> M3[BLDC Servo Motor 3]
        SD4 --> M4[BLDC Servo Motor 4]
    end

    M1 --> ARM[Robot Arm End Effector]
    M2 --> ARM
    M3 --> ARM
    M4 --> ARM

    subgraph Feedback["Encoders and Feedback"]
        E1[Absolute Encoder on M1] --> SD1
        E2[Absolute Encoder on M2] --> SD2
        E3[Resolver on M3] --> SD3
        E4[Resolver on M4] --> SD4
    end
```

### 4.5 Comparative Mermaid Chart — Use Case Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the application in a hazardous or explosive area?}
    Q1 -- YES --> A1[Use Pneumatic Actuator]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is the required force greater than 50 kN?}
    Q2 -- YES --> A2[Use Hydraulic Actuator]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is precise positioning and speed control required?}
    Q3 -- YES --> A3[Use Electrical Servo Actuator]
    Q3 -- NO --> A4[Use Pneumatic Cylinder Low Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Reading Strategy:** In exams, when asked to "draw the block diagram of a hydraulic press / pick-and-place system," always show **(i) Power Source → (ii) Conditioning Unit → (iii) Control Valve → (iv) Actuator → (v) Load → (vi) Feedback Sensor → back to controller.** Marks are awarded for each block you correctly identify and connect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting pneumatic cylinder. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points Allocation):**

- **(1 Mark) Single-acting cylinder:** Has **one air port**. Air pressure acts on one side of the piston only. The return stroke is achieved by an **internal spring** or **external load** (gravity).
- **(1 Mark) Double-acting cylinder:** Has **two air ports**. Compressed air is alternately applied to either side of the piston to produce motion in both directions. No spring is required.
- **(1 Mark) Examples:**
  - Single-acting: A clamping cylinder on a drill machine that presses the workpiece down, spring returns it.
  - Double-acting: A pick-and-place cylinder on a packaging conveyor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
**State two advantages and two disadvantages of using hydraulic actuators over pneumatic actuators in industrial automation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(1 Mark) Advantage 1:** Hydraulic actuators produce **much higher forces** (working pressure 50–350 bar) compared to pneumatic (4–8 bar).
- **(1 Mark) Advantage 2:** Hydraulic fluid is **incompressible**, providing **high stiffness and excellent positioning accuracy**.
- **(1 Mark) Disadvantages (any two):** Higher cost (pump, reservoir, filters), risk of oil leakage/fire, requires more maintenance, noisier system.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Regular Exam, Modified]**
**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 35 mm. The pump delivers oil at 160 bar to the cap end, and the back pressure on the rod side is 3 bar. If the stroke length is 250 mm and the cylinder extends in 4 seconds, calculate: (i) the extension thrust, (ii) the oil flow rate in L/min, and (iii) the hydraulic power delivered.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \, \text{mm} = 0.080 \, \text{m}$
- $d = 35 \, \text{mm} = 0.035 \, \text{m}$
- $p_1 = 160 \, \text{bar} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa}$
- $p_2 = 3 \, \text{bar} = 3 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- $L = 0.250 \, \text{m}$, $t = 4 \, \text{s}$

**Step 1 — Areas.** **(1 Mark)**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(0.080)^2 - (0.035)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064 - 0.001225)$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.005175) = 4.0647 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension thrust.** **(2 Marks)**
$$F_{ext} = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2$$
$$F_{ext} = (1.6 \times 10^7)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) - (3 \times 10^5)(4.0647 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ext} = 80{,}424 - 1{,}219.4$$
$$F_{ext} = 79{,}204.6 \, \text{N} \approx 79.2 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Oil flow rate.** **(2 Marks)**
Volume of oil entering cap end:
$$\Delta V = A_1 \cdot L = (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})(0.250) = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$$

Flow rate:
$$Q = \frac{\Delta V}{t} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 18.85 \, \text{L/min}$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic power.** **(2 Marks)**
$$P = p_1 \cdot Q = (1.6 \times 10^7)(3.1416 \times 10^{-4}) = 5026.5 \, \text{W} \approx 5.03 \, \text{kW}$$

---

**Q3(b) [7 Marks]**
**Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a hydraulic actuation system used in an industrial press. Label the function of each block.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — Block + Explanation):**

The block diagram is:

```
[Oil Reservoir] → [Electric Motor] → [Hydraulic Pump]
        ↑                                  ↓
[Filter] ← [Pressure Relief Valve]   [DCV 4/3 Solenoid]
        ↑                                  ↓
[Oil Cooler] ← [Return Line]      [Flow Control Valve]
                                           ↓
                              [Double-Acting Cylinder]
                                           ↓
                                  [Platen / Load]
        ↑                                  ↓
[Pressure Transducer] ← [PLC] ← [LVDT Position Sensor]
```

- **(1 Mark)** **Oil Reservoir** — Stores hydraulic oil and allows air/debris separation.
- **(1 Mark)** **Electric Motor + Pump** — Converts electrical to hydraulic energy, pressurizes oil to 100–250 bar.
- **(1 Mark)** **Pressure Relief Valve** — Safety device; vents excess oil back to tank if pressure exceeds set value.
- **(1 Mark)** **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — Routes pressurized oil to either side of the cylinder.
- **(1 Mark)** **Flow Control Valve** — Regulates cylinder extension/retraction speed.
- **(1 Mark)** **Hydraulic Cylinder** — Converts hydraulic pressure into linear thrust.
- **(1 Mark)** **Sensors + PLC** — Pressure transducer and LVDT provide closed-loop feedback to the PLC for accurate force/position control.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative, 14 Marks)**

**Q4(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**
**A pick-and-place pneumatic system uses a double-acting cylinder with bore 40 mm, rod diameter 16 mm, and stroke 150 mm. The supply pressure is 5 bar, and the rod-side back pressure is 0.3 bar. If the cylinder completes 30 working cycles per minute, calculate: (i) the extension force, (ii) the retraction force, and (iii) the average air consumption per minute.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.040 \, \text{m}$, $d = 0.016 \, \text{m}$, $L = 0.150 \, \text{m}$
- $p_1 = 5 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$, $p_2 = 0.3 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$, $N = 30$ cycles/min

**Step 1 — Areas.** **(1 Mark)**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.040)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(0.040)^2 - (0.016)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0016 - 0.000256) = 1.0568 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension force.** **(2 Marks)**
$$F_{ext} = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2 = (5 \times 10^5)(1.2566 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.3 \times 10^5)(1.0568 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ext} = 628.3 - 31.7 = 596.6 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Retraction force.** **(2 Marks)**
$$F_{ret} = p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_1 = (5 \times 10^5)(1.0568 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.3 \times 10^5)(1.2566 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$F_{ret} = 528.4 - 37.7 = 490.7 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Air consumption per cycle.** **(2 Marks)**
Volume per cycle (sum of extension and retraction stroke volumes):
$$V_{cycle} = A_1 L + A_2 L = L(A_1 + A_2)$$
$$V_{cycle} = 0.150 \times (1.2566 + 1.0568) \times 10^{-3} = 0.150 \times 2.3134 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$V_{cycle} = 3.470 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3 = 0.347 \, \text{L per cycle}$$

**Per minute:**
$$Q_{air} = 0.347 \times 30 = 10.41 \, \text{L/min}$$

---

**Q4(b) [7 Marks]**
**Compare the use of electrical servo motors and pneumatic cylinders in a packaging industry bottle-filling line. Mention 4 key points.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 4 Comparison Points + 3 for Examples):**

- **(2 Marks) Accuracy:** Servo motors provide **closed-loop position control with sub-mm accuracy**, essential for index-table alignment and capping torque control. Pneumatic cylinders are **end-of-stroke only** with ±1–2 mm repeatability — adequate for simple pick-and-place but not for precision capping.
- **(2 Marks) Speed and Cycle Time:** Pneumatic cylinders achieve **faster cycle times (under 1 second)** and are simpler to retrofit. Servo motors offer **variable speed profiles (acceleration/deceleration ramps)** but are limited by inertia.
- **(1.5 Marks) Cleanliness and Safety:** Pneumatic is **intrinsically safe** (no sparks, no oil) and dominates in food/pharma. Servo motors are clean but involve **electrical cabinets** requiring IP-rated enclosures.
- **(1.5 Marks) Cost and Maintenance:** Pneumatic systems are **cheaper and easier to maintain** (just FRL and valve replacement). Servo systems have **higher upfront cost** but provide **better diagnostics and longer-term flexibility** for product changeovers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rod-side area reduction** (2 Marks lost): In double-acting cylinders, $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$, NOT $A_1$. Most students incorrectly use $A_1$ for both extension and retraction.
>
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** (2 Marks lost): 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa, not $10^6$ Pa. Bore given in mm must be converted to meters before squaring. Final force in Newtons (N), not kN, unless asked.
>
> 3. **Confusing Power formulas** (1.5 Marks lost): Hydraulic/pneumatic power is $P = p \cdot Q$ (pressure × flow). Motor power is $P = T \cdot \omega$ (torque × angular velocity). Students frequently mix them up.
>
> 4. **Missing the back pressure term** (1.5 Marks lost): In real systems, the opposite side of the cylinder always has a non-zero back pressure (0.3–1 bar). The formula is always $F = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2$, NEVER just $F = p \cdot A$.
>
> 5. **In block diagrams, missing the feedback loop** (1 Mark lost): KTU valuators check for closed-loop arrows. Always include the sensor → PLC arrow.
>
> 6. **Stroke length in meters** (0.5–1 Mark lost): Never use cm or mm in energy/work formulas. Always convert to SI units (m, s, Pa) before plugging into equations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- **Actuator** = final control element that converts energy into physical motion.
- **Three families:** Electrical (motors/solenoids), Hydraulic (oil, high force, 50–350 bar), Pneumatic (air, low force, 4–8 bar).
- **Master formula for linear force:** $F = p \cdot A$ (pressure × piston area).
- **Double-acting cylinder area:** $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ — accounts for the rod.
- **Net thrust (real system):** $F_{net} = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2$.
- **Hydraulic power:** $P = p \cdot Q$ where $p$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s.
- **Motor power:** $P = T \cdot \omega$ where $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s.
- **Pneumatic flow rate:** $Q = A \cdot v$ (area × piston velocity).
- **Conversion:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa. 1 L = $10^{-3}$ m³.
- **Pneumatic components in order:** Compressor → Tank → FRL → DCV → Cylinder.
- **Hydraulic components in order:** Reservoir → Pump → Relief Valve → DCV → Cylinder.
- **Servo components in order:** Controller → Drive → Motor → Gearbox → Load → Encoder (feedback).
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Pneumatic for clean/safe/low-force, Hydraulic for high-force, Electrical for precision.
- **Typical exam numerical:** Always a double-acting cylinder with extension + retraction force + power question (7 marks).
- **Typical exam theory:** Block diagram of press / pick-and-place system with component labeling (7 marks).
- **Common 3-mark topics:** Difference between actuator types, advantages/disadvantages, single vs. double acting, components of pneumatic circuit.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Examples: Use of Electrical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic Actuators in Industrial Automation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic/Hydraulic Automation: Control Valves - Direction, Pressure and Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic/Hydraulic Automation: Control Valves - Direction, Pressure and Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Control Valves in Fluid Power Automation

In **Pneumatic** (gas-based, typically air) and **Hydraulic** (liquid-based, typically mineral oil) automation systems, **Control Valves** are the *decision-making heart* of the circuit. They regulate the flow of the working fluid to actuators (cylinders, motors) and govern the system's pressure and flow rate. Without these valves, an automation system would simply be a passive plumbing network with no controllable behaviour.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Introduction to Industrial Automation*, control valves are broadly classified into **three functional categories**:

| # | Valve Type | Primary Function | Typical Pneumatic Range | Typical Hydraulic Range |
|---|------------|------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Direction Control Valves (DCV)** | Routes fluid to the correct port of the actuator | 2–10 bar | 50–350 bar |
| 2 | **Pressure Control Valves (PCV)** | Limits, regulates, or sequences system pressure | Up to **10 bar** standard | Up to **350 bar** standard |
| 3 | **Flow Control Valves (FCV)** | Controls the speed of actuators by metering fluid | 1–10 bar (low pressure) | 50–210 bar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the graphical **ISO 1219 symbol-based identification** of these valves (number of positions, number of ports, method of actuation, and return). You must be able to draw and interpret these symbols for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plumbing Traffic Police" Intuition

Imagine a multi-lane highway interchange. The **fluid** (air or oil) is the traffic. The **actuator** (cylinder/motor) is the destination city. Now:
- A **Direction Control Valve** is like the *traffic policeman at the junction* — he decides *which lane* the cars go to, effectively deciding whether the cylinder extends or retracts.
- A **Pressure Control Valve** is like the *speed governor on the highway* — if traffic density (pressure) gets too high, it opens a bypass to release the excess, protecting the system from bursting.
- A **Flow Control Valve** is like a *toll booth with a single gate* — even if the highway is full, only one car can pass per second, which slows the entire traffic flow (actuator speed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Context:** Modern manufacturing plants (e.g., Bosch, Festo, SMC) use standardized **ISO 5599** valves for pneumatics and **ISO 4401 / CETOP** standards for hydraulics. KTU expects familiarity with these standard naming conventions.

### 1.3 Why These Valves Matter in Industry

In automated bottling plants, CNC machines, robotic arms, and assembly lines, control valves ensure:
- **Repeatability:** Every cycle of the actuator behaves identically.
- **Safety:** Over-pressure protection prevents catastrophic system failure.
- **Energy efficiency:** Metered flow reduces pump/compressor load.
- **Process control:** Speed, force, and direction are precisely tunable.

The standard reference pressure values for industrial pneumatics and hydraulics are typically:

$$
P_{\text{pneumatic, std}} = 6 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
P_{\text{hydraulic, std}} = 100 \text{ to } 200 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** **1 bar** = $10^5$ Pascal (Pa) = $10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$. This conversion is essential for KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direction Control Valves (DCV) — Detailed Analysis

A **DCV** is defined by the number of **ports** (connection points) and **positions** (distinct switching states). The general notation is:

$$
\text{Number of ports} \times \text{Number of positions}
$$

For example, a **4/3 valve** means *4 ports, 3 positions* — the most common configuration for double-acting cylinders.

### 2.1.1 Key DCV Configurations (High-Yield for KTU)

- **2/2 Valve:** Two ports, two positions. Used as an on/off valve.
- **3/2 Valve:** Three ports, two positions. Used for single-acting cylinders (spring return).
- **4/2 Valve:** Four ports, two positions. Used for double-acting cylinders.
- **4/3 Valve:** Four ports, three positions. Includes a *centre position* (e.g., tandem centre, closed centre, open centre).
- **5/2 and 5/3 Valves:** Five ports allow for separate exhaust paths.

### 2.1.2 Methods of Actuation and Return

KTU frequently asks about *how* the valve is shifted and *how* it returns. The symbols are read sequentially:
- **Solenoid (S):** Electrical signal shifts the valve.
- **Push Button (P):** Manual mechanical actuation.
- **Pilot (P):** Pneumatic/hydraulic signal actuates.
- **Spring (←):** Spring returns the valve to its default position.
- **Detent:** Mechanical latch holds position.

## 2.2 Pressure Control Valves (PCV) — Detailed Analysis

PCVs are governed by **Pascal's Law** and the force balance across a spool/poppet. The fundamental equation is:

$$
P \cdot A_{\text{spool}} = F_{\text{spring}} + P_{\text{downstream}} \cdot A_{\text{spool}}
$$

### 2.2.1 Types of PCV

1. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV):** Limits maximum system pressure; opens to tank when set pressure is exceeded.
2. **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRedV):** Maintains a constant lower pressure in a secondary circuit.
3. **Sequence Valve:** Opens secondary circuit only when primary pressure reaches a set value.
4. **Counterbalance Valve:** Maintains back-pressure to hold a vertical load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** A *sequence valve* is *not* a relief valve. A relief valve is *normally closed* and opens only on over-pressure. A sequence valve is *normally closed* but opens to direct flow to a secondary actuator.

## 2.3 Flow Control Valves (FCV) — Detailed Analysis

FCVs regulate the volume of fluid passing per unit time. For incompressible fluids (hydraulic oil), the volumetric flow rate is:

$$
Q = A \cdot v
$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the orifice and $v$ is the fluid velocity.

### 2.3.1 Types of FCV

1. **Needle Valve:** Adjustable throttling for fine speed control.
2. **Throttle (Orifice) Valve:** Fixed or variable restriction.
3. **One-way Flow Control Valve (Meter-out / Meter-in):** Combines a check valve and throttle to control flow in one direction only.
4. **Proportional/Servo Valve:** Electronically variable orifice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** *Meter-in* controls extension speed; *Meter-out* controls retraction speed. In pneumatics, *meter-out* is preferred because air is compressible — meter-in causes "cushioning" and jerky motion.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **all key equations** required for KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic:

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Pascal's Law | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $P$ in Pa, $F$ in N, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | Pressure Conversion | $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$ | Industry standard |
| 3 | Cylinder Force (Push) | $F_{\text{push}} = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}}$ | Full piston area |
| 4 | Cylinder Force (Pull) | $F_{\text{pull}} = P \cdot (A_{\text{piston}} - A_{\text{rod}})$ | Annular area only |
| 5 | Continuity Equation | $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Incompressible flow |
| 6 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 7 | Continuity of Cylinder | $v_{\text{piston}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{piston}}}$ | Speed of actuator |
| 8 | Time to Extend | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A_{\text{piston}} \cdot L}{Q}$ | $L$ = stroke length |
| 9 | Bernoulli (incompressible) | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g h = \text{const}$ | Energy conservation |
| 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $Re < 2300$ → laminar; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent |
| 11 | Valve Flow Coefficient | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}$ | $C_v$ = valve sizing coefficient (US units) |
| 12 | Power (Hydraulic) | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{600}$ | $Q$ in L/min, $p$ in bar → kW |
| 13 | Air Consumption | $Q_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cyl}} \cdot (p_{\text{work}} + p_{\text{atm}})}{p_{\text{atm}}}$ | For pneumatic systems |
| 14 | Pump Flow Rate | $Q_{\text{pump}} = V_{\text{disp}} \cdot n \cdot \eta_v$ | $V_{\text{disp}}$ = displacement, $n$ = rpm |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- The **Cylinder Force equation** is used to size actuators in press machines and injection moulding.
- The **Reynolds Number** is critical for selecting between *laminar* and *turbulent* flow regimes, which determines valve design (ball vs. poppet).
- The **Valve Flow Coefficient ($C_v$)** is the universal metric used in process industries (oil & gas) for valve sizing in pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Force Produced by a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

This is one of the most frequently asked KTU numericals. We will derive the force equation from first principles.

### Step 1: Statement of Problem

A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston of diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ and a rod of diameter $d = 25 \text{ mm}$. The pump supplies a pressure of $P = 100 \text{ bar}$. Find:
- (a) The extending (push) force.
- (b) The retracting (pull) force.
- (c) The piston velocity if the pump flow is $Q = 15 \text{ L/min}$.

### Step 2: Compute the Full Piston Area

The piston area is the cross-section of the cylinder bore:

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025
$$

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Annular (Rod-Side) Area

The annular area is the piston area minus the rod area:

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^2 - d^2 \right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.050)^2 - (0.025)^2 \right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 0.0025 - 0.000625 \right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.001875
$$

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 4: Convert Pressure to SI Units

$$
P = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Extending Force

$$
F_{\text{push}} = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{push}} = 1 \times 10^7 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{\text{push}} = 19635 \text{ N} \approx 19.635 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Retracting Force

$$
F_{\text{pull}} = P \cdot A_{\text{annulus}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{pull}} = 1 \times 10^7 \times 1.4726 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{\text{pull}} = 14726 \text{ N} \approx 14.726 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 7: Convert Pump Flow to SI Units

$$
Q = 15 \text{ L/min} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 8: Compute Piston Velocity (Extension)

From the continuity equation $Q = A \cdot v$:

$$
v_{\text{piston}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{piston}}}
$$

$$
v_{\text{piston}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v_{\text{piston}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s} = 127.3 \text{ mm/s}
$$

### Step 9: Final Result Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Piston Area | $A_{\text{piston}}$ | $1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ |
| Annular Area | $A_{\text{annulus}}$ | $1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ |
| Push Force | $F_{\text{push}}$ | $\approx 19.64 \text{ kN}$ |
| Pull Force | $F_{\text{pull}}$ | $\approx 14.73 \text{ kN}$ |
| Extension Velocity | $v_{\text{piston}}$ | $\approx 0.127 \text{ m/s}$ |

## 3.2 Derivation: Cylinder Extension Time Under Throttled Flow

### Step 1: Problem Setup

A hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, stroke $L = 300 \text{ mm}$. A flow control valve restricts flow to $Q = 8 \text{ L/min}$. Find the extension time.

### Step 2: Compute the Cylinder Volume

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = A_{\text{piston}} \cdot L
$$

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 \times 0.300
$$

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.300
$$

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.300
$$

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = 1.5080 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Convert Flow to SI

$$
Q = 8 \text{ L/min} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.3333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Time

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{1.5080 \times 10^{-2}}{1.3333 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
t = 113.1 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.88 \text{ minutes}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Valve Selection Decision Table

Use this matrix to choose the right valve for a KTU design question:

| Application Requirement | Recommended Valve | Why? |
|--------------------------|-------------------|------|
| Hold a vertical load against gravity | Counterbalance valve (PCV) | Maintains back-pressure to prevent drift |
| Run two actuators in sequence | Sequence valve (PCV) | Opens second circuit only after first pressure reached |
| Supply a low-pressure clamping circuit from a high-pressure main | Pressure Reducing Valve | Steps down pressure safely |
| Smooth retraction of a pneumatic cylinder | Meter-out FCV | Avoids compressibility-induced jerk |
| Precise proportional speed control of an actuator | Proportional/Servo valve | Electronic variable orifice |
| Emergency shut-off of a hydraulic press | 2/2 Normally-Closed DCV (Solenoid) | Fail-safe on power loss |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Generic Fluid Power Automation Loop

The following block diagram shows how the three valve types interact with the source, actuator, and controller in an industrial fluid power system:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Power Source Pump or Compressor] --> PCV1
    PCV1[Pressure Control Valve PCV] --> DCV1
    DCV1[Direction Control Valve DCV] --> FCV1
    FCV1[Flow Control Valve FCV] --> ACT1
    ACT1[Actuator Cylinder or Motor] --> LOAD1
    LOAD1[Mechanical Load] --> SENS1
    SENS1[Feedback Sensor] --> CTRL1
    CTRL1[Controller PLC or Microcontroller] --> DCV1
    CTRL1 --> FCV1
    PCV1 -.overpressure bypass.-> TANK1
    TANK1[Reservoir or Tank]
    DCV1 -.exhaust.-> TANK1
    TANK1 --> SRC1
```

## 4.2 Direction Control Valve — 4/3 Closed Centre (Schematic Logic)

This diagram shows the spool shifting logic of a 4/3 DCV, which is the most-asked DCV type in KTU exams:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "Position 1 (Neutral)" as pos1
    state "Position 2 (Extend)" as pos2
    state "Position 3 (Retract)" as pos3
    pos1 --> pos2 : Solenoid A energised
    pos2 --> pos1 : Solenoid A de-energised
    pos1 --> pos3 : Solenoid B energised
    pos3 --> pos1 : Solenoid B de-energised
```

**Spool position logic (in words, since Mermaid cannot draw physical spools):**
- **Position 1 (Centre):** All ports blocked — cylinder is hydraulically locked.
- **Position 2 (Left):** Pressure → cap end; rod end → tank. Cylinder **extends**.
- **Position 3 (Right):** Pressure → rod end; cap end → tank. Cylinder **retracts**.

## 4.3 Pressure Control Valve — Relief Valve Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[Inlet Port High Pressure] --> SPOOL1
    SPOOL1[Spring Loaded Spool or Poppet] --> OUT1
    OUT1[Outlet Port to System]
    SPOOL1 -.opens when.-> EX1
    EX1[Exhaust to Tank] --> TANK2
    TANK2[Reservoir]
    SPR1[Adjustment Spring] -.sets cracking pressure.-> SPOOL1
    SENSE1[Pressure Feedback] --> SPOOL1
```

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step):**
1. System pressure enters at the **Inlet Port**.
2. The pressure acts on the spool area $A_{\text{spool}}$.
3. When $P \cdot A_{\text{spool}} > F_{\text{spring}}$, the spool shifts and opens the path to the **Exhaust (Tank)**.
4. Excess pressure is dumped, protecting downstream components.

## 4.4 Flow Control Valve — One-Way (Meter-Out) Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN2[Cylinder Port Cap End] --> CHK1
    CHK1[Check Valve Free Flow Direction] --> OUT2
    OUT2[To Tank or Return Line]
    IN2 -.reverse flow.-> THR1
    THR1[Throttle Needle Valve] --> OUT2
```

**Operating Principle:**
- **Free flow direction:** Fluid pushes the check valve open and returns unrestricted (fast movement).
- **Metered direction:** Fluid must pass through the throttle (needle valve), creating a controlled restriction (slow movement).

## 4.5 Master Circuit Diagram — Double-Acting Cylinder with All Three Valves

This is the *full integration schematic* showing all three valve types in a single hydraulic circuit:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP1[Pump] --> FILT1
    FILT1[Filter] --> TANK3
    TANK3[Reservoir with PRV inside] --> PRV2
    PRV2[Pressure Relief Valve Set at 150 bar] --> MAN1
    MAN1[Pressure Manometer Gauge] --> DCV2
    DCV2[4/3 DCV Solenoid Actuated] --> FCV2
    FCV2[Flow Control Valve Meter Out] --> CYL1
    CYL1[Double Acting Cylinder]
    CYL1 --> EXH1
    EXH1[Exhaust and Return to Tank] --> FILT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw a hydraulic circuit" in the ESE, always include the **filter**, **reservoir**, **pressure gauge**, and **relief valve** as standard peripheral elements. Examiners allocate at least 1–2 marks for these standard symbols.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Differentiate between a 3/2 DCV and a 5/2 DCV with the help of ISO symbols. State one industrial application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **3/2 Direction Control Valve** has *3 ports* (Pressure, Cylinder, Exhaust) and *2 positions* (normally closed / normally open). It is used for **single-acting cylinders** where spring return is required. Common application: controlling a small pneumatic clamping cylinder in a packaging machine.

A **5/2 DCV** has *5 ports* (Pressure, 2 Cylinder ports, 2 separate Exhaust ports) and *2 positions*. It allows **double-acting cylinders** to extend and retract with *separate exhaust paths*, which prevents back-pressure build-up. Common application: controlling the up/down motion of a hydraulic press ram.

| Feature | 3/2 DCV | 5/2 DCV |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Number of Ports | 3 | 5 |
| Positions | 2 | 2 |
| Cylinder Type | Single-acting | Double-acting |
| Exhaust Paths | 1 (shared) | 2 (separate) |
| Symbol Blocks | 2 | 2 |

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Difference (1) + Application (1) = **3 marks**.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Explain the working of a pressure reducing valve with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRedV)** is a normally-open pressure control valve that maintains a *constant reduced pressure* in a secondary circuit, regardless of higher primary pressure fluctuations. It works on a force-balance principle:

- Primary pressure $P_1$ acts on the spool's inlet side.
- Secondary (reduced) pressure $P_2$ acts on the spool's outlet side and opposes the spring force $F_s$.
- When $P_2 \cdot A_{\text{spool}} > F_s + P_1 \cdot A_{\text{spool, net}}$, the spool shifts to throttle the inlet, reducing $P_2$.
- When $P_2$ drops below the set value, the spring reopens the spool, allowing more flow.

**Application:** Used to supply a *low-pressure clamping circuit* (e.g., 50 bar) from a *high-pressure main system* (e.g., 200 bar) without affecting the main circuit.

**Marks Distribution:** Working (2) + Application (1) = **3 marks**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**A hydraulic system uses a double-acting cylinder with a bore of 100 mm and a rod diameter of 60 mm. The pump supplies oil at a flow rate of 30 L/min at a pressure of 120 bar.**

**a) (7 marks) Calculate:**
- (i) The full extension force.
- (ii) The full retraction force.
- (iii) The extension velocity of the piston.

**b) (7 marks) Explain the working of a 4/3 DCV with closed-centre position. Sketch its ISO symbol and describe how it controls a double-acting cylinder. Also state the purpose of using a closed-centre position instead of an open-centre.**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Compute the piston area**

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute the annular area**

$$
A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.010 - 0.0036) = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Convert pressure to SI**

$$
P = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4: Convert flow to SI**

$$
Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5: Extension force**

$$
F_{\text{push}} = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 94247.8 \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6: Retraction force**

$$
F_{\text{pull}} = P \cdot A_{\text{annulus}} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 60318.6 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7: Extension velocity**

$$
v_{\text{piston}} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{piston}}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0637 \text{ m/s} \approx 63.66 \text{ mm/s}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

A **4/3 Direction Control Valve with Closed Centre** has 4 ports (P, T, A, B) and 3 positions. In the **centre (neutral) position**, all four ports are blocked. The working is as follows:

- **Position 1 (Centre, spring-centred):** P, T, A, and B are all blocked. The cylinder is *hydraulically locked* and remains stationary. Pump flow is blocked, so a relief valve must dump the entire pump flow to tank.
- **Position 2 (Left, e.g., Solenoid A energised):** P → B (cap end), A → T (rod end). Cylinder **extends**.
- **Position 3 (Right, e.g., Solenoid B energised):** P → A (rod end), B → T (cap end). Cylinder **retracts**.

**ISO Symbol Description (narrative):** Two adjacent squares represent the two shifted positions; a central square represents the spring-centred neutral. Arrows in the squares show flow paths between P, T, A, B.

**Purpose of Closed Centre:**
1. **Energy efficiency:** No pump circulation in neutral → less heat generation.
2. **Safety:** Cylinder is positively locked — it will not drift if there is an external load.
3. **Suitable for fixed-displacement pumps with accumulator circuits.**

**Marks Distribution for (b):** Working (3) + ISO symbol explanation (2) + Closed-centre purpose (2) = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**a) (7 marks) With neat diagrams, explain the working of the following pressure control valves:**
- (i) Pressure Relief Valve.
- (ii) Sequence Valve.

**State two differences between them.**

**b) (7 marks) A pneumatic cylinder of bore 50 mm and stroke 200 mm operates at a working pressure of 6 bar (gauge). The cylinder extends in 2 seconds. Calculate:**
- (i) The average air consumption per cycle in litres (at atmospheric conditions).
- (ii) The force exerted by the piston during extension.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**(i) Pressure Relief Valve (PRV):**
A **normally closed** valve that opens only when the system pressure exceeds a preset value. Working:
- System pressure acts on the spool/poppet area.
- When $P_{\text{sys}} \cdot A_{\text{spool}} > F_{\text{spring}}$, the spool lifts and connects the system to the tank.
- Excess flow is dumped, limiting maximum system pressure.
- Used as a *safety device* downstream of the pump.

**(ii) Sequence Valve:**
Also **normally closed**, but its outlet is connected to a *secondary actuator* (not tank). Working:
- When primary pressure $P_1$ exceeds the spring setting, the valve opens.
- Flow is directed to the secondary circuit, *enabling* a second actuator only after the first has completed its stroke.
- Used for *sequential operations* (e.g., clamp first → then drill).

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Pressure Relief Valve | Sequence Valve |
|--------|----------------------|----------------|
| Outlet Connection | Direct to tank (exhaust) | Direct to secondary actuator |
| Function | Safety / pressure limit | Sequencing of actuators |
| Adjustment | Set just above working pressure | Set to required sequencing pressure |
| Symbol | Arrow pointing to dashed line (tank) | Arrow pointing to a working port |

**Marks Distribution:** (i) PRV working (2) + (ii) Sequence valve working (2) + Diagram (1) + Differences table (2) = **7 marks**.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Bore $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$
- Working pressure (gauge) $P_{\text{work}} = 6 \text{ bar}$
- Extension time $t = 2 \text{ s}$
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{\text{atm}} = 1 \text{ bar} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Absolute working pressure $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{work}} + P_{\text{atm}} = 7 \text{ bar} = 7 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Compute cylinder geometric volume**

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 (0.200)
$$

$$
V_{\text{cyl}} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 0.3927 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 2: Compute free air consumption per cycle**

The free air volume is the volume the air *would occupy at atmospheric pressure*. Using Boyle's Law ($P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$):

$$
V_{\text{free}} = V_{\text{cyl}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{abs}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} = 0.3927 \times \frac{7}{1} = 2.749 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3: Compute extension force**

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
P_{\text{work, SI}} = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
F_{\text{push}} = P_{\text{work, SI}} \cdot A_{\text{piston}} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{\text{push}} = 1178.1 \text{ N} \approx 1.178 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Free air consumption per cycle = **$\approx 2.75$ L**
- (ii) Extension force = **$\approx 1178$ N (1.18 kN)**

**Marks Distribution:** (i) Air consumption (3.5) + (ii) Force (3.5) = **7 marks**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert L/min to m³/s, or bar to Pa. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly in the answer script.
> 2. **Forgetting Absolute vs Gauge Pressure (–1 mark):** In pneumatic calculations, students often use *gauge* pressure instead of *absolute* pressure. Air consumption formulas require **absolute pressure**.
> 3. **Confusing Relief and Sequence Valves (–1 mark):** A relief valve exhausts to tank; a sequence valve exhausts to a working port. Many students interchange them.
> 4. **Missing Cylinder Symbol (–1 mark):** In drawing questions, omitting the standard *pump + filter + reservoir + gauge* peripheral symbols loses easy marks.
> 5. **Wrong Force Formula (–2 marks):** Using full piston area for *retraction* force. Always use the *annular area* for the rod side.
> 6. **Symbol Arrows (–1 mark):** Forgetting to show arrows in DCV symbols indicating flow direction. ISO 1219 strictly mandates arrows.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- [x] **Three control valve categories:** Direction (DCV), Pressure (PCV), Flow (FCV).
- [x] **DCV notation:** "Ports/Positions" — e.g., 4/3, 5/2.
- [x] **ISO 1219 symbol reading rule:** Read *boxes left to right* for positions; *arrows in boxes* show flow direction; *blocked ports* = T-bar; *spring return* = arrow returning to box; *solenoid* = diagonal arrow with coil.
- [x] **Cylinder force formulas:**
  - Push: $F = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}}$
  - Pull: $F = P \cdot A_{\text{annulus}}$
- [x] **Piston velocity:** $v = Q / A_{\text{piston}}$.
- [x] **Cylinder extension time:** $t = V_{\text{cyl}} / Q = A \cdot L / Q$.
- [x] **Pressure unit:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa.
- [x] **Flow unit:** 1 L/min = $\tfrac{1}{60} \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s.
- [x] **Pascal's Law:** $P = F / A$.
- [x] **Continuity Equation:** $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ (incompressible).
- [x] **Bernoulli's Equation:** $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g h = \text{constant}$.
- [x] **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$; laminar if $Re < 2300$, turbulent if $Re > 4000$.
- [x] **Pneumatic standard working pressure:** 6 bar gauge.
- [x] **Hydraulic standard working pressure:** 100–200 bar.
- [x] **PRV:** Normally closed, exhausts to tank, used for overpressure safety.
- [x] **PRedV:** Normally open, maintains lower secondary pressure.
- [x] **Sequence Valve:** Directs flow to second actuator after set pressure.
- [x] **Meter-in FCV:** Controls extension speed.
- [x] **Meter-out FCV:** Controls retraction speed (preferred in pneumatics).
- [x] **Free air consumption formula:** $V_{\text{free}} = V_{\text{cyl}} \cdot (P_{\text{work}} + P_{\text{atm}}) / P_{\text{atm}}$.
- [x] **Hydraulic power formula:** $P = Q \cdot p / 600$ (kW, with Q in L/min, p in bar).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequential Control of Single/Multiple Actuator Systems, Cascade and Karnaugh Veitch Map Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sequential Control of Single/Multiple Actuator Systems, Cascade and Karnaugh Veitch Map Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — Sequential Control of Single/Multiple Actuator Systems

## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Sequential Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequential Control** is a control strategy in which the state of an automated system progresses through a defined, ordered sequence of steps, where each step is triggered only after the *preceding step has been physically completed* (sensed by limit switches, sensors, or feedback devices). Unlike *combinational control* where outputs depend solely on the *current* input combination, sequential control relies on the **history** (memory) of past events.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, sequential control of actuators refers to commanding **double-solenoid directional control valves** (or equivalent relay/contactor coils) such that pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders (actuators) move in a precisely timed **A+ → B+ → A− → B−** style progression. The "+" denotes the *extend* stroke and "−" denotes the *retract* stroke.

### 1.2 The Three Standard Multiple-Actuator Configurations

| Configuration | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **A+ A−** | Single actuator, reciprocating motion | Clamping, stamping |
| **A+ B+ B− A−** | Two actuators, overlapping strokes | Drilling + indexing |
| **A+ B+ A− B−** | Two actuators, alternating strokes | Loading + unloading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** A student must be able to design relay logic for **all three** configurations using *both* the **Cascade Method** and the **Karnaugh-Veitch (KV) Map Method**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Train Station Ticket Gate"

Imagine a ticket gate at a railway station:

- **Step 1:** You insert a coin → **Gate A** opens (you pass through).
- **Step 2:** A sensor detects you have cleared Gate A → **Gate B** opens.
- **Step 3:** A sensor detects you have cleared Gate B → **Gate A** closes behind you.
- **Step 4:** A sensor detects Gate A is fully closed → **Gate B** closes.

Notice the **chained dependency**: no gate moves until the *previous* gate has completed its motion and a sensor confirms it. This is *exactly* how sequential control of actuators works — **limit switches are the "sensors," and solenoid valves are the "gates."**

### 1.4 Key Vocabulary (KTU Standard)

- **Actuator (A):** Pneumatic/hydraulic cylinder producing linear motion.
- **Limit Switch (a₀, a₁):** a₀ = actuator fully retracted; a₁ = actuator fully extended.
- **Solenoid Coil (Y):** The electrical coil energising a directional control valve.
- **Sequence Step:** A discrete logical state in the working cycle.
- **Memory Element (Set/Reset Latch):** A relay holding contact that retains state across steps.
- **Cascade Relay Chain:** A daisy-chain of auxiliary relays (c₁, c₂, c₃ …) that advances one link per completed step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Control Problem — Formal Statement

Given:

- A set of $n$ actuators $\mathcal{A} = \{A, B, C, \dots\}$
- Each actuator $X$ has two solenoid coils $Y_{X+}$ (extend) and $Y_{X-}$ (retract)
- A defined **sequence string** $S$ (e.g., `A+ B+ A- B-`)
- Limit switches $x_0$ (retracted limit) and $x_1$ (extended limit) for each actuator $X$

**Goal:** Synthesise Boolean equations
$$Y_{X\pm} = f(\text{limit switches}, \text{start button}, \text{cascade chain})$$
such that *every step is uniquely permitted* and *no two conflicting coils energise simultaneously*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Constraint:** $Y_{X+}$ and $Y_{X-}$ **must never** both equal `1` at the same instant (would short the valve). This is called the **Interlock Condition**.

## 2.2 The Two Solution Strategies Compared

### 2.2.1 Cascade Method (Chain Method)

- **Philosophy:** A linear chain of *n* memory elements $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n$ advances **one link at a time**.
- Each actuator coil $Y_{X\pm}$ is AND-ed with the *corresponding* cascade relay: the coil may energise **only when its chain link is active**.
- **Pros:** Simple, visual, easy to draw on ladder diagrams.
- **Cons:** Wastes auxiliary relays; not minimal.

### 2.2.2 Karnaugh-Veitch (KV) Map Method

- **Philosophy:** Treat the limit-switch states as Boolean variables; tabulate the **minterms** (rows of the truth table) for which each coil should energise; minimise via KV-map grouping.
- **Pros:** Produces a *minimised* Boolean expression — fewer contacts in hardware.
- **Cons:** Algebraic; tedious for $>4$ variables; prone to algebraic mistakes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Sequential Control Design
