| # | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{A+} = \text{START} \cdot a_0 \cdot b_0 \cdot c_1$ | Coil $Y_{A+}$ energises if Start pressed, both actuators retracted, chain link $c_1$ active | Cascade method, Step 1 |
| 2 | $Y_{B+} = a_1 \cdot c_2$ | Coil $Y_{B+}$ needs $A$ extended and link $c_2$ | Cascade method, Step 2 |
| 3 | $Y_{A-} = b_1 \cdot c_3$ | Coil $Y_{A-}$ needs $B$ extended and link $c_3$ | Cascade method, Step 3 |
| 4 | $Y_{B-} = a_0 \cdot c_4$ | Coil $Y_{B-}$ needs $A$ retracted and link $c_4$ | Cascade method, Step 4 |
| 5 | $c_1 = (\text{START}) + (c_1 \cdot \overline{c_2})$ | Set/Reset latch for link $c_1$ | Cascade chain |
| 6 | $c_2 = (a_1 \cdot c_1) + (c_2 \cdot \overline{c_3})$ | Link $c_2$ set by $a_1$, reset by next link | Cascade chain |
| 7 | $c_k = (X_{k-1} \cdot c_{k-1}) + (c_k \cdot \overline{c_{k+1}})$ | General cascade-link latch | Recursive form |
| 8 | $\text{Interlock: } Y_{X+} \cdot Y_{X-} = 0$ | Mutual exclusion of extend/retract coils | Safety |
| 9 | $f(X_1, X_2, X_3) = \sum m_i$ | Sum-of-minterms (canonical SOP) | KV-map input |
| 10 | $\text{Group size} = 2^k$ | KV-map grouping rule | Minimisation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real factories, the *Cascade Method* dominates in **electromechanical relay panels** (older machinery) while the **KV Map Method** dominates in **PLC programming** (Ladder Logic / Structured Text) because software memory is cheap but hardware contacts are expensive.

## 2.4 Worked Design Recipe — Cascade Method

1. **Enumerate** every distinct operational step $k = 1, 2, \dots, N$ in the sequence.
2. **Identify the conditional input** $I_k$ — the limit switch whose *true* state signals completion of the previous step.
3. **Allocate one cascade relay** $c_k$ to each step.
4. **Latch equations** for cascade chain:
   $$c_k = (I_k \cdot c_{k-1}) + (c_k \cdot \overline{c_{k+1}})$$
5. **Coil equations** for each actuator:
   $$Y_{\text{step } k} = I_k \cdot c_k$$
6. **Final step** reset: the last cascade relay must be cleared by the end-of-cycle signal (e.g., $a_0$).

## 2.5 KV-Map Minimisation Recipe

1. **Build truth table** with rows for every combination of limit switches.
2. **Mark `1`s** in rows where the coil must energise.
3. **Map** minterms onto a KV-map of size $2^n$.
4. **Group** the `1`s in pairs, quads, octets (powers of 2).
5. **Read off** the SOP expression from group coverings.
6. **Verify** interlock by OR-ing both coil expressions — they must not share an implicant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example

We design control logic for **two actuators A, B** executing sequence:

$$S = A+ \quad B+ \quad A- \quad B-$$

with the following boundary conditions:

| Boundary | Condition |
|---|---|
| **Initial state** | $a_0 = 1$ (A retracted), $b_0 = 1$ (B retracted) |
| **End state** | $a_0 = 1$, $b_0 = 1$ (cycle returns to start) |
| **Trigger** | Normally-open push button **START** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The example below is **standardised KTU textbook material** and is the canonical problem appearing in past Board papers.

---

### 3.2 Solution via Cascade Method

#### Step 1 — Enumerate the four steps

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & A+ \quad \text{triggered by: START} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & B+ \quad \text{triggered by: } a_1 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & A- \quad \text{triggered by: } b_1 \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & B- \quad \text{triggered by: } a_0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Allocate cascade relays $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$

Each $c_k$ represents "we are now in step $k$."

#### Step 3 — Write coil equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+} &= \text{START} \cdot a_0 \cdot b_0 \cdot c_1 \\
Y_{B+} &= a_1 \cdot c_2 \\
Y_{A-} &= b_1 \cdot c_3 \\
Y_{B-} &= a_0 \cdot c_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each coil may energise **only when** its step's cascade link is active *and* the necessary limit-switch condition is true.

#### Step 4 — Write cascade-chain latch equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \text{START} + (c_1 \cdot \overline{c_2}) \\
c_2 &= (a_1 \cdot c_1) + (c_2 \cdot \overline{c_3}) \\
c_3 &= (b_1 \cdot c_2) + (c_3 \cdot \overline{c_4}) \\
c_4 &= (a_0 \cdot c_3) + (c_4 \cdot \overline{c_1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $c_1$ is set by `START` and held by its own contact until $c_2$ becomes active (which resets it). This is a textbook *set-dominant latch with priority reset*.

#### Step 5 — Verify Interlock

Check that no step ever energises both $Y_{X+}$ and $Y_{X-}$:

- $Y_{A+}$ active only when $c_1=1$ ⇒ $c_2=c_3=c_4=0$ ⇒ $Y_{A-}$ (needs $c_3$) cannot fire. ✔
- $Y_{A-}$ active only when $c_3=1$ ⇒ $c_4=c_1=0$ ⇒ $Y_{A+}$ (needs $c_1$) cannot fire. ✔

---

### 3.3 Solution via Karnaugh-Veitch Map Method

#### Step 1 — Build the truth table

There are $n=4$ binary variables: $a_0, a_1, b_0, b_1$. The truth table has $2^4 = 16$ rows. Mark `1` in the rows where each coil must fire, given the sequence.

For coil $Y_{A+}$: it fires when $a_0=1, b_0=1$ AND START is pressed (we treat START as a momentary gating condition; for static minimisation we consider the *post-START* state). After START, the very first row encountered is $a_0=1, a_1=0, b_0=1, b_1=0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minterm for } Y_{A+}: \quad & m_5 \quad (a_0=1, a_1=0, b_0=1, b_1=0) \\
\text{Minterm for } Y_{B+}: \quad & m_3 \quad (a_0=0, a_1=1, b_0=1, b_1=0) \\
\text{Minterm for } Y_{A-}: \quad & m_{14} \quad (a_0=0, a_1=1, b_0=0, b_1=1) \\
\text{Minterm for } Y_{B-}: \quad & m_4 \quad (a_0=1, a_1=0, b_0=0, b_1=0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are **don't-care** transitions in many rows — the KV-map allows grouping with `X` entries, yielding *minimal* expressions.

#### Step 2 — KV-Map for $Y_{A+}$ (3-variable map on $a_0, a_1, b_0$)

Construct a $2\times4$ KV map with $a_0 a_1$ on rows and $b_0 b_1$ on columns:

| $a_0a_1 \backslash b_0b_1$ | `00` | `01` | `11` | `10` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **`00`** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **`01`** | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** |
| **`11`** | X | X | X | X |
| **`10`** | **1** | X | X | X |

**Grouping:** The `1` at $(a_0a_1=01, b_0b_1=10)$ groups with `X` at $(a_0a_1=11, b_0b_1=10)$ → cell $(01,10)$ and $(11,10)$ are adjacent in the $a_0a_1$ dimension → eliminates $a_0$.

**Reading off:** $a_1 \cdot b_0$.

The `1` at $(10,00)$ groups with `X` at $(11,00)$ and $(10,01)$ and $(11,01)$ → quadruple eliminating $a_0$ and $b_1$.

**Reading off:** $a_1 \cdot b_0 \cdot \overline{a_0}$ ... let us recheck.

Actually, the canonical KV-map for $Y_{A+}$ yields:

$$
Y_{A+} = \text{START} \cdot a_0 \cdot b_0 \cdot (\overline{a_1}) \cdot (\overline{b_1})
$$

After two-group simplification, the minimum SOP form is:

$$
\boxed{Y_{A+} = \text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $a_0$ term drops because $a_0 = \overline{a_1}$ (a single actuator cannot be simultaneously extended and retracted — they are mechanically interlocked). This is the **critical KTU observation** students often miss.

#### Step 3 — KV-Map for $Y_{B+}$

Analogous derivation:

$$
\boxed{Y_{B+} = a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1}}
$$

#### Step 4 — KV-Map for $Y_{A-}$

$$
\boxed{Y_{A-} = b_1 \cdot \overline{a_1}}
$$

#### Step 5 — KV-Map for $Y_{B-}$

$$
\boxed{Y_{B-} = a_0 \cdot \overline{b_1}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Interlock Verification

Cross-product $Y_{A+} \cdot Y_{A-}$:

$$
Y_{A+} \cdot Y_{A-} = (\text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0) \cdot (b_1 \cdot \overline{a_1}) = 0
$$

Because $b_0 \cdot b_1 = 0$ (mechanically interlocked). ✔ **Interlock satisfied.**

---

## 3.4 Comparative Results Matrix

| Coil | Cascade Method Expression | KV-Map Method (Minimised) Expression | Contact Count (KV wins) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Y_{A+}$ | $\text{START} \cdot a_0 \cdot b_0 \cdot c_1$ | $\text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0$ | **3 vs 4** |
| $Y_{B+}$ | $a_1 \cdot c_2$ | $a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1}$ | **2 vs 2** |
| $Y_{A-}$ | $b_1 \cdot c_3$ | $b_1 \cdot \overline{a_1}$ | **2 vs 2** |
| $Y_{B-}$ | $a_0 \cdot c_4$ | $a_0 \cdot \overline{b_1}$ | **2 vs 2** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KV-Map method *eliminates* the cascade chain entirely — saving 4 auxiliary relays but requiring the designer to think algebraically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Sequential Control System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a sequential control system for the canonical `A+ B+ A- B-` cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["START Push Button"] --> InpMux["Input Multiplexer - Limit Switch Aggregator"]
    LS_a0["a0 Limit Switch A retracted"] --> InpMux
    LS_a1["a1 Limit Switch A extended"] --> InpMux
    LS_b0["b0 Limit Switch B retracted"] --> InpMux
    LS_b1["b1 Limit Switch B extended"] --> InpMux

    InpMux --> LogicCore["Boolean Logic Core - Cascade or KV Map Equations"]

    LogicCore --> SolAplus["Solenoid Coil Y_A plus"]
    LogicCore --> SolAminus["Solenoid Coil Y_A minus"]
    LogicCore --> SolBplus["Solenoid Coil Y_B plus"]
    LogicCore --> SolBminus["Solenoid Coil Y_B minus"]

    SolAplus --> ValveA["DCV A Directional Control Valve"]
    SolAminus --> ValveA
    SolBplus --> ValveB["DCV B Directional Control Valve"]
    SolBminus --> ValveB

    ValveA --> CylA["Pneumatic Cylinder A"]
    ValveB --> CylB["Pneumatic Cylinder B"]

    CylA -.feedback.-> LS_a0
    CylA -.feedback.-> LS_a1
    CylB -.feedback.-> LS_b0
    CylB -.feedback.-> LS_b1
```

---

## 4.2 Cascade Chain — Step-by-Step Progression

The following state-transition diagram shows how the **cascade relay chain** advances through the four steps.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: Idle - awaiting START
    Step1: Step 1 - c1 active - A plus firing
    Step2: Step 2 - c2 active - B plus firing
    Step3: Step 3 - c3 active - A minus firing
    Step4: Step 4 - c4 active - B minus firing
    Idle --> Step1: START pressed and a0 and b0
    Step1 --> Step2: limit switch a1 closes
    Step2 --> Step3: limit switch b1 closes
    Step3 --> Step4: limit switch a0 closes
    Step4 --> Idle: limit switch b0 closes - cycle complete
```

---

## 4.3 KV-Map Visualisation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Enumerate Minterms"] --> B["Step 2 Plot on KV Grid"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Form Groups of 2 to power k"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Read SOP Implicants"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Verify Interlock Y_A+ times Y_A- equals 0"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Draw Ladder Diagram"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric (`Start`, `LS_a0`, `SolAplus`, etc.) and contain no reserved keywords. All labels are quoted plain text — no inline markdown formatting — ensuring successful compilation.

---

## 4.4 Physical-System Schematic (Block Adaptation)

Because Mermaid cannot render a true pneumatic-circuit drawing, the following block diagram captures the **functional equivalence** of a two-cylinder cascade sequence in an automated drilling cell.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sub1["Subsystem 1 - Loader Cylinder A pushes blank into drilling station"]
    Sub2["Subsystem 2 - Drill Spindle with B extends and clamps"]
    Sub3["Subsystem 3 - A retracts while drill runs"]
    Sub4["Subsystem 4 - B retracts and drill retracts - finished part ejected"]

    Sub1 -- limit switch a1 triggers --> Sub2
    Sub2 -- limit switch b1 triggers --> Sub3
    Sub3 -- limit switch a0 triggers --> Sub4
    Sub4 -- limit switch b0 triggers --> Sub1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Differentiate between *combinational control* and *sequential control* in the context of industrial automation. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Combinational Control | Sequential Control |
|---|---|---|
| Output dependency | Depends *only* on present inputs | Depends on present inputs **and** past state (memory) |
| Memory element | Not required | Mandatory (latches, cascade relays, flip-flops) |
| Example | Logic gate controlling a lamp from two switches | Conveyor start sequence: Sensor 1 → Motor A → Sensor 2 → Motor B |
| KTU typical use | Safety interlocks, simple annunciators | Multi-actuator pneumatic cells, automated assembly |

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1b]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** What is a *cascade relay chain*? Why is it called "cascade"?

**Model Answer:**
A cascade relay chain is a series of auxiliary relays $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n$ connected in a *daisy-chain* topology, where each relay $c_k$ is set by the limit-switch signal from step $k-1$ and resets when relay $c_{k+1}$ energises. The term *cascade* is borrowed from fluid mechanics — meaning a *waterfall-like* sequential progression where each stage triggers the next. Its primary engineering purpose is to **partition** a long sequence into discrete steps so that each solenoid coil is gated by exactly one chain link.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q8]**

### ✦ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**Q:** For two double-acting pneumatic cylinders **A** and **B** executing the sequence **`A+ B+ A− B−`**, with initial condition $a_0 = b_0 = 1$ and end condition $a_0 = b_0 = 1$:

**(a)** Derive the Boolean equations using the **Cascade Method** and draw the ladder diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Repeat the design using the **KV-Map Method** and show the minimised expressions. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [Cascade Method]

**Step 1:** Enumerate steps.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1: \quad & A+ \quad \text{triggered by START} \\
k=2: \quad & B+ \quad \text{triggered by } a_1 \\
k=3: \quad & A- \quad \text{triggered by } b_1 \\
k=4: \quad & B- \quad \text{triggered by } a_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Allocate cascade relays $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$.

**Step 3:** Coil equations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+} &= \text{START} \cdot a_0 \cdot b_0 \cdot c_1 \\
Y_{B+} &= a_1 \cdot c_2 \\
Y_{A-} &= b_1 \cdot c_3 \\
Y_{B-} &= a_0 \cdot c_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Cascade chain latches.

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \text{START} + (c_1 \cdot \overline{c_2}) \\
c_2 &= (a_1 \cdot c_1) + (c_2 \cdot \overline{c_3}) \\
c_3 &= (b_1 \cdot c_2) + (c_3 \cdot \overline{c_4}) \\
c_4 &= (a_0 \cdot c_3) + (c_4 \cdot \overline{c_1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Ladder diagram (textual representation for the rungs).

```
Rung 1:  |--[ START ]--[ a0 ]--[ b0 ]--[ c1 ]--+--( Y_A+ )--|
         |                                      |
         |--[ c1 ]--[/ c2 ]---------------------+

Rung 2:  |--[ a1 ]--[ c1 ]--+--( c2 )--|
         |                  |
         |--[ c2 ]--[/ c3 ]-+

Rung 3:  |--[ b1 ]--[ c2 ]--+--( c3 )--|
         |                  |
         |--[ c3 ]--[/ c4 ]-+

Rung 4:  |--[ a0 ]--[ c3 ]--+--( c4 )--|
         |                  |
         |--[ c4 ]--[/ c1 ]-+

Rung 5:  |--[ a1 ]--[ c2 ]-------------- ( Y_B+ )
Rung 6:  |--[ b1 ]--[ c3 ]-------------- ( Y_A- )
Rung 7:  |--[ a0 ]--[ c4 ]-------------- ( Y_B- )
```

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [KV-Map Method]

**Step 1:** List minterms per coil (as derived in §3.3):

| Coil | Minterm Indices | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $Y_{A+}$ | $m_5$ | $\overline{a_1}, b_0, \overline{b_1}$ |
| $Y_{B+}$ | $m_3$ | $a_1, \overline{b_1}$ |
| $Y_{A-}$ | $m_{14}$ | $b_1, \overline{a_1}$ |
| $Y_{B-}$ | $m_4$ | $a_0, \overline{b_1}$ |

**Step 2:** Apply the Boolean identity that mechanically $a_0 = \overline{a_1}$ and $b_0 = \overline{b_1}$.

**Step 3:** Minimised SOP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+} &= \text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0 \\
Y_{B+} &= a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1} \\
Y_{A-} &= b_1 \cdot \overline{a_1} \\
Y_{B-} &= a_0 \cdot \overline{b_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify interlock $Y_{A+} \cdot Y_{A-} = (\text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0) \cdot (b_1 \cdot \overline{a_1}) = 0$ because $b_0 \cdot b_1 = 0$. ✔

**Comparison Summary:** KV-map method eliminates the entire cascade chain (4 relays saved) at the cost of an additional normally-closed contact per rung. In modern PLC implementations, the KV-map form is **strictly superior**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting mechanical interlock identity** — students write both $a_0$ and $\overline{a_1}$ in the same equation, wasting 1 mark.
> 2. **Failing to include START gating** on $Y_{A+}$ only — the other coils should *not* require START (they fire from limit switches), but $Y_{A+}$ must. Many students wrongly gate all four coils. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Mis-drawing the cascade-chain latches** — examiners specifically look for the *set-dominant with priority reset* pattern $(I_k \cdot c_{k-1}) + (c_k \cdot \overline{c_{k+1}})$. Wrong latch ⇒ −3 marks.
> 4. **Missing the cycle-reset signal** — without $c_4$'s reset on $c_1$ (via $\overline{c_1}$), the chain never restarts. Examiner deducts **−2 marks** if absent.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q9]**

**Q:** For two double-acting cylinders **A** and **B** executing the sequence **`A+ A− B+ B−`**, with initial condition $a_0 = b_0 = 1$ and end condition $a_0 = b_0 = 1$:

**(a)** Design the cascade-method Boolean equations and explain the chain-latch principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify using a KV-map that no conflicting coils can energise simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) [Cascade Method for `A+ A− B+ B−`]

**Step 1:** Step enumeration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k=1: \quad & A+ \quad \text{triggered by START} \\
k=2: \quad & A- \quad \text{triggered by } a_1 \\
k=3: \quad & B+ \quad \text{triggered by } a_0 \\
k=4: \quad & B- \quad \text{triggered by } b_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Coil equations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+} &= \text{START} \cdot a_0 \cdot b_0 \cdot c_1 \\
Y_{A-} &= a_1 \cdot c_2 \\
Y_{B+} &= a_0 \cdot c_3 \\
Y_{B-} &= b_1 \cdot c_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Cascade chain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \text{START} + (c_1 \cdot \overline{c_2}) \\
c_2 &= (a_1 \cdot c_1) + (c_2 \cdot \overline{c_3}) \\
c_3 &= (a_0 \cdot c_2) + (c_3 \cdot \overline{c_4}) \\
c_4 &= (b_1 \cdot c_3) + (c_4 \cdot \overline{c_1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Chain-Latch Principle Explanation (the 7-mark content):**
Each cascade relay $c_k$ is a *bistable memory element*. It enters the *set* state when the *previous* step completes (limit switch $I_k$ closes while the previous link $c_{k-1}$ is active). It enters the *reset* state when the *next* link $c_{k+1}$ becomes active, because the holding contact $c_k \cdot \overline{c_{k+1}}$ then breaks. Thus, only one link is active at any instant, and only the corresponding actuator coil is permitted to fire. The chain forms a *one-hot encoded* finite-state machine with $N$ states for an $N$-step sequence.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) [KV-Map Interlock Verification]

Minterms for the `A+ A− B+ B−` cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+}: \quad & m_5 \rightarrow \overline{a_1}, b_0, \overline{b_1} \\
Y_{A-}: \quad & m_{10} \rightarrow a_1, b_0, \overline{b_1} \\
Y_{B+}: \quad & m_4 \rightarrow a_0, \overline{b_1}, \overline{a_1} \\
Y_{B-}: \quad & m_{14} \rightarrow a_1, b_1, \overline{a_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimisation yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+} &= \text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0 \\
Y_{A-} &= a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1} \\
Y_{B+} &= a_0 \cdot \overline{b_1} \\
Y_{B-} &= b_1 \cdot a_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interlock Verification:**

| Conflict Pair | Cross-product | Status |
|---|---|---|
| $Y_{A+} \cdot Y_{A-}$ | $\text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0 \cdot a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1}$ | **Zero** because $\overline{a_1} \cdot a_1 = 0$ ✔ |
| $Y_{B+} \cdot Y_{B-}$ | $a_0 \cdot \overline{b_1} \cdot b_1 \cdot a_1$ | **Zero** because $\overline{b_1} \cdot b_1 = 0$ ✔ |
| $Y_{A+} \cdot Y_{B-}$ | $\text{START} \cdot \overline{a_1} \cdot b_0 \cdot b_1 \cdot a_1$ | **Zero** because $b_0 \cdot b_1 = 0$ ✔ |
| $Y_{A-} \cdot Y_{B+}$ | $a_1 \cdot \overline{b_1} \cdot a_0 \cdot \overline{b_1}$ | $\overline{b_1} \cdot \overline{b_1} = \overline{b_1}$ — **NON-ZERO!** ⚠ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Hazard Detected:** In the `A+ A− B+ B−` sequence, the pairs $Y_{A-}$ and $Y_{B+}$ share the implicant $\overline{b_1}$. If $a_1 = 1, a_0 = 1, b_1 = 0$ momentarily occurs during the transition (e.g., due to switch-bounce overlap), both coils could theoretically energise simultaneously. In practice this is **physically impossible** because $a_1$ and $a_0$ cannot both be `1` on the same cylinder (mechanical interlock), but the algebraic hazard is why the **Cascade Method is often preferred for safety-critical relay panels** — it inherently prevents overlap by enforcing a strict one-hot chain.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- ✅ **Sequential control** = combinational logic **+ memory** (latches / cascade relays).
- ✅ **Three canonical sequences** for two actuators: `A+ A−`, `A+ B+ B− A−`, `A+ B+ A− B−`. Know each.
- ✅ **Limit switches** $x_0, x_1$ are the *only* feedback signals — never assume open-loop motion completion.
- ✅ **Cascade Method** uses one auxiliary relay *per step*; latch equation is always
  $c_k = (I_k \cdot c_{k-1}) + (c_k \cdot \overline{c_{k+1}})$.
- ✅ **KV-Map Method** uses *algebraic minimisation* — eliminate the cascade chain entirely.
- ✅ **Mechanical identity** $x_0 = \overline{x_1}$ simplifies expressions dramatically — **always** apply it before reading off the KV-map.
- ✅ **Interlock condition** $Y_{X+} \cdot Y_{X-} = 0$ must be algebraically proven — KTU examiners **will** ask for this verification.
- ✅ **START gating** belongs *only* on the *first* coil in the sequence; subsequent coils fire from limit switches.
- ✅ **Cycle reset:** the final cascade relay must reset the first — without this, the system latches and never restarts.
- ✅ **Hazards:** algebraically possible overlaps (like $Y_{A-} \cdot Y_{B+} = \overline{b_1} \neq 0$) must be flagged even when mechanically impossible — this is a **favourite 2-mark question**.
- ✅ **PLC translation:** each Boolean equation maps directly to a ladder-diagram rung with one coil output.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** ticket-gate at a railway station — chained dependency, no gate moves until previous gate confirms completion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Pneumatic/Electrohydraulic Automation: Symbols - Basic Electrical Elements (Relay, Solenoid, Timers), Pneumatic-Electrical Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electro Pneumatic/Electrohydraulic Automation: Symbols - Basic Electrical Elements (Relay, Solenoid, Timers), Pneumatic-Electrical Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Electro-Pneumatic Automation** is a control methodology in which electrical / electronic control signals are used to actuate, regulate, and monitor pneumatic power circuits (compressed air at typical working pressure of **4 bar to 8 bar**). It combines the logical flexibility of electrical control with the high force / high speed advantages of pneumatics, forming a hybrid mechatronic system governed by standards such as **ISO 1219-1:2012** (graphic symbols) and **ISO 1219-2:2012** (circuit diagrams).

**Electro-Hydraulic Automation** is the analogous hybrid system where electrical control signals drive hydraulic actuators working with incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) at pressures of **50 bar to 350 bar**, used in heavy-duty applications such as injection moulding, press tools, and aircraft systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – KTU 2024):** Students must be able to **identify, draw, and interpret** the standard graphical symbols of basic electrical elements (relays, solenoids, timers, switches) and the **interface (converter) symbols** that link the electrical domain to the pneumatic/hydraulic power domain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car:

- Your **brain's electrical signal** (decision to brake) is sent through **wires** to the **brake lights and brake actuators** (relays and solenoids).
- The brake pedal then pushes **hydraulic fluid** (incompressible, like water) which multiplies your foot force through Pascal's principle and stops the car.
- The **brake light switch** is a *pneumatic-to-electrical converter* — it converts a mechanical/pressure event into an electrical ON/OFF signal.

In industrial automation:

- The **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** is the "brain."
- The **relays, solenoids, timers** are the "nervous system."
- The **pneumatic / hydraulic cylinder** is the "muscle."
- The **sensors** (pressure switch, limit switch, proximity switch) are the "senses" — converting physical variables back into electrical feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Electro-Pneumatic and Electro-Hydraulic systems are **interface-driven** systems. The student must master the *boundary symbols* — the components that sit at the electrical-fluid interface — because these are the most frequently asked concepts in KTU university examinations.

### 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical

- It forms the **bridge** between the logical (electrical) and power (fluid) domains in any automated machine.
- Standardised **symbols** allow engineers worldwide to read, draw, and troubleshoot circuits irrespective of language.
- Mastering this topic enables design of **sequential control circuits** for real industrial processes (e.g., bottling plants, CNC tool changers, robotic pick-and-place).

### 1.4 Standard Governing Bodies and Metrics

- **ISO 1219-1** – Fluid power symbols and circuits
- **IEC 60617** – Graphical symbols for electrical diagrams
- **DIN 24300** – German equivalent (still widely used in textbooks)
- **Working pressure (Pneumatic):** Typically **6 bar (≈ 0.6 MPa)**
- **Working pressure (Hydraulic):** Typically **100–200 bar (10–20 MPa)**
- **Standard supply voltage (Control):** **24 V DC** (preferred in modern systems) and **230 V AC** (legacy systems)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Basic Electrical Elements Used in Electro-Pneumatic / Electro-Hydraulic Systems

The control-side (electrical) elements are broadly classified into:

1. **Input Devices (Sensors & Switches)**
   - Push buttons (NO, NC)
   - Limit switches (mechanically actuated)
   - Pressure switches (pneumatic-to-electrical converter)
   - Proximity sensors (inductive, capacitive, magnetic)
   - Selector switches

2. **Processing Devices (Logic Elements)**
   - Electromagnetic Relays
   - Timer Relays (ON-delay, OFF-delay, Star-Delta, Multifunction)
   - Counters
   - Contactors (high-power relays)
   - PLC (in modern systems)

3. **Output Devices (Actuators)**
   - Solenoid coils (for valves)
   - Solenoid-actuated directional control valves
   - Indicator lamps
   - Buzzer / Alarm

4. **Power Supply**
   - Transformer (230 V AC → 24 V AC)
   - Rectifier + Filter (24 V DC supply)
   - Battery backup

### 2.2 Standard Graphical Symbols — Basic Electrical Elements

#### 2.2.1 Push Button (Input Element)

| Type | Symbol Description | Behaviour |
|------|-------------------|-----------|
| **NO Push Button (Normally Open)** | Two parallel lines with a horizontal bar above (actuator link) | Conducting **only when pressed** |
| **NC Push Button (Normally Closed)** | Same shape but with a cross-bar (actuator link) already touching | Conducting **only when released** |
| **Push-Pull / Emergency Stop** | Mushroom-head symbol with mechanical latch | Latched OFF until manually reset |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** In ISO 1219, a *normally open* contact is drawn in its **de-energised / unactuated** state. The contact is OPEN at rest and CLOSES on actuation. Do not confuse this with electrical schematic convention where the coil's energised state may close or open the contact.

#### 2.2.2 Limit Switch

A limit switch is a mechanically actuated switch triggered by the cam/lever of a moving part. It is essentially a pneumatic-to-electrical (or mechanical-to-electrical) converter.

- **NO Limit Switch:** Contact closes when the cam strikes the lever.
- **NC Limit Switch:** Contact opens when the cam strikes the lever.
- **Changeover (CO) Limit Switch:** Has both NO and NC contacts in a single housing.

#### 2.2.3 Relay (Electromagnetic Control Element)

A **relay** is an electrically operated switch. A small current in the coil creates a magnetic field that pulls a movable armature, which in turn opens or closes one or more sets of contacts.

**Symbol Components of a Relay:**

- **Coil Symbol:** A rectangle (or a square box in ISO 1219) labelled with the coil designation (e.g., **K1**, **K2**).
- **Contact Symbols:** Identified by the coil label (e.g., **K1-NO**, **K1-NC**, **K1-CO**).

| Relay Contact Type | Symbol (ISO 1219) | Function |
|--------------------|-------------------|----------|
| **NO Contact (Make)** | Two parallel lines separated by a gap | Closes when coil is energised |
| **NC Contact (Break)** | Two parallel lines joined with a small diagonal strike | Opens when coil is energised |
| **Changeover (CO) Contact** | NO + NC with common terminal | Toggles output between two lines |

**Operating Principle (in 4 logical steps):**

1. **Energisation:** Current flows through the relay coil → magnetic flux builds up.
2. **Field Development:** Flux density $B$ reaches the *pull-in threshold* (typ. $\approx 0.3$ T for small relays).
3. **Mechanical Actuation:** Armature is attracted → contacts snap to the energised position.
4. **De-energisation:** Coil current removed → spring returns armature → contacts restore.

#### 2.2.4 Solenoid

A **solenoid** is a coil of wire wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger. When energised, the magnetic field pulls (or pushes) the plunger, converting electrical energy into linear mechanical motion. In electro-pneumatic systems, the solenoid plunger is mechanically coupled to the **spool of a directional control valve**.

**Solenoid Symbol (ISO 1219):**
- A **diagonal arrow** through a rectangle (or a slanted line) on the side of the valve symbol.
- The arrow indicates the direction of thrust produced by the solenoid.
- A solenoid with **two arrows** indicates a **bi-stable** valve (latching).
- A **spring return** on the opposite side is shown as a zig-zag spring.

**Force Generated by a Solenoid:**

$$F = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Axial magnetic force (N)
- $B$ = Magnetic flux density (T)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the plunger ($m^2$)
- $\mu_0$ = Permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; H/m$

For a long solenoid with $N$ turns and current $I$:

$$B = \mu_0 \cdot \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$

So the force becomes:

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot L^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Observation:** Solenoid force is **proportional to the SQUARE of the current** ($F \propto I^2$). This is why AC and DC solenoids behave differently — AC produces oscillating force at $2f$ (i.e., 100 Hz for 50 Hz supply), while DC produces steady pull. This is a frequent Part A question.

#### 2.2.5 Timers (Time Delay Relays)

Timers introduce a **delayed switching action** to the control circuit. They are essential in sequential automation (e.g., dwell time between operations, cooling time, lubrication time).

| Timer Type | Symbol Notation | Behaviour |
|------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **ON-Delay (TDE - Time Delay Energise)** | $\boxed{\text{T}}$ with a clock symbol on a NO contact | Contact closes **after** a preset delay $T$ once coil is energised |
| **OFF-Delay (TDD - Time Delay De-energise)** | $\boxed{\text{T}}$ with a clock on an NC contact; or with a diagonal strike on the contact | Contact opens **after** a preset delay once coil is de-energised |
| **Star-Delta Timer** | Specific sub-type with two outputs: Star (Y) and Delta (Δ) | Used for motor starting |
| **Multifunction Timer** | Selector switch to choose ON/OFF/Flashing modes | Configurable behaviour |

**Time Delay for RC-Type Timer:**

The fundamental RC time constant:

$$\tau = R \cdot C$$

The standard time delay (to reach 63.2% of final value) is one time constant. The total time delay is:

$$t_d = k \cdot R \cdot C$$

Where $k$ is a multiplier (typically $k = 1$ to $1.5$ depending on the trigger threshold of the comparator inside the timer).

### 2.3 Pneumatic-to-Electrical Converters (Interface Sensors)

These are the components that **convert a fluid-domain physical quantity into an electrical signal** for feedback to the control system.

| Converter Type | Physical Input | Electrical Output | Symbol |
|----------------|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Pressure Switch** | Pneumatic pressure (bar) | SPDT contact (NO/NC) | Circle with spring-loaded diaphragm + electrical contact |
| **Limit Switch** | Mechanical position | SPDT contact | Two parallel lines with hinge and roller |
| **Proximity Sensor (Inductive)** | Metal target approach | Solid-state NO/NC | Rectangle with oscillator waves |
| **Reed Switch (Magnetic)** | Magnetic field (from cylinder piston magnet) | SPDT contact | Rectangle with magnet symbol |
| **Flow Switch** | Fluid flow rate | SPDT contact | Circle with flow arrow + paddle |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Solenoid Magnetic Flux Density | $B = \mu_0 \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | T | Calculate pull force |
| 2 | Solenoid Force | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 L^2}$ | N | Solenoid sizing |
| 3 | Air Consumption (Pneumatic) | $Q = V \cdot n \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{abs}}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$ | $m^3/\text{min}$ | Sizing compressor |
| 4 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{600}$ | kW | Hydraulic drive design |
| 5 | Cylinder Thrust | $F = p \cdot A - p' \cdot A'$ | N | Actuator sizing |
| 6 | RC Time Constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | s | Timer delay |
| 7 | Timer Delay (Comparator Triggered) | $t_d = k \cdot R \cdot C$ | s | ON/OFF delay design |
| 8 | Inductive Reactance (AC Solenoid) | $X_L = 2 \pi f L$ | $\Omega$ | AC coil design |
| 9 | Power Dissipated in Coil | $P = I^2 \cdot R = V \cdot I$ | W | Heat calculation |
| 10 | Pull-In Voltage | $V_{\text{pull-in}} \approx 0.7 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}$ | V | Relay design margin |
| 11 | Drop-Out Voltage | $V_{\text{drop-out}} \approx 0.3 \cdot V_{\text{rated}}$ | V | Relay de-energisation |
| 12 | Ohm's Law (Coil) | $V = I \cdot R$ | V | Circuit analysis |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Solenoid Pull Force (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the axial force produced by a solenoid of $N$ turns, length $L$, carrying current $I$, acting on a plunger of cross-sectional area $A$.

**Step 1 — Establish the magnetic field inside an ideal long solenoid.**

By Ampère's law, for a long solenoid of length $L$ with $N$ turns carrying current $I$:

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I$$

For an ideal solenoid, the field is uniform inside and zero outside. The line integral over the closed path equals $B \cdot L$:

$$B \cdot L = \mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I$$

Solving for the magnetic flux density $B$:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Express the magnetic energy stored per unit volume.**

The energy density (energy per unit volume) of a magnetic field is given by:

$$u = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 3 — Consider the plunger displacement.**

When the plunger moves a small distance $dx$ into the solenoid, the volume of the magnetic field that gets "filled" with ferromagnetic material increases by $A \cdot dx$. The change in magnetic energy stored in this volume is:

$$dW = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A \cdot dx$$

**Step 4 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**

The mechanical work done by the magnetic force $F$ as the plunger moves a distance $dx$ is:

$$F \cdot dx = dW = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0} \cdot dx$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the magnetic force.**

Cancelling $dx$ from both sides:

$$F = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $B$ from Step 1.**

$$F = \frac{1}{2 \mu_0} \cdot \left( \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{L} \right)^2 \cdot A$$

Expanding the square:

$$F = \frac{1}{2 \mu_0} \cdot \frac{\mu_0^2 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2}{L^2} \cdot A$$

Simplifying by cancelling one $\mu_0$:

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 L^2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 L^2}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The force is **quadratically proportional to the current**, **quadratically proportional to the number of turns**, and **inversely proportional to the square of the solenoid length**. Designers therefore prefer **short, fat coils with many turns** for high-force applications such as valve actuators.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Delay of an ON-Delay Timer (RC Network)

**Statement:** A timer uses an RC network where $R = 100 \; k\Omega$ and $C = 10 \; \mu F$. The comparator triggers when the capacitor voltage reaches $V_{th} = 0.632 \cdot V_{cc}$. Find the time delay.

**Step 1 — Write the standard charging equation for a capacitor.**

For a capacitor charging from 0 V to a supply $V_{cc}$ through resistance $R$, the instantaneous voltage is:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the time constant.**

$$\tau = R \cdot C = (100 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6}) = 1 \; s$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the trigger threshold condition.**

At the trigger instant $t = t_d$:

$$V_{th} = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_d / \tau}\right)$$

$$0.632 \cdot V_{cc} = V_{cc} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_d / \tau}\right)$$

$$0.632 = 1 - e^{-t_d / \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the exponential term.**

$$e^{-t_d / \tau} = 1 - 0.632 = 0.368$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-t_d / \tau = \ln(0.368) = -1.0$$

$$t_d = 1.0 \cdot \tau$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\tau = 1 \; s$.**

$$\boxed{t_d = 1 \; \text{second}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A comparator triggering at 63.2% of the supply voltage produces a delay of exactly **one time constant $\tau$**. If the trigger threshold is at 95% of $V_{cc}$ (a common design), the delay becomes $t_d = 3\tau$ because $1 - e^{-3} \approx 0.95$. **Memorise: 1$\tau$ = 63.2%, 3$\tau$ = 95%, 5$\tau$ = 99.3%.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pull-In and Drop-Out Voltage of a Relay

**Statement:** A relay coil is rated at $V_{\text{rated}} = 24 \; V \; DC$, with coil resistance $R_c = 960 \; \Omega$. Find the pull-in and drop-out voltages.

**Step 1 — Rated current at rated voltage.**

$$I_{\text{rated}} = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{R_c} = \frac{24}{960} = 0.025 \; A = 25 \; mA$$

**Step 2 — Pull-in voltage (empirical rule).**

The relay armature begins to move when flux reaches a critical value. The pull-in voltage is approximately **70%** of the rated voltage:

$$V_{\text{pull-in}} \approx 0.7 \cdot V_{\text{rated}} = 0.7 \cdot 24 = 16.8 \; V$$

**Step 3 — Drop-out voltage (empirical rule).**

The relay armature releases when the spring force overcomes the weakened magnetic pull. The drop-out voltage is approximately **30%** of the rated voltage:

$$V_{\text{drop-out}} \approx 0.3 \cdot V_{\text{rated}} = 0.3 \cdot 24 = 7.2 \; V$$

**Step 4 — Operating margin calculation.**

$$V_{\text{operating}} = V_{\text{rated}} - V_{\text{pull-in}} = 24 - 16.8 = 7.2 \; V$$

This **7.2 V** margin ensures reliable operation even with supply voltage dips (e.g., during motor inrush).

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Electro-Pneumatic automation. List any four advantages of electro-pneumatic systems over pure pneumatic systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Electro-Pneumatic automation is a control technique in which **electrical / electronic control signals** are used to operate pneumatic power elements (such as cylinders, valves) to perform a desired mechanical work sequence. The electrical side handles logic, while the pneumatic side delivers power.

**Four Advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Long-distance signal transmission** — Electrical signals can travel hundreds of metres without significant loss, whereas pneumatic signals suffer from transmission lag.
2. **Complex logic implementation** — Relays, timers, counters, and PLCs can implement Boolean logic, sequencing, and counting easily.
3. **No exhaust air consumption in control circuit** — Pure pneumatics uses air continuously for shifting; electro-pneumatics uses air only at the actuator.
4. **Easier integration with sensors** — Standardised electrical sensor outputs (24 V DC) interface directly with PLCs and relays, enabling closed-loop control.

**Additional Advantage (if asked):** Faster response, lower noise in the control room, easier fault diagnosis with indicator lamps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Explain the working principle of a solenoid with a neat sketch. State the relationship between solenoid force and current.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
A solenoid consists of a helical coil of insulated copper wire wound around a hollow cylindrical former. Inside the former is a soft-iron plunger held by a spring. When DC current is passed through the coil, a magnetic field is established along the axis. The magnetic flux lines prefer to pass through the iron plunger (low reluctance path) rather than air, thereby exerting a strong axial **pulling force** on the plunger toward the centre of the coil. The plunger moves against the spring and performs mechanical work — typically shifting the spool of a directional control valve. When the current is switched off, the spring pushes the plunger back to its original position.

**Symbol:** A rectangle with a diagonal arrow on the side of the valve block.

**Force-Current Relationship (1 Mark):**

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 L^2}$$

Therefore, the force is **directly proportional to the square of the current** ($F \propto I^2$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of standard ISO 1219 symbols, explain the construction and working of a **5/2-way directional control valve (DCV) with single solenoid actuation and spring return**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design a simple electro-pneumatic circuit for the following sequence: A double-acting cylinder must extend only when push button PB1 is pressed **AND** the cylinder is at the retracted end (limit switch LS1 = ON). It must retract automatically after a **3-second dwell** at the extended end. Use a timer and draw the circuit in **ladder diagram format**. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 5/2 DCV with Single Solenoid + Spring Return (7 Marks)**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A 5/2 DCV has **5 ports** and **2 distinct positions**. The five ports are:
- **Port 1** — Compressed air supply (P) from FRL unit
- **Port 2** — Working port A (cylinder cap-end)
- **Port 3** — Working port B (cylinder rod-end)
- **Port 4** — Exhaust port R (from cap-end side)
- **Port 5** — Exhaust port S (from rod-end side)

The valve body houses a **sliding spool** that can take two extreme positions. The spool is moved by a **solenoid (Y1)** on one side and a **return spring** on the other side.

**Two Positions:**

- **Position 1 (Normal — Spring side, Solenoid de-energised):** Port 1 connects to Port 3 (rod-end), and Port 2 connects to Port 4 (exhaust). The cylinder retracts (or holds retracted).
- **Position 2 (Actuated — Solenoid energised):** Port 1 connects to Port 2 (cap-end), and Port 3 connects to Port 5 (exhaust). The cylinder extends.

**Working (2 Marks):**
When **Y1 is energised** (24 V DC applied), the solenoid plunger pulls the spool against the spring to Position 2. Compressed air flows from Port 1 → Port 2 → cylinder cap-end, extending the piston. The displaced air from the rod-end exits via Port 3 → Port 5.

When **Y1 is de-energised**, the spring pushes the spool back to Position 1. Compressed air flows from Port 1 → Port 3 → rod-end, retracting the piston. The displaced air from the cap-end exits via Port 2 → Port 4.

**Symbol (2 Marks):**
- Two adjacent square boxes (one for each position).
- Arrows inside the boxes show the air path connections.
- A **diagonal arrow** on the right side of one box (solenoid) and a **spring symbol** on the left side of the other box.
- L1, L2 labels for working ports and 1, 3, 5 labels for supply/exhaust.

---

**(b) Ladder Diagram for Auto-Retract with 3 s Dwell (7 Marks)**

**Components Used:**
- PB1 — Push button (NO), input address I0.0
- LS1 — Limit switch at retracted end (NO), input I0.1
- LS2 — Limit switch at extended end (NO), input I0.2
- K1 — Relay (latching) for extension command
- T1 — ON-Delay Timer, preset 3 s
- Y1 — Solenoid of 5/2 DCV, output Q0.0

**Rung 1 — Latch K1:**

```
|     PB1     LS1     K1 (NO contact)              K1 (coil)         |
|----[ ]------[ ]------+----------+----------------( )--------------|
                          |     LS2 (NC)             |
                          +----[ / ]------------------+
```

**Explanation:** K1 latches when PB1 is pressed AND LS1 is active (cylinder is at retracted end). The seal-in contact K1-NO holds K1 energised. The NC contact LS2 breaks the latch when the cylinder reaches the extended end.

**Rung 2 — Start Timer T1 when extended:**

```
|     K1 (NO)    LS2 (NO)              T1 (coil, preset 3 s)    |
|----[ ]---------[ ]------------------( TON )---------------------|
```

**Explanation:** Timer T1 starts counting when K1 is ON AND the cylinder reaches the extended end (LS2 closes).

**Rung 3 — Energise Solenoid Y1 while K1 is ON; de-energise on Timer done:**

```
|        K1 (NO)      T1 (NC)                Y1 (solenoid)      |
|------[ ]------------[ / ]+----------------( )------------------|
```

**Explanation:** Y1 is energised (cylinder extends) as long as K1 is latched. When T1 completes its 3 s preset, the T1-NC contact opens, breaking K1's hold path, dropping K1, and the spring returns the cylinder to retracted position.

**Sequence Summary:**
1. Press PB1 (with LS1 already ON) → K1 latches → Y1 ON → cylinder extends.
2. Cylinder reaches extended end → LS2 closes → T1 starts.
3. After 3 s → T1-NC opens → K1 drops → Y1 OFF → cylinder retracts.
4. Cylinder reaches retracted end → LS1 closes → ready for next cycle.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Component selection & reasoning: 2 marks
- Ladder logic drawing: 3 marks
- Sequence explanation: 2 marks

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the construction, working, and ISO 1219 symbol of a **5/3-way directional control valve with two solenoid actuators (bi-stable, centre-blocked)**. State **two industrial applications** of such a valve. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **pneumatic-to-electrical pressure switch**. How is it used as a feedback element in an electro-pneumatic clamping system? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 5/3 DCV with Two Solenoids — Centre-Blocked Bi-Stable (7 Marks)**

**Construction (2 Marks):**
A 5/3 DCV has 5 ports and 3 positions. The centre position is normally **blocked** (no port connected to any other). The valve has **two solenoids (Y1 and Y2)** — one on each end — but no return spring. It is therefore **bi-stable**: it stays in the last commanded position even after power is removed.

**Port Layout:**
- Port 1: P (Supply)
- Port 2: A (Cap-end)
- Port 3: B (Rod-end)
- Port 4: R (Exhaust from A)
- Port 5: S (Exhaust from B)

**Three Positions:**

- **Position 1 (Left — Y1 energised):** Port 1 → Port 2 (extend); Port 3 → Port 5 (exhaust).
- **Centre (Y1 OFF, Y2 OFF — Blocked):** All ports are sealed. Cylinder is hydraulically locked in place.
- **Position 2 (Right — Y2 energised):** Port 1 → Port 3 (retract); Port 2 → Port 4 (exhaust).

**Symbol (2 Marks):**
Three adjacent square boxes. The middle box shows **blocked connections** (T symbols with no flow arrows). The left and right boxes show flow arrows. Two diagonal solenoid arrows on the outer sides (one each side). No spring symbol.

**Working (1 Mark):**
- Energise Y1 → spool shifts left → cylinder extends.
- De-energise Y1 → spool stays in extended position (latched by detent or by trapped pilot pressure).
- Energise Y2 → spool shifts right → cylinder retracts.
- De-energise Y2 → spool stays in retracted position.

**Two Industrial Applications (2 Marks — 1 each):**
1. **Hydraulic press tool clamping system** — the centre-blocked position holds the tool firmly without continuous power, saving energy and providing safety.
2. **Robotic gripper / pick-and-place actuator** — the valve maintains the grip on the workpiece even during power failure, ensuring safety in collaborative robotics.

---

**(b) Pneumatic-to-Electrical Pressure Switch — Operation & Clamping System Use (7 Marks)**

**Construction (2 Marks):**
A pressure switch consists of a **diaphragm** (or bellows) housed in a metal casing. The diaphragm separates the air pressure inlet (pneumatic side) from the electrical contact mechanism. A **spring-loaded plunger** rests against the diaphragm and is connected to a **snap-action SPDT electrical switch**. An **adjustment screw** sets the switching pressure.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- When the air pressure in the sensing port **rises above** the set threshold, the diaphragm deflects against the spring, pushing the plunger. The plunger trips the snap-action switch, closing the NO contact (and opening the NC contact).
- When the pressure **falls below** the set threshold (minus the hysteresis band), the spring pushes the diaphragm back, and the contacts restore.

The **hysteresis** (typically 0.5–2 bar) prevents rapid chattering of the contacts near the setpoint.

**ISO Symbol:** A circle representing the diaphragm with a spring + NO/NC electrical contact line emerging from the top.

**Use as Feedback in a Clamping System (3 Marks):**

Consider a workpiece-clamping application in a CNC milling machine:

1. The hydraulic cylinder extends to clamp the workpiece. A pressure switch (PS1) is connected to the **clamp-side line**.
2. As the cylinder extends, the line pressure rises. When it reaches the set value (e.g., **50 bar**), the PS1 contact closes.
3. The PS1 contact sends a **24 V DC signal** to the PLC input I0.5.
4. The PLC verifies that the clamp is firm **before** allowing the spindle motor to start.
5. If the pressure drops below the threshold (e.g., due to a leak), the PS1 contact opens, the PLC detects the fault, and the spindle is **immediately stopped** — a critical safety interlock.

**Block Diagram of Feedback Loop:**

```
Hydraulic Cylinder → Pressure Line → Pressure Switch PS1 → PLC Input → PLC Logic → Output to Spindle Drive
```

This **closed-loop safety architecture** is mandatory in modern FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems) per ISO 12100 and EN 60204-1.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Construction & working of pressure switch: 2 marks
- Symbol & hysteresis: 1 mark
- Clamping system use-case: 4 marks (sequence, safety interlock, PLC feedback, diagram)

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Scheme Electro-Pneumatic Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to label the solenoid terminal letters (e.g., Y1, Y2)** on valve symbols — **0.5 to 1 mark** is routinely lost.
> 2. **Drawing relays with wrong contact orientation** — the contact is shown in the *de-energised* state, not the energised state. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Mixing ISO 1219 (fluid power) and IEC 60617 (electrical) symbols** in the same circuit without a clear demarcation. Always follow ISO 1219 for fluid power, IEC 60617 for electrical.
> 4. **Stating the formula for solenoid force without the constant $\mu_0$ and units** — full 1 mark is lost if units are missing.
> 5. **Omitting the hysteresis value** in pressure switch answers. The hysteresis is what prevents contact chattering and is a frequent sub-question.
> 6. **In ladder diagrams, forgetting the seal-in (latching) contact** — the momentary push button cannot hold the relay continuously; the K1 NO contact in parallel with PB1 is essential.
> 7. **Confusing ON-delay with OFF-delay timer symbols** — the diagonal strike on the contact is the only visual clue.
> 8. **Forgetting to convert bar → Pa** in numerical problems. $1 \; \text{bar} = 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electro-Pneumatic Automation** = electrical control + pneumatic power. **Electro-Hydraulic** = electrical control + hydraulic power.
- The **standard governing body** is **ISO 1219-1/-2** for fluid power and **IEC 60617** for electrical.
- **Relay coil symbol** is a rectangle labelled K1/K2. **Contact symbols** are identified by the same label.
- **Solenoid force is $F \propto I^2$** — this quadratic dependence is critical for both design and protection (inrush current at start).
- **Pull-in voltage ≈ 0.7 × V_rated; Drop-out voltage ≈ 0.3 × V_rated** — empirical rules for DC relays.
- **Timers:** ON-Delay = contact closes **after** delay; OFF-Delay = contact opens **after** delay.
- **Time constant $\tau = RC$**; 1$\tau$ = 63.2%, 3$\tau$ = 95%, 5$\tau$ = 99.3% (memorise this).
- **5/2 DCV** = 5 ports, 2 positions. **5/3 DCV** = 5 ports, 3 positions (centre blocked or centre exhaust or centre tandem).
- **Solenoid + Spring** = mono-stable (returns on power failure). **Two Solenoids** = bi-stable (stays in last position).
- **Pneumatic-to-Electrical Converters** = Pressure Switch, Limit Switch, Reed Switch, Proximity Sensor, Flow Switch.
- **ISO 1219 Symbols must be drawn in DE-ENERGISED / UNACTUATED state** — this is the most violated rule in KTU answer sheets.
- **Ladder diagrams use NO contacts `[ ]`, NC contacts `[ / ]`, and coils `( )` as standard symbols.**
- **Working pressure:** Pneumatic = **6 bar typical**; Hydraulic = **100–200 bar typical**.
- **Control voltage:** Modern = **24 V DC**; Legacy = **230 V AC**.
- **Solenoid symbol on valve:** **Diagonal arrow** on the side where thrust is applied; **spring** shown on the opposite side.
- **Pressure switch hysteresis** prevents chatter; typically **0.5–2 bar** depending on rating.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Circuits and Hands-on Models on Material Handling Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Circuits and Hands-on Models on Material Handling Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Material Handling System?

A **Material Handling System (MHS)** in industrial automation is an integrated arrangement of mechanical, electrical, electronic, and control subsystems used to move, protect, store, and control materials and products throughout the manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, consumption, and disposal stages of production.

According to the KTU 2024 OER syllabus, the design of circuits and hands-on models for MHS involves the application of **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)**, **ladder logic diagrams (LLD)**, **pneumatic circuits**, **sensors**, and **actuators** to automate the flow of goods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A material handling system is a combination of equipment, processes, and control logic designed to carry, transfer, sort, or position workpieces or products automatically, using sensors for feedback and actuators (motors, cylinders, conveyors) for execution, all orchestrated by a central controller (PLC or microcontroller).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory Highway"

Imagine a **bottling plant** where empty bottles travel on a moving belt, get filled with water, capped, labeled, and finally packed into boxes.

- The **conveyor belt** is like a *highway* carrying goods.
- The **sensors** (proximity, optical, limit switches) act as *traffic police*, detecting what's there.
- The **actuators** (motors, solenoid valves, pneumatic cylinders) are the *vehicles* that perform the work.
- The **PLC ladder logic** is the *traffic control room* — it sees the inputs and decides the outputs.

Without automation, a human would press every button. With an automated MHS circuit, the **PLC receives sensor signals, evaluates the ladder program cycle-by-cycle**, and energizes the appropriate output coils within milliseconds (typical scan time: **5–20 ms**).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Material Handling Operations

| Operation Type | Real-World Example | Core Components Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Conveying** | Bottles on a belt | DC/AC motor, conveyor, IR sensor |
| **Sorting** | Parcel routing by destination | Proximity sensor, pneumatic push cylinder, diverter |
| **Pick & Place** | Robotic arm transfers part | Servo/stepper motor, pneumatic gripper, solenoid valve |
| **Storage/Retrieval** | AS/RS warehouse | Stacker crane, encoder, limit switches |
| **Packaging** | Box sealing and palletizing | Cylinder, heating element, conveyor |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metrics in MHS design are: **throughput (units/hour)**, **cycle time (seconds)**, **positioning accuracy (mm)**, and **repeatability (±0.1 mm typical for servo systems)**.

---

## 1.4 Why Design Circuits for MHS?

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic builds the following Course Outcomes (CO):
- **CO3:** *Design and develop ladder logic programs and pneumatic/electrical control circuits for industrial material handling applications.*
- **CO4:** *Implement and test hands-on models demonstrating sensor–actuator–controller integration.*

Hands-on models bridge the gap between the **theoretical study of PLC programming** and the **real industrial shop floor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Material Handling Control Circuit

Every MHS control circuit has a **3-block architecture**:

### Block A — Input Section (Sensors & Switches)
Converts physical phenomena into electrical signals.
- **Push Buttons (PB):** NO / NC contacts, manually operated
- **Limit Switches (LS):** Mechanical contact, used for end-of-travel
- **Inductive Proximity Sensor:** Detects metallic objects (range: **2–15 mm**)
- **Capacitive Proximity Sensor:** Detects any material (plastic, liquid, metal)
- **Photoelectric / IR Sensor:** Detects presence using light beam
- **Encoder:** Provides rotational position/velocity feedback (PPR: **100–10,000**)

### Block B — Controller (PLC / Relay Logic)
Executes a stored ladder program every scan cycle.

**PLC Scan Cycle Equation:**

$$T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$$

Where:
- $T_{input}$ = Time to read all input status into the **Input Image Table (IIT)** (typically **1–3 ms** for 16 I/O)
- $T_{program}$ = Time to execute user logic rung-by-rung
- $T_{output}$ = Time to write Output Image Table to physical outputs
- $T_{housekeeping}$ = Diagnostics, communication overhead

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** Total $T_{scan}$ must be **less than** the smallest process event interval. If the conveyor moves a part every 200 ms, $T_{scan} < 200$ ms is mandatory, else the PLC will **miss events**.

### Block C — Output Section (Actuators)
Converts electrical signals into mechanical work.
- **Contactor / Motor Starter:** Switches 3-phase induction motor
- **Solenoid Valve (5/2, 3/2):** Controls pneumatic cylinder direction
- **Relay Coil:** Energizes auxiliary circuits
- **Indicator Lamp / Hooter:** Provides operator feedback

---

## 2.2 Ladder Logic Design Rules (KTU Board Standard)

1. **Power rails:** Vertical lines on the left (L) and right (N or L2).
2. **Rungs:** Horizontal lines containing inputs (contacts) on the left and outputs (coils) on the right.
3. **Inputs:** Represented as **Normally Open (NO)** `--| |--` or **Normally Closed (NC)** `--|/|--` contacts.
4. **Outputs:** Represented as **coils** `--( )--`.
5. **Reading order:** Top-to-bottom, left-to-right.
6. **PLC Addressing convention (Siemens S7-200 / Allen-Bradley):**

| Address | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `I0.0` or `X0` | Digital Input, byte 0, bit 0 |
| `Q0.1` or `Y1` | Digital Output, byte 0, bit 1 |
| `M0.0` or `M100` | Internal Memory / Marker / Hold bit |
| `T37` or `T200` | Timer (ON-delay, 100 ms resolution) |
| `C0` or `C1` | Counter (CTU/CTD) |
| `AIW0` | Analog Input Word |
| `AQW0` | Analog Output Word |

> [!CAUTION]
> In **ladder logic, the NC contact is *drawn* as `|/|`, but its *function* depends on the physical wiring.** A field device wired NC will use a `|/|` symbol and is TRUE when the input is *not* activated. This is a common KTU exam pitfall.

---

## 2.3 Pneumatic Circuit Design — Key Principles

A pneumatic MHS uses **compressed air (typically 6 bar / 600 kPa)** to drive cylinders.

**Force generated by a pneumatic cylinder:**

$$F = P \times A = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2$$

Where:
- $F$ = Thrust force in **Newtons (N)**
- $P$ = Air pressure in **N/m²** (1 bar = $10^5$ N/m²)
- $A$ = Piston area in **m²**
- $D$ = Piston bore diameter in **m**

**Cylinder speed approximation:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

Where $Q$ = air flow rate in **m³/s**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Use Case | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | PLC response limit | ms |
| 2 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Cylinder force | N |
| 3 | $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Piston area | m² |
| 4 | $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Cylinder velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $N_{stops} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Pitch}}$ | Conveyor indexing | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f_{encoder} = \frac{N_{rpm} \times PPR}{60}$ | Encoder pulse rate | Hz |
| 7 | $T_{cycle} = T_{forward} + T_{return} + T_{dwell}$ | Pick & place cycle | s |
| 8 | $T_{ON-Delay} = PT \times 0.1$ | TON timer (S7-200) | seconds |
| 9 | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$ | Parts per hour | units/hr |
| 10 | $P_{elec} = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | Motor power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** For S7-200 PLC, the preset value (PT) in a TON timer is multiplied by the **time base** (10 ms, 100 ms, or 1 s depending on timer number T32–T96 range) to get actual delay.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of MHS Circuit Design

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Body-in-white transfer, robotic welding cell sequencing.
- **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods):** Bottle filling, capping, labeling, shrink-wrapping.
- **Warehousing:** Amazon-style Kiva robots, conveyor sortation hubs.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Vial inspection, blister packaging with vision-guided rejection.
- **E-commerce:** Dimensioning, weighing, and label-apply stations.

Mastering circuit design for MHS makes a graduate employable as an **Automation Engineer**, **PLC Programmer**, or **Control Systems Integrator**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pneumatic Cylinder Force at Working Pressure

**Problem:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has bore $D = 50$ mm and rod diameter $d = 20$ mm. Working pressure $P = 6$ bar. Calculate the **forward thrust** and **return thrust** of the cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> 1 bar = $10^5$ N/m². We will convert all dimensions to SI (meters) before computing.

**Step 1 — Convert dimensions to SI units**

$$D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

$$d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Convert pressure to SI units**

$$P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate full piston area (forward stroke)**

$$A_{forward} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{forward} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate annular area (return stroke, rod side)**

$$A_{return} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times ((0.05)^2 - (0.02)^2)}{4}$$

$$A_{return} = \frac{\pi \times (0.0025 - 0.0004)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0021}{4} = 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Calculate forward thrust force**

$$F_{forward} = P \times A_{forward} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{forward} = 1178.1 \text{ N} \approx 1.18 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate return thrust force**

$$F_{return} = P \times A_{return} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.6493 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{return} = 989.6 \text{ N} \approx 0.99 \text{ kN}$$

> [!TIP]
> The return thrust is always **less than** forward thrust because the rod side has less effective area. This is why designers use a *regenerative circuit* to equalize the speed in both directions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conveyor Throughput Calculation

**Problem:** A conveyor carries 24 parts in 1 minute, with each part occupying 200 mm on the belt. Belt speed $v = 0.5$ m/s. Verify the throughput using the formula and find the required motor RPM if the driving pulley has diameter $D_{pulley} = 100$ mm.

**Step 1 — Measured throughput**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{24 \text{ parts}}{60 \text{ s}} = 0.4 \text{ parts/s}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical time per part (spacing/speed)**

$$T_{part} = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{v} = \frac{0.2 \text{ m}}{0.5 \text{ m/s}} = 0.4 \text{ s/part}$$

This matches 0.4 parts/s ✓ (verification successful)

**Step 3 — Throughput in parts per hour**

$$\text{Throughput}_{hr} = 0.4 \times 3600 = 1440 \text{ parts/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Pulley circumference**

$$C = \pi \times D_{pulley} = \pi \times 0.1 = 0.3142 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Motor RPM from belt linear speed**

$$v = \pi \times D_{pulley} \times N_{rpm} \div 60$$

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{v \times 60}{\pi \times D_{pulley}} = \frac{0.5 \times 60}{\pi \times 0.1}$$

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{30}{0.3142} = 95.49 \text{ rpm} \approx 96 \text{ rpm}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Hands-on MHS Models

| Parameter | Conveyor Sorting | Pick & Place Robot | Bottle Filling Line | AGV Pallet Mover |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Main Sensor** | IR proximity | Limit switches | Level sensor | Floor magnetic tape |
| **Main Actuator** | DC motor + pneumatic push | Servo + pneumatic grip | Pump + solenoid valve | DC gear motor + steer |
| **PLC I/O count** | 8 DI / 6 DO | 12 DI / 8 DO | 10 DI / 8 DO | 6 DI / 4 DO |
| **Ladder rungs** | ~12 | ~25 | ~18 | ~10 |
| **Cycle time** | 2 s | 4 s | 3 s | 6 s |
| **Throughput** | 1800 parts/hr | 900 parts/hr | 1200 bottles/hr | 600 pallets/hr |
| **Difficulty level (KTU lab)** | Easy | Medium | Medium | Hard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Conveyor Sorting** model is the most commonly asked in KTU lab exams and semester papers because it uses a minimal but complete subset of concepts: 1 sensor, 1 actuator, 1 timer, 1 counter, 1 output coil.

---

## 3.4 Design Algorithm for a Generic MHS Circuit (Step-by-Step)

Below is the systematic method a KTU student must follow when designing a hands-on model:

1. **Define the problem statement** — e.g., "Sort metal from non-metal parts on a conveyor".
2. **List inputs (I) and outputs (Q)** with addresses.
3. **Draw the I/O assignment table.**
4. **Develop the process flowchart** — sequence of operations.
5. **Convert flowchart to ladder rungs** — one rung per state transition.
6. **Add safety interlocks** — Emergency Stop (E-Stop) as a **hardwired NC** in series with all outputs.
7. **Assign memory bits (M)** for sequential steps.
8. **Include timers and counters** for time delays and event counting.
9. **Simulate** using Ladder Diagram software (e.g., LOGO! Soft Comfort, PLC Ladder Simulator, or TIA Portal).
10. **Wire to physical PLC and test on the kit.**
11. **Document** with an I/O list, program listing, and operation description.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Material Handling System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[POWER SUPPLY 24V DC / 230V AC] --> nodeB[PLC UNIT]
    nodeC[PUSH BUTTON START NO] --> nodeB
    nodeD[PUSH BUTTON STOP NC] --> nodeB
    nodeE[PROXIMITY SENSOR METAL DETECT] --> nodeB
    nodeF[PHOTOELECTRIC SENSOR OBJECT PRESENT] --> nodeB
    nodeG[LIMIT SWITCH CYLINDER HOME] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeH[MOTOR CONTACTOR CONVEYOR]
    nodeB --> nodeI[SOLENOID VALVE 5 2 PNEUMATIC PUSH]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[GREEN LAMP RUN]
    nodeB --> nodeK[RED LAMP STOP]
    nodeB --> nodeL[HOOTER FAULT]
    nodeH --> nodeM[CONVEYOR BELT]
    nodeI --> nodeN[PNEUMATIC CYLINDER DIVERTER]
    nodeM --> nodeO[SORTED PART BIN A]
    nodeN --> nodeP[SORTED PART BIN B]
```

---

## 4.2 Ladder Logic Diagram — Conveyor Sorting (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RUNG1["RUNG 1  START SEAL IN"]
        A1[PB_START NO] --> A2[M0 RUN LATCH]
        A3[PB_STOP NC] -.interrupts.-> A2
    end
    subgraph RUNG2["RUNG 2  CONVEYOR MOTOR"]
        B1[M0 RUN] --> B2[Q0 CONVEYOR MOTOR]
    end
    subgraph RUNG3["RUNG 3  PART DETECTED COUNT"]
        C1[IR_SENSOR NO] --> C2[CTU COUNTER C0]
        C2 --> C3[Q1 INDICATOR LAMP]
    end
    subgraph RUNG4["RUNG 4  METAL PART DIVERTER"]
        D1[PROX_SENSOR METAL] --> D2[T37 ON DELAY 2 SEC]
        D2 --> D3[Q2 SOLENOID DIVERTER]
    end
    subgraph RUNG5["RUNG 5  FAULT HOOTER"]
        E1[M0 RUN NC] --> E2[Q3 HOOTER]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Pneumatic Circuit — Pick and Place Cylinder Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[COMPRESSED AIR 6 BAR] --> F1[FILTER REGULATOR LUBRICATOR FRL]
    F1 --> V1[5 BY 2 SOLENOID VALVE DCV]
    V1 --> CY1[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER 50MM BORE]
    V1 --> V2[5 BY 2 SOLENOID VALVE GRIPPER]
    V2 --> GR[PNEUMATIC GRIPPER]
    CY1 --> LS1[LIMIT SWITCH EXTENDED]
    CY1 --> LS2[LIMIT SWITCH RETRACTED]
    LS1 --> SV1_ENER[SOLENOID 1 ENERGIZE EXTEND]
    LS2 --> SV1_DEEN[SOLENOID 1 DE ENERGIZE RETURN]
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flowchart — Automated Bottle Filling Station

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST0[IDLE] --> ST1[CONVEYOR ON BOTTLE ARRIVES]
    ST1 --> ST2[IR SENSOR DETECTS BOTTLE STOP CONVEYOR]
    ST2 --> ST3[TIMER 1 SEC FILL CYCLE WAIT]
    ST3 --> ST4[OPEN INLET SOLENOID VALVE FILL 500 ML]
    ST4 --> ST5[LEVEL SENSOR FULL CLOSE VALVE]
    ST5 --> ST6[TIMER 0 5 SEC DWELL]
    ST6 --> ST7[CONVEYOR RESTART BOTTLE MOVES TO CAPPING]
    ST7 --> ST1
```

---

## 4.5 I/O Assignment Table (Sample)

| Symbol | Address | Type | Device | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PB_S | I0.0 | DI NO | Push Button Green | Start |
| PB_ST | I0.1 | DI NC | Push Button Red | Stop |
| IR_S | I0.2 | DI NO | Photo Sensor | Detect part on belt |
| PROX_M | I0.3 | DI NO | Inductive Prox | Detect metal part |
| LS_EXT | I0.4 | DI NO | Limit Switch | Cylinder extended |
| LS_RET | I0.5 | DI NO | Limit Switch | Cylinder retracted |
| M_RUN | M0.0 | Internal | Memory bit | Run latch |
| C0 | C0 | Counter | Count parts | Total count |
| T37 | T37 | Timer | ON-delay 2 s | Diverter delay |
| M_CONV | Q0.0 | DO | Contactor coil | Run conveyor motor |
| M_DIV | Q0.1 | DO | Solenoid valve | Activate diverter |
| L_GRN | Q0.2 | DO | Lamp Green | Run indication |
| L_RED | Q0.3 | DO | Lamp Red | Fault / Stop |
| HTR | Q0.4 | DO | Hooter | Alarm |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the main components of a material handling system and explain the role of any three with respect to industrial automation.**

**Model Answer:**
A material handling system comprises the following key components:

1. **Conveyor System / Transfer Mechanism** — Transports parts between workstations. Examples: belt conveyor, roller conveyor, chain conveyor.
2. **Sensors** — Provide feedback to the controller about part presence, position, and quality. Examples: IR sensor, inductive proximity, photoelectric sensor.
3. **Actuators** — Perform the physical work such as pushing, gripping, lifting, or rotating. Examples: DC motor, pneumatic cylinder, servo motor.
4. **Controller (PLC)** — The brain of the system that executes ladder logic and coordinates the sequence.

In automation, **sensors** (e.g., IR sensor) detect a part and send a 24 V signal to the PLC input; the **PLC** processes this in its scan cycle; the **actuator** (e.g., solenoid valve) is energized by the output module to perform the next action, enabling **unmanned, repeatable, and high-speed production**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define scan time of a PLC. Why is it important in material handling systems?**

**Model Answer:**
**Scan time** of a PLC is the total time taken by the processor to complete one full cycle of reading all inputs, executing the user program, updating all outputs, and performing housekeeping tasks.

$$\boxed{T_{scan} = T_{input read} + T_{program execution} + T_{output update} + T_{housekeeping}}$$

**Importance in MHS:**
- The scan time must be **less than the shortest event interval** on the shop floor.
- For a high-speed conveyor with parts arriving every 50 ms, the PLC scan time must be < 50 ms, otherwise the controller will **miss part detections** leading to sorting errors.
- It determines the **maximum achievable throughput** and **response time** of the system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output}$, missing the **housekeeping term** (communications, diagnostics, memory refresh). Always include all four terms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice Q1]`

**(a)** Design a ladder logic program for an **automatic bottle filling and capping station** with the following specifications: *(7 marks)*
- A conveyor carries empty bottles.
- An IR sensor (`I0.0`) detects the bottle and stops the conveyor.
- A 2-second timer allows the bottle to settle, then a fill solenoid (`Q0.0`) opens.
- A level sensor (`I0.1`) detects when the bottle is full.
- The solenoid closes, and a 1-second timer starts.
- After 1 s, the conveyor restarts (`Q0.1`) and a green lamp (`Q0.2`) indicates operation.
- A red lamp (`Q0.3`) and hooter (`Q0.4`) activate on any fault (sensor stuck).
- An emergency stop (`I0.7`, NC) halts all outputs.

**(b)** Draw the **pneumatic circuit diagram** for a double-acting cylinder used to push sorted parts into two bins, controlled by a 5/2 solenoid valve, and explain its working. Calculate the **forward thrust** if bore = 80 mm and pressure = 5 bar. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Ladder Logic Program

**Step 1 — I/O Assignment**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | IR_SENSOR | DI NO |
| I0.1 | LEVEL_SENSOR | DI NO |
| I0.7 | E_STOP | DI NC |
| Q0.0 | FILL_SOLENOID | DO |
| Q0.1 | CONVEYOR_MOTOR | DO |
| Q0.2 | GREEN_LAMP | DO |
| Q0.3 | RED_LAMP | DO |
| Q0.4 | HOOTER | DO |
| T37 | SETTLE_TIMER | TON 2 s |
| T38 | DWELL_TIMER | TON 1 s |
| M0.0 | FILL_DONE | Memory |

**Step 2 — Rung-by-Rung Ladder**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R1["RUNG 1  E STOP INTERLOCK"]
        R1A[I0_7 E STOP NC] --> R1B[Q0_1 CONVEYOR]
        R1A --> R1C[Q0_0 FILL]
        R1A --> R1D[Q0_2 GREEN]
    end
    subgraph R2["RUNG 2  CONVEYOR STOPPED BOTTLE DETECTED"]
        R2A[I0_0 IR SENSOR] --> R2B[T37 SETTLE TIMER 2S]
    end
    subgraph R3["RUNG 3  FILL OPEN"]
        R3A[T37 DONE BIT] --> R3B[Q0_0 FILL SOLENOID]
    end
    subgraph R4["RUNG 4  FILL CLOSE WHEN FULL"]
        R4A[I0_1 LEVEL FULL] --> R4B[M0_0 FILL DONE]
        R4A --> R4C[T38 DWELL 1S]
    end
    subgraph R5["RUNG 5  CONVEYOR RESTART AND LAMP"]
        R5A[M0_0 FILL DONE] --> R5B[Q0_1 CONVEYOR]
        R5A --> R5C[Q0_2 GREEN LAMP]
    end
    subgraph R6["RUNG 6  FAULT HOOTER"]
        R6A[T38 DONE NC] --> R6B[Q0_3 RED LAMP]
        R6A --> R6C[Q0_4 HOOTER]
    end
```

**Step 3 — Explanation of Working**

1. When the **IR sensor** detects a bottle, it energizes the **settle timer T37** for 2 seconds. This allows the bottle to come to a stable position.
2. Once T37 is done, **Q0.0 (fill solenoid)** is energized, opening the inlet valve.
3. When the **level sensor I0.1** detects the bottle is full, it sets the **M0.0** bit and starts the **dwell timer T38** for 1 second.
4. The M0.0 bit **restarts the conveyor** and turns on the **green lamp**.
5. The E-stop (I0.7 NC) is **hardwired in series** with all outputs for safety.
6. In case of fault (timer not completing), the **red lamp and hooter** indicate an alarm.

---

### Part (b) — Pneumatic Circuit & Force Calculation

**Working of the Pneumatic Circuit:**

A **5/2 solenoid-operated directional control valve (DCV)** is used to actuate the **double-acting cylinder**. The valve has two solenoids and two positions:

- **Position 1 (Solenoid A energized):** Air flows to the **blank side (cap end)** of the cylinder → piston **extends** → cylinder rod pushes the part into **Bin A**.
- **Position 2 (Solenoid B energized):** Air flows to the **rod side** → piston **retracts** → cylinder returns to home position.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[COMPRESSOR 6 BAR] --> A2[FRL UNIT]
    A2 --> A3[5 BY 2 DCV SOLENOID OPERATED]
    A3 -->|extend| A4[BLANK END OF CYLINDER]
    A3 -->|retract| A5[ROD END OF CYLINDER]
    A4 --> A6[PISTON EXTENDS BIN A]
    A5 --> A7[PISTON RETRACTS HOME]
    A6 --> A8[LIMIT SWITCH EXTENDED]
    A7 --> A9[LIMIT SWITCH RETRACTED]
```

**Force Calculation:**

Given: $D = 80$ mm = $0.08$ m; $P = 5$ bar = $5 \times 10^5$ N/m²

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{forward} = P \times A = 5 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{forward} = 2513.5 \text{ N} \approx 2.51 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice Q2]`

**(a)** With a neat ladder logic diagram, explain the working of a **conveyor-based metal part sorting system**. The system must use an inductive proximity sensor to detect metal, a pneumatic diverter to push the metal part into a separate bin, and a counter to count the total parts sorted. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List any **five sensors and five actuators** commonly used in industrial material handling systems. For each, mention **one typical application**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Metal Sorting System Ladder Logic

**Working Principle:**
The conveyor continuously runs carrying mixed parts. An **inductive proximity sensor** detects metal parts. After a **2-second delay** (allowing the part to reach the diverter position), the **solenoid valve** is energized, activating the **pneumatic cylinder** that pushes the metal part into a separate bin.
