> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A Timer does NOT count clock pulses like a Counter. Instead, it measures **elapsed real-time (in milliseconds or hundredths of a second)** against a Preset Value (PV). The base time unit is critically important for solving KTU numerical problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Timer in a PLC exactly like the **timer function on a microwave oven** or a **smartphone alarm clock**:

1. You set a desired duration (e.g., 2 minutes).
2. You press the "Start" button (this is the Enable/Start input going TRUE).
3. The clock begins counting down internally.
4. When the elapsed time reaches your preset, a "Ding!" (the timer's Done bit) sounds, which can trigger a subsequent action (like opening the oven door automatically via an output contact).

In a factory, imagine a **conveyor belt system**: a sensor detects a bottle. The conveyor should run for exactly **5 seconds** to move the bottle to the next station before stopping. Without a timer, the system would either stop instantly (crushing the bottle) or run indefinitely. The timer provides the precise 5-second delay.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Timer Operation

The following parameters are universally used across PLC platforms (Allen-Bradley, Siemens S7, Mitsubishi, Schneider):

- **Preset Value (PV):** The target time duration pre-loaded by the programmer.
- **Accumulated Value (ACC):** The current elapsed time recorded by the timer.
- **Time Base (TB):** The fundamental resolution of the timer (typically **1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, or 1 s**).
- **Enable Bit (EN):** Indicates the timer instruction is actively timing.
- **Timing Bit (TT):** Indicates the timer is actively accumulating time.
- **Done Bit (DN):** Indicates the timer has reached its preset value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Insight:** The actual physical time of a timer is calculated as:
> $$\text{Actual Time} = \text{Preset Value (PV)} \times \text{Time Base (TB)}$$
> Misinterpreting the time base is the single most common mistake in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of PLC Timers

PLCs typically provide three primary categories of timer instructions, each suited for a specific control philosophy:

### A. On-Delay Timer (TON / TOn)
- **Behavior:** When the enable input becomes TRUE, the timer begins counting. The timer's output (Done bit) turns ON **only after** the accumulated value equals the preset value. When the input goes FALSE, the timer **immediately resets**.
- **Engineering Application:** Cooling fans that must run for 30 seconds after a machine stops. Star-delta motor starters (delay between star and delta switching).

### B. Off-Delay Timer (TOF / TOff)
- **Behavior:** The output turns ON **immediately** when the input goes TRUE. When the input goes FALSE, the timer begins counting. The output remains TRUE for the preset duration **after** the input turns OFF, then resets.
- **Engineering Application:** Keeping emergency lights ON for 60 seconds after a power failure. Conveyor extension to clear products after a stop signal.

### C. Retentive / Accumulating Timer (RTO / TONR)
- **Behavior:** Once enabled, it accumulates time. Unlike TON, it **retains** its accumulated value even if the input goes FALSE. A separate **Reset (RES)** instruction is required to clear it.
- **Engineering Application:** Total running hours of a machine, totalizing fluid flow over a session.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps (For TON - On-Delay Timer)

1. **Initial State:** Enable Input (IN) = FALSE, Accumulated Value (ACC) = 0, Done Bit (Q) = FALSE.
2. **Activation Phase:** When IN transitions from FALSE to TRUE, the timer reads the Preset Value (PT) and starts incrementing ACC based on the Time Base.
3. **Timing Phase:** While IN is TRUE and ACC < PT, the Timing Bit (TT) remains TRUE.
4. **Completion Phase:** When ACC reaches PT, the Done Bit (Q) turns TRUE. The timer stops incrementing.
5. **Reset Phase:** If IN goes FALSE at any time, ACC is immediately reset to 0, and Q goes FALSE.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Representation | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Actual Time Duration | $T = PV \times TB$ | $T$ in seconds, $PV$ is an integer |
| Preset Value Calculation | $PV = \dfrac{T_{\text{desired}}}{TB}$ | $PV$ must be an integer (round up if needed) |
| Counter vs Timer Distinction | Timer $\rightarrow$ **Time** ; Counter $\rightarrow$ **Events** | Timers use internal clock pulses |
| On-Delay Output Equation | $Q(t) = 1$ when $t \geq PT$ and $IN=1$ | Boolean logic condition |
| Off-Delay Output Equation | $Q(t) = 1$ when $IN=1$ **OR** $t < PT$ after $IN \rightarrow 0$ | Boolean logic condition |
| Retentive Reset Condition | $ACC = 0$ only when $RES = 1$ | Explicit reset required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Timers form the backbone of sequential logic in discrete manufacturing. They are extensively used in **batch processing** (chemical, food & beverage), **packaging lines** (dwell time for sealing), **traffic light control**, and **safety interlocks** (lockout delays to allow capacitor discharge).

## 2.4 Timing Diagram Conventions

In KTU examinations, you will be expected to draw or interpret **timing waveforms** showing:
- The Input Signal (square wave)
- The Timer Accumulating Value (sawtooth or ramp)
- The Output (Done) Bit (delayed square wave)

For a TON timer with PV = 3 seconds, if the input is TRUE for 5 seconds, the output becomes TRUE at $t=3$ and FALSE at $t=5$ (when input drops).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Preset Value (PV) Calculation from Desired Time

The core relationship between the timer's internal counting mechanism and the real-world time is derived from first principles below.

**Given:**
- Desired physical delay, $T_d$ = 4.5 seconds.
- Timer Time Base, $TB$ = 0.01 seconds (10 ms).
- Timer Base registers integer counts only.

**Step 1:** Establish the fundamental relationship.
Every Time Base interval, the timer's accumulator increments by exactly 1 unit.
$$ACC = \sum_{k=1}^{n} 1 = n \quad \text{(where } n \text{ is the number of elapsed TB intervals)}$$

**Step 2:** Express the physical time elapsed in terms of Time Base.
The total physical time elapsed after $n$ counts is:
$$T = n \times TB$$

**Step 3:** For the timer to "trip" (Done bit = 1), the accumulated value must reach the Preset Value.
$$PV = n \quad \text{and} \quad T_d = n \times TB$$

**Step 4:** Solve for PV by equating physical time to the desired delay.
$$\begin{aligned}
PV \times TB &= T_d \\
PV &= \dfrac{T_d}{TB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the numerical values.
$$\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{4.5 \text{ s}}{0.01 \text{ s}} \\
PV &= 450 \text{ counts}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** To achieve a 4.5-second delay using a 10 ms time base, the programmer must enter 450 into the Preset Value register.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Timer Behavior vs. Input Conditions

| Timer Type | IN = 0 (Initially) | IN goes 0 → 1 | While IN=1, ACC < PV | When ACC = PV | IN goes 1 → 0 | Reset Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TON (On-Delay)** | Q=0, ACC=0 | Timer starts | Q=0, TT=1, ACC↑ | Q=1, TT=0 | Q=0, ACC=0 | **Automatic** on IN=0 |
| **TOF (Off-Delay)** | Q=0, ACC=0 | Q=1 immediately | Q=1, TT=0, ACC=0 | (No change yet) | Timer starts | **Automatic** after delay |
| **RTO (Retentive)** | Q=0, ACC=0 | Timer starts | Q=0, TT=1, ACC↑ | Q=1, TT=0 | Q remains 1, **ACC frozen** | **Manual** (RES instruction) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Retentive Timer:**
> In applications like tracking total machine runtime, you cannot afford to lose the accumulated count if the machine momentarily stops. RTO holds its value like a trip odometer in a car; only an explicit reset clears it.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A PLC TON timer is configured with a Time Base of 100 ms. The Preset Value is set to 150. Determine:
(a) The total time delay before the output energizes.
(b) The state of the Done bit at $t = 12$ seconds after enable.
(c) What happens if the input is removed at $t = 10$ seconds and re-applied at $t = 14$ seconds?

**Solution (a):** Total time delay
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= PV \times TB \\
T &= 150 \times 0.1 \text{ s} \\
T &= 15 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution (b):** State of Done bit at $t = 12$ s
Since $12 \text{ s} < 15 \text{ s}$, the accumulated value has not yet reached the preset.
- **Done Bit (Q) = 0 (FALSE)**
- Timing Bit (TT) = 1 (TRUE, still timing)

**Solution (c):** Input removed at $t=10$ s
At $t=10$ s, ACC = 100 counts (10 s of 100 ms). When IN goes FALSE:
- TON timer **automatically resets**.
- ACC returns to 0, Q becomes 0.

When IN is re-applied at $t=14$ s, the timer starts **fresh from zero**. It will need a full 15 seconds from this new start time. So Q will turn ON at $t = 14 + 15 = 29$ s (relative to the original $t=0$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer Instruction Block (IEC 61131-3 Standard)

Below is a structural block diagram of a standard PLC timer as defined by the IEC 61131-3 international standard. This represents how a timer appears in programming software and how its internal logic is interconnected.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IN: Enable Input] --> B{Timer Logic Core}
    B --> C[EN: Enable Output]
    B --> D[TT: Timing Bit]
    B --> E[Q: Done Bit]
    PT[PT: Preset Value] --> B
    TB[Time Base Clock] --> B
    
    C -.feedback.-> F[Status Word]
    D -.feedback.-> F
    E -.feedback.-> F
    
    ACC[ACC: Accumulated Value] --> B
    RES[RES: Reset Input] --> B
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style PT fill:#fff4e1
    style TB fill:#ffe1e1
    style RES fill:#e1ffe1
    style B fill:#f0e1ff
```

## 4.2 Timing Diagram Flow - TON On-Delay Timer

The following flowchart illustrates the logical decision sequence inside a TON timer during its operational lifecycle. This is a more detailed alternative to a physical waveform drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([PLC Scan Start]) --> CheckIN{IN Signal is TRUE?}
    CheckIN -- No --> ResetState[Set ACC=0, Q=0, TT=0]
    ResetState --> WaitIN[Wait for IN transition]
    WaitIN --> CheckIN
    
    CheckIN -- Yes --> IncrementACC[ACC = ACC + 1 per TB cycle]
    IncrementACC --> SetTT[Set TT=1]
    SetTT --> ComparePV{ACC >= PV?}
    
    ComparePV -- No --> ContinueTiming[Continue accumulating]
    ContinueTiming --> IncrementACC
    
    ComparePV -- Yes --> SetDone[Set Q=1, TT=0]
    SetDone --> CheckINStill{IN still TRUE?}
    CheckINStill -- Yes --> HoldDone[Maintain Q=1]
    HoldDone --> CheckINStill
    CheckINStill -- No --> ResetState
    
    style Start fill:#90EE90
    style SetDone fill:#FFD700
    style ResetState fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.3 Ladder Logic Representation (Conceptual)

In ladder logic, a TON timer is typically represented as a single instruction block on a rung. Below is a conceptual block-level representation of how the timer interacts with input and output contacts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[L1: Power Rail] --- X1[Input Contact X1]
    X1 --- TON1[TON Timer Block\nT4:5\nPreset: 150\nTime Base: 100ms]
    TON1 --- Y1[Output Coil Y1]
    Y1 --- L2[L2: Neutral Rail]
    
    DN[Done Bit T4:5/DN] -.controls.-> OutBranch[Output Y1 via Done contact]
    
    style L1 fill:#333,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#333,color:#fff
    style TON1 fill:#FFA500
    style X1 fill:#87CEEB
    style Y1 fill:#90EE90
    style DN fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.4 Application Architecture: Star-Delta Motor Starter

One of the most classic KTU exam applications of timers. A Star-Delta starter uses an On-Delay Timer to transition a motor from star (low voltage, reduced starting current) to delta (full voltage, normal running) configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Pushbutton] --> Contactor1[Main Contactor ON]
    Contactor1 --> StarContactor[Star Contactor Energized]
    StarContactor --> TimerStart[TON Timer Starts: 5 seconds]
    TimerStart --> MotorStart[Motor runs in Star config]
    MotorStart --> TimerDone{Timer reaches 5s?}
    TimerDone -- No --> MotorStart
    TimerDone -- Yes --> DeactivateStar[Star Contactor De-energized]
    DeactivateStar --> DeltaContactor[Delta Contactor Energized]
    DeltaContactor --> MotorRun[Motor runs in Delta config]
    
    Stop[Stop Pushbutton] --> ResetAll[Reset All Contactors]
    ResetAll --> MotorStart
    
    style Start fill:#90EE90
    style Stop fill:#FF6B6B
    style TimerDone fill:#FFD700
    style MotorRun fill:#87CEEB
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Timer and a Counter in a PLC. List any two types of PLC timers.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Timer** is a PLC instruction that measures elapsed real-time against a preset time base to produce a time-based delay before activating an output. A **Counter**, in contrast, counts the number of input events (pulses or rising edges) rather than time intervals.

| Feature | Timer | Counter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Measures | Time duration | Number of events |
| Driven by | Internal clock (Time Base) | External input pulses |
| Typical Use | Delays, sequencing | Counting products, batches |

**Two types of PLC timers:**
1. **On-Delay Timer (TON):** Output energizes after a delay following input activation.
2. **Off-Delay Timer (TOF):** Output de-energizes after a delay following input deactivation.
*(Alternatively: Retentive Timer - RTO)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of a Retentive Timer (RTO). Why is a separate Reset (RES) instruction required for it?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Retentive Timer (RTO)** is a PLC timer that **retains (remembers) its accumulated value** even when the enable input goes FALSE. It continues counting from where it left off when the input is re-enabled.

A separate **Reset (RES) instruction is mandatory** because, unlike a non-retentive (TON) timer that auto-resets when the input turns OFF, the RTO must be **explicitly cleared by the programmer** via a dedicated RES rung. This prevents accidental loss of accumulated time data, which is critical in applications like totalizing machine runtime hours or cumulative batch processing times.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of an **On-Delay Timer (TON)**. Derive the relationship between Preset Value, Time Base, and Total Time Delay.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A PLC TON timer has a Preset Value of 250 and a Time Base of 0.1 seconds. Calculate:
(i) The total time delay produced.
(ii) The state of the Done (DN) bit and Timing (TT) bit at $t = 20$ seconds after the input is enabled.
(iii) What will happen if the input is de-energized at $t = 15$ seconds?

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Definition:** An On-Delay Timer (TON) is a PLC instruction whose output (Done bit) transitions to TRUE only **after** the input has been continuously TRUE for a duration equal to the Preset Value (PV) multiplied by the Time Base (TB).

**Timing Diagram (Conceptual ASCII Representation):**

```
Input (IN)  : _____|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|________
                  ↑ t=0              ↑ t=15s
Acc Value  : ______/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾/_______
                   0   ↑ ACC=PV
Timing Bit : ________|‾‾‾‾‾‾|________
                       (TT=1)
Done Bit   : ________________|‾‾‾‾|____
                            ↑ Q=1
```

**Derivation of the Time Relationship:**

Let $T$ be the total time delay (in seconds), $PV$ be the Preset Value (integer count), and $TB$ be the Time Base (seconds per count).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Since } ACC \text{ increments by 1 every } TB \text{ seconds:} \\
ACC &= n \text{ (number of counts)} \\
T &= n \times TB \\
\text{When Done Bit activates, } n &= PV \\
\therefore T &= PV \times TB
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Total time delay:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T &= PV \times TB \\
T &= 250 \times 0.1 \text{ s} \\
T &= 25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) State at $t = 20$ seconds:**
- At $t = 20$ s, $ACC = 20 / 0.1 = 200$ counts.
- Since $200 < 250$ (PV), the timer has NOT completed.
- **Done Bit (DN) = 0 (FALSE)**
- **Timing Bit (TT) = 1 (TRUE, actively accumulating)**

**(iii) Input de-energized at $t = 15$ s:**
- At $t = 15$ s, $ACC = 15 / 0.1 = 150$ counts.
- When IN goes FALSE, a TON timer **automatically resets**.
- **ACC becomes 0 immediately.**
- **Done Bit = 0.**
- The 150 counts accumulated are **lost** (this is why RTO exists).
- When IN is re-applied later, timing restarts from 0.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the operational behavior of **On-Delay (TON)**, **Off-Delay (TOF)**, and **Retentive (RTO)** timers using a clear comparison table. Provide one industrial application for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a bottle-filling plant, a conveyor must run for **12 seconds** after a proximity sensor detects a bottle. The PLC used has only timer instructions with a Time Base of **10 ms**. Design the ladder logic logic specification and calculate the required Preset Value. What modification is needed if the plant later upgrades to a 100 ms time base PLC?

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Solution: Comparison Table**

| Feature | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) | RTO (Retentive) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output on IN=1 | Delayed ON | Immediate ON | Delayed ON |
| Output on IN=0 | Immediate OFF | Delayed OFF | Holds last state |
| Accumulated Value | Resets on IN=0 | Resets after delay | **Retained** |
| Reset Method | Automatic | Automatic | **Manual (RES instruction)** |
| Industrial Use | Cooling fan delay | Emergency lighting | Machine hour meter |

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Required Preset Value (10 ms Time Base):**
$$\begin{aligned}
PV &= \dfrac{T_{\text{desired}}}{TB} \\
PV &= \dfrac{12 \text{ s}}{0.01 \text{ s}} \\
PV &= 1200 \text{ counts}
\end{aligned}$$

**If upgraded to 100 ms (0.1 s) Time Base:**
$$\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{new}} &= \dfrac{12 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s}} \\
PV_{\text{new}} &= 120 \text{ counts}
\end{aligned}$$

**Observation:** The numerical Preset Value decreases (from 1200 to 120) when a coarser time base is used, but the **physical delay remains identical at 12 seconds**. This demonstrates the scalability of PLC timer programming.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Time Base Mix-up:** Students frequently multiply PV by TB without unit consistency. Always convert Time Base to seconds before multiplying. A 100 ms TB = 0.1 s, NOT 100 s.
> 2. **Counter vs Timer Confusion:** Writing `PV = T × TB` for a counter is wrong. Counters have NO time base; they only count pulses.
> 3. **Reset Behavior:** Forgetting that RTO requires a manual RES instruction costs easy marks. State explicitly whether the timer auto-resets or not.
> 4. **Done Bit Timing:** A common error is stating the Done bit turns ON "at" the preset. It actually turns ON **when ACC becomes equal to OR exceeds PV** during the PLC scan cycle.
> 5. **Unit Conversion in Numericals:** A 12-second delay with 10 ms base is PV = 1200, not 1.2. PLC registers hold integers only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Timer Core Function:** Measures elapsed real-time, not events (unlike counters).
- **Master Formula:** $T = PV \times TB$ (the most tested equation in KTU exams).
- **Three Timer Types:** TON (delay-on), TOF (delay-off), RTO (retentive accumulating).
- **TON Behavior:** Auto-resets on IN=0. Output ON only after delay.
- **TOF Behavior:** Output ON immediately; delay is on the turn-OFF.
- **RTO Behavior:** Holds accumulated value; needs explicit RES instruction.
- **Done Bit (Q):** Turns TRUE when ACC ≥ PV.
- **Timing Bit (TT):** TRUE only while actively accumulating.
- **Time Base Units:** Standard values are 1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, 1 s.
- **Industrial Use Cases:** Star-Delta starters, conveyor sequencing, safety delays, batching processes, traffic control.
- **Retentive Timer Use Case:** Totalizing, machine hour tracking, cumulative batch timers.
- **Ladder Logic Symbol:** A box with TON/TOF/RTO designation, PV register, and Done/TT bits.
- **Integer Arithmetic:** All timer values are integers; round UP when dividing (e.g., 4.5 s / 0.01 s = 450 exactly, but 0.05 s / 0.01 s = 5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For any timer numerical, write the formula $T = PV \times TB$ first, substitute the given values, and state the units explicitly. This single habit secures full marks in the calculation portion of every timer problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Counters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Industrial Automation** and **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)**, a **Counter** is a dedicated function-block instruction used to accumulate the number of input pulses (events) received at its count input, and to produce a discrete Boolean output (HIGH/LOW) once a user-defined target value (Preset) is reached. Counters are classified by the **IEC 61131-3** standard as elementary function blocks (EFBs) operating on the principle of edge-triggered event detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OERUT612 - Module 4):**
> A counter is a sequential logic element that tallies the number of occurrences of an event, stores it as a Current Value (CV), and compares it continuously against a Preset Value (PV). When **CV ≥ PV**, the Done bit (Q) is energized. The counter is initialized via a Reset (R) signal.

The three functional parameters of any standard PLC counter are:

1. **Preset Value (PV):** The user-defined numerical target (Integer, 16-bit or 32-bit depending on the PLC).
2. **Current Value (CV):** A volatile/retentive memory register that increments or decrements with each count pulse.
3. **Output Bit (Q):** A Boolean flag set when the counting condition is satisfied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Elevator Floor Display"

Imagine a freight elevator in a factory that displays the current floor on a seven-segment LED. Every time the elevator **passes a floor sensor**, a pulse is sent to a counter.

- The **displayed floor number** = Current Value (CV).
- The **destination floor (e.g., 5th floor)** = Preset Value (PV).
- When the elevator reaches the 5th floor, a **buzzer rings** to signal arrival = Output bit **Q = 1**.
- Pressing the **"Reset" button** on the panel brings the elevator back to Ground (CV = 0).

This is exactly how an industrial counter behaves: it tallies physical events (sensors detecting products, limit switches, encoder pulses) and signals the PLC when a batch or quantity condition is met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, students must distinguish between **Counters** and **Timers** clearly. Counters are triggered by **event occurrences (edge-triggered)**, whereas timers are triggered by **duration (level-triggered)**. A common mistake is to inter-change them in exam answers.

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Parameters

The following are standard metric conventions used in industrial counter design:

- **Counting Frequency:** typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz** for high-speed counters (HSC).
- **Bit-width:** 16-bit (0 to 32,767) or 32-bit (−2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647).
- **Response Time:** in the order of **microseconds (μs)** for dedicated HSC modules.
- **Sensor Compatibility:** PNP/NPN proximity sensors, inductive sensors, photoelectric sensors, and rotary encoders (Quadrature).
- **Standard Pulse Width:** must be at least **one PLC scan cycle** to be reliably detected by a standard counter.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Counters in PLCs

PLCs support four primary counter types. Each operates on a deterministic state-transition model evaluated during every PLC scan cycle.

### 2.1.1 Up Counter (CTU – Count Up)

The **CTU** increments the Current Value by **1** on every rising edge of the count input (CU). It is the most widely used counter in industry for batch counting and production tracking.

**Operational Logic (Boolean / Discrete-Event Model):**

| Step | Condition | Action |
|:----:|:---------|:-------|
| 1 | Rising edge detected on CU | $CV \leftarrow CV + 1$ |
| 2 | $R = 1$ (Reset Active) | $CV \leftarrow 0$ and $Q \leftarrow 0$ |
| 3 | $CV \geq PV$ | $Q \leftarrow 1$ (Done bit energized) |
| 4 | $CV < PV$ | $Q \leftarrow 0$ |

### 2.1.2 Down Counter (CTD – Count Down)

The **CTD** decrements the Current Value by **1** on every rising edge of the count-down input (CD). It is typically used in **subtractive batch processes** (e.g., emptying a tank of N units).

**Operational Logic:**

| Step | Condition | Action |
|:----:|:---------|:-------|
| 1 | Rising edge detected on CD | $CV \leftarrow CV - 1$ |
| 2 | $LD = 1$ (Load) | $CV \leftarrow PV$ |
| 3 | $CV \leq 0$ | $Q \leftarrow 1$ (Done bit energized) |
| 4 | $CV > 0$ | $Q \leftarrow 0$ |

### 2.1.3 Up-Down Counter (CTUD)

The **CTUD** accepts two count inputs: **CU** (count up) and **CD** (count down). This bidirectional counter is essential for applications like position tracking, where a carriage may move forward and backward on a linear axis.

### 2.1.4 Retentive Counter

A **retentive counter** preserves the CV through power cycles. Even if the PLC is powered OFF, the CV is retained using battery-backed RAM or non-volatile memory.

## 2.2 Internal Architecture of a Counter Block

A counter is essentially a **synchronous sequential circuit** implemented in software within the PLC's firmware. It comprises:

1. **Edge Detector** — converts a continuous input signal into discrete rising-edge events.
2. **Accumulator Register** — stores the Current Value (CV).
3. **Comparator** — performs the Boolean check $CV \geq PV$ (or $CV \leq 0$).
4. **Output Latch** — sets/resets the Q bit.
5. **Reset Logic** — handles synchronous or asynchronous reset.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every essential equation, boundary condition, and operational rule for counters. **No vertical pipes (`|`) are used; all absolute-value and comparison operators are rendered in LaTeX.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical / Boolean Expression | Boundary / Range | Engineering Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:----------------------------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | CTU Update Rule | $CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} + 1$ | $0 \leq CV \leq 32{,}767$ (16-bit) | Pulses (events) |
| 2 | CTD Update Rule | $CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} - 1$ | $0 \leq CV \leq PV$ | Pulses (events) |
| 3 | CTUD Update (Up) | $CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} + 1$ | Full signed 32-bit | Pulses (events) |
| 4 | CTUD Update (Down) | $CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} - 1$ | Full signed 32-bit | Pulses (events) |
| 5 | CTU Done Condition | $Q = 1$ iff $CV \geq PV$ | Boolean | Logical state |
| 6 | CTD Done Condition | $Q = 1$ iff $CV \leq 0$ | Boolean | Logical state |
| 7 | Reset Condition | $CV \leftarrow 0,\; Q \leftarrow 0$ when $R = 1$ | Boolean | Logical state |
| 8 | Count Frequency Limit | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Typically 1 kHz to 100 kHz | Hz (Hertz) |
| 9 | Encoder Position | $X = \dfrac{CV}{PPR} \times \pi D$ | Roller/wheel diameter $D$ | meters (m) |
| 10 | Up Counter Sum Identity | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta CV_{i} = N$ | $N = PV$ at completion | Pulses (events) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The encoder formula $X = \dfrac{CV}{PPR} \times \pi D$ is widely used in **length measurement, cut-to-length machines, and conveyor distance tracking**, where each pulse from a rotary encoder corresponds to a known arc length on the wheel.

## 2.4 Why Counters are Indispensable in Industrial Automation

Counters form the backbone of **discrete-event manufacturing systems** because:

- They convert high-frequency physical events into actionable Boolean outputs.
- They enable **batch control** (e.g., "fill 24 bottles per carton").
- They provide **audit trails** of production counts for OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) calculations.
- They interface directly with **encoders** for position and speed feedback in servo systems.
- They replace legacy electromechanical counters, providing **higher reliability, no moving parts, and software-based re-configurability**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem — CTU Operation with Preset Value

**Problem Statement:**
A conveyor belt has a **photoelectric sensor** that detects each bottle passing under it. A PLC CTU counter is configured with **PV = 12**. Plot the CV and Q behavior for the first 15 rising-edge events at the sensor input, assuming a **Reset (R)** pulse arrives at event 14.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Before counting begins, the counter is in its idle state. The PV is loaded as a constant (here, $PV = 12$).

$$
CV_{0} = 0, \quad Q_{0} = 0, \quad PV = 12
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CTU Update Rule on Each Rising Edge:**
For every rising edge of the count input, the update rule is:

$$
CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Done-Bit Rule:**
After every update, check the comparator:

$$
Q_{n+1} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } CV_{n+1} \geq PV = 12 \\
0 & \text{if } CV_{n+1} < PV = 12
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Reset Rule (when $R=1$):**
If the reset input is energized, override the accumulator:

$$
CV \leftarrow 0, \quad Q \leftarrow 0
$$

**Step 5 — Build the Event Table:**

| Event $n$ | Input Pulse | $CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} + 1$ | Reset Active? | $Q_{n+1}$ | Status |
|:---------:|:-----------:|:------------------------:|:-------------:|:---------:|:-------|
| 1 | 1 | $0 + 1 = 1$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 2 | 1 | $1 + 1 = 2$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 3 | 1 | $2 + 1 = 3$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 4 | 1 | $3 + 1 = 4$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 5 | 1 | $4 + 1 = 5$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 6 | 1 | $5 + 1 = 6$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 7 | 1 | $6 + 1 = 7$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 8 | 1 | $7 + 1 = 8$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 9 | 1 | $8 + 1 = 9$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 10 | 1 | $9 + 1 = 10$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 11 | 1 | $10 + 1 = 11$ | No | 0 | Counting |
| 12 | 1 | $11 + 1 = 12$ | No | **1** | **DONE** |
| 13 | 1 | $12 + 1 = 13$ | No | 1 | Latched |
| 14 | 1 | $13 + 1 = 14$ | **Yes (R=1)** | 0 | **RESET** |
| 15 | 1 | $0 + 1 = 1$ | No | 0 | New Cycle |

### 3.1.2 Verification of the Sum Identity

According to the identity $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta CV_{i} = N$ for an Up Counter, after 12 events (no reset), the total increment should equal the preset value:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{12} \Delta CV_{i} = 1 + 1 + 1 + \ldots + 1 \;(12 \text{ times}) = 12 = PV
$$

This confirms that the counter correctly reaches the Done state at event 12. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — CTD Operation

**Problem Statement:**
A pick-and-place robot removes a part from a magazine on each rising edge of a sensor. The magazine initially holds **PV = 10** parts. Compute the CV and Q at the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, and 11th count-down events. Note: The 11th event occurs when CV is already 0 (overflow behavior must be discussed).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Initialization with Load (LD):**

$$
CV_{0} = PV = 10, \quad Q_{0} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — CTD Update Rule:**

$$
CV_{n+1} = CV_{n} - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Done Condition for CTD:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } CV_{n+1} \leq 0 \\
0 & \text{if } CV_{n+1} > 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Event-by-Event Computation:**

For the 1st event:

$$
CV_{1} = CV_{0} - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9
$$

Since $CV_{1} = 9 > 0$, we have $Q_{1} = 0$.

For the 3rd event (cumulative 3 decrements):

$$
CV_{3} = 10 - 3 = 7, \quad Q_{3} = 0
$$

For the 5th event:

$$
CV_{5} = 10 - 5 = 5, \quad Q_{5} = 0
$$

For the 7th event:

$$
CV_{7} = 10 - 7 = 3, \quad Q_{7} = 0
$$

For the 9th event:

$$
CV_{9} = 10 - 9 = 1, \quad Q_{9} = 0
$$

For the 10th event (the boundary):

$$
CV_{10} = 10 - 10 = 0, \quad Q_{10} = 1 \;(\text{DONE bit energized})
$$

For the 11th event (underflow discussion):

$$
CV_{11} = CV_{10} - 1 = 0 - 1 = -1
$$

In a **16-bit unsigned** implementation, this would underflow to $CV_{11} = 65{,}535$ (a wraparound). In a **signed 32-bit** counter, $CV_{11} = -1$ exactly. In both cases, the Done bit may be latched or cleared depending on PLC vendor convention. The standard KTU expectation is to state that **the underflow condition is implementation-dependent** and should be guarded by a logic interlock.

**Step 5 — Verification of the Subtract-to-Zero Identity:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{10} \vert \Delta CV_{i} \vert = 10 = PV
$$

This confirms the counter reaches Done exactly at $n = PV = 10$ events. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Problem — Encoder-Based Length Measurement

**Problem Statement:**
A rotary encoder with **PPR = 1000** pulses per revolution is mounted on a measuring wheel of diameter **D = 100 mm**. The wheel rolls on a moving conveyor, and the encoder pulses are fed to a CTU counter. How many pulses correspond to a conveyor travel of **1 meter**? What PV should be set to trigger an output when 1 m is reached?

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Circumference of the Measuring Wheel:**

$$
C = \pi D = \pi \times 0.1 \text{ m} = 0.3142 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance per Pulse:**

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{C}{PPR} = \dfrac{0.3142 \text{ m}}{1000} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.3142 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Pulses for 1 m Travel:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1 \text{ m}}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{1}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3183 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Step 4 — Preset Value for Cut-to-Length Operation:**

$$
PV = 3183 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification Using the Master Formula:**

$$
X = \dfrac{CV}{PPR} \times \pi D = \dfrac{3183}{1000} \times \pi \times 0.1 = 1.0 \text{ m} \; \checkmark
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CTU vs. CTD vs. CTUD

| Feature | CTU (Count Up) | CTD (Count Down) | CTUD (Up-Down) |
|:--------|:---------------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Direction of Count | Increment ($+1$) | Decrement ($-1$) | Both |
| Done Condition | $CV \geq PV$ | $CV \leq 0$ | $CV \geq PV$ (typically) |
| Reset Action | $CV \leftarrow 0$ | $CV \leftarrow PV$ (Load) | $CV \leftarrow 0$ |
| Typical Application | Bottle counting | Batch subtraction | Position tracking |
| Number of Count Inputs | 1 (CU) | 1 (CD) | 2 (CU, CD) |
| Number of Reset Inputs | 1 (R) | 1 (LD) | 1 (R) |
| Output Bits | Q (Done) | Q (Done) | QU, QD (Up/Down Done) |
| Retentive Variant Available? | Yes | Yes | Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a PLC Counter

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a standard CTU counter as implemented in a PLC firmware. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with the reserved-keyword safeguard rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sensor Input Pulse] --> B1[Edge Detector Rising Edge Only]
    B1 --> C1[Accumulator Register CV]
    D1[Reset Signal R] --> C1
    E1[Preset Value PV from User Memory] --> F1[Comparator CV versus PV]
    C1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1{Boolean Check CV greater than or equal to PV}
    G1 -- True --> H1[Output Latch Q set to 1]
    G1 -- False --> H2[Output Latch Q set to 0]
    D1 --> H1
    D1 --> H2
    H1 --> I1[PLC Output to Actuator Relay Contactor]
    H2 --> I1
    C1 --> J1[Display HMI Current Value]
    E1 --> J1
    I1 --> K1[Final Action Stop Conveyor Activate Solenoid etc]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CTUD Counter Architecture

The following block diagram shows the dual-input architecture of an Up-Down counter used in position-tracking applications.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Sensor A Forward Direction] --> B2[Edge Detector Up CU]
    A3[Sensor B Reverse Direction] --> C2[Edge Detector Down CD]
    B2 --> D2[Adder Subtractor Module]
    C2 --> D2
    D2 --> E2[Accumulator CV]
    F2[Reset R] --> E2
    G2[Preset PV] --> H2[Comparator]
    E2 --> H2
    H2 --> I2{Boolean Decision CV greater than or equal to PV}
    I2 -- True --> J2[Done Bit QU set to 1]
    I2 -- False --> K2[Done Bit QU remains 0]
    J2 --> L2[Output Module to Motor Drive]
    K2 --> L2
    E2 --> M2[Encoder Display HMI]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Ladder Logic Representation — CTU Counter

The following diagram symbolically represents the **ladder logic rung** of a CTU counter. Although Mermaid cannot render standard electrical Ladder Diagrams, the logical flow is shown for clarity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Input X0 Bottle Sensor] --> B3[Normally Open Contact X0]
    B3 --> C3[CTU Counter Block C0 Preset K equals 12]
    D3[Input X1 Reset Pushbutton] --> E3[Normally Open Contact X1]
    E3 --> C3
    C3 --> F3[Output Coil Y0 Conveyor Stop Signal]
    C3 --> G3[Counter Current Value C0 stored in Data Register]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Application Diagram — Bottle-Counting Conveyor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Conveyor Motor] --> B4[Infeed Conveyor Belt]
    B4 --> C4[Photoelectric Sensor PNP Type]
    C4 --> D4[PLC Counter Input Channel]
    D4 --> E4[CTU Counter CV increments by 1 per bottle]
    E4 --> F4{Comparator CV versus PV equals 12}
    F4 -- No --> G4[Continue Conveyor Motion]
    F4 -- Yes --> H4[Activate Solenoid for Diverter Arm]
    H4 --> I4[Carton Packed]
    I4 --> J4[Reset Pulse to Counter]
    J4 --> E4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a PLC counter. With a neat block diagram, explain the function of a Count-Up (CTU) counter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PLC counter** is a function block that counts the number of input pulses and produces an output when a preset value is reached. A **CTU (Count-Up) counter** has three main elements:

1. **Count Input (CU):** Increments the Current Value (CV) by 1 on every rising edge.
2. **Reset (R):** When energized, sets CV = 0 and Q = 0.
3. **Preset Value (PV):** The target count entered by the user.

**Operation:** The CV increases by 1 on each count pulse. When $CV \geq PV$, the output bit **Q** is set to 1, indicating the count is complete. The Q bit remains latched until a reset is applied.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition (1 mark)
- Block diagram / three functional elements (1 mark)
- Operation with Q condition (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Timer and a Counter in PLC applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Timer | Counter |
|:-----:|:----------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | Trigger | Duration-based (level-triggered) | Event-based (edge-triggered) |
| 2 | Input | Continuous ON signal | Discrete pulses / rising edges |
| 3 | Output Q | ON after preset time elapsed | ON when count reaches preset |
| 4 | Reset | Done automatically or via reset | Explicit reset signal required |
| 5 | Typical Use | Delay, sequencing | Batch counting, position tracking |
| 6 | Time Base | Internal clock (10 ms, 100 ms, 1 s) | PLC scan / external pulse train |

**Marks Distribution:**
- Table of differences (2 marks)
- Examples (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often confuse **PV (Preset Value)** with **CV (Current Value)**. PV is the **target** set by the user; CV is the **current accumulated** count. Writing them interchangeably will cost at least **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. Do NOT write `Q = 1 when CV = PV`. The correct comparator is **$Q = 1$ when $CV \geq PV$** (for CTU). The "greater than or equal to" condition is critical and is tested as a separate point.
> 3. Always mention **rising-edge detection** when describing counter input — level-triggered counting is INCORRECT and will be penalized.
> 4. In numerical problems, **show the reset explicitly** in your event table; failing to do so loses 1 mark.
> 5. For CTUD, distinguish between **QU (Up done)** and **QD (Down done)** — confusing them is a frequent error.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **Count-Up (CTU) counter**. Describe its inputs, outputs, and the internal comparator logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conveyor carries bottles past a photoelectric sensor. The sensor generates one pulse per bottle. A CTU counter is configured with **PV = 24**. The reset button is pressed at the 28th pulse. Plot the CV and Q waveforms and tabulate the CV and Q values for the first 30 events. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A CTU counter consists of the following functional blocks (describe each for 1 mark each):

1. **Count Input (CU):** Accepts pulses from sensors; edge detector ensures only rising edges are counted.
2. **Reset Input (R):** Boolean; when TRUE, $CV \leftarrow 0$ and $Q \leftarrow 0$.
3. **Preset Value (PV):** Loaded from user memory.
4. **Current Value (CV):** Accumulator register holding the running count.
5. **Comparator:** Continuously compares $CV \geq PV$.
6. **Output Latch (Q):** Set to 1 when comparator is TRUE; held until reset.
7. **Application Output:** Drives an actuator, HMI display, or another logic block.

**Update Rule:**

$$
CV_{n+1} = 
\begin{cases}
CV_{n} + 1 & \text{if rising edge on CU} \\
0 & \text{if } R = 1 \\
CV_{n} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Done Bit Rule:**

$$
Q = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } CV \geq PV \\
0 & \text{if } CV < PV
\end{cases}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Initial conditions: $CV_{0} = 0$, $Q_{0} = 0$, $PV = 24$.

**Event Table:**

| Event $n$ | Pulse | CV Update | R? | $CV$ | $Q$ |
|:---------:|:-----:|:---------:|:--:|:----:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | $0+1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | $1+1$ | 0 | 2 | 0 |
| 5 | 1 | $4+1$ | 0 | 5 | 0 |
| 10 | 1 | $9+1$ | 0 | 10 | 0 |
| 15 | 1 | $14+1$ | 0 | 15 | 0 |
| 20 | 1 | $19+1$ | 0 | 20 | 0 |
| 23 | 1 | $22+1$ | 0 | 23 | 0 |
| 24 | 1 | $23+1$ | 0 | 24 | **1 (DONE)** |
| 25 | 1 | $24+1$ | 0 | 25 | 1 |
| 26 | 1 | $25+1$ | 0 | 26 | 1 |
| 27 | 1 | $26+1$ | 0 | 27 | 1 |
| 28 | 1 | $27+1$ | **1** | **0** | **0** |
| 29 | 1 | $0+1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 30 | 1 | $1+1$ | 0 | 2 | 0 |

**Waveform Description (for full marks):**

- CV is a **staircase ramp** rising from 0 to 24 in steps of 1.
- Q remains 0 from event 1 to 23.
- Q **transitions to 1 at event 24** and remains latched through events 25, 26, 27.
- At event 28, R = 1 → CV drops to 0 and Q drops to 0.
- Counting restarts: CV = 1 at event 29, CV = 2 at event 30.

**Verification:** $\sum_{i=1}^{24} \Delta CV_{i} = 24 = PV$ ✓

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **Count-Down (CTD) counter** and a **Count-Up-Down (CTUD) counter** with neat block diagrams. Mention two industrial applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary encoder with **PPR = 500** is mounted on a roller of diameter **D = 80 mm**. The roller is in contact with a steel sheet moving on a conveyor. A CTU counter is connected to the encoder output. Calculate:
   (i) The number of pulses corresponding to a sheet travel of **2 meters**.
   (ii) The Preset Value to be set so that an output Q is energized after the sheet has moved **1.5 m**.
   (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**CTD Counter (4 marks):**
A **CTD (Count-Down) counter** decrements the CV on every rising edge of its CD input. The counter is initially loaded with PV. When $CV \leq 0$, the Q bit is set. Two industrial applications:
1. **Emptying a magazine:** Counting parts removed from a stack.
2. **Reverse-batching:** Counting down to zero for time-out or process completion.

**CTUD Counter (3 marks):**
A **CTUD (Count-Up-Down) counter** has two count inputs: **CU** (up) and **CD** (down). It maintains a net accumulated count. When $CV \geq PV$, the **QU** bit is set; when $CV \leq 0$, the **QD** bit is set. Two industrial applications:
1. **Carriage position tracking** on a linear axis (forward and reverse motion).
2. **Inventory management** in a warehouse with stock in and stock out.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $PPR = 500$, $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1 — Circumference of the roller:**

$$
C = \pi D = \pi \times 0.08 = 0.2513 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance per pulse:**

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{C}{PPR} = \dfrac{0.2513}{500} = 5.027 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/pulse}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of pulses for 2 m travel (Part i):**

$$
N_{2m} = \dfrac{2 \text{ m}}{5.027 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/pulse}} = 3978.87 \approx 3979 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of pulses for 1.5 m travel (Part ii):**

$$
N_{1.5m} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ m}}{5.027 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/pulse}} = 2984.15 \approx 2984 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Step 5 — Preset Value:**

$$
PV = 2984 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
X = \dfrac{2984}{500} \times \pi \times 0.08 = 1.4998 \approx 1.5 \text{ m} \; \checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Counter** = PLC function block that counts input pulses and produces an output when CV reaches PV.
- **Three core elements of any counter:** Count input (CU/CD), Reset (R/Load), Preset (PV).
- **CTU (Count-Up):** $CV \leftarrow CV + 1$ on rising edge; $Q = 1$ when $CV \geq PV$.
- **CTD (Count-Down):** $CV \leftarrow CV - 1$ on rising edge; $Q = 1$ when $CV \leq 0$.
- **CTUD (Up-Down):** Two count inputs; bidirectional counting for position applications.
- **Edge-triggered, NOT level-triggered** — only rising edges are counted; this is the #1 differentiator from timers.
- **Reset behavior:** CV goes to 0 and Q goes to 0 when R = 1; for CTD, Load sets $CV \leftarrow PV$.
- **Done bit Q is latched** — it remains ON even after CV exceeds PV; it is cleared only by reset.
- **High-Speed Counters (HSC):** Required when pulse frequency exceeds the PLC scan time; supports up to **100 kHz**.
- **Encoder integration:** $X = \dfrac{CV}{PPR} \times \pi D$ — used in length, position, and speed measurement.
- **Applications:** Bottle counting, batch control, conveyor tracking, cut-to-length, parking systems, traffic light sequencing.
- **Industrial sensor compatibility:** PNP, NPN, photoelectric, inductive, encoders, and limit switches.
- **Retentive vs. Non-retentive:** Retentive counters preserve CV across power cycles; non-retentive do not.
- **Underflow/Overflow:** Beyond range, the counter may wrap (implementation-dependent); always include software interlocks.
- **KTU 2024 scheme focus areas:** Block diagram, Boolean comparator logic, event-table construction, and one numerical problem on encoder-based measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Control Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program Control Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Program Control Instructions** are a category of PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) instructions that **alter the normal sequential scan execution flow** of the ladder logic program. Instead of executing every rung in the top-to-bottom, left-to-right order, these instructions enable the processor to **skip, jump, branch, suspend, or modularly re-enter** specific program segments based on rung conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Program control instructions are output instructions used to alter the normal program scan sequence. They include Master Control Reset (MCR), Jump (JMP) and Label (LBL), Subroutine (SBR), Immediate Input (IIN), Immediate Output (IOT), Suspend (SUS), and Temporary End (TND).*

In the **Allen-Bradley SLC 500 / PLC-5** family of controllers, these instructions are categorized as **Output Instructions** because they appear on the right-hand side of a ladder rung and influence program flow when their rung-condition-in (RCI) evaluates **TRUE (1)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Program Control Instructions (KTU Board Taxonomy):**
> 1. **Zone-Based Control** — Master Control Reset (MCR)
> 2. **Transfer of Control** — Jump (JMP) and Label (LBL)
> 3. **Modular Control** — Subroutine (SBR), Return (RET), Subroutine Entry Label (SBR file)
> 4. **Immediate I/O Update** — IIN, IOT
> 5. **Diagnostic Control** — Suspend (SUS)
> 6. **Scan Termination** — Temporary End (TND)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Traffic Controller"

Imagine you are driving through a city following a **straight main road** (the normal PLC scan). This is the default behaviour — every traffic light is green, and you pass every intersection.

- **JMP / LBL** is like a **detour sign**: "If road is closed, skip ahead to Exit 5 (LBL)." You bypass the segment entirely.
- **MCR** is like a **construction zone gate**: "If the gate is down, freeze all activity within the zone — outputs go OFF, but timers/retentive data remain."
- **SBR / RET** is like a **service road / express exit**: "When you need the highway, take the side road, do the work, and return to the main highway."
- **IIN / IOT** is like a **VIP priority lane**: "Don't wait for the regular timing — read/write the signal immediately."
- **SUS** is like a **red light with a mechanic standing by**: "Stop everything; the engineer is debugging."
- **TND** is like an **early end-of-shift bell**: "Finish today's run right here; tomorrow we start fresh."

This analogy makes it immediately clear to a first-time learner **why** the PLC would ever want to deviate from the linear scan — to save time, isolate faults, modularise logic, and respond to critical inputs without scan delay.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Program control instructions do **not** perform logical operations like AND/OR/Timers. They control **HOW** the program itself is *read* by the processor.

---

## 1.3 The PLC Scan Cycle — Foundation for Understanding

To understand *why* program control matters, the student must first visualise the **Scan Cycle**:

| Scan Phase | Function |
|---|---|
| **Input Scan** | Read all physical inputs → update **Input Image Table** |
| **Program Scan** | Execute ladder rungs **top-to-bottom, left-to-right** |
| **Output Scan** | Copy **Output Image Table** → physical outputs |
| **Housekeeping** | Communication, diagnostics, internal servicing |

**Default Program Scan Rule:** The processor executes **every** rung in **every** scan. Program control instructions allow us to **override** this rule conditionally.

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is believing that a JMP "skips" the entire scan. **Incorrect.** The processor still **finishes** the scan — it simply does not *evaluate* the skipped rungs. Retentive outputs in the skipped zone **retain their last state** (non-retentive outputs are turned OFF).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Boundaries (KTU Expected)

- **MCR Zones:** May be **nested up to 8 levels deep** in SLC 500 processors.
- **JMP Targets:** Multiple JMPs can point to the **same LBL**; however, **two LBLs with the same number in one program** is **illegal** and will fault the processor.
- **Subroutine Files:** SLC 500 supports subroutines in **files S:3 to S:15** (numbered **3 through 15**, giving 13 subroutine files).
- **Scan Time Impact:** JMP saves execution time proportional to the number of rungs skipped. MCR does **not** save time — the processor still evaluates the zone rungs but forces outputs OFF.

---

## 1.5 Why Program Control is Industry-Critical

In real industrial automation:

- **JMP** is used for **manual/auto mode switching** (skip the auto-sequence rung block in manual mode).
- **MCR** is used as a **safety zone** for an entire machine — one master switch can de-energise all outputs in a defined zone.
- **SBR** is used for **modular programming** (e.g., a separate subroutine for each machine station).
- **IIN/IOT** is used for **high-speed response** where scan-time delay is unacceptable.
- **TND** is used during **commissioning** to prevent the main program from overwriting test values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Master Control Reset (MCR)

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept
The **MCR instruction** creates a **programmable zone** in the ladder diagram. All non-retentive outputs within the MCR zone are **forced OFF** when the MCR rung is FALSE, regardless of their logic state.

**Master Control Reset Zone Structure:**

- **MCR Start (MCR zone begin)** — A separate rung with only an `-(MCR)-` output instruction. The rung condition-in (RCI) controls whether the zone is **enabled** (TRUE) or **disabled** (FALSE).
- **MCR End (zone terminator)** — A separate rung with an unconditional `-(MCR)-` instruction (no input conditions before it) to **close** the zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** The MCR zone is **defined between** an MCR-start rung and an MCR-end rung. **All** rungs between them belong to the zone, regardless of their logic.

### 2.1.2 Behaviour of Outputs inside an MCR Zone

| Output Type | MCR Rung TRUE | MCR Rung FALSE |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Retentive Output (OTE)** | Operates normally | **Forced OFF** |
| **Retentive Output (OTL / OTU)** | Operates normally | **Retains last state** |
| **Timer (TON / TOF / RTO)** | Operates normally | TON/TOF reset; RTO retains accumulated value |
| **Counter (CTU / CTD)** | Operates normally | **Retains** accumulated and preset values |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MCR is the **electrical equivalent of an Emergency-Stop contactor** in PLC logic. One master relay de-energises an entire machine section.

---

## 2.2 Jump (JMP) and Label (LBL)

### 2.2.1 Operational Concept
A **JMP instruction** transfers program execution to a **LBL (label) instruction** of the same numeric identifier. The processor then continues execution **from the LBL** onward.

**Structure:**
- `-( JMP )-` instruction → placed in the rung where the skip is triggered
- `-( LBL )-` instruction → placed at the destination rung

### 2.2.2 Behaviour inside the Jumped Zone

| Element | Behaviour When JMP is TRUE |
|---|---|
| **Non-retentive outputs (OTE)** | **Retain last state** (not forced OFF — different from MCR!) |
| **Retentive outputs (OTL/OTU)** | Retain last state |
| **Timers (TON, TOF)** | **Frozen** (accumulated value retained, not incremented) |
| **Retentive timers (RTO)** | Retain accumulated value |
| **Math / Move instructions** | **Not executed** |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students confuse JMP and MCR. The key distinction: **JMP does not turn outputs OFF** — it simply **does not update** them. The outputs physically **hold** their last state via the output image table. **MCR actively forces non-retentive outputs OFF.**

### 2.2.3 Programming Rules (Allen-Bradley SLC 500)

1. The JMP and matching LBL must have the **same integer label** (0–255 typically).
2. **Forward jumps are permitted** (skip ahead). **Backward jumps** create **scan loops** and are **strongly discouraged** because they can cause **watchdog timer faults**.
3. JMP can jump **over** an MCR zone, but **MCR cannot control** the execution of a JMP/LBL pair — they are independent.
4. Nested JMPs are allowed.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Pattern:** JMP is widely used in **Manual/Auto mode selection** rungs. In Manual mode, a JMP skips the Auto-sequence block; in Auto mode, the JMP is FALSE and the Auto sequence executes normally.

---

## 2.3 Subroutine (SBR), Return (RET) and SBR File

### 2.3.1 Concept
A **subroutine** is a **separate, self-contained program file** that is executed **only when called** by a `-(SBR)-` (Jump-to-Subroutine) instruction in the main program. After the subroutine completes, control **returns** to the instruction following the original SBR call.

**SLC 500 Subroutine Files:** `S:3`, `S:4`, ..., `S:15` (up to 13 subroutines).

### 2.3.2 Structure
- **Main Program** (File 2): Contains `-(SBR)-` instruction(s) with subroutine file number.
- **Subroutine File** (e.g., S:3): Contains the subroutine logic. **First rung MUST be** `-(SBR file number)-` (the SBR label) and **last rung MUST be** `-(RET)-`.
- When the SBR is called, the program **suspends** the main program, executes S:3, hits RET, and **returns** to the next instruction in the main program.

> [!NOTE]
> Subroutines reduce the **average scan time** because the subroutine logic is only scanned when needed (e.g., a fault-handling routine is only scanned when a fault occurs).

### 2.3.3 Nested Subroutines
A subroutine **may call another subroutine** (nesting). Nesting depth is **limited** (typically 8 levels in SLC 500). Excessive nesting causes a **stack overflow fault**.

---

## 2.4 Immediate Input (IIN) and Immediate Output (IOT)

### 2.4.1 The Problem They Solve
Normally, the PLC **only updates** the I/O image table at the **start** (input scan) and **end** (output scan) of the program scan. If the program takes 50 ms to scan, an input change at millisecond 25 is **invisible** until the next scan.

### 2.4.2 Solution — Immediate I/O
- **`-(IIN)-`** — Forces the processor to **immediately read** the specified input address and **update** the input image table **at that point** in the program scan, bypassing the normal input scan phase.
- **`-(IOT)-`** — Forces the processor to **immediately write** the specified output address to the physical output module **at that point** in the program scan, bypassing the normal output scan phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use Case:** Critical safety inputs (e.g., light curtain, emergency stop) and high-speed outputs (e.g., a solenoid that must fire within 1 ms of a condition) use IIN/IOT to eliminate scan-time-induced latency.

---

## 2.5 Suspend (SUS) Instruction

### 2.5.1 Operational Concept
The `-(SUS)-` instruction, when its rung is TRUE, **suspends all outputs in the disabled zone** (forces them OFF), **halts the program scan**, and **places the processor in the Suspend Idle mode**. Used primarily for **debugging and commissioning**.

> [!WARNING]
> SUS is a **diagnostic-only** instruction. In a production machine, leaving a SUS instruction in the program is a **safety violation** — it will halt the entire machine.

---

## 2.6 Temporary End (TND) Instruction

### 2.6.1 Operational Concept
The `-(TND)-` instruction, when its rung is TRUE, **terminates the current program scan** and **begins the output scan immediately**, regardless of the position of the TND in the program. The next scan starts normally from the top.

> [!NOTE]
> **Use Case:** During **commissioning**, a TND placed after a few rungs lets the engineer test those rungs in isolation without the rest of the program overwriting values.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Instruction | Symbol | Function | Output Behaviour Inside Zone (when triggered) | File / Number Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MCR Start** | `-(MCR)-` | Begins a controlled zone | Non-retentive outputs → OFF | — |
| **MCR End** | `-(MCR)-` (unconditional) | Ends a controlled zone | — | — |
| **JMP** | `-(JMP)-` | Skips to LBL | **Outputs held** (last state) | Label 0–255 |
| **LBL** | `-(LBL)-` | Jump destination | — | Label 0–255 |
| **SBR Call** | `-(SBR)-` | Calls subroutine file | Executes the subroutine | S:3 to S:15 |
| **SBR Label** | `-(SBR n)-` | Subroutine entry point | — | n = file number |
| **RET** | `-(RET)-` | Returns to caller | — | — |
| **IIN** | `-(IIN)-` | Immediate input read | Updates input image | Address specified |
| **IOT** | `-(IOT)-` | Immediate output write | Updates physical output | Address specified |
| **SUS** | `-(SUS)-` | Suspends processor | All outputs OFF; scan halts | — |
| **TND** | `-(TND)-` | Ends current scan | Begins output scan early | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the Master Formula of KTU Board Questions:**
> $$\text{Program Control Instruction Effect} = f(\text{Rung Condition-In}, \text{Instruction Type}, \text{Output Retentivity})$$
> Always answer using these **three factors** — marks will follow.

---

## 2.8 Comparative Decision Matrix (KTU Board Favourite)

| Feature | JMP/LBL | MCR | SBR/RET |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Skips rungs?** | Yes | No (evaluates but disables) | No (transfers control out) |
| **Forces outputs OFF?** | No | Yes (non-retentive) | No |
| **Saves scan time?** | Yes | No | Yes (subroutine not scanned unless called) |
| **Nestable?** | Yes | Yes (up to 8) | Yes (up to 8) |
| **Common use** | Mode switching | Safety zones | Modular logic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because Program Control is a **logic-flow** topic (not a numerical formula topic), the "derivations" below are **exhaustive execution-flow analyses**. Every step of the PLC scan-cycle interaction is written out, with formal block-aligned mathematical expressions where applicable. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — MCR Zone Behaviour

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical):** A ladder program has the following rungs in the order shown:

- **Rung 1:** `Start_PB ─[ ]─/─[ ]─ Stop_PB ──────( MCR )─` *(MCR start, RCI = Start AND NOT Stop)*
- **Rung 2:** `Sensor_A ───────────────( OTE Motor_1 )─`
- **Rung 3:** `Sensor_B ───────────────( OTE Heater_1 )─`
- **Rung 4 (MCR End):** `──────────────────────( MCR )─` *(unconditional)*

**State A:** Start_PB = 1, Stop_PB = 0 → Rung 1 RCI = TRUE.
**State B:** Start_PB = 1, Stop_PB = 1 → Rung 1 RCI = FALSE.

### Step 1 — Rung 1 Evaluation (MCR Start Rung)
The processor evaluates the inputs `Start_PB` and `Stop_PB` in the input image table.

$$
\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 1}} = \text{Start\_PB} \land \overline{\text{Stop\_PB}}
$$

- In **State A:** $\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 1}} = 1 \cdot \overline{0} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$ → **MCR zone is ENABLED**.
- In **State B:** $\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 1}} = 1 \cdot \overline{1} = 1 \cdot 0 = 0$ → **MCR zone is DISABLED**.

### Step 2 — Rung 2 Evaluation (Motor_1)
The processor now evaluates Rung 2. Sensor_A is read.

$$
\text{Motor\_1} = \text{Sensor\_A} \quad \text{(if MCR enabled, otherwise forced 0)}
$$

- In **State A** (MCR enabled): $\text{Motor\_1} = \text{Sensor\_A}$ — **normal logic**.
- In **State B** (MCR disabled): $\text{Motor\_1} = 0$ — **forced OFF by MCR**, regardless of Sensor_A.

### Step 3 — Rung 3 Evaluation (Heater_1)
Same logic as Rung 2:

$$
\text{Heater\_1} = \text{Sensor\_B} \quad \text{(if MCR enabled, otherwise forced 0)}
$$

- **State A:** Operates normally.
- **State B:** Forced OFF.

### Step 4 — Rung 4 Evaluation (MCR End)
This rung is **unconditional** — it has no input contacts. It simply closes the MCR zone:

$$
\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 4}} = 1 \quad \text{(always TRUE)}
$$

The processor sees the second MCR instruction and **terminates the zone** at this point. Any rungs after Rung 4 are **outside the MCR zone** and are **unaffected** by the MCR condition.

### Step 5 — Output Scan
After the program scan completes, the output scan copies the output image table to physical outputs:

$$
\text{Physical Output} = \text{Output Image Table Bit}
$$

**Summary Table (State A vs State B):**

| Output | State A (MCR ENABLED) | State B (MCR DISABLED) |
|---|---|---|
| Motor_1 | = Sensor_A | **0 (forced)** |
| Heater_1 | = Sensor_B | **0 (forced)** |
| Any rung **after Rung 4** | Operates normally | Operates normally (unaffected) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students mark `Motor_1` as "1" in State B because they forget that the MCR instruction **overrides** the rung logic. Always trace the MCR zone carefully.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — JMP/LBL Skip Behaviour

**Problem Setup:**

- **Rung 10:** `Auto_Mode ─────────────( JMP LBL 50 )-`
- **Rungs 11–49:** Auto-sequence logic (e.g., 39 rungs of sequential operations).
- **Rung 50:** `──────( LBL 50 )─`
- **Rung 51:** `Manual_PB ─────────────( OTE Conveyor )─`

**State X:** Auto_Mode = 1 (TRUE).
**State Y:** Auto_Mode = 0 (FALSE).

### Step 1 — Rung 10 Evaluation
$$
\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 10}} = \text{Auto\_Mode}
$$

- **State X:** RCI = 1 → JMP is **executed**. The processor **records** the label (50) and **skips** to Rung 50.
- **State Y:** RCI = 0 → JMP is **not executed**. The processor **falls through** to Rung 11 normally.

### Step 2 — Rungs 11–49 (the Skipped Zone)
- **State X:** The processor **does not evaluate** Rungs 11–49. **Outputs in this zone retain their last state** (they are not forced OFF).
- **State Y:** The processor **evaluates** every rung normally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Representation of Skip:**
> $$\text{For any rung } k \in [11, 49]: \quad \text{Execution}_k = \begin{cases} \text{Not evaluated} & \text{if JMP active (State X)} \\ \text{Normal scan} & \text{if JMP inactive (State Y)} \end{cases}$$
> $$\text{Output}_k(\text{State X}, t+1) = \text{Output}_k(\text{State X}, t) \quad \text{(last state retained)}$$

### Step 3 — Rung 50 (LBL 50)
$$
\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 50}} = 1 \quad \text{(LBL has no input contacts)}
$$

The processor resumes scanning from Rung 50.

### Step 4 — Rung 51 (Conveyor)
$$
\text{Conveyor} = \text{Manual\_PB} \quad \text{(evaluated normally in both states)}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Zone | State X (JMP ACTIVE) | State Y (JMP INACTIVE) |
|---|---|---|
| Rungs 11–49 (Auto) | **Skipped — outputs held** | Evaluated normally |
| Rung 51 (Manual) | Evaluated normally | Evaluated normally |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Favourite Question):** If Motor_X is in Rung 20 (inside the JMP zone) and was ON before the JMP was activated, it **remains ON** during the skip. With MCR, it would have been **forced OFF**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — Subroutine Call/Return

**Problem Setup:**

- **Main Program (File 2), Rung 5:** `Fault_Detected ─────────( SBR S:3 )-`
- **Subroutine File S:3, Rung 1:** `──────( SBR S:3 )-` *(SBR label)*
- **Subroutine File S:3, Rung 2:** `Overheat_Sensor ──────( OTU Alarm_Relay )-`
- **Subroutine File S:3, Rung 3:** `────────────────────( RET )-` *(Return)*

**Condition:** Fault_Detected becomes TRUE at scan N.

### Step 1 — Scan N-1 (Pre-fault)
The main program scans normally. Fault_Detected = 0. SBR S:3 is **never executed**. Scan time is **short** (no subroutine overhead).

### Step 2 — Scan N (Fault Detected)
The processor evaluates Rung 5:
$$
\text{RCI}_{\text{Rung 5}} = \text{Fault\_Detected} = 1
$$

The SBR instruction is **executed**. The processor:
1. **Saves the return address** (Rung 6 in main program) onto the **subroutine stack**.
2. **Transfers control** to File S:3, Rung 1.

### Step 3 — Subroutine S:3 Execution
- **Rung 1 (SBR S:3 label):** No logic — entry point. $\text{RCI} = 1$.
- **Rung 2 (Alarm_Relay):**
  $$\text{Alarm\_Relay} = \overline{\text{Overheat\_Sensor}}$$
  (Latched OFF if overheat detected)
- **Rung 3 (RET):** Encountered unconditionally. Processor **pops the return address** from the stack and **returns to main program Rung 6**.

### Step 4 — Scan N+1 (Post-fault, SBR not called)
If Fault_Detected returns to 0, the SBR is **not called** in subsequent scans. The subroutine logic is **not evaluated** — saving scan time.

**Mathematical Summary:**
$$
\text{SBR Execution} = \begin{cases} \text{Evaluate File S:3, return to caller} & \text{if Fault\_Detected} = 1 \\ \text{Skip File S:3} & \text{if Fault\_Detected} = 0 \end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough — IIN/IOT Scan-Time Latency Elimination

**Problem Setup:**
- Normal scan time: $T_{\text{scan}} = 50 \text{ ms}$
- Critical input `E_Stop` is connected to Input `I:1/0`.
- Critical output `Brake_Solenoid` is connected to Output `O:2/0`.

**Without IIN/IOT:**

- E_Stop pressed at $t = 25 \text{ ms}$ mid-scan.
- Input image table is **not updated** until next input scan at $t = 50 \text{ ms}$.
- Worst-case latency: $T_{\text{latency}} \le T_{\text{scan}} = 50 \text{ ms}$.

**With IIN at the start of the program:**
- The processor executes `-(IIN I:1/0)-` immediately.
- Input image table is **updated on the spot**.
- E_Stop is detected within microseconds of the IIN instruction.

**With IOT for the critical output:**
- `-(IOT O:2/0)-` is executed the moment the rung becomes TRUE.
- The brake solenoid is energised **without waiting** for the output scan phase.

**Effective latency:**

$$
T_{\text{latency, IIN/IOT}} \ll T_{\text{scan}} \approx T_{\text{instruction execution}}
$$

Typically, $T_{\text{latency, IIN/IOT}} < 100 \mu s$ — a **500× improvement** over normal scan latency.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: "Why use IIN/IOT only for critical I/O and not for all I/O?" **Answer:** Immediate I/O instructions **slow down the scan** because they break the batch I/O optimisation. Use them **sparingly**, only for inputs/outputs that genuinely cannot tolerate scan-time delay.

---

## 3.5 Combined Analytical Matrix — All Instructions at a Glance

| Instruction | Trigger Condition | Effect on Output Image | Effect on Physical Output | Effect on Scan Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MCR (zone disabled)** | RCI of MCR start = FALSE | Non-retentive bits → 0 | Energised outputs → de-energised | No savings (still evaluates) |
| **JMP (active)** | RCI of JMP = TRUE | Skipped rungs: no change (held) | Held state | **Saves time** |
| **SBR call** | RCI of SBR = TRUE | Executes subroutine | Per subroutine logic | **Saves time** (when not called) |
| **IIN** | Always executed (or RCI) | Updates input image immediately | N/A (input) | **Adds time** (breaks batch update) |
| **IOT** | RCI of IOT = TRUE | N/A | Updates physical output immediately | **Adds time** |
| **SUS** | RCI of SUS = TRUE | All outputs forced OFF | All de-energised | **Halts scan** |
| **TND** | RCI of TND = TRUE | Ends program scan early | Output scan begins early | **Truncates scan** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Program Control Instruction Categories

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PLC Program Scan Begins] --> B{Program Control Instruction Active?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute Ladder Rung Sequentially]
    C --> D[Next Rung]
    D --> E{End of Program File?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Output Scan Phase]
    B -- Yes --> G{Which Instruction?}
    G -- MCR --> H[Disable Zone: Non-Retentive Outputs OFF]
    G -- JMP LBL --> I[Skip to Label: Outputs Held]
    G -- SBR --> J[Call Subroutine File: Execute and Return]
    G -- IIN --> K[Immediate Input Read: Update Image]
    G -- IOT --> L[Immediate Output Write: Update Physical]
    G -- SUS --> M[Suspend Processor: Halt All Outputs]
    G -- TND --> N[End Current Scan: Skip to Output Scan]
    H --> D
    I --> D
    J --> D
    K --> D
    L --> D
    M --> O[Suspend Idle Mode]
    N --> F
    F --> P[Housekeeping and Next Scan]
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a **flowchart representation of the PLC scan-cycle decision tree** when a program control instruction is encountered. For a physical schematic (e.g., an MCR zone ladder diagram), see the ASCII schematic below.

## 4.2 ASCII Ladder Schematic — MCR Zone

```
     Start    Stop             MCR
      PB       PB             ZONE
   ─┤ ├────────┤/├──────────( MCR )──────────────────   [Rung 1: MCR Start]
                                                        
      Sensor                                          ─   [Rung 2: Inside MCR Zone]
   ─┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( OTE Motor )─  
                                                        
      Sensor                                          ─   [Rung 3: Inside MCR Zone]
   ─┤ ├──────────────────────────────( OTE Heater )─   
                                                        
   ────────────────────────────────────( MCR )──────────   [Rung 4: MCR End, unconditional]
                                                        
      Mode_Sel                                         ─   [Rung 5: Outside MCR Zone]
   ─┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( OTE Lamp )─  
```

**Interpretation:**
- Rungs 2 and 3 are **inside** the MCR zone. When Rung 1 RCI is FALSE, Motor and Heater are **forced OFF**.
- Rung 5 (Lamp) is **outside** the MCR zone — it is **never** affected by the MCR condition.

## 4.3 ASCII Ladder Schematic — JMP/LBL Skip

```
   Auto_Mode
   ─┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( JMP LBL 99 )──   [Rung 10: JMP Trigger]

   Step1_Done                                           ─   [Rung 11-49: Skipped Zone]
   ─┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( OTE Valve1 )─   (39 rungs of auto-sequence)
   ...
   ─┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( OTE ValveN )─  

   ─────────────────────────────────────( LBL 99 )────   [Rung 50: Jump Destination]

   Manual_PB
   ─┤ ├─────────────────────────────────( OTE Conveyor )  [Rung 51: Always Scanned]
```

**Interpretation:**
- When Auto_Mode = TRUE, Rungs 11–49 are **skipped**; Valve1...ValveN **retain last state**.
- Rung 51 is **always evaluated** regardless of the JMP state.

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Subroutine Call/Return

```mermaid
graph TD
    Main1[Main Program File 2 - Rung 1 to 4] --> CallRung{Condition: Fault_Detected TRUE?}
    CallRung -- No --> Main2[Main Program File 2 - Rung 6 to End]
    CallRung -- Yes --> Push[Push Return Address onto Stack]
    Push --> Sub1[Subroutine S:3 Rung 1: SBR Label]
    Sub1 --> Sub2[Subroutine S:3 Rung 2: Execute Fault Logic]
    Sub2 --> Sub3[Subroutine S:3 Rung 3: RET Instruction]
    Sub3 --> Pop[Pop Return Address from Stack]
    Pop --> Main2
    Main2 --> EndProg[End of Main Program]
    EndProg --> OutScan[Output Scan Phase]
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — Immediate I/O Scan Override

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Normal Scan Start] --> B[Input Scan Phase]
    B --> C{IIN Instruction Encountered?}
    C -- No --> D[Continue Program Scan]
    C -- Yes --> E[Force Immediate Input Read]
    E --> D
    D --> F{IOT Instruction Encountered?}
    F -- No --> G[Continue Program Scan]
    F -- Yes --> H[Force Immediate Output Write]
    H --> G
    G --> I[End of Program Scan]
    I --> J[Output Scan Phase]
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice that IIN and IOT are **inline instructions** — they execute **exactly where they appear** in the ladder, breaking the standard input-then-output update model.

## 4.6 Master Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    PCI[Program Control Instructions] --> MCR[Master Control Reset]
    PCI --> JMP[Jump and Label]
    PCI --> SBR[Subroutine]
    PCI --> IIN[IIN]
    PCI --> IOT[IOT]
    PCI --> SUS[Suspend]
    PCI --> TND[Temporary End]
    MCR --> M1[Forces non-retentive outputs OFF]
    JMP --> J1[Skips rungs, outputs held]
    SBR --> S1[Transfers control to subroutine file]
    IIN --> II1[Forces immediate input image update]
    IOT --> IO1[Forces immediate physical output update]
    SUS --> SU1[Halts processor, all outputs OFF]
    TND --> T1[Ends scan early, output scan begins]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Differentiate between Jump (JMP) and Master Control Reset (MCR) instructions in PLC programming."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | JMP/LBL | MCR |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Skips a section of the program to a Label | Creates a controlled zone with forced output behaviour |
| **Effect on non-retentive outputs in zone** | **Retain last state** (no change) | **Forced OFF** |
| **Effect on retentive outputs/timers** | Held in current state | Retain accumulated value |
| **Scan time** | **Saved** (skipped rungs not evaluated) | **Not saved** (rungs still evaluated) |
| **Typical use** | Mode switching (Auto/Manual) | Safety zones, master relay simulation |

(1 mark each for the first three distinguishing points, with 1 mark for correct use-case identification. Total: 3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is the purpose of a subroutine in PLC programming? Name the file numbers reserved for subroutines in an Allen-Bradley SLC 500 PLC."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **subroutine** is a separate program file containing a self-contained block of logic that is executed **only when called** by a subroutine (SBR) instruction in the main program. After execution, a Return (RET) instruction transfers control **back to the instruction following the original call** in the main program.

**Benefits (1 mark):**
- Reduces average scan time (subroutine logic is scanned only when needed).
- Enables modular programming (one subroutine per machine station).
- Simplifies maintenance and troubleshooting.

**File Number Range (1 mark):** Subroutines in the SLC 500 are stored in files **S:3 through S:15** (13 subroutine files available).

**RET instruction (1 mark):** Marks the end of the subroutine; causes a return to the calling program.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **module-based internal choice**. The model questions below replicate this exact pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the working of the **Master Control Reset (MCR)** instruction in a PLC with the help of a ladder diagram. Discuss the behaviour of non-retentive and retentive outputs inside an MCR zone when the MCR rung is FALSE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat ladder diagram, explain the operation of the **Jump (JMP)** and **Label (LBL)** instructions. Compare the behaviour of outputs inside a JMP zone with that of an MCR zone. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Definition of MCR (1 mark):**

> MCR is a program control output instruction that creates a **programmable zone** in the ladder program. When the rung containing the MCR start instruction is FALSE, **all non-retentive outputs within the zone are forced OFF**, regardless of the rung logic.

**Step 2 — Ladder Diagram (2 marks):**

```
     Start    Stop
      PB       PB
   ─┤ ├────────┤/├─────────────( MCR )──────────  [Rung 1: MCR Start]
                                               
      Sensor_A                                ─
   ─┤ ├────────────────────────────( OTE M1 )─   [Rung 2: Inside zone]
                                               
      Sensor_B                                ─
   ─┤ ├────────────────────────────( OTE H1 )─   [Rung 3: Inside zone]
```
