```
   ──────────────────────────────────( MCR )─────  [Rung 4: MCR End]
                                               
      Always_On                               ─
   ─┤ ├────────────────────────────( OTE Lamp )  [Rung 5: Outside zone]
```

**Step 3 — Behavioural Analysis (2 marks):**

| Element | MCR Rung TRUE | MCR Rung FALSE |
|---|---|---|
| **OTE Motor (non-retentive)** | Operates normally | **Forced OFF** |
| **OTL/OTU Heater (retentive)** | Operates normally | **Retains last state** |
| **TON Timer** | Operates normally | **Reset** (accumulator = 0) |
| **RTO Timer** | Operates normally | **Retains** accumulated value |

**Step 4 — Engineering Significance (1 mark):**

MCR simulates an **electromechanical master control relay** in software. It enables **one master signal** (e.g., E-Stop) to de-energise an entire machine section. The MCR instruction can be **nested up to 8 levels** in the SLC 500.

**Step 5 — Key Distinction (1 mark):**

> The MCR zone is **defined between** an MCR-start rung and an MCR-end rung. **All** rungs between them are subject to the MCR control, **regardless of their individual logic**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Definition of JMP/LBL (1 mark):**

> A `-(JMP)-` instruction transfers program execution to a `-(LBL)-` instruction of the same numeric label. The processor then continues scanning from the LBL rung onward, **skipping** all rungs in between.

**Step 2 — Ladder Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   Auto_Mode
   ─┤ ├──────────────────────────( JMP LBL 77 )──  [Rung 1: Jump Trigger]
                                               
      Step1                                    ─
   ─┤ ├──────────────────────────( OTE Valve1 )  [Rung 2-10: Skipped Zone]
      ...
   ─┤ ├──────────────────────────( OTE ValveN )  
                                               
   ─────────────────────────────────( LBL 77 )──  [Rung 11: Jump Destination]
                                               
   Manual_PB
   ─┤ ├──────────────────────────( OTE Conveyor )  [Rung 12: Always Scanned]
```

**Step 3 — Behaviour Inside JMP Zone (2 marks):**

When JMP is active (Auto_Mode = TRUE):
- Rungs 2–10 are **not evaluated**.
- Valve1 ... ValveN **retain their last state** (held in output image table).
- The processor resumes scanning at Rung 11.

**Step 4 — Comparison with MCR (2 marks):**

| Aspect | JMP Zone | MCR Zone |
|---|---|---|
| **Rungs evaluated?** | **No** (skipped) | **Yes** (but outputs forced) |
| **Non-retentive outputs** | **Held** | **Forced OFF** |
| **Retentive outputs** | Held | Held |
| **Scan time saved?** | **Yes** | **No** |
| **Use case** | Auto/Manual mode switching | Safety master relay |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (a) and (b):**
> **Most students lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Drawing the MCR zone without the **unconditional MCR-end rung** (forgetting the second MCR instruction).
> 2. Confusing JMP and MCR output behaviour — saying JMP "turns outputs OFF" instead of "holds the last state."
> 3. Not drawing the ladder diagram at all — KTU board examiners award **2 marks** for a clear ladder diagram with proper rung numbers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Subroutine (SBR)** and **Return (RET)** instructions in PLC programming. Draw the ladder diagram structure for a main program that calls a subroutine. State **two advantages** of using subroutines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Immediate Input (IIN)** and **Immediate Output (IOT)** instructions. Why are they used? Give **one industrial application** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**

> A **subroutine** is a self-contained program file that is executed **only when called** by an `-(SBR)-` instruction in the main program. The subroutine ends with a `-(RET)-` instruction, which returns control to the instruction **following the original SBR call** in the main program.

**Step 2 — File Allocation (1 mark):**

In the Allen-Bradley SLC 500, subroutines are stored in **file numbers S:3 through S:15** (13 files available).

**Step 3 — Ladder Diagram Structure (3 marks):**

**Main Program (File 2):**
```
   Fault_Detected
   ─┤ ├────────────────────────────( SBR S:3 )──  [Rung 1: Call Subroutine]
                                               
   Conveyor_Start
   ─┤ ├────────────────────────────( OTE Conveyor)  [Rung 2: Execution resumes here after RET]
```

**Subroutine File (S:3):**
```
   ───────────────────────────────( SBR S:3 )──  [Rung 1: Subroutine Entry Label]
                                               
   Overheat_Sensor
   ─┤/├────────────────────────────( OTU Alarm )  [Rung 2: Fault Logic]
                                               
   ───────────────────────────────( RET )────────  [Rung 3: Return to Main]
```

**Step 4 — Two Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **Reduced average scan time** — The subroutine logic is scanned **only when called**. The remaining scans skip the subroutine entirely, saving milliseconds per scan.
2. **Modular programming** — Each machine station or fault-handling routine can be a **separate subroutine**, simplifying development, debugging, and maintenance.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — The Problem (1 mark):**

In normal PLC operation, the input image table is updated **only at the start** of the scan and the output image table is written to physical outputs **only at the end** of the scan. The worst-case **latency** for any input or output equals the **full scan time** $T_{\text{scan}}$.

$$T_{\text{latency, normal}} \le T_{\text{scan}}$$

For a typical SLC 500 scan of 50 ms, this is unacceptable for safety-critical signals.

**Step 2 — Immediate Input (IIN) (2 marks):**

The `-(IIN)-` instruction **forces the processor to immediately read** the specified input address and **update the input image table** at that exact point in the program scan, bypassing the normal input scan phase.

**Ladder Example:**
```
   Critical_Sensor
   ─┤ ├─────( IIN I:1/0 )────  [Forces immediate read of I:1/0]
        │
        └─( OTE Emergency_Action )  [Uses the freshly updated value]
```

**Step 3 — Immediate Output (IOT) (2 marks):**

The `-(IOT)-` instruction **forces the processor to immediately write** the specified output address to the **physical output module**, bypassing the normal output scan phase.

**Ladder Example:**
```
   E_Stop_Pressed
   ─┤ ├─────( IOT O:2/0 )────  [Immediately de-energises Brake Solenoid O:2/0]
```

**Step 4 — Industrial Applications (2 marks):**

- **IIN use case:** Reading an **emergency stop button** mid-scan. The E-Stop signal cannot wait up to 50 ms — it must be acted upon within microseconds.
- **IOT use case:** Activating a **brake solenoid** on a press machine. The brake must engage within the same scan as the over-travel detection, not at the output scan phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (a) and (b):**
> **Common mark-loss points:**
> 1. Students draw the subroutine **without the SBR entry label** on the first rung of the subroutine file. **Always include** `-(SBR S:n)-` as the first rung.
> 2. Students confuse IIN with IOT — IIN **reads** the input image; IOT **writes** to physical outputs. The direction matters.
> 3. Failing to give a **specific industrial application** — generic answers like "for fast response" get only 1 mark. A named example (E-Stop, light curtain, brake solenoid) gets the full 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 4 High-Yield Revision Notes**

### **The Seven Program Control Instructions**

1. **MCR (Start) + MCR (End)** — Creates a **zone**; non-retentive outputs forced **OFF** when MCR is FALSE. Up to **8 nested zones**.
2. **JMP + LBL** — **Skips** a section of program; outputs **retained** (held). Can save scan time. **No backward jumps.**
3. **SBR (call) + SBR (label) + RET** — Modular subroutine execution in files **S:3 to S:15**. Average scan time **reduced**. **Nesting limit: 8 levels**.
4. **IIN** — Forces **immediate input image update** at the instruction location. Use only for **critical inputs**.
5. **IOT** — Forces **immediate physical output write** at the instruction location. Use only for **critical outputs**.
6. **SUS** — **Suspends processor**; all outputs OFF; processor enters Suspend Idle mode. **Diagnostic-only.**
7. **TND** — **Ends the current scan** early; output scan begins immediately. **Commissioning tool.**

### **The Three "Golden Rules" of KTU Board Answers**

1. **State the trigger condition first** (RCI of the instruction = TRUE/FALSE).
2. **State the effect on outputs second** (Forced OFF / Held / Not Evaluated).
3. **State the engineering purpose third** (Mode switching, Safety, Modularisation, Latency).

### **The Master Comparison**

| Instruction | Forces Output OFF? | Skips Rung? | Saves Scan Time? | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MCR | Yes (non-retentive) | No (evaluates) | No | Safety zones |
| JMP | No (holds) | Yes | **Yes** | Mode switching |
| SBR | No | N/A (transfers) | **Yes** | Modular logic |
| IIN/IOT | N/A | N/A | No (adds time) | Critical I/O |
| SUS | Yes (all) | Yes (halts) | N/A (halts) | Debugging |
| TND | No | Yes (stops scan) | **Yes** | Commissioning |

### **Common KTU Board Errors to Avoid**

- ❌ Saying "JMP turns outputs OFF" → ✅ "JMP **holds** the last state of outputs."
- ❌ Forgetting the **unconditional MCR end rung** → ✅ Always draw the second MCR instruction.
- ❌ Confusing **SBR (call)** with **SBR (label)** → ✅ The label is the first rung of the **subroutine file**; the call is in the **main program**.
- ❌ Forgetting that **IIN/IOT add scan time** → ✅ Use sparingly; not for every I/O point.
- ❌ Writing "subroutine" as a file number S:2 → ✅ Main program is S:2; subroutines are S:3 to S:15.

### **Scan-Cycle Memory Aid**

> **"I-P-O-H"** — **I**nput scan, **P**rogram scan, **O**utput scan, **H**ousekeeping.
> Program control instructions manipulate the **P**rogram scan phase. IIN manipulates the **I**nput phase. IOT manipulates the **O**utput phase. SUS halts the **H**ousekeeping transition.

### **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE)**

- **Definition** → 1 mark
- **Ladder diagram** → 2–3 marks
- **Truth table / Behavioural analysis** → 2–3 marks
- **Comparison table** → 2–3 marks
- **Industrial application** → 1–2 marks

**Total: 14 marks (Part B) | 3 marks (Part A)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies on PLC Ladder Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies on PLC Ladder Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Case Studies on PLC Ladder Programming

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **PLC Ladder Logic Case Study** is a structured industrial problem statement that describes a real-world automation scenario along with its associated **input sensors, output actuators, sequence of operations, interlocks, and timing requirements**, and is solved by translating that narrative into a **Ladder Diagram (LD)** conforming to the **IEC 61131-3** standard.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OERUT612), a case study is treated as an **integrative engineering problem** that consolidates knowledge of:
- Bit logic instructions (NO, NC, OUT, SET, RESET)
- Timer instructions (TON, TOF, TP)
- Counter instructions (CTU, CTD)
- Latching/Interlocking patterns
- Sequencer / Drum programming concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4):** Students must be able to **interpret an industrial automation problem statement, identify I/Os, design the Ladder Logic, and justify the sequence of operations** using standard PLC instructions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC ladder program as a **recipe written for a robotic chef**. The *inputs* are the ingredients arriving on the conveyor (sensor signals), the *outputs* are the cooking actions (motors, valves, heaters), and the *ladder rungs* are the step-by-step instructions: "if the dough is ready **AND** the oven is preheated, **THEN** open the oven door."

A *case study* is simply that **recipe in story form** — for example, "Design the ladder logic for a traffic light that turns red after 30 seconds and green after 45 seconds." The PLC programmer's job is to convert this *story* into a *ladder diagram* that a PLC CPU can execute scan-by-scan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (per IEC 61131-3):** A typical scan cycle of a mid-range PLC is **10–50 ms**, which is the unit of time within which all ladder rungs must be evaluated top-to-bottom, left-to-right.

---

## 2. Common Categories of Case Studies in KTU Module 4

| # | Case Study Category | Primary Concept Tested |
|---|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Traffic Light Controller | Timer sequencing, state-based logic |
| 2 | Conveyor Belt / Sorting System | Sensor-driven branching, latching |
| 3 | Elevator Control | Up/Down counting, floor-level detection |
| 4 | Bottle Filling Plant | Level sensor + counter + timer |
| 5 | Star–Delta Motor Starter | Time-delayed contactor switching |
| 6 | Water Tank Level Control | Comparator logic, pump interlocking |
| 7 | Washing Machine Controller | Sequencer/drum, multi-stage timing |
| 8 | Automatic Door (Sliding) | Edge-triggered limit switches |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 ESE, the most frequently tested case studies (based on standard 2024–2025 pattern) are **Traffic Light**, **Bottle Filling**, and **Star–Delta Starter**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Method to Solve Any PLC Case Study

A KTU-grade solution to a ladder-logic case study **must** follow this five-step template:

### Step 1 — I/O Address Assignment
List every physical input and output with a logical tag.
- Inputs: `I0.0`, `I0.1`, … (or `X0`, `X1` on Mitsubishi)
- Outputs: `Q0.0`, `Q0.1`, … (or `Y0`, `Y1`)

### Step 2 — Identify Sequence of Operations
Translate the narrative into a **numbered state diagram**:
1. Initial State → 2. Start Condition → 3. Process A → 4. Process B → 5. Stop.

### Step 3 — Select PLC Instructions
Match each state to a ladder element:
- **NO contact** `—| |—` for normally-open (active-high) inputs
- **NC contact** `—|/|—` for normally-closed (active-low) inputs
- **Coil** `—( )—` for momentary energization
- **Latch** `—(S)—` and **Unlatch** `—(R)—` for retentive outputs
- **TON Timer** for ON-delay
- **CTU Counter** for event counting

### Step 4 — Draw the Ladder Diagram
Two vertical rails (L1 = positive, L2 = negative/return), rungs evaluated **top → bottom, left → right** per scan.

### Step 5 — Verify & Justify
- Check **all** boundary conditions (initial state, power-on reset, simultaneous presses).
- Justify **why** an NC contact is used (safety / interlock).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Instruction Sheet

| Symbol | Instruction | Behavior | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `—|  |—` | NO (Normally Open) | Conducts only when input = 1 | Start push-button |
| `—| / |—` | NC (Normally Closed) | Conducts only when input = 0 | Stop push-button (fail-safe) |
| `—( )—` | Output Coil | Energizes the addressed bit | Motor contactor |
| `—(S)—` | Set (Latch) | Bit latched ON until reset | Sequential stages |
| `—(R)—` | Reset (Unlatch) | Bit forced OFF | Stage termination |
| `TON T37, +T#30s` | On-Delay Timer | Output ON after time delay | Stage dwell time |
| `CTU C1, +5` | Up Counter | Counts up to preset | Counting bottles |
| `==` | Comparator | Equality check | Floor level match |
| `>=` | Comparator | Greater-or-equal | Tank full |

### 2.3 Boolean Form of a Ladder Rung (Conceptual Translation)

Each rung implements a Boolean expression of the form:

$$
Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) \quad \text{where} \quad X_i \in \{0,1\}
$$

For a series connection (AND logic):

$$
Y = X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdot X_3
$$

For a parallel connection (OR logic):

$$
Y = X_1 + X_2 + X_3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** A process engineer can read these Boolean equations to *predict* the output for any input combination — this is the same skill used in designing **Failsafe Logic** for nuclear and aerospace PLC systems (per IEC 61508 SIL ratings).

---

## 2.4 Timers & Counters — Boundary Conditions

For a **TON (On-Delay Timer)** with preset $T_p$ and current value $T_c$:

$$
\text{Output} = 
\begin{cases}
0, & T_c < T_p \\
1, & T_c \geq T_p
\end{cases}
$$

For a **CTU (Up-Counter)** with preset $C_p$ and current value $C_c$:

$$
\text{Output} = 
\begin{cases}
0, & C_c < C_p \\
1, & C_c \geq C_p
\end{cases}
$$

These are the **only two conditions** a KTU examiner will test for timer/counter outputs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study A — Traffic Light Controller (Sequential Timers)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> "Design a PLC ladder program for a **4-way traffic intersection** with Red (R), Yellow (Y), and Green (G) lamps. The sequence is: **R → G → Y → R** with timings **30 s, 45 s, 5 s** respectively. The cycle must repeat indefinitely. A start push-button (PB) begins the cycle; a stop push-button halts it safely (returning all lamps to RED)."

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — I/O Assignment

| Address | Tag | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | PB_START | NO | Start push-button |
| I0.1 | PB_STOP | NC | Stop push-button (fail-safe) |
| Q0.0 | L_RED | Output | Red lamp contactor |
| Q0.1 | L_GRN | Output | Green lamp contactor |
| Q0.2 | L_YEL | Output | Yellow lamp contactor |

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Sequence of Operations (State Table)

| State | Active Output | Duration | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|
| S0 | RED | 30 s | S1 |
| S1 | GREEN | 45 s | S2 |
| S2 | YELLOW | 5 s | S0 |
| Stop | ALL RED | — | Idle |

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Ladder Logic Construction (Rung-by-Rung)

**Rung 1 — Master Latch (Cycle Enable):**
$$\text{ENABLE} = \text{PB\_START} + (\text{ENABLE} \cdot \overline{\text{PB\_STOP}})$$

When PB_START is pressed, ENABLE latches. It only resets when PB_STOP (NC) opens.

**Rung 2 — Red Lamp Logic:**
$$\text{L\_RED} = \text{ENABLE} \cdot (\overline{\text{T\_GRN}} \cdot \overline{\text{T\_YEL}}) + \text{STOPPED}$$

**Rung 3 — Green Lamp Logic with Timer:**
$$\text{L\_GRN} = \text{ENABLE} \cdot \overline{\text{T\_YEL}} \cdot \overline{\text{STOP}}$$
$$\text{T\_GRN: TON, Preset} = 45\,\text{s}$$

**Rung 4 — Yellow Lamp Logic with Timer:**
$$\text{L\_YEL} = \text{T\_GRN\_{\text{Done}}} \cdot \overline{\text{STOP}}$$
$$\text{T\_YEL: TON, Preset} = 5\,\text{s}$$

**Rung 5 — Reset Cycle:**
When T_YEL_Done fires, it resets the cycle to S0 by using the master latch to release GREEN and re-energize RED.

### 3.1.5 Boolean Simplification

The full boolean for the green lamp can be simplified:

$$
\text{L\_GRN} = \text{ENABLE} \cdot \overline{\text{T\_YEL}} \cdot \text{PB\_STOP\_NC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how an **NC contact is used for STOP** (I0.1). This is a fundamental **fail-safe principle** in PLC design: if the wire to the STOP button breaks, the system treats it as a stop command — the same principle used in **emergency stop (E-Stop)** circuits per ISO 13850.

---

## 3.2 Case Study B — Star–Delta Motor Starter (3-Phase Induction Motor)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> "Design a PLC ladder program for an automatic **Star–Delta starter** for a 3-phase induction motor. Sequence: Press START → Motor contactor (K1) ON, Star contactor (K2) ON for 5 s → K2 OFF, pause 100 ms (to prevent short-circuit), Delta contactor (K3) ON. STOP must immediately de-energize all contactors. Include electrical and mechanical interlocks."

### 3.2.2 I/O Assignment

| Address | Tag | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | PB_START | NO | Start button |
| I0.1 | PB_STOP | NC | Stop button |
| I0.2 | OVL | NC | Overload relay contact |
| Q0.0 | K_MAIN | Output | Main contactor |
| Q0.1 | K_STAR | Output | Star contactor |
| Q0.2 | K_DELT | Output | Delta contactor |

### 3.2.3 Ladder Logic — Rung Construction

**Rung 1 — Master Latch with Overload Interlock:**

$$
\text{MASTER} = (\text{PB\_START} + \text{MASTER}) \cdot \overline{\text{PB\_STOP}} \cdot \overline{\text{OVL}}
$$

**Rung 2 — Main Contactor K_MAIN:**

$$
K\_\text{MAIN} = \text{MASTER} \cdot \overline{\text{PB\_STOP}} \cdot \overline{\text{OVL}}
$$

**Rung 3 — Star Contactor K_STAR (ON for 5 s):**

$$
K\_\text{STAR} = \text{MASTER} \cdot \overline{K\_\text{DELTA}} \cdot \overline{T\_5s\_done}}
$$

The **NC contact of K_DELTA** is the **electrical interlock** that prevents Star and Delta from energizing simultaneously — a critical safety requirement.

**Rung 4 — Delta Contactor K_DELTA (After 5 s + 100 ms):**

$$
K\_\text{DELTA} = T\_\text{STAR\_done} \cdot T\_\text{100ms\_done} \cdot \text{MASTER} \cdot \overline{K\_\text{STAR}}
$$

**Rung 5 — Timer Definitions:**

$$
T\_\text{STAR: TON, Preset} = 5{,}000\,\text{ms} \quad ; \quad T\_\text{GAP: TON, Preset} = 100\,\text{ms}
$$

### 3.2.4 Verification of Interlock Logic

For simultaneous energization to be impossible, the following must always hold:

$$
K\_\text{STAR} \cdot K\_\text{DELTA} = 0
$$

Substituting the rung equations:

$$
(\text{MASTER} \cdot \overline{K\_\text{DELTA}} \cdot \overline{T\_5s}) \cdot (T\_\text{5s} \cdot T\_\text{100ms} \cdot \overline{K\_\text{STAR}}) = 0
$$

This product is **identically zero** because:
- The first bracket contains $\overline{K\_\text{DELTA}}$.
- The second bracket contains $K\_\text{DELTA}$ (transitively, since $K\_\text{DELTA}$ can only be true when the first bracket's $K\_\text{DELTA}$ is also true via different path — which is *impossible* due to the interlock term).

$$\therefore \text{Interlock} \equiv \text{SAFE} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Case Study C — Bottle Filling Plant (Counter + Timer + Sensor)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> "A conveyor carries empty bottles past a **proximity sensor (PS)**. When PS detects a bottle, the conveyor stops for **2 s** while a **solenoid valve (SV)** fills the bottle. After 2 s, the conveyor restarts. When **12 bottles** have been filled, the conveyor stops and a **completion lamp (L)** glows. Pressing RESET restarts the process."

### 3.3.2 I/O Assignment

| Address | Tag | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | PS | NO | Proximity sensor |
| I0.1 | PB_RESET | NO | Reset push-button |
| Q0.0 | CONV | Output | Conveyor motor |
| Q0.1 | SV | Output | Solenoid valve |
| Q0.2 | L_DONE | Output | Completion lamp |

### 3.3.3 Ladder Logic

**Rung 1 — Bottle Detection (Edge Detection via SET):**

$$
\text{FLAG} = \text{PS} \uparrow \quad \text{(rising edge of PS)}
$$

In ladder form, this is implemented using a one-shot (OSR / EU) instruction or a self-resetting contact.

**Rung 2 — Conveyor Stop When Bottle Present:**

$$
\text{CONV} = \overline{\text{FLAG}} \cdot \overline{\text{CT\_DONE}}
$$

**Rung 3 — Solenoid Valve Timing:**

$$
\text{SV} = \text{FLAG} \cdot \overline{T\_2s\_done}}
$$

$$
T\_\text{FILL: TON, Preset} = 2{,}000\,\text{ms}
$$

**Rung 4 — Counter (Bottle Count):**

$$
\text{CT\_BOTTLE: CTU, Preset} = 12, \text{ Count input} = T\_\text{FILL\_done} \uparrow
$$

**Rung 5 — Completion Lamp:**

$$
\text{L\_DONE} = \text{CT\_BOTTLE.done}
$$

**Rung 6 — Reset on PB_RESET:**

$$
\text{CT\_BOTTLE: Reset when PB\_RESET} = 1
$$

### 3.3.4 Time-Cycle Mathematical Model

If each bottle takes $T_{\text{cycle}} = 2\,\text{s}$ for filling, the time to fill 12 bottles is:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 12 \times 2\,\text{s} = 24\,\text{s} \quad \text{(excluding conveyor transit time)}
$$

If the conveyor runs at $v$ m/s and bottles are spaced $\Delta x$ m apart, the additional time per cycle is $\Delta x / v$. Thus the realistic cycle time is:

$$
T_{\text{real}} = 12 \times \left( 2 + \frac{\Delta x}{v} \right) \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This same ladder template — counter + timer + sensor — is the blueprint used in **pharmaceutical packaging lines, beverage plants, and automotive paint shops** worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ladder Logic Rung Format (Standard IEC 61131-3)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rungA[Rung 1 - Master Latch]
        L1A[L1 Power Rail] --> N1A[PB_START NO]
        N1A --> N2A[Master NC]
        N2A --> N3A[Out MASTER]
        L1A --> N4A[Master NO]
        N4A --> N3A
    end
    L2A[L2 Return Rail] --- N3A
```

## 4.2 Traffic Light Case Study — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Idle State - All RED] -->|PB_START pressed| s1A[State S0 - RED ON]
    s1A -->|T_RED 30s done| s2A[State S1 - GREEN ON]
    s2A -->|T_GRN 45s done| s3A[State S2 - YELLOW ON]
    s3A -->|T_YEL 5s done| s1A
    startA -->|PB_STOP pressed| stopA[Safe State - All RED]
    s1A -->|PB_STOP pressed| stopA
    s2A -->|PB_STOP pressed| stopA
    s3A -->|PB_STOP pressed| stopA
```

## 4.3 Star–Delta Starter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputsA[Input Section]
        inA1[PB_START NO] -->|I0.0| plcA
        inA2[PB_STOP NC] -->|I0.1| plcA
        inA3[OVL NC] -->|I0.2| plcA
    end
    plcA[PLC CPU - Scan Engine]
    subgraph logicA[Internal Logic]
        logicA1[Master Latch] --> logicA2[Timer T1 5s]
        logicA2 --> logicA3[Timer T2 100ms]
        logicA3 --> logicA4[Interlock Block]
    end
    plcA --- logicA
    subgraph outputsA[Output Section]
        plcA -->|Q0.0| outA1[K_MAIN Contactor]
        plcA -->|Q0.1| outA2[K_STAR Contactor]
        plcA -->|Q0.2| outA3[K_DELTA Contactor]
    end
    outA1 --> motorA[3-Phase Induction Motor]
    outA2 --> motorA
    outA3 --> motorA
```

## 4.4 Bottle Filling — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sA[Conveyor Running] -->|PS detects bottle| sB[Conveyor STOP]
    sB -->|Immediate| sC[Solenoid Valve OPEN]
    sC -->|2s timer| sD[Solenoid Valve CLOSE]
    sD -->|Counter +1| sE{Count = 12?}
    sE -->|No| sA
    sE -->|Yes| sF[Conveyor STOP and LAMP ON]
    sF -->|PB_RESET pressed| sA
```

## 4.5 Ladder Rung Anatomy (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1B[L1] --> X1[Input A - NO Contact]
    X1 --> X2[Input B - NC Contact]
    X2 --> X3[Timer TON Preset 5s]
    X3 --> OUTX[Output Coil or SET Reset]
    OUTX --> L2B[L2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading convention:** All contacts to the **left** of the coil are evaluated **left-to-right**; rungs are evaluated **top-to-bottom** in each PLC scan.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Set A]**
**Differentiate between a NO contact and an NC contact in PLC ladder logic. Why is the STOP push-button always wired as NC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **NO (Normally Open)** contact conducts current **only when the input bit is logic 1**; symbolically represented as `—| |—`. It is used for **start** commands and active sensors.
- **NC (Normally Closed)** contact conducts current **only when the input bit is logic 0**; symbolically represented as `—|/|—`. It is used for **stop** commands and fail-safe interlocks.
- The STOP push-button is wired NC because of the **fail-safe principle**: if the connecting wire is broken (loose terminal, cut cable), the contact opens, and the PLC interprets the open circuit as a STOP command, immediately halting the process. *(1 mark for safety reasoning — KTU marker expects this explicitly.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**Explain the function of a TON (On-Delay Timer) instruction in PLC ladder logic with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **TON (On-Delay Timer)** starts timing when its **enabling input becomes logic 1**, and its output bit becomes logic 1 **only after the current value equals or exceeds the preset time**. The current value resets to zero the moment the enabling input goes to logic 0.
- **Equation:** $\text{Output} = 1$ if $T_c \geq T_p$, else $0$.
- **Example:** In a Star–Delta starter, the star contactor is held ON for 5 s using a TON timer; once the timer completes, the star contactor is de-energized and the delta contactor is energized.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q4 (a)+(b)]**
> **(a)** Design a PLC ladder logic program for a **4-way traffic light controller** with the sequence R → G → Y → R and timings **30 s, 45 s, 5 s**. Draw the complete ladder diagram using at least three timers and a master latching coil. Tabulate the I/O assignment. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the function of the **interlock contact** used in the Star–Delta starter ladder logic. Why is the **overload relay (OLR)** contact wired as NC in the master rung? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — I/O Table (1 mark):**

| Address | Tag | Description |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | PB_START | Start push-button (NO) |
| I0.1 | PB_STOP | Stop push-button (NC) |
| Q0.0 | L_RED | Red lamp |
| Q0.1 | L_GRN | Green lamp |
| Q0.2 | L_YEL | Yellow lamp |

**Step 2 — Ladder Diagram (5 marks):**

- **Rung 1 — Master Latch:** `PB_START` (NO) **OR** `M` (NO) in parallel, both in series with `PB_STOP` (NC) and `M` (NC, self-reset), driving coil `M` (master).
- **Rung 2 — RED:** `M` (NO) in series with `(T_GRN done) (NC) AND (T_YEL done) (NC)` driving coil `L_RED`.
- **Rung 3 — GREEN:** `M` (NO) in series with `L_RED` (NC) and `T_YEL done` (NC) driving coil `L_GRN`, which also enables **TON T_GRN, preset 45 s**.
- **Rung 4 — YELLOW:** `T_GRN done` (NO) in series with `L_GRN` (NC) and `M` (NO) driving coil `L_YEL`, which also enables **TON T_YEL, preset 5 s**.
- **Rung 5 — Cycle Reset:** `T_YEL done` (NO) in parallel with `PB_STOP` (NC inverted logic) resets the master `M` only on STOP; cycle is implicit.

**Step 3 — Boolean Equations (1 mark):**

$$
M = (\text{PB\_START} + M) \cdot \overline{\text{PB\_STOP}}
$$

$$
L\_\text{GRN} = M \cdot \overline{\text{PB\_STOP}} \cdot \overline{T\_\text{YEL\_done}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Function of the Interlock Contact (3 marks):**
- The **interlock contact** is an **NC contact of one contactor placed in series with another contactor's coil**. In a Star–Delta starter, the NC contact of K_DELTA is placed in the rung of K_STAR, and the NC contact of K_STAR is placed in the rung of K_DELTA.
- This guarantees that **K_STAR and K_DELTA can never be energized simultaneously**, which would otherwise cause a **dead short circuit** across the line contactor.

**Why OLR is Wired as NC (4 marks):**
- The **Overload Relay (OLR)** protects the motor from sustained over-current.
- Its contact is wired **NC (Normally Closed)** in the master rung so that in a healthy state the rung is complete and the motor can run.
- On an overload, the OLR contact **opens**, which breaks the master rung and de-energizes **K_MAIN, K_STAR, and K_DELTA simultaneously**, isolating the motor from the supply.
- The NC wiring is also **fail-safe**: if the OLR coil is damaged or disconnected, the contact remains in the same state as a trip — the motor is protected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write `K_DELTA = T_5s_done` directly **without** the interlock NC contact of K_STAR. This is a **2-mark deduction** in valuation. Always include the interlock — it is a **mandatory safety element** as per IEC 60204-1.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4, Q4 (a)+(b)]**
> **(a)** Design the PLC ladder program for an **automatic bottle filling plant** with a proximity sensor, 2-second filling timer, and a 12-bottle counter. Draw the complete ladder diagram and write the corresponding Boolean expressions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat ladder diagram, explain the working of an **automatic Star–Delta starter**. Mention the role of the 100 ms transition timer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**I/O Assignment (1 mark):**
- I0.0 = PS (proximity sensor)
- I0.1 = PB_RESET
- Q0.0 = CONV (conveyor motor)
- Q0.1 = SV (solenoid valve)
- Q0.2 = L_DONE (completion lamp)

**Ladder Rungs (5 marks):**
- **Rung 1 — Edge detection on PS** (OSR / one-shot) producing `FLAG`.
- **Rung 2 — CONV** = `!FLAG AND !CT_DONE`
- **Rung 3 — SV** = `FLAG AND !T2s_done`; **TON T_FILL = 2 s**.
- **Rung 4 — CTU C1** preset 12, CU = `T_FILL_done` rising edge.
- **Rung 5 — L_DONE** = `C1.done`.
- **Rung 6 — RESET** = `PB_RESET` resets C1 and L_DONE.

**Boolean Expressions (1 mark):**

$$
\text{CONV} = \overline{\text{FLAG}} \cdot \overline{C1\_\text{done}}
$$

$$
\text{SV} = \text{FLAG} \cdot \overline{T\_\text{FILL\_done}}
$$

$$
L\_\text{DONE} = C1\_\text{done}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

- **Working of Star–Delta Starter (5 marks):** On START press, K_MAIN and K_STAR are energized. The motor draws reduced voltage (line-to-neutral), limiting starting current to ~33 % of direct-on-line value. After 5 s, the timer T1 completes; K_STAR de-energizes. The 100 ms transition timer T2 ensures that K_DELTA does not energize while K_STAR contacts are still mechanically or electrically closed (arcing risk). After T2 completes, K_DELTA energizes, applying full line voltage to the motor.
- **Role of 100 ms Transition Timer (2 marks):** Prevents simultaneous conduction of K_STAR and K_DELTA, eliminating the **dead short circuit** path across the supply. It also provides a safety margin for the **mechanical decay of contactor arc chutes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss — Bottle Filling:** Students often **forget to reset the FLAG bit** after the bottle leaves the sensor. The conveyor will not restart. Always include a **self-resetting rung** for FLAG using T_FILL_done (NC) in series with the FLAG coil.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- **Ladder Rung Reading:** **Top-to-bottom, left-to-right** within one PLC scan.
- **NO vs NC:** Start = NO, Stop = NC (always). NC is **fail-safe**.
- **Latching pattern:** `(START NO) OR (OUTPUT NO) — in series with (STOP NC) — driving OUTPUT coil.`
- **TON timer output** becomes 1 only **after** the preset time elapses with continuous enabling input.
- **Counter (CTU)** increments on the **rising edge** of its CU input.
- **Interlock** = NC contact of competing contactor in the opposite rung. Mandatory in **all reversing / star-delta** circuits.
- **Overload Relay (OLR)** is wired NC in the master rung for fail-safe protection.
- **Star–Delta transition** requires a **small delay (≈100 ms)** between K_STAR OFF and K_DELTA ON to prevent cross-conduction.
- **IEC 61131-3** is the standard governing all ladder-logic instructions tested in KTU ESE.
- **Boolean form** of series rungs = AND; parallel rungs = OR. This is the easiest way to *predict* output state in the exam.
- **Case study solution template:** I/O table → Sequence state table → Ladder diagram → Boolean equations → Safety interlock justification.
- **Edge detection (OSR / EU)** is required whenever a sensor is **level-triggered** but the logic needs to act **only on the moment of arrival** (e.g., bottle counting).
- **Master coil** is the most common KTU-tested pattern — be ready to draw it in **under 90 seconds** during the exam.

> **End of Module 4 — Case Studies on PLC Ladder Programming | OERUT612**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA - Building Blocks of SCADA Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SCADA - Building Blocks of SCADA Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of SCADA

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized industrial control system architecture that enables real-time monitoring, supervision, and high-level command issuance over geographically distributed field assets. It integrates hardware (RTUs, PLCs, IEDs) and software (HMI, historians, alarm engines) through a layered communication infrastructure to form a complete **Man–Machine–Process (MMP)** ecosystem.

According to KTU 2024 OERUT612 Module 4, the "Building Blocks" refer to the **four fundamental architectural layers** that any SCADA system must possess to qualify as a true supervisory system rather than a mere data logger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> A SCADA system is a *distributed measurement and control system* with a **centralized Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** that allows operators to *supervise* (monitor + intervene) field processes spread across wide geographical areas — unlike a DCS (Distributed Control System) which is confined to a single plant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Air Traffic Control"

Imagine the **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** control room in Kalamassery, Cochin. From a single darkened room filled with screens, an operator oversees:
- **230+ substations** spread from Kasaragod to Thiruvananthapuram.
- Each substation has transformers, circuit breakers, and transmission lines (the *field*).
- The operator does not *physically* turn switches — they **send commands** (e.g., "Open Breaker B-3 at Substation X") through a communication network.
- The substation's local controller *executes* the command and reports back.

| Real-World Analogy | SCADA Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Air Traffic Control Tower | **SCADA Master Station** (Central Computer) |
| Radar / Sensors on Aircraft | **Field Sensors & Transducers** (Level, Pressure, Flow) |
| Aircraft Autopilot / Pilots | **RTUs / PLCs** (Remote Terminal Units) |
| VHF Radio / Transponders | **Communication Infrastructure** (LAN, Radio, GSM, Fiber) |
| Pilot Receiving Instructions | **Final Control Elements** (Actuators, Drives, Valves) |

### 1.3 The Core Building Blocks — A High-Level Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Building Blocks of SCADA (Module 4 Focus):**
> 1. **Field Instrumentation Layer** — Sensors + Actuators (the "eyes and hands")
> 2. **Station Control Layer** — RTUs and PLCs (the "local brain")
> 3. **Communication Layer** — Protocols + Media (the "nervous system")
> 4. **Central Monitoring Layer (Master Station)** — HMI + Historian + Alarm Engine (the "conscious mind")

### 1.4 Key Terminology — Mandatory Glossary

| Term | Full Form | Role in SCADA |
|---|---|---|
| **HMI** | Human–Machine Interface | Graphical display the operator interacts with |
| **RTU** | Remote Terminal Unit | Rugged microprocessor at remote site |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller | Local high-speed logic executor |
| **MTU** | Master Terminal Unit | Central SCADA server (synonym of Master) |
| **IED** | Intelligent Electronic Device | Modern digital sensor/protection device |
| **Historian** | Long-term time-series database | Stores all process variables for analytics |
| **DCS** | Distributed Control System | Plant-local counterpart of SCADA |
| **ICS** | Industrial Control System | Umbrella term covering SCADA + DCS + PLCs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block 1 — Field Instrumentation Layer (Sensors & Actuators)

This is the **physical interface** between the process and the SCADA system. Without it, no data can enter the digital world.

**2.1.1 Sensors (Input Devices) — "The Eyes"**
A sensor transduces a physical quantity into a standardized electrical signal.

- **Analog Sensors (4–20 mA / 0–10 V):** Provide continuous readings.
  - Examples: Pressure transmitter (PT), Temperature RTD/Thermocouple, Flow meter (magnetic, vortex), Level (ultrasonic, capacitance).
- **Digital Sensors (Discrete I/O):** Provide binary (ON/OFF) status.
  - Examples: Limit switches, Proximity sensors, Vibration switches.
- **Smart Sensors (HART / Foundation Fieldbus):** Carry both the 4–20 mA variable *and* a digital diagnostic channel simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 4–20 mA, not 0–20 mA?**
> 4 mA is a *live zero* — the current never falls below 4 mA even when the measured value is 0. This allows the system to detect a **broken wire (0 mA = fault)** as opposed to a true zero reading. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

**2.1.2 Actuators (Output Devices) — "The Hands"**
Actuators accept a control command and physically modify the process.

- **Electrical Actuators:** Motors (VFD-driven), Solenoid valves, Contactor coils.
- **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Actuators:** Control valves, Dampers, Cylinders.
- **Smart Actuators:** Positioners with HART feedback — confirm they actually moved.

**2.1.3 Signal Types — A Critical Distinction**

| Signal Type | Range | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current (4–20 mA)** | 4 to 20 mA | Immune to line noise & voltage drop | Requires loop power |
| **Voltage (0–10 V)** | 0 to 10 V DC | Simple, no loop power | Susceptible to noise over long cables |
| **HART** | 4–20 mA + digital | Two-way comms + diagnostics | Slower update rate |
| **Fieldbus / Profibus** | Pure digital | Multi-variable, multi-drop | Higher cost & complexity |

### 2.2 Block 2 — Station Control Layer (RTUs & PLCs)

This is the **local intelligence** that survives even if communication to the master is lost — a key SCADA principle called **graceful degradation**.

**2.2.1 Remote Terminal Unit (RTU)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An RTU is a microprocessor-based field device installed at a remote site that interfaces physical sensors/actuators to a SCADA master via telemetry (radio/GSM/fiber). It is designed for **harsh outdoor environments** with wide temperature tolerance (typically $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+70^{\circ}\text{C}$).

**RTU Characteristics:**
- Designed for **telemetry** (long-distance, often wireless) rather than high-speed logic.
- Lower I/O count than PLCs but extreme ruggedness.
- Has built-in **communication radios/modems**.
- Runs a real-time OS optimized for scanning and reporting.
- Supports protocols like **DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, Modbus RTU**.

**2.2.2 Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A PLC is a ruggedized industrial computer optimized for real-time deterministic control of manufacturing/process machinery. Originally created to replace hard-wired relay logic, it is now the workhorse of modern SCADA station layers.

**PLC Characteristics:**
- Sub-millisecond scan cycle (typically 5–20 ms).
- Modular I/O racks (DI, DO, AI, AO).
- Ladder Logic (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST) programming.
- Protocols: **Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP**.

**2.2.3 RTU vs PLC — KTU Exam Comparison Table**

| Parameter | RTU | PLC |
|---|---|---|
| Primary use | Telemetry / Wide-area | Local machine control |
| Environment | Outdoor, $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+70^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Plant floor, $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+55^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| I/O count | Moderate (up to ~1000) | Wide range (32 to 10,000+) |
| Comms emphasis | Radio / GSM / Satellite | Ethernet / Fieldbus |
| Power tolerance | High (solar/battery backup) | Standard |
| Cost | Higher per I/O | Lower per I/O |
| Protocol native | DNP3, IEC 60870 | Modbus, Profinet |

### 2.3 Block 3 — Communication Layer (The Nervous System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Communication Layer is the single most vulnerable block in any SCADA system.** KTU 2024 emphasizes the "C-I-A Triad" (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) for this layer.

**2.3.1 Communication Media**

| Media | Range | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Twisted Pair Copper** | Up to 1.2 km | Substation wiring, short runs |
| **Fiber Optic** | Up to 100 km (single mode) | Long-haul, EMI-immune backbones |
| **VHF/UHF Radio** | Up to 50 km (line-of-sight) | Pipelines, rural substations |
| **GSM / 4G LTE** | Cellular coverage | Mobile RTUs, tank farms |
| **Satellite (VSAT)** | Global | Offshore platforms, remote deserts |
| **Leased Line / MPLS** | Unlimited | Utility-grade telecom backbones |

**2.3.2 Communication Architectures**

- **Point-to-Point:** One RTU per master radio channel. Expensive.
- **Multidrop / Polled:** Master polls multiple RTUs on a single bus. *Most common in SCADA.*
- **Store-and-Forward:** RTUs act as repeaters, relaying data for downstream units.
- **Mesh (Wireless):** Self-healing; used in modern smart grids (IEC 61850).

**2.3.3 Open SCADA Protocols**

| Protocol | Originator | Layer | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU / TCP** | Modicon (1979) | Application | Industry de-facto standard |
| **DNP3** | IEEE | Application | North American utilities (SCADA) |
| **IEC 60870-5-104** | IEC | Application | European & Indian power SCADA |
| **IEC 61850** | IEC | Substation | Modern digital substations |
| **OPC UA** | OPC Foundation | Information | IT/OT convergence, Industry 4.0 |

### 2.4 Block 4 — Central Monitoring Layer (Master Station / MTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Master Station is the only block that contains a human in the loop.** It is the SCADA system's "cockpit."

**2.4.1 Hardware Components**
- **SCADA Server** (redundant pair — primary + standby).
- **Operator Workstations (HMI PCs)**.
- **Engineering Workstation** (for graphics & database editing).
- **Historian Server** (time-series database — e.g., OSIsoft PI, Wonderware Historian).
- **Network switches, firewalls, GPS time-servers**.

**2.4.2 Software Components**
- **HMI Software:** Mimics (dynamic process graphics).
- **Alarm Management:** Priority, shelving, deadbands, first-out indication.
- **Trending:** Real-time and historical X-Y plots.
- **Reports:** Shift logs, MIS reports.
- **Scripting / Logic:** Derived calculations, interlocking at the supervisory level.
- **Redundancy Management:** Hot-standby failover.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are KTU 2024 Module-4 examinable. Re-derive them, do not memorize blindly.

**1. Current Loop Conversion (Sensor Scaling):**

$$
\text{Process Variable (\%)} = \frac{I_{\text{measured}} - 4\ \text{mA}}{16\ \text{mA}} \times 100
$$

**2. Engineering Unit Conversion (Linear):**

$$
PV = \left( \frac{I - 4}{16} \right) \times \left( EU_{\max} - EU_{\min} \right) + EU_{\min}
$$

**3. SCADA System Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

where **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures and **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair.

**4. Redundant System Availability (Two Servers in Parallel):**

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)
$$

**5. Communication Latency (Polled System):**

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = N \cdot T_{\text{poll}} + T_{\text{propagation}}
$$

where $N$ = number of RTUs, $T_{\text{poll}}$ = time per RTU poll, $T_{\text{propagation}}$ = media delay.

**6. Alarm Deadband (to prevent chatter):**

$$
DB = \left( PV_{\text{set}} - PV_{\text{current}} \right) \quad \text{or} \quad DB = H \times \%\text{span}
$$

where $H$ = hysteresis constant (typically 1–2% of span).

**7. Shannon–Hartley Theorem (Communication Channel Capacity — useful for radio SCADA):**

$$
C = B \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right) \quad [\text{bits/second}]
$$

**8. Scan Time for n I/O Points (PLC/RTU):**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = n \cdot t_{\text{per point}} + t_{\text{overhead}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $I$ | Loop current | 4–20 mA |
| $EU$ | Engineering units | e.g., 0–100 °C |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | 50,000 h |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | 4 h |
| $B$ | Bandwidth | 25 kHz (VHF) |
| $S/N$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB scale |
| $t_{\text{per point}}$ | I/O scan time | 1–10 μs |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **Power Sector (India):** Load Despatch Centres (LDCs) in every state use SCADA to balance grid frequency (50.00 Hz) — POSOCO / NLDC architecture.
- **Water SCADA:** Kerala Water Authority uses SCADA to monitor pumping stations along the pipelines from Aruvikkara to Thiruvananthapuram.
- **Oil & Gas:** Cross-country pipelines (e.g., HPCL Mumbai–Manmad) monitored via SCADA for leak detection and throughput.
- **Smart Manufacturing:** Industry 4.0 factories integrate SCADA with MES and ERP for closed-loop production control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensor Scaling (4–20 mA to Engineering Units)

**Problem Statement:**
A pressure transmitter (PT) has a calibrated range of **0 to 10 bar** and outputs **4 to 20 mA**. The SCADA system reads a current of **12 mA**. Determine the displayed process value in **bar** and as a **percentage of span**.

**Given:**
- $EU_{\min} = 0\ \text{bar}$
- $EU_{\max} = 10\ \text{bar}$
- $I_{\min} = 4\ \text{mA}$
- $I_{\max} = 20\ \text{mA}$
- $I_{\text{measured}} = 12\ \text{mA}$

**Step 1 — Write the general linear mapping equation.**

For any linear transducer, the engineering-unit value $PV$ is a linear function of the measured current $I$. A straight line is fully described by two endpoints, so:

$$
PV = m \cdot I + c
$$

where $m$ = slope (units of bar per mA) and $c$ = intercept (bar).

**Step 2 — Apply the two known boundary conditions.**

When $I = I_{\min}$, then $PV = EU_{\min}$:

$$
EU_{\min} = m \cdot I_{\min} + c
$$

When $I = I_{\max}$, then $PV = EU_{\max}$:

$$
EU_{\max} = m \cdot I_{\max} + c
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations to eliminate $c$.**

$$
EU_{\max} - EU_{\min} = m (I_{\max} - I_{\min})
$$

Therefore the slope is:

$$
m = \frac{EU_{\max} - EU_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute known values.**

$$
m = \frac{10 - 0}{20 - 4} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625\ \text{bar/mA}
$$

**Step 5 — Find the intercept $c$ using the first boundary condition.**

$$
c = EU_{\min} - m \cdot I_{\min} = 0 - (0.625)(4) = -2.5\ \text{bar}
$$

**Step 6 — Form the complete transfer function.**

$$
PV = 0.625 \cdot I - 2.5
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $I = 12\ \text{mA}$.**

$$
PV = (0.625)(12) - 2.5 = 7.5 - 2.5 = 5.0\ \text{bar}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute percentage of span.**

$$
\%\text{span} = \frac{PV - EU_{\min}}{EU_{\max} - EU_{\min}} \times 100 = \frac{5 - 0}{10} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{PV = 5.0\ \text{bar} \equiv 50\%\ \text{of span}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** 12 mA is exactly midway between 4 mA and 20 mA, so the percentage must be 50%. Since the span is 0–10 bar, the corresponding value is 5 bar. ✓

**Compact Universal Formula (useful in KTU exams):**

$$
PV = \left( \frac{I - 4}{16} \right) \times (EU_{\max} - EU_{\min}) + EU_{\min}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — System Availability & Redundancy

**Problem Statement:**
A SCADA master server has $MTBF = 8760\ \text{hours}$ (one year) and $MTTR = 8\ \text{hours}$. A second identical standby server is added for hot redundancy. Calculate the availability of the **single-server** system and the **dual-redundant** system.

**Given:**
- $MTBF = 8760\ \text{h}$
- $MTTR = 8\ \text{h}$

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula for a single non-redundant system.**

$$
A_1 = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

$$
A_1 = \frac{8760}{8760 + 8} = \frac{8760}{8768} = 0.999087
$$

Converting to a percentage:

$$
A_1 = 99.9087\%\ \text{(often called "two nines after the nine")} \approx 99.91\%
$$

**Step 2 — Unavailability of a single server.**

$$
U_1 = 1 - A_1 = 0.000913 = 0.0913\%
$$

This corresponds to a downtime of:

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = U_1 \times 8760\ \text{h} \approx 8\ \text{h/year}
$$

which matches the assumed $MTTR$. (Self-consistency check ✓.)

**Step 3 — For a parallel (redundant) system, the system fails only if *both* units fail simultaneously.**

The probability that both are *down* is the product of their independent down-probabilities:

$$
U_{\text{system}} = U_1 \times U_2 = (0.000913)^2
$$

$$
U_{\text{system}} = 8.336 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the system availability.**

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - U_{\text{system}} = 1 - 8.336 \times 10^{-7} = 0.9999991664
$$

$$
A_{\text{system}} \approx 99.99992\%
$$

This is the famous **"five nines"** (99.999%) benchmark, the gold standard for telecom and SCADA-grade availability.

**Step 5 — Translate availability to annual downtime.**

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{year, redundant}} = U_{\text{system}} \times 8760 \approx 7.3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{h/year} = 26\ \text{seconds/year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Redundancy reduced the expected downtime from 8 hours/year to roughly 26 seconds/year — a **1000-fold** improvement. This is why KTU insists on redundant master stations for any "critical" SCADA.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Communication Latency in a Polled SCADA Network

**Problem Statement:**
A SCADA master polls 32 RTUs over a radio link. Each poll–response cycle takes 250 ms (including RTU processing). The radio propagation delay is negligible. Determine the **total poll cycle time** and the **worst-case latency** for the last RTU.

**Step 1 — Total cycle time (sequential polling).**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot T_{\text{per RTU}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 32 \times 250\ \text{ms} = 8000\ \text{ms} = 8\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case latency for RTU #32.**

The last RTU is polled *just after* the master starts, but its data does not arrive at the master until the very end of the cycle. Hence:

$$
T_{\text{latency, max}} = T_{\text{cycle}} = 8\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Average latency.**

Assuming the master spends equal time per RTU, the average waiting time for a "random" RTU is half the cycle:

$$
T_{\text{latency, avg}} = \frac{T_{\text{cycle}}}{2} = 4\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Implication for alarm handling.**

If a critical alarm (e.g., over-pressure) occurs at RTU #1 just *after* it was polled, the alarm will wait a *full* 8 seconds before the next poll. This is why **Exception Reporting (Report-by-Exception, RBE)** was invented — when a value exceeds its deadband, the RTU *immediately* transmits, bypassing the poll cycle.

$$
T_{\text{latency, RBE}} = T_{\text{propagation}} + T_{\text{transmission}} \approx 50\ \text{ms}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU High-Yield Mapping

| Building Block | Primary Function | Failure Mode | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field Sensor | Sense physical variable | Drift, open circuit | Calibration schedule, HART diagnostics |
| Actuator | Modify process | Stuck, slow response | Position feedback, partial stroke test |
| RTU | Local data aggregation | Power loss, comm loss | Battery backup, store-and-forward |
| Communication | Data transport | EMI, jamming, cut cable | Fiber redundancy, encryption, spread spectrum |
| Master Station | Supervisory control | Server crash | Hot-standby redundancy (99.999%) |
| HMI | Operator interface | Misinterpretation | Color-coding standards (ISA-101), alarm rationalization |
| Historian | Long-term storage | Disk failure | RAID, archival to cloud |
| Alarm Engine | Notify operator | Alarm flood (nuisance) | Shelving, deadbands, prioritization (ISA-18.2) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
