topics.title: Interfacing PLC to SCADA/DCS Using Communication Link (RS232, RS485)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interfacing PLC to SCADA/DCS Using Communication Link (RS232, RS485)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Building Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** The ability of a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) to "talk" to supervisory systems (SCADA) or distributed control systems (DCS) is governed entirely by the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** and **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the **OSI model**. In industrial automation, this physical handshake is almost always achieved through **EIA (Electronic Industries Alliance) serial standards**, specifically **RS-232** and **RS-485**.

### 1.1.1 Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)
A **PLC** is a ruggedized, solid-state, digital industrial computer used for the real-time automation of electromechanical processes. It operates in a harsh industrial environment (heat, dust, moisture, electrical noise) and uses a **scan-cycle architecture** (Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan). The PLC is the *field-level decision-maker* — it reads sensors and drives motors/valves directly.

### 1.1.2 Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)
**SCADA** is a centralized software-hardware system that monitors, gathers, and processes real-time data from geographically dispersed PLCs/RTUs. It provides **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** graphics, trending, alarm management, and historical data logging. SCADA is *supervisory* — it does not close fast control loops; it tells the PLC what to do and watches the result.

### 1.1.3 Distributed Control System (DCS)
A **DCS** is a tightly integrated, plant-wide control architecture where control functions are distributed across multiple controllers (often called *controllers* or *process controllers*) interconnected by a high-speed, redundant **Plant Network** (e.g., industrial Ethernet, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Profibus). Unlike SCADA (which is event-driven, asynchronous, and geographically broad), a DCS is process-driven, tightly time-synchronized, and geographically concentrated (within a single plant).

### 1.1.4 Communication Link
A **Communication Link** is the physical + logical pathway over which serialized binary data travels between the PLC (field device) and the SCADA/DCS (supervisory layer). The link defines:
- **Electrical characteristics** (voltage levels, impedance)
- **Mechanical characteristics** (connector type, pinout)
- **Functional characteristics** (handshaking, signaling direction)
- **Procedural characteristics** (bit ordering, framing)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Term — "Communication Link":** In the KTU 2024 scheme, this phrase specifically refers to the **serial physical medium and protocol layer** (RS-232, RS-485, RS-422, or Ethernet) that physically carries Modbus, DF1, or PPI frames between a PLC's communication port and the supervisory station's COM/serial card.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Industrial "Postal System"

Imagine a factory floor as a country with two major cities:

| Element | Real-World Analogy | Industrial Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **City A — Field** | Local post office handling daily letters | **PLC** executing scan cycles |
| **City B — Headquarters** | Central government monitoring every district | **SCADA / DCS** |
| **The Road Network** | Highways, lanes, traffic rules | **Communication Link (RS-232 / RS-485)** |
| **Letter Format** | Envelope, postage, address format | **Communication Protocol (Modbus RTU, DF1, PPI)** |
| **Postal Van** | One truck on a single-lane road | **RS-232** (one sender → one receiver, point-to-point) |
| **Highway with Lanes** | Many trucks on a multi-lane highway | **RS-485** (multi-drop, 32+ devices on one twisted pair) |

Just as the *road design* (single-lane vs. multi-lane, paved vs. gravel) decides how many letters travel and how fast, the *communication link* (RS-232 vs. RS-485) decides how many PLCs can talk to SCADA and over what distance.

---

## 1.3 The Two Industrial Workhorses — RS-232 & RS-485 at a Glance

### 1.3.1 RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)
- **Origin:** EIA/TIA-232-F (revised 1997), originally issued in 1962.
- **Topology:** **Point-to-point, full-duplex** (one DTE ↔ one DCE).
- **Signaling:** **Single-ended, unbalanced.** Logic '1' (MARK) = **−3 V to −25 V**; Logic '0' (SPACE) = **+3 V to +25 V** (note the *inverted polarity* compared to TTL).
- **Max Cable Length:** **15 metres (50 feet)** at maximum specified baud rate of **20 kbps** (the standard mandates ≤ 20 kbps for the full 15 m).
- **Max Devices:** **1 driver + 1 receiver** per port (no multi-drop).
- **Standard Connector:** **DB-9 (DE-9)** male on DTE, female on DCE.
- **Common Use:** Direct laptop-to-PLC programming cable, short-distance local HMI.

### 1.3.2 RS-485 (Recommended Standard 485)
- **Origin:** EIA/TIA-485-A (1998), introduced in 1983.
- **Topology:** **Multi-drop (party-line), half-duplex (2-wire)** or **full-duplex (4-wire)**.
- **Signaling:** **Differential, balanced.** Logic is determined by the **voltage difference** between two twisted wires (A and B): $\;V_{AB} = V_A - V_B\;$. If $V_{AB} > +200\,\text{mV}$ → MARK; if $V_{AB} < -200\,\text{mV}$ → SPACE.
- **Max Cable Length:** **1200 metres (4000 feet)** at slow baud rates (≤ 100 kbps).
- **Max Devices:** **32 unit loads** (drivers + receivers) per segment — extendable to 256+ with repeaters.
- **Common Use:** Plant-floor backbone connecting dozens of PLCs to a single SCADA server.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** A single line that is *guaranteed to score a mark* — *"RS-485 uses **differential signaling** to reject common-mode noise, making it the de-facto standard for noisy industrial environments longer than 15 m."* Memorize this verbatim.

---

## 1.4 Why SCADA/DCS Needs PLCs in the First Place

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Industrial Automation (ISA-95 / Purdue Model):**
> **Level 0** (Sensors/Actuators) → **Level 1** (PLC / DCS Controller) → **Level 2** (SCADA HMI / Supervisory) → **Level 3** (MES — Manufacturing Execution) → **Level 4** (ERP — Enterprise Resource Planning).

The PLC cannot generate rich operator graphics, long-term trends, or plant-wide reports. The SCADA/DCS cannot survive the millisecond-level scan cycles needed for safety interlocks. The **communication link** is the *only* bridge between the reactive field (Level 1) and the reflective supervisory layer (Level 2).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Serial Communication Link

Regardless of whether RS-232 or RS-485 is used, the *logical* data must be packaged identically. A standard **asynchronous serial frame** consists of:

| Field | Width | Default Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start bit | 1 bit | Always '0' (SPACE) | Tells the receiver "data is coming now" |
| Data bits | 5–9 bits | **8 bits** (1 byte) | The actual payload |
| Parity bit | 0 or 1 bit | **None / Even / Odd** | Optional single-bit error check |
| Stop bit(s) | 1, 1.5, or 2 bits | **1 bit** ('1' = MARK) | Allows the line to return to idle state |
| Idle line | — | Continuous '1' (MARK) | Line at rest between frames |

A typical factory configuration — **8-N-1** — therefore carries **10 bits per transmitted byte** (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop), and the line is held in the **MARK** state between bytes.

---

## 2.2 The Five Key Communication Parameters (Asynchronous Serial)

These five parameters **must be identical on every device** on the link, or the receiver will sample the wrong bits and produce garbage:

1. **Baud Rate (bps / symbols per second):** Speed of bit transmission. Common values: **1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 bps**.
2. **Data Bits:** Length of payload (typically 7 or 8).
3. **Parity:** None (N), Even (E), Odd (O) — single-bit parity.
4. **Stop Bits:** 1 or 2 (1.5 only valid at 5 data bits).
5. **Flow Control:** Hardware (RTS/CTS, DTR/DSR) or software (XON/XOFF). RS-485 is half-duplex, so it relies on *direction-control* timing rather than RTS/CTS.

---

## 2.3 RS-232 — Detailed Electrical & Mechanical Specification

### 2.3.1 DB-9 (DE-9) Pinout (the connector you will *definitely* see in your lab)

| Pin (DB-9) | Name | Direction (DTE → DCE) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DCD | ← DCE | Data Carrier Detect |
| 2 | **RD (RXD)** | ← DCE | **Received Data** |
| 3 | **TD (TXD)** | → DCE | **Transmitted Data** |
| 4 | DTR | → DCE | Data Terminal Ready |
| 5 | **GND (SG)** | — | **Signal Ground** (mandatory reference) |
| 6 | DSR | ← DCE | Data Set Ready |
| 7 | RTS | → DCE | Request To Send |
| 8 | CTS | ← DCE | Clear To Send |
| 9 | RI | ← DCE | Ring Indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin 5 (Signal Ground) is mandatory** for RS-232 single-ended operation. This ground reference is also the *Achilles' heel* of RS-232 — ground-potential differences between two buildings can exceed the ±25 V signal swing and destroy ports.

### 2.3.2 Voltage & Logic Levels
- **Logic 0 (SPACE):** Driver output = **+5 V to +15 V** (typ. +12 V); Receiver accepts **+3 V to +25 V**.
- **Logic 1 (MARK):** Driver output = **−5 V to −15 V** (typ. −12 V); Receiver accepts **−3 V to −25 V**.
- **Maximum slew rate limit:** 30 V/µs (to limit EMI).
- **Maximum short-circuit current:** 500 mA per driver.

### 2.3.3 Maximum Cable Length — Why 15 m?
The original RS-232 standard (1962) specified the **maximum capacitive load** of 2500 pF on the driver. For a typical PVC-insulated cable with ~150 pF/m, this yields:

$$L_{\max} = \frac{2500\;\text{pF}}{150\;\text{pF/m}} \approx 16.7\;\text{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Spec conservatively rated as } \mathbf{15\;m}$$

Higher-quality low-capacitance cables (C ≈ 50 pF/m) can extend this to ~30 m, but this is **outside the formal standard** and is not guaranteed by the EIA spec.

---

## 2.4 RS-485 — Detailed Electrical & Mechanical Specification

### 2.4.1 Differential Signaling — The Heart of RS-485
Two wires, labelled **A (or "−" / "inverting")** and **B (or "+" / "non-inverting")**, carry *equal and opposite* currents. The receiver computes the *difference* $V_{AB} = V_A - V_B$.

- **MARK (idle / logic 1):** $V_A < V_B$, i.e., $V_{AB} < -200$ mV.
- **SPACE (active / logic 0):** $V_A > V_B$, i.e., $V_{AB} > +200$ mV.
- **Common-mode voltage range:** −7 V to +12 V (receiver tolerates this range on both lines simultaneously without damage).
- **Driver output (differential):** ±1.5 V minimum into a 54 Ω load (representing 32 unit loads of 12 kΩ in parallel terminated with two 120 Ω resistors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why differential rejects noise:** External EMI (e.g., a VFD switching at 8 kHz) induces the *same* voltage on both A and B. The receiver's *difference amplifier* cancels this common-mode noise. This gives RS-485 a **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) > 60 dB** at 50/60 Hz, making it immune to ground loops and motor noise.

### 2.4.2 Cable, Topology, and Termination
- **Cable type:** **Twisted pair**, characteristic impedance **Z₀ = 120 Ω** (use Belden 3106A / 9841 or equivalent).
- **Topology:** **Daisy-chain only** — no stubs, no star, no T-taps. Each branch > ~1/10 of the signal rise time corrupts the waveform.
- **Termination resistors:** **120 Ω at each end** of the bus (matches the cable's Z₀, eliminates reflections at high baud). **Do not terminate intermediate nodes.**
- **Fail-safe biasing:** Pull-up on B, pull-down on A (typically 680 Ω to 4.7 kΩ) so the line idles in a defined MARK state when no driver is active.

### 2.4.3 Unit Loads
A "**unit load (UL)**" is a measure of DC loading on the bus. The RS-485 standard allows **32 ULs per segment**. Modern transceivers (e.g., TI SN65HVD75) consume only **1/8 UL**, so a modern bus can host **256 transceivers** per segment, far exceeding the original 32.

### 2.4.4 Maximum Cable Length vs. Baud Rate — The Inverse Relationship

| Baud Rate (bps) | Max Recommended Distance (m) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 100 k | **1200** | Slowest, longest — distance-limited |
| 500 k | ~ 240 | |
| 1 M | **120** | |
| 2 M | 60 | |
| 10 M | 12 | EIA-485-A limit at 10 Mbps |
| 35 M | < 5 | Modern 1/8 UL transceivers |

The relationship is governed by the **propagation time** of the signal and the **bit period**. To stay synchronous, the round-trip propagation time must be < 10 % of the bit period.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** These are the *only* formulas the KTU board has asked in the last 5 years for the "Communication Link" topic. Master them.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bit period (T_bit)** | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds (s) | Inverse of baud rate |
| 2 | **Frame duration (T_frame)** | $T_f = N_{bits} \times T_b$ | seconds (s) | $N_{bits}$ = start + data + parity + stop |
| 3 | **Bytes per second (Bps)** | $\text{Bps} = \dfrac{\text{Baud Rate}}{N_{bits\,per\,byte}}$ | bytes/s | For 8-N-1: Bps = Baud/10 |
| 4 | **Maximum cable length (rule of thumb, RS-485)** | $L_{\max} \le \dfrac{10^8}{\text{Baud Rate (bps)}}\;\text{metres}$ | metres | Empirical rule: 1200 m @ 100 k, 120 m @ 1 M |
| 5 | **Maximum cable length (RS-232)** | $L_{\max} \le 15\;\text{m}$ (spec); 30 m possible with low-C cable | metres | Capacitive-load limited |
| 6 | **Propagation time on cable** | $t_p = \dfrac{L \times \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | seconds | $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.5$–2.0 for PE-insulated TP; $c = 3\times10^8$ m/s |
| 7 | **Signal velocity in cable** | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | m/s | Typical twisted pair: $\sim 2 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 8 | **Maximum devices per RS-485 segment** | $N_{\text{UL}} \le 32$ (1 UL each); up to 256 with 1/8 UL transceivers | — | Each receiver/driver = 1 UL |
| 9 | **Round-trip time budget** | $t_{\text{RTT}} = \dfrac{2L}{v_p} < 0.10 \times T_b$ | seconds | Distortion-free sampling |
| 10 | **Number of bits per Modbus RTU frame** (worst case, 1 byte payload) | $N_b = 1_{\text{start}} + 8_{\text{data}} + 2_{\text{stop}} = 11$ bits (no parity, 2 stop) | bits | Used in throughput calculations |
| 11 | **Modbus RTU silence gap (frame delimiter)** | $t_{\text{gap}} = 3.5 \times T_b$ | seconds | Mandatory between frames |
| 12 | **Common-mode rejection (CMRR)** | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | $A_d$ = diff gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety Rule (No `|` inside table cells):** All vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table syntax. Board exam answers should use the same convention or a code block.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (Upstream):** An offshore platform uses RS-485 to link 50+ wellhead PLCs to a topside SCADA server over 1 km of cable trays running parallel to high-voltage power cables.
- **Water Treatment:** A municipal SCADA polls dozens of lift-station RTUs over RS-485 multi-drop — replacing legacy leased-line modems.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Control:** A DCS controller (e.g., Emerson DeltaV) talks to PLCs (Allen-Bradley ControlLogix) through a serial gateway, with RS-485 handling the long-haul trunk and RS-232 handling the local programming port.
- **Power Substations:** IEC 61850-aware IEDs use RS-485 for legacy teleprotection links between substations up to 1200 m apart.

The communication link is *not* a luxury; it is the **nervous system** of every modern automated plant.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RS-232 Maximum Cable Length from Capacitive Loading

> **Given:** The EIA-232-F driver must drive a total load capacitance $C_L \le 2500\;\text{pF}$ while maintaining a maximum slew rate of $30\;\text{V/µs}$. A standard PVC-jacketed shielded twisted pair has a typical capacitance per metre of $C' = 150\;\text{pF/m}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship.**
The maximum cable length is determined by the cable's distributed capacitance:

$$L_{\max} = \frac{C_{L,\text{max}}}{C'}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$L_{\max} = \frac{2500\;\text{pF}}{150\;\text{pF/m}}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$L_{\max} = 16.67\;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the conservative standard rating.**

Since this is the *theoretical* limit and real-world factors (slewing, jitter, receiver hysteresis) erode the margin, the EIA-232-F specification conservatively rates the maximum cable length as:

$$\boxed{L_{\max,\,\text{RS-232}} = 15\;\text{m}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering implication.**
A PLC-to-HMI link longer than 15 m *cannot* use RS-232 reliably. The engineer must migrate to RS-485, RS-422, or industrial Ethernet (e.g., EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RS-485 Maximum Cable Length from Bit-Period Budget

> **Goal:** Derive why the RS-485 max length drops from 1200 m to 12 m as baud rate rises from 100 kbps to 10 Mbps.

**Step 1 — Define the bit period.**

For a baud rate $R$, the time taken to transmit one bit is:

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R}$$

For $R = 100{,}000$ bps: $T_b = 1 / 100{,}000 = 10\;\mu\text{s}$.

**Step 2 — Define the propagation velocity in the cable.**

The signal travels at a fraction of the speed of light, governed by the dielectric's relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. For a polyethylene (PE) insulated twisted pair, $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.3$:

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}}{\sqrt{2.3}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5166} \approx 1.978 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — State the round-trip time for a cable of length L.**

A reflection or echoed signal must travel down the line and back:

$$t_{\text{RTT}} = \frac{2L}{v_p}$$

For L = 1200 m: $t_{\text{RTT}} = (2 \times 1200) / (1.978 \times 10^8) = 1.213 \times 10^{-5}$ s ≈ **12.13 µs**.

**Step 4 — Apply the "10 % bit period" rule.**

To keep the receiver's sample clock synchronized, the round-trip propagation should consume less than 10 % of one bit period:

$$t_{\text{RTT}} \le 0.10 \times T_b$$

For $R = 100$ kbps, $0.10 \times T_b = 1.0\;\mu\text{s}$ — this is *violated* by 12.13 µs.

This shows the strict 10 % rule is too pessimistic at 100 kbps; in practice, RS-485 is specified for **1200 m up to 100 kbps** using a more lenient **≤ 50 % bit period / 1 bit** of accumulated jitter budget. The rigorous rule (10 % of $T_b$) gives:

$$L_{\max} = 0.10 \times T_b \times \frac{v_p}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the formula for 1 Mbps baud rate.**

$$T_b = 1\;\mu\text{s}, \quad 0.10 \times T_b = 0.1\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$L_{\max} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{1.978 \times 10^8}{2} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.989 \times 10^8 = 9.89\;\text{m}$$

This *under*-estimates the 120 m figure because real transceivers tolerate up to ~50 % of $T_b$ as propagation. The **empirical rule of thumb** (universally cited in industry) is:

$$\boxed{L_{\max,\,\text{RS-485}} \approx \frac{10^8}{\text{Baud Rate (bps)}}\;\text{metres}}$$

Quick check at 1 Mbps: $10^8 / 10^6 = 100$ m (matches the table). At 100 kbps: $10^8 / 10^5 = 1000$ m (≈ 1200 m with low-loss cable).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput of a Modbus RTU Link (8-N-1)

> **Given:** A Modbus RTU master (SCADA) polls 30 PLC slaves sequentially over an RS-485 bus at **9600 bps, 8-N-1, 3.5-character inter-frame silence**.

**Step 1 — Bits per byte with 8-N-1.**

For 8-N-1 (8 data, no parity, 1 stop): $N_b = 1 + 8 + 1 = 10$ bits/byte.

**Step 2 — Useful payload bit-rate (excluding start/stop).**

$$R_{\text{payload}} = \text{Baud Rate} \times \frac{8}{10} = 9600 \times 0.8 = 7680\;\text{bps}$$

**Step 3 — Inter-frame silence time.**

$$t_{\text{gap}} = 3.5 \times T_b = 3.5 \times \frac{1}{9600} = 3.646 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{s} = 364.6\;\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case Modbus RTU frame size.**

A standard Modbus RTU request frame (function code 03, Read Holding Registers) is **8 bytes** (1 addr + 1 FC + 2 start reg + 2 qty reg + 2 CRC). Adding the silence gap:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = (8 \times 10 \times T_b) + (3.5 \times T_b) = 83.5 \times T_b$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle time for 30 slaves polled round-robin.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = 30 \times 83.5 \times \frac{1}{9600} = \frac{2505}{9600} = 0.2609\;\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Result.**

$$\boxed{\text{Refresh time for 30 slaves at 9600 bps} \approx 261\;\text{ms}}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
- This is well within the **1-second** SCADA refresh guideline.
- At 115200 bps, the same poll would take ≈ 21.7 ms, giving the SCADA plenty of headroom for alarm/event bursts.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Common-Mode Rejection of RS-485

> **Given:** A 50 Hz ground-loop noise of 5 Vp-p is induced on both A and B lines. The differential amplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 1$ and a common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 10^{-3}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the CMRR.**

$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{10^{-3}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^3) = 60\;\text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the residual noise at the receiver output.**

$$V_{\text{noise,out}} = V_{\text{CM}} \times A_{cm} = 5\;\text{V} \times 10^{-3} = 5\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with signal swing.**

The differential signal swing is ±1.5 V (1500 mV). The noise residue is 5 mV, so the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the receiver is:

$$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1500\;\text{mV}}{5\;\text{mV}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(300) \approx 49.5\;\text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{A 5 V ground-loop noise degrades the receiver SNR by only 10.5 dB, leaving } \approx 49.5\;\text{dB margin.}}$$

This is why RS-485 *dominates* industrial serial communication — even in electrically hostile environments like arc furnaces or VFD-rich motor control centres.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Total Number of Bits on a 32-Device Polling Cycle

For completeness, the number of **bits transmitted per second** across an RS-485 bus polling 32 slaves at 19.2 kbps, 8-N-2, 8-byte request / 8-byte response:

**Step 1 — Bits per request/response frame (with 2 stop bits).**

$$N_b = 1_{\text{start}} + 8_{\text{data}} + 1_{\text{stop\,req}} + 8_{\text{data}} + 2_{\text{stop}} = 20\;\text{bits}$$

(Simplified: 2 frames × 10 bits/frame = 20 bits)

**Step 2 — Inter-frame silence in bits.**

$$N_{\text{gap}} = 3.5 \times 1 = 3.5\;\text{bits per frame (approx 4 bits)}$$

**Step 3 — Bits per poll cycle (request + response + 2 gaps).**

$$N_{\text{cycle}} = 20 + 2(3.5) = 27\;\text{bits}$$

**Step 4 — Time per poll cycle.**

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{27}{19200} = 1.406\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle time for 32 slaves.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = 32 \times 1.406\;\text{ms} = 45.0\;\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Refresh rate} \approx 22\;\text{Hz}}$$

This is suitable for process monitoring (level, temperature, flow), but **not** for high-speed motion control, which would require EtherCAT or PROFINET IRT.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix (RS-232 vs RS-485)

| Specification | RS-232 | RS-485 |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Point-to-point | Multi-drop (up to 32 ULs) |
| **Mode** | Full-duplex | Half-duplex (2-wire) or full-duplex (4-wire) |
| **Signaling** | Single-ended, unbalanced | Differential, balanced |
| **Max devices** | 1 DTE + 1 DCE | 32 drivers + 32 receivers |
| **Max cable length** | 15 m | 1200 m |
| **Max baud rate** | 20 kbps (spec) / 115 kbps (driver-limited) | 10 Mbps (short) / 100 kbps (long) |
| **Voltage levels (driver)** | ±5 V to ±15 V | ±1.5 V differential (min into 54 Ω) |
| **Receiver sensitivity** | ±3 V | ±200 mV differential |
| **Common-mode range** | None (signal-grounded) | −7 V to +12 V |
| **Connectors** | DB-9, DB-25 (legacy) | Screw terminals, RJ-45 (Modbus), DB-9 |
| **Impedance** | n/a | 120 Ω twisted pair |
| **Noise immunity** | Low (ground-referenced) | Very high (CMRR > 60 dB) |
| **Cost per port** | Low | Moderate |
| **Typical use** | Local programming, short HMI | Plant backbone, multi-PLC SCADA |

---

## 4.3 Diagram 2 — RS-485 Multi-Drop SCADA Bus Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SCADA["SCADA SERVER With RS485 Card"]
    PLC1["PLC 1 Slave Address 01"]
    PLC2["PLC 2 Slave Address 02"]
    PLC3["PLC 3 Slave Address 03"]
    PNT["PLC N Slave Address 32"]
    RTERM["120 OHM Termination Resistor at Far End"]
    BTERM["120 OHM Termination Resistor at SCADA End"]
    BIAS["Fail Safe Bias Network 680 ohm pull up and pull down"]

    SCADA -- "Wire A Inverting" --> PLC1
    SCADA -- "Wire B Non Inverting" --> PLC1
    PLC1 -- "Daisy Chain TP" --> PLC2
    PLC2 -- "Daisy Chain TP" --> PLC3
    PLC3 -- "Daisy Chain TP" --> PNT
    PNT -- "End of Bus" --> RTERM
    SCADA -- "Back to Source" --> BTERM
    SCADA -- "Bias at Master" --> BIAS
```

**Key engineering takeaways from the diagram:**
1. **Daisy-chain only** — no stars, no T-taps.
2. **Termination at both ends** (120 Ω matching the cable's $Z_0$).
3. **One pair of wires** for half-duplex; A and B twisted together.
4. **Fail-safe bias** at the master to keep the bus in a known MARK state when idle.
5. **Up to 32 unit loads** (or 256 with 1/8 UL transceivers).

---

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — RS-232 Point-to-Point Connection (Laptop ↔ PLC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LAPTOP["DTE Laptop DB9 Male"]
    CABLE["RS232 Serial Cable Maximum 15 m"]
    PLC["DCE PLC Programming Port DB9 Female"]

    LAPTOP -- "Pin 2 RXD Receive" --> PLC
    LAPTOP -- "Pin 3 TXD Transmit" --> PLC
    LAPTOP -- "Pin 5 GND Signal Ground" --> PLC
    LAPTOP -- "Pin 7 RTS Request to Send" --> PLC
    LAPTOP -- "Pin 8 CTS Clear to Send" --> PLC

    PLC -- "Pin 2 RXD Back to Laptop" --> LAPTOP
    PLC -- "Pin 3 TXD Back to Laptop" --> LAPTOP
```

**Key engineering takeaways:**
1. **Cross-over wiring** is *not* automatic — RXD and TXD must be swapped (a "null modem" cable).
2. **Pin 5 (Signal Ground)** is mandatory and is the noise-coupling path.
3. **RTS/CTS** handshaking (pins 7, 8) is used for flow control in full-duplex.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 4 — Functional Architecture of PLC ↔ SCADA ↔ DCS Communication Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["APPLICATION LAYER Modbus DF1 PPI OPC"]
    DL["DATA LINK LAYER Frame Start Stop Address CRC"]
    PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER RS232 or RS485"]
    MEDIA["MEDIUM Twisted Pair Copper"]
    FW["FIRMWARE PLC Scan Cycle 10 to 50 ms"]
    SCADASW["SCADA HMI Software Polling at 1 to 5 s"]

    SCADASW -- "Read Holding Register Function 03" --> APP
    APP -- "Encodes Frame" --> DL
    DL -- "Bit Serialization 8N1" --> PHY
    PHY -- "Differential Voltage on A and B" --> MEDIA
    MEDIA -- "Electrical Coupling" --> FW
    FW -- "Response" --> MEDIA
    MEDIA -- "Reply Frame" --> PHY
    PHY -- "Bit Decode" --> DL
    DL -- "Addressed to Master" --> APP
    APP -- "Update SCADA Tag" --> SCADASW

    classDef app fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef dl fill:#b7791f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef phy fill:#22543d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef media fill:#4a5568,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef fw fill:#9b2c2c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef scada fill:#553c9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    class APP app
    class DL dl
    class PHY phy
    class MEDIA media
    class FW fw
    class SCADASW scada
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Application Layer** (Modbus) and **Data Link Layer** (frame structure) are *protocol-software* concerns; the **Physical Layer** (RS-232 / RS-485) and **Medium** (twisted pair) are *hardware* concerns. The PLC firmware executes its scan cycle independently, and SCADA polls asynchronously.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 5 — RS-485 Bus Voltage & Logic States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IDLE["IDLE MARK Logic 1 A less than B less than -200 mV"]
    START["START BIT SPACE Logic 0 A greater than B greater than +200 mV"]
    D0["DATA BIT 0 b0 LSB First"]
    D7["DATA BIT 7 b7 MSB Last"]
    PAR["PARITY BIT Even or Odd"]
    STOP["STOP BIT 1 Return to MARK"]
    GAP["3.5 CHARACTER SILENCE End of Modbus RTU Frame"]

    IDLE --> START
    START --> D0
    D0 --> D7
    D7 --> PAR
    PAR --> STOP
    STOP --> GAP
    GAP --> IDLE
```

This sequence diagram shows the **bit-by-bit state** on an RS-485 bus during one Modbus RTU byte transmission. The 3.5-character silence gap is the **frame delimiter** that Modbus RTU uses to mark the end of a frame.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Semester VI]**
**Differentiate between RS-232 and RS-485 communication standards in terms of signaling, topology, and maximum cable length.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RS-232 | RS-485 |
|---|---|---|
| **Signaling** | Single-ended, unbalanced (uses a common signal ground; logic determined by voltage on TX/RX line relative to ground) | Differential, balanced (logic determined by voltage *difference* between A and B lines) |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point (1 DTE ↔ 1 DCE) | Multi-drop (up to 32 unit loads on a single twisted pair) |
| **Max Cable Length** | 15 m (capacitance-limited) | 1200 m at ≤ 100 kbps (distance × baud rate ≈ 10⁸) |
| **Noise Immunity** | Low (no CMRR) | High (CMRR > 60 dB) |
| **Voltage Levels** | ±5 to ±15 V (single-ended) | ±1.5 V differential minimum (balanced) |

**Conclusion:** RS-485 is preferred for plant-floor SCADA backbones due to its differential signaling, multi-drop capability, and 1200 m reach; RS-232 is restricted to short-distance local programming and HMI.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Semester VI]**
**Explain the role of the signal ground pin in an RS-232 DB-9 connector. Why is the absence of this pin hazardous in a multi-machine link?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

In RS-232, the **signal ground (Pin 5 on DB-9, "SG")** is the **common voltage reference** for all other RS-232 signals (TXD, RXD, RTS, CTS, DTR, DSR, DCD, RI). Because RS-232 uses **single-ended signaling**, every receiver interprets a logic '1' or '0' by comparing the incoming voltage to this ground.

If two pieces of equipment (e.g., a SCADA PC and a PLC) are powered from **separate mains supplies**, their local "earth grounds" can be at different potentials — a **ground-potential difference (GPD)** of several volts is common in industrial sites. Without Pin 5, the receiver has no reference and may interpret noise as data, OR (worse) the ±25 V signal swing may **exceed the receiver's absolute-maximum ratings**, causing permanent port damage.

Thus, the **signal ground pin is mandatory**, and over long distances RS-485 is preferred because its *differential* signaling is **immune to ground loops** (it does not need a common ground reference).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Students must answer only one of the two alternatives.**

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a SCADA system and clearly show the role of the communication link in connecting PLCs to the SCADA server. Discuss why RS-485 is preferred over RS-232 for this role.* *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *A SCADA server polls 25 PLCs over an RS-485 multi-drop bus using Modbus RTU. Each Modbus request is 8 bytes and the response is 9 bytes. The link is configured at 9600 bps, 8-E-1 (8 data, even parity, 1 stop). Compute the (i) total number of bits in a complete poll cycle, (ii) cycle time per slave, and (iii) total refresh time for all 25 slaves. State one assumption.* *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **SCADA system** consists of three hierarchical layers (refer to Section 4.2 for the architecture):

1. **Field Layer (Level 0–1):** Sensors, actuators, and PLCs/RTUs that directly interact with the physical process. The PLC executes a scan cycle (input scan → program scan → output scan) typically every 10–50 ms.

2. **Communication Layer (the "link"):** The serial or network infrastructure that carries data between field devices and the supervisory station. **For multi-PLC, multi-drop, > 15 m plants, this is RS-485**, with **Modbus RTU** as the application-layer protocol.

3. **Supervisory Layer (Level 2):** The SCADA server (PC + SCADA software like WinCC, iFIX, Citect) with HMIs, alarm management, trending, and historical databases.

**Why RS-485 is preferred over RS-232 for the SCADA–PLC link:**

| Criterion | RS-232 | RS-485 |
|---|---|---|
| Multi-drop (one cable for many PLCs) | ❌ No (point-to-point only) | ✅ Yes (up to 32 ULs) |
| Cable length (plant-floor) | 15 m | 1200 m |
| Noise immunity (VFDs, motors, welding) | ❌ Poor (ground-referenced) | ✅ Excellent (CMRR > 60 dB, differential) |
| Cost per PLC connected | High (each needs its own port) | Low (shared bus) |

A **block diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 / 4.3) shows the SCADA server at the centre, connected via a single RS-485 twisted pair to 25 PLCs in a daisy-chain, with 120 Ω termination at both ends and fail-safe bias at the master.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Number of slaves $N = 25$
- Request frame = 8 bytes
- Response frame = 9 bytes
- Configuration: 9600 bps, 8-E-1 → bits per byte = 1 (start) + 8 (data) + 1 (parity) + 1 (stop) = **11 bits/byte**
- Inter-frame silence = **3.5 character times** (Modbus RTU standard)

**Step (i) — Total bits per poll cycle:**

Each cycle consists of one request and one response. Total bytes per cycle = $8 + 9 = 17$ bytes.

$$N_{bits,\text{cycle}} = 17 \times 11 = 187\;\text{bits (payload)}$$

Add the inter-frame silence (3.5 characters per frame boundary; 2 boundaries per cycle = 7 character times):

$$N_{silence} = 7 \times 11 = 77\;\text{bits}$$

$$\boxed{N_{bits,\text{total}} = 187 + 77 = 264\;\text{bits per cycle}}$$

**Step (ii) — Cycle time per slave:**

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{264}{9600} = 0.0275\;\text{s} = 27.5\;\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{cycle} = 27.5\;\text{ms per slave}}$$

**Step (iii) — Total refresh time for 25 slaves:**

$$T_{refresh} = 25 \times 27.5\;\text{ms} = 687.5\;\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{refresh} \approx 0.688\;\text{s} \approx 690\;\text{ms}}$$

**Assumption:** The master polls slaves sequentially with no inter-poll overhead (i.e., back-to-back polls) and the propagation delay is negligible at the given cable length.

**Conclusion:** A 690 ms refresh time is well within the **1-second guideline** for SCADA operator displays; alarms will surface within 1 second at worst.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *Draw the DB-9 pin assignment of an RS-232 port and label the data, handshaking, and ground pins. Explain the functions of TXD, RXD, RTS, CTS, and GND. State the function of the signal ground pin and the reason for not using RS-232 for distances exceeding 15 m.* *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *An industrial plant uses an RS-485 bus with 20 PLC slaves. The bus length is 800 m. The PLC-to-PLC round-trip propagation delay in the twisted-pair cable is 8 µs. The bus is operated at (i) 9600 bps and (ii) 115200 bps. Determine whether each baud rate satisfies the "10 % bit-period round-trip propagation" rule. Comment on the suitability of each rate for this 800 m link.* *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**DB-9 (DE-9) Pinout (as in Section 2.3.1):**

| Pin | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | DCD | Data Carrier Detect (← DCE) |
| 2 | **RD (RXD)** | **Received Data** (← DCE) |
| 3 | **TD (TXD)** | **Transmitted Data** (→ DCE) |
| 4 | DTR | Data Terminal Ready (→ DCE) |
| 5 | **GND (SG)** | **Signal Ground** — *mandatory* common reference |
| 6 | DSR | Data Set Ready (← DCE) |
| 7 | **RTS** | **Request To Send** (→ DCE) — flow control |
| 8 | **CTS** | **Clear To Send** (← DCE) — flow control |
| 9 | RI | Ring Indicator (← DCE) |

**Functions of the five highlighted pins:**

- **TXD (Pin 3):** Carries data *from* the DTE (e.g., SCADA PC) *to* the DCE (e.g., PLC). Held at logic '1' (MARK, negative voltage) when idle.
- **RXD (Pin 2):** Carries data *from* the DCE *to* the DTE. Held at logic '1' (MARK) when idle.
- **RTS (Pin 7):** Driven by the DTE to tell the DCE, *"I have data to send, are you ready to receive?"* Asserted (positive voltage, +12 V) when active.
- **CTS (Pin 8):** Driven by the DCE in response, *"Yes, I am ready, you may send."* Forms a hardware handshake with RTS.
- **GND (Pin 5):** The 0 V reference for ALL other signals. Without it, single-ended receivers cannot interpret any voltage level.

**Why RS-232 fails beyond 15 m:**

1. **Capacitive loading:** The driver must drive ≤ 2500 pF total. Standard cable (150 pF/m) limits the run to 16.7 m, conservatively rated as 15 m. Longer cables round off the bit edges and the receiver cannot reliably sample.
2. **Ground potential differences:** Beyond ~15 m, the signal ground between two machines may differ by several volts (industrial environments have noisy earth). This voltage adds to the signal and can saturate the receiver, destroy the port, or invert the logic.
3. **No common-mode rejection:** Single-ended signaling is fundamentally vulnerable to induced noise. In contrast, RS-485 uses *differential* signaling, which rejects common-mode noise and can run 1200 m.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** Round-trip propagation delay $t_{\text{RTT}} = 8\;\mu\text{s}$ for 800 m.

**Rule:** For distortion-free sampling, $t_{\text{RTT}} \le 0.10 \times T_b$.

**Case (i) — Baud rate = 9600 bps:**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{9600} = 104.17\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$0.10 \times T_b = 10.42\;\mu\text{s}$$

Check: $t_{\text{RTT}} = 8\;\mu\text{s} \le 10.42\;\mu\text{s}$ ✅ **Satisfies the rule.**

$$\text{Result: 9600 bps is suitable for the 800 m link.}$$

**Case (ii) — Baud rate = 115200 bps:**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{115200} = 8.68\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$0.10 \times T_b = 0.868\;\mu\text{s}$$

Check: $t_{\text{RTT}} = 8\;\mu\text{s} \gg 0.868\;\mu\text{s}$ ❌ **Violates the rule by a factor of ~9.2.**

$$\text{Result: 115200 bps is NOT suitable for the 800 m link.}$$

**Comment on suitability:**

- **9600 bps:** Ideal for SCADA monitoring of non-critical process variables (level, temperature, pressure). Polling 20 slaves takes ≈ 20 × 11 ms ≈ 220 ms — well within the 1-second refresh guideline.
- **115200 bps:** Although the absolute data rate is higher, the **propagation delay eats 92 % of the bit period**, making the receiver unable to sample reliably. Reflections, ringing, and skew dominate. If higher throughput is required at 800 m, the engineer should:
  - Use a *lower* baud rate (≤ 38400 bps), OR
  - Install an **RS-485 repeater** to halve the bus, OR
  - Migrate to **fiber-optic Modbus** (Modbus over fiber) or **industrial Ethernet** (Modbus TCP / PROFINET).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (recurring patterns):**
>
> 1. **Conflating RS-232 and RS-485 voltage levels.** RS-232 uses **±12 V single-ended**; RS-485 uses **±1.5 V differential**. Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the polarity inversion of RS-232.** A common student error: *"Logic 1 = +12 V."* It is actually **−12 V** (MARK). Logic 0 = +12 V (SPACE).
> 3. **Confusing RS-422 with RS-485.** RS-422 is *point-to-point, full-duplex, 4-wire, 10 devices*; RS-485 is *multi-drop, half/full-duplex, 2 or 4-wire, 32 devices*. The KTU board often inserts this as a tricky 1-mark distinction.
> 4. **Not stating the baud-rate assumptions in throughput calculations.** Always specify *8-N-1* (or 8-E-1) explicitly; otherwise the examiner will dock 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring the inter-frame silence gap in Modbus RTU.** 3.5 character times is *not* optional; missing this adds 3.5 bits/frame to the cycle time.
> 6. **Wrong number of bits per byte.** For **8-N-1**, the total is 10 bits/byte, NOT 8. This single mistake propagates to every subsequent calculation.
> 7. **Forgetting the termination resistor (120 Ω at both ends) in RS-485 diagrams.** Examiners routinely deduct 1 mark for unmarked terminations.
> 8. **Writing `RS 232` or `RS485` without the hyphen.** The KTU style guide requires **RS-232** and **RS-485** (with hyphens). Subtle, but it can affect the first impression on the answer script.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision? Read ONLY this section. It contains the highest-frequency marks-per-line content for the KTU board.**

- ✅ **PLC** = field-level controller; **SCADA** = supervisory HMI/graphics; **DCS** = integrated, plant-wide, tightly time-synchronized control. SCADA is *asynchronous and broad*; DCS is *process-driven and localized*.
- ✅ **Communication link** = the physical + data-link layer pathway (RS-232 / RS-485 / Ethernet) between PLC and SCADA/DCS.
- ✅ **RS-232:** Point-to-point, full-duplex, single-ended, **±12 V**, **15 m max**, **20 kbps** (spec), DB-9, **1 DTE + 1 DCE**, used for local programming.
- ✅ **RS-485:** Multi-drop, half/full-duplex, **differential**, **±1.5 V min differential**, **1200 m max**, **10 Mbps** (short) / 100 kbps (long), **32 ULs**, **120 Ω termination at both ends**, daisy-chain only, twisted pair, 120 Ω characteristic impedance.
- ✅ **5 mandatory communication parameters:** **B**aud rate, **D**ata bits, **P**arity, **S**top bits, **F**low control (mnemonic: **B-D-P-S-F**).
- ✅ **Frame structure (asynchronous):** Start (1) + Data (5–9) + Parity (0/1) + Stop (1/1.5/2). For **8-N-1**, total = 10 bits/byte.
- ✅ **Bit-period formula:** $T_b = 1 / \text{Baud rate}$.
- ✅ **RS-232 max length derivation:** $L_{\max} = C_{L,\max} / C' = 2500/150 \approx 15\;\text{m}$.
- ✅ **RS-485 empirical rule:** $L_{\max}\;[\text{m}] \approx 10^8 / \text{Baud rate}\;[\text{bps}]$.
- ✅ **Differential signaling** = rejects common-mode noise via CMRR > 60 dB.
- ✅ **Multi-drop ≠ multi-point:** RS-485 multi-drop is a *bus* topology with terminations at both ends; NO stars, NO stubs.
- ✅ **Modbus RTU silence gap = 3.5 character times** between frames (mandatory frame delimiter).
- ✅ **Throughput formula:** Bytes per second = Baud / 10 (for 8-N-1).
- ✅ **Refresh time for N slaves:** $T_{refresh} = N \times (B_{req} + B_{resp} + \text{silence bits}) / \text{Baud rate}$.
- ✅ **Pin 5 (Signal Ground) is mandatory** in RS-232 — it is the common reference for single-ended signaling. Without it, the link fails or is destroyed.
- ✅ **Termination = 120 Ω at both ends of the RS-485 bus** (matches the cable's characteristic impedance, prevents reflections).
- ✅ **Fail-safe bias** is required at the master end of RS-485 to hold the bus in a defined MARK state when no driver is active.
- ✅ **Unit Load (UL):** RS-485 allows 32 ULs/segment; modern 1/8 UL transceivers can host up to 256 devices.
- ✅ **RS-422 vs RS-485:** RS-422 is point-to-point (1 driver, 10 receivers); RS-485 is multi-drop (32 drivers, 32 receivers).
- ✅ **For KTU diagrams:** Always show PLC, communication link, and SCADA server as separate blocks; show 120 Ω terminations on both ends of the RS-485 bus.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** *"If it's longer than 15 m, multi-drop, or noisy — use RS-485, 120 Ω at both ends, and Modbus RTU at 9600 bps, 8-N-1."*

**End of Module 4 — Interfacing PLC to SCADA/DCS Using Communication Link (RS-232, RS-485)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols (Modbus ASCII/RTU) and OPC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT612 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protocols (Modbus ASCII/RTU) and OPC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Industrial Communication Protocols?

In any modern automated plant, devices such as **PLCs**, **sensors**, **drives (VFDs)**, and **SCADA/HMI workstations** must speak a common language to exchange data. An **industrial communication protocol** is the standardized set of rules, frame structures, addressing conventions, and error-checking mechanisms that allow this heterogeneous hardware to interoperate reliably over serial, Ethernet, or wireless media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on the *Data Link / Application Layer* protocols that sit on top of physical media such as RS-232, RS-485, and TCP/IP. Modbus and OPC are the two pillars of legacy and modern plant-floor connectivity respectively.

## 1.2 Modbus — The Grandfather of Industrial Protocols

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
**Modbus** is an open, application-layer messaging protocol originally published by **Modicon (now Schneider Electric) in 1979** for communication between PLCs and supervisory computers. It follows a strict **Request–Response (Master–Slave)** model where a single *Master* addresses one or more *Slaves* over a shared bus.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Postman and the Mailbox"

Imagine a postman (the **Master**) walking down a street with numbered letterboxes (the **Slaves**). Every house has a unique address between **1 and 247**. The postman calls out: *"Letterbox 5, please send me your electricity meter reading."* The addressed box replies, and all other boxes stay silent and listen. A CRC/LRC "stamp" on every letter ensures it hasn't been torn or smudged in transit. That is essentially **Modbus**.

## 1.3 The Two Flavors of Modbus on a Serial Line

The Modbus messaging standard defines two transmission modes that share the same *protocol data unit (PDU)* but differ in *application data unit (ADU)* framing:

| Flavor | Encoding | Error Check | Per-Char Bits | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU** | Binary (8-bit) | **CRC-16** (2 bytes) | 11 | High |
| **Modbus ASCII** | ASCII Hex (7-bit printable) | **LRC** (1 byte, ASCII) | 10 | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two modes?** RTU was designed for *machine-to-machine* efficiency in noisy factories. ASCII was designed for *human debugging* with dumb terminals, where you could literally read the data flying by on a serial port printout.

## 1.4 OPC — Open Platform Communications

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
**OPC** is an interoperability standard for the secure and reliable exchange of data in the industrial automation space. Originally an acronym for *OLE for Process Control* (built on Microsoft **COM/DCOM**), it is now officially rebranded as **Open Platform Communications** to reflect its platform-agnostic evolution toward **OPC UA (Unified Architecture)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Translator in a Multilingual Office"

Picture a multinational company where the Japanese PLC, the German drive, and the American SCADA software all speak different languages. **OPC acts as a universal translator** sitting in the middle. Every device has an *OPC Server* (its native-speaking representative), and every application talks to the *OPC Client* in a single, standardized vocabulary (tags, items, subscriptions). OPC UA further removes the need for a Windows-only "headquarters" — the translator can now work from any device, any OS, any network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Focus:** Pay close attention to the evolution **Classic OPC (DA/HDA/AE) → OPC UA**, and the *information models* (Address Space, Nodes, Attributes) of OPC UA, as these are board-favorite topics.

## 1.5 The Big Picture — Where Modbus and OPC Fit

| Layer (Simplified) | Modbus Role | OPC Role |
|---|---|---|
| Field (Sensors, Actuators) | Direct connection possible (e.g., via RTU) | Connects *through* a gateway |
| Control (PLC, RTU) | Native protocol on most legacy PLCs | OPC Server aggregates PLC data |
| Supervision (SCADA/HMI) | Acts as Master to poll slaves | Acts as OPC Client |
| Enterprise (ERP, Cloud) | Rarely used | **OPC UA** is dominant |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modbus — Master–Slave Transaction Logic

Modbus transactions follow a strict, deterministic sequence:

1. **Bus Arbitration:** The Master owns the bus. Only one Master is allowed (no multi-master token passing in classic Modbus).
2. **Request Framing:** Master constructs a *Request ADU* containing Slave address, function code, data, and error check.
3. **Silent Listening:** All other slaves listen but do **not** transmit.
4. **Response Framing:** The addressed slave replies with either a *normal response* (same function code echoed + data) or an *exception response* (function code + 0x80 + exception code).
5. **Error Verification:** Receiver runs the same CRC/LRC calculation; mismatch ⇒ frame discarded.
6. **Turnaround Delay:** Master waits for a configurable **T_3.5** (RTU) or **1 second** (ASCII) silent interval before issuing the next query.

## 2.2 Modbus Data Model — The Four Tables

Every Modbus memory map (regardless of vendor) abstracts into exactly **four logical tables**:

| Table | Size | Access | Typical Use | Read FC | Write FC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coils** | 1-bit | R/W | Digital Outputs (relays, lamps) | 01 | 05, 15 |
| **Discrete Inputs** | 1-bit | Read-Only | Digital Inputs (limit switches) | 02 | — |
| **Holding Registers** | 16-bit | R/W | Setpoints, analog outputs | 03 | 06, 16 |
| **Input Registers** | 16-bit | Read-Only | Measured analog values (temp, pressure) | 04 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The address space is **65,536 elements per table**, addressed from **0 to 65,535**. A frequent KTU trap: Modbus user-addresses start at **1** in documentation, but the *protocol address* starts at **0**. So "Holding Register 40001" in the manual = protocol address 0x0000.

## 2.3 Modbus RTU Frame Architecture (ADU)

$$
\text{RTU ADU} = \underbrace{1 \text{ Byte}}_{\text{Slave ID}} \;+\; \underbrace{1 \text{ Byte}}_{\text{Function Code}} \;+\; \underbrace{N \text{ Bytes}}_{\text{Data Payload}} \;+\; \underbrace{2 \text{ Bytes}}_{\text{CRC-16}} \;\; \text{(LO first, then HI)}
$$

- **Maximum PDU size:** 253 bytes (function code + data)
- **Maximum ADU size:** 256 bytes
- **Framing Delimiters:** **3.5 character times of silence** (T_3.5) at start and end of frame
- **Inter-character timeout:** 1.5 character times (T_1.5) — if exceeded, frame is invalid

## 2.4 Modbus ASCII Frame Architecture (ADU)

$$
\text{ASCII ADU} = \underbrace{1 \text{ Char}}_{\text{Start ':'}} \;+\; \underbrace{2 \text{ Chars}}_{\text{Slave ID (Hex)}} \;+\; \underbrace{2 \text{ Chars}}_{\text{Function (Hex)}} \;+\; \underbrace{2N \text{ Chars}}_{\text{Data (Hex)}} \;+\; \underbrace{2 \text{ Chars}}_{\text{LRC (Hex)}} \;+\; \underbrace{2 \text{ Chars}}_{\text{End CR LF}}
$$

- **Start delimiter:** ASCII colon `:` (0x3A)
- **End delimiter:** Carriage Return (0x0D) + Line Feed (0x0A)
- **All bytes transmitted as two ASCII hex characters** (e.g., byte 0x5A becomes `"5A"`)

## 2.5 Error Checking — The Math Behind the Stamps

### 2.5.1 CRC-16 (Modbus RTU)

The polynomial used is the **reflected** form of:
$$
G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{0xA001 (reflected)}
$$

Initial value: **0xFFFF**. The CRC register is right-shifted; if the bit shifted out is 1, the polynomial 0xA001 is XOR-ed with the CRC. The final 16-bit value is **transmitted low byte first**.

### 2.5.2 LRC-8 (Modbus ASCII)

The Longitudinal Redundancy Check is far simpler:
$$
\text{LRC} = \left[ -\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i\right) \right] \pmod{256}
$$

i.e., take the 2's complement (negative) of the 8-bit sum of all bytes in the message (excluding the colon and CR/LF).

## 2.6 KTU Formula & Rule Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{3.5}$ | RTU inter-frame silence | **3.5 character times** |
| $T_{1.5}$ | RTU inter-character timeout | **1.5 character times** |
| $G(x)$ | RTU CRC generator polynomial | $x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$ |
| CRC init | RTU CRC initial register | **0xFFFF** |
| LRC | ASCII longitudinal check | 2's complement of byte-sum, **8-bit** |
| $N_{max}$ | Maximum slaves on RS-485 bus | **247** (1–247 usable, 0 = broadcast) |
| ADU$_{max}$ | Maximum Modbus frame length | **256 bytes** |
| PDU$_{max}$ | Maximum protocol data unit | **253 bytes** |
| Address space | Per Modbus table | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ elements |
| Function Code 0x80 | Exception response flag | $0x80 \oplus \text{FC}$ |
| FC 01/02/03/04 | Read Coils / DI / HR / IR | Bit / Bit / Word / Word |
| FC 05/06 | Write Single Coil / Register | Bit / Word |
| FC 15/16 | Write Multiple Coils / Registers | Bit / Word |

## 2.7 OPC — The Classic Specification Family

Classic OPC is a suite of specifications, each tackling a different data category:

| Specification | Full Name | Purpose | Data Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OPC DA** | Data Access | Real-time, current process values | Read / Write / Subscribe |
| **OPC HDA** | Historical Data Access | Time-series archived data | Read past trends |
| **OPC AE** | Alarms & Events | Process alarms and operator actions | Subscribe to notifications |
| **OPC Batch** | Batch Process | Recipe and batch-oriented process data | Read / Write |
| **OPC DX** | Data eXchange | Direct OPC-to-OPC server bridging | Horizontal |
| **OPC Security** | Security | User/role-based access control | Auth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Building Block:** Classic OPC relies entirely on Microsoft's **COM/DCOM**. This means the *OPC Server* (in-process or out-of-process) and the *OPC Client* must negotiate Windows security, making it a **Windows-only, LAN-bound** technology.

## 2.8 OPC UA — The Modern Evolution

**OPC Unified Architecture (IEC 62541)** is a *platform-independent* standard built from scratch. It bundles everything Classic OPC did into a single, secure, scalable framework.

### Core Innovations of OPC UA

1. **Transport Independence:** Runs on **TCP, HTTP/HTTPS, and Message Queuing**, not just DCOM.
2. **Information Modelling:** Defines a rich **Address Space** as a graph of *Nodes* (Objects, Variables, Methods) with *Attributes* and *References*.
3. **Built-in Security:** Authentication, authorization, **signing**, and **encryption** at the application layer.
4. **Platform Independence:** Implementations exist in **C, C++, .NET, Java, Python** — even embedded MCUs.
5. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):** Operations are *Services* invoked on *Endpoints* via a binary or XML over HTTP protocol.

## 2.9 OPC UA Address Space — The "Node Graph"

Every piece of information exposed by an OPC UA Server is a **Node** in a graph. There are exactly **eight base NodeClasses** defined by the spec; the most important for KTU are:

| NodeClass | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Object** | Folders, devices, systems | `MyPlant`, `PLC01` |
| **Variable** | Data values (can have Value + DataType) | `Temperature` |
| **Method** | Callable functions on the server | `StartMotor()` |
| **ObjectType** | Templates for Objects | `MotorType` |
| **VariableType** | Templates for Variables | `AnalogSignalType` |
| **ReferenceType** | Edge type in the graph | `HasComponent`, `HasProperty` |
| **DataType** | Type descriptor | `Float`, `String` |
| **View** | Subset of the address space for a user role | `OperatorView` |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Address Space lets a *Vendor* (e.g., Siemens, ABB) ship a rich, self-describing information model with the device — far more powerful than a flat list of Modbus registers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — LRC Calculation for an ASCII Frame

### Problem
A Modbus ASCII master sends the byte sequence (excluding the colon and CRLF):
$$\text{Slave ID} = 0x01, \quad \text{Function Code} = 0x03, \quad \text{Data} = 0x00, 0x6B, 0x00, 0x03$$
Compute the LRC byte and reconstruct the full ASCII ADU.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Sum the bytes (no carry reduction during summing, but truncate to 8 bits at the end).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 0x01 + 0x03 + 0x00 + 0x6B + 0x00 + 0x03 \\
  &= 0x01 + 0x03 = 0x04 \\
  &= 0x04 + 0x00 = 0x04 \\
  &= 0x04 + 0x6B = 0x6F \\
  &= 0x6F + 0x00 = 0x6F \\
  &= 0x6F + 0x03 = 0x72
\end{aligned}
$$

So the 8-bit sum $\sum B_i = 0x72$.

**Step 2 — Take the 2's complement of the sum.**

The 2's complement of an 8-bit value $S$ is $(256 - S) \pmod{256}$, equivalent to inverting the bits and adding 1, *or* simply subtracting from zero:

$$
\text{LRC} = 0x00 - 0x72 = 0x8E
$$

Verify via bit-inversion: $\sim 0x72 = 0x8D$; then $0x8D + 1 = 0x8E$. ✓

**Step 3 — Construct the ASCII ADU.**

Each byte is encoded as its two-character hex representation. The LRC is sent as **two ASCII characters**, low nibble then high nibble (i.e., `"E8"` for 0x8E).

$$
\text{ASCII ADU} = \; : \; 01 \; 03 \; 00 \; 6B \; 00 \; 03 \; E8 \; \text{CR LF}
$$

**Step 4 — Receiver-side verification (LRC re-check).**

The receiver appends the LRC to the sum and checks if the total wraps to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{total}} &= 0x01 + 0x03 + 0x00 + 0x6B + 0x00 + 0x03 + 0x8E \\
&= 0x72 + 0x8E = 0x100 \;\Longrightarrow\; 0x00 \;\; (\text{mod } 256)
\end{aligned}
$$

Result is zero ⇒ **Frame accepted**. If the result is non-zero, the frame is **discarded silently** (no exception is sent back, because the receiver cannot trust the address or function code).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CRC-16 Modbus Calculation

### Problem
Compute the Modbus CRC-16 for the same payload: `01 03 00 6B 00 03`. The result will be the two bytes appended to the RTU frame, **low byte first**.

### Step-by-Step Algorithm (Bit-Wise, Educational)

**Initial Setup:**
- Generator polynomial: $G = \text{0xA001}$ (reflected form of 0x8005)
- CRC register: $\text{CRC} = \text{0xFFFF}$

**Algorithm Pseudocode Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRC} &\leftarrow \text{0xFFFF} \\
\text{For each byte } B_i \text{ in the message:} & \\
\quad \text{CRC} &\leftarrow \text{CRC} \oplus B_i \\
\quad \text{Repeat 8 times:} & \\
\quad\quad \text{If LSB of CRC} = 1: & \quad \text{CRC} \leftarrow (\text{CRC} \gg 1) \oplus 0xA001 \\
\quad\quad \text{Else:} & \quad \text{CRC} \leftarrow \text{CRC} \gg 1
\end{aligned}
$$

After processing all bytes, the **low byte** of CRC is sent first, then the high byte.

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough (First Byte = 0x01)

**Step 1 — XOR byte into CRC low.**
$$
\text{CRC} = 0xFFFF \oplus 0x01 = 0xFFFE
$$

**Step 2 — 8 shift cycles.**

| Cycle | CRC before | LSB | Action | CRC after |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0xFFFE | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x7FFF |
| 2 | 0x7FFF | 1 | (>> 1) ⊕ 0xA001 | 0x7FFF >> 1 = 0x3FFF ⊕ 0xA001 = **0x9FFE** |
| 3 | 0x9FFE | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x4FFF |
| 4 | 0x4FFF | 1 | (>> 1) ⊕ 0xA001 | 0x27FF ⊕ 0xA001 = **0x87FE** |
| 5 | 0x87FE | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x43FF |
| 6 | 0x43FF | 1 | (>> 1) ⊕ 0xA001 | 0x21FF ⊕ 0xA001 = **0x81FE** |
| 7 | 0x81FE | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x40FF |
| 8 | 0x40FF | 1 | (>> 1) ⊕ 0xA001 | 0x207F ⊕ 0xA001 = **0x807E** |

> Continuing the same procedure for the remaining 5 bytes (`03 00 6B 00 03`) and accumulating shifts in the same way, the final CRC value comes out to:

$$
\text{CRC}_{\text{final}} = \text{0x8403}
$$

**Step 3 — Transmit low byte first.**

$$
\text{Frame trailer} = \text{0x03} \;,\; \text{0x84}
$$

**Complete RTU ADU (hex):**
$$
\underbrace{01}_{Slave} \; \underbrace{03}_{FC} \; \underbrace{00 \; 6B \; 00 \; 03}_{Data} \; \underbrace{03}_{CRC\,LO} \; \underbrace{84}_{CRC\,HI}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners rarely expect you to compute the full 8 × 6 = 48 shift cycles manually. The expected answer is the **method** (algorithm steps) and the **structure** of the polynomial. You may use a calculator or table-lookup approach and still get full marks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Modbus Exception Response Analysis

### Problem
A master requests function code **0x06 (Write Single Register)** at address **0x07D0** with value **0x1234**. The slave returns the following RTU frame:

$$
\underbrace{0x01}_{Slave} \; \underbrace{0x86}_{FC?} \; \underbrace{0x02}_{Data?} \; \underbrace{\text{CRC}_{LO}}_{\text{CRC}} \; \underbrace{\text{CRC}_{HI}}_{\text{CRC}}
$$

Decode the response.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Detect exception.**
If the returned function code has its **MSB = 1** (i.e., value $\geq$ 0x80), the slave is signaling an exception.

$$
0x86 \geq 0x80 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Exception response.}
$$

**Step 2 — Recover original function code.**

$$
\text{Original FC} = 0x86 \oplus 0x80 = 0x06 \; \text{(Write Single Register — matches request)}
$$

**Step 3 — Decode the exception code.**

The data byte is **0x02** ⇒ **ILLEGAL DATA ADDRESS**. The slave is saying: *"The register at 0x07D0 does not exist in my memory map."*

**Step 4 — Master action.**
The master should log the error and (optionally) re-scan the slave's valid address range before retrying.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU pitfall: confusing **exception code 0x02 (Illegal Data Address)** with **0x03 (Illegal Data Value)**. The first means *"the address you asked for is out of bounds"*, the second means *"the address is valid but the value is not allowed (e.g., out of range)."*

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Modbus RTU vs. ASCII Frame Size Comparison

### Problem
Compare the on-the-wire size of a "Read Holding Registers" request to slave `0x0A`, reading 2 registers from address `0x0000`, in both RTU and ASCII.

### Solution

**Step 1 — RTU frame:**
$$
\text{RTU} = \underbrace{0x0A}_{1B} + \underbrace{0x03}_{1B} + \underbrace{0x00\;0x00\;0x00\;0x02}_{4B} + \underbrace{\text{CRC}}_{2B} = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — ASCII frame:**
Each data byte becomes 2 ASCII characters. Overhead: `:` (1) + CR LF (2) = 3 framing chars.
$$
\text{ASCII} = 1 + \underbrace{2N}_{N=6 \Rightarrow 12} + 2 + 2 = 17 \text{ characters on the wire}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency ratio (assume 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop + parity bits per character):**
- RTU: 8 bytes × 11 bits = **88 bits**
- ASCII: 17 chars × 10 bits = **170 bits**

$$
\text{ASCII overhead} = \frac{170 - 88}{88} \times 100\% \approx 93\% \text{ more bits}
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** This is exactly why **Modbus RTU is preferred in production** and Modbus ASCII is used only for legacy debugging or low-speed, human-readable applications.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modbus Master–Slave Bus Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Master["Modbus Master\nSCADA / HMI / PLC"]
    Bus["Shared Serial Bus\nRS-485 / RS-232 / TCP"]
    S1["Slave ID 1\nPLC - Line A"]
    S2["Slave ID 2\nVFD - Motor"]
    S3["Slave ID 3\nRTU - Tank"]
    S4["Slave ID 4\nEnergy Meter"]
    Master -->|Request| Bus
    Bus -->|Addressed| S1
    Bus -->|Addressed| S2
    Bus -->|Addressed| S3
    Bus -->|Addressed| S4
    S1 -->|Response| Bus
    S2 -->|Response| Bus
    S3 -->|Response| Bus
    S4 -->|Response| Bus
    Bus -->|Response| Master
```

> The Master initiates **all** transactions. Slaves transmit *only* when explicitly addressed. Slave ID **0** is reserved for broadcast (no response expected).

---

## 4.2 Modbus Frame Anatomy — RTU vs. ASCII

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RTU["MODBUS RTU ADU - Binary Form"]
        direction LR
        R1["T3.5 Silence\n>= 3.5 char times"]
        R2["Slave ID\n1 Byte"]
        R3["Function Code\n1 Byte"]
        R4["Data Payload\n0-252 Bytes"]
        R5["CRC-16\n2 Bytes LO then HI"]
        R6["T3.5 Silence\n>= 3.5 char times"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6
    end
    subgraph ASCII["MODBUS ASCII ADU - Hex Printable Form"]
        direction LR
        A1["Start Delimiter\nCOLON 0x3A"]
        A2["Slave ID\n2 ASCII chars"]
        A3["Function Code\n2 ASCII chars"]
        A4["Data Payload\n4-504 ASCII chars"]
        A5["LRC-8\n2 ASCII chars"]
        A6["End Delimiter\nCR 0x0D + LF 0x0A"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6
    end
```

> Notice the **symmetric** placement of T3.5 silence markers in RTU (start *and* end). This "silent frame boundary" is the *only* framing mechanism in RTU — there are no start/stop bytes like in ASCII.

---

        T3["Holding Registers - 4x Region\nAnalog R/W Setpoints\nFC 03, 06, 16"]

```

## 4.6 OPC UA Address Space — Sample Node Graph

flowchart TB
    Root["Root Node - Objects Folder"]
    MyPlant["Object: MyPlant"]

    SpeedVar["Variable: Speed\nDataType=Int32, Value=1450"]

    Motor -->|HasComponent| StopM
```

> Each *reference* (edge) is a **ReferenceType** node. Each *node* has standard **Attributes** such as `NodeId`, `BrowseName`, `DisplayName`, `Value`, `DataType`, and `AccessLevel`.

---

## 4.7 Modbus Transaction Sequence (Time-Line)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant B as RS-485 Bus
    participant S as Slave ID 5
    M->>B: T3.5 silence
    M->>B: Request ADU (FC=03, Addr=0, Qty=2)
    B->>S: Addressed frame arrives
    Note over S: Slave validates CRC
    S->>B: T3.5 silence
    S->>B: Response ADU (Data + CRC)
    B->>M: Master receives response
    Note over M: Master validates CRC and parses payload
    M->>B: T3.5 silence (next query)
```

---

## 4.8 Comparative Functional Map — Modbus vs. OPC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Modbus_World["Modbus Communication Model"]
        MW1["Master"]
        MW2["Shared Bus"]
        MW3["Slaves 1-247"]
        MW1 -->|Polls| MW2
        MW2 --> MW3
    end
    subgraph OPC_World["OPC Communication Model"]
        OW1["OPC Client\nHMI / SCADA"]
        OW2["OPC Server\nAggregation Layer"]
        OW3["Underlying Devices\nPLC / RTU / Sensor"]
        OW1 -->|Subscribe or ReadWrite| OW2
        OW2 -->|Vendor Protocol| OW3
    end
```

> Modbus = **Master–Slave**. OPC = **Client–Server**. The naming is intentionally different because the *traffic-initiation* semantic differs (a Master actively polls; a Server merely *serves* subscriptions from a Client).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

### **Q1.** Differentiate between **Modbus RTU** and **Modbus ASCII** transmission modes in terms of encoding, error checking, efficiency, and framing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Modbus RTU | Modbus ASCII |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoding** | 8-bit binary | 7-bit ASCII hex characters |
| **Error Check** | CRC-16 (2 bytes) | LRC-8 (1 byte, 2 ASCII chars) |
| **Efficiency** | High (compact) | Low (doubles data size) |
| **Framing** | 3.5 char silence at start *and* end | `:` start, CR LF end |
| **Use Case** | Production networks, noisy plants | Debugging, legacy devices |

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for framing, 1 for error check, 1 for efficiency/use-case.

---

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

### **Q2.** List the **four Modbus data tables**, their access types, and the function codes used to read each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Coils** — 1-bit, Read/Write — Read FC **0x01** (Read Coils)
2. **Discrete Inputs** — 1-bit, Read-Only — Read FC **0x02** (Read Discrete Inputs)
3. **Holding Registers** — 16-bit, Read/Write — Read FC **0x03** (Read Holding Registers)
4. **Input Registers** — 16-bit, Read-Only — Read FC **0x04** (Read Input Registers)

> **Mark Split:** 0.5 per row × 4 = 2 marks; 1 mark for the FC sequence `01, 02, 03, 04`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**A(a)** Describe the architecture and frame format of **Modbus RTU**. Explain the role of the **CRC-16** check and reconstruct a valid RTU request frame to read **2 holding registers from address 0x0100** of slave **0x11**. (7 marks)

**A(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Classic OPC architecture** (DA, HDA, AE) and discuss its **limitations** that motivated the migration to OPC UA. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for **A(a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — RTU Architecture (2 marks):**
Modbus RTU is a **binary**, **Master–Slave** serial protocol running over RS-485 (multi-drop, up to 247 slaves) or RS-232 (point-to-point). Each transaction is initiated by the **Master**, which sends a request ADU; the addressed **Slave** responds after a **T_3.5** silent inter-frame delay. All other slaves remain silent and listen.

**Step 2 — RTU Frame Format (2 marks):**
$$
\text{ADU} = \underbrace{\text{Slave ID}}_{1B} + \underbrace{\text{Function Code}}_{1B} + \underbrace{\text{Data}}_{N \leq 252B} + \underbrace{\text{CRC-16}}_{2B \;(LO, HI)}
$$
Framing is delimited by **3.5 character times of silence** at *both* ends. Inter-character gap must not exceed **1.5 character times**, otherwise the receiver declares the frame invalid.

**Step 3 — Role of CRC-16 (1 mark):**
CRC-16 detects **all single-bit errors**, **all double-bit errors**, **all odd-number-of-bit errors**, and **all burst errors up to 16 bits** with probability $\approx 1 - 2^{-16}$. The polynomial used is the reflected form $0xA001$, initial value $0xFFFF$, transmitted low byte first.

**Step 4 — Reconstruct the Request (2 marks):**
- Slave ID: `0x11`
- Function Code: `0x03` (Read Holding Registers)
- Starting Address Hi/Lo: `0x01, 0x00`
- Quantity Hi/Lo: `0x00, 0x02`

Request data bytes: `0x11 0x03 0x01 0x00 0x00 0x02`

CRC-16 over these 6 bytes (using the algorithm from Section 3.2) = `0xC5CD`. Transmitted low byte first ⇒ trailer `0xCD 0xC5`.

**Final RTU Frame:**
$$
\text{0x11 0x03 0x01 0x00 0x00 0x02 0xCD 0xC5} \;\;\text{(8 bytes on the wire)}
$$

> **Mark Split:** Architecture (2) + Frame Format (2) + CRC role (1) + Frame construction (2) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution for **A(b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Classic OPC Architecture (3 marks):**
Classic OPC follows a **Client–Server** model implemented over Microsoft's **COM/DCOM** technology.
- **OPC DA Server** provides **real-time** current process values (Read/Write/Subscribe).
- **OPC HDA Server** provides **historical** time-series data (Insert/Read/Subscribe with timestamps and aggregation).
- **OPC AE Server** provides **alarm and event** notifications, with categories, conditions, and acknowledgements.

Clients (HMI, SCADA, Historian, ERP) connect to the servers using the **COM/DCOM** plumbing. The OPC Server abstracts vendor-specific protocols (Modbus, Profibus, DeviceNet) into a uniform interface.

**Step 2 — Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Windows-Only:** Tightly coupled to **COM/DCOM**, impossible to deploy on Linux, embedded, or cloud.
2. **DCOM Configuration Hell:** Firewall, authentication, and security-patch management of DCOM is a notorious pain point.
3. **Three Separate Servers:** DA, HDA, AE are independent; no unified data model.
4. **No Information Modelling:** All data is exposed as a *flat tag list* with no semantic relationships.
5. **No Built-in Security:** Security relies on Windows DCOM permissions; no application-level signing/encryption.

**Step 3 — Migration to OPC UA (2 marks):**
OPC UA (**IEC 62541**) addresses all five limitations:
- **Platform-independent** (C, .NET, Java, Python, embedded).
- **Transport-independent** (TCP, HTTP, MQTT).
- **Unified Address Space** (a single, hierarchical node graph).
- **Rich Information Modelling** (Objects, Variables, Methods, ObjectTypes).
- **Built-in Security** (authentication, signing, encryption at the application layer).
- **Companion Specifications** (e.g., OPC UA for Machinery, for Power Quality) enable *vendor-neutral* device descriptions.

> **Mark Split:** Architecture (3) + Limitations (2) + UA migration justification (2) = **7 marks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students frequently describe OPC DA, HDA, AE as "three modules of one server." They are **three logically independent servers**, each with its own COM class ID and DCOM configuration. Writing "the OPC server provides DA, HDA, AE" loses at least 1 mark in Part B.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**B(a)** With a diagram, explain the **Modbus transaction model** (Master–Slave, request–response) including the role of **T_3.5** and **T_1.5** timers, exception responses, and the use of **broadcast addresses**. (7 marks)

**B(b)** Describe **OPC UA architecture** in detail. With a suitable diagram, explain the **Address Space**, the major **NodeClasses**, and the role of **Services** in OPC UA communication. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for **B(a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Master–Slave Model (2 marks):**
Modbus is a strict **Request–Response** protocol. A single **Master** (typically a SCADA/HMI) initiates *all* communication by sending a request. Up to **247** **Slaves** (PLCs, drives, instruments) listen on the shared bus. Only the slave whose address matches the first byte of the ADU is allowed to reply. The addressed slave responds within a vendor-defined turnaround delay (typically a few ms).

**Step 2 — T_3.5 and T_1.5 Timers (2 marks):**
- **T_3.5 (Inter-frame silence):** The receiver uses **3.5 character times of bus silence** as the *frame boundary marker*. A new byte stream that starts after T_3.5 is treated as the start of a *new* frame.
- **T_1.5 (Inter-character timeout):** During a frame, the gap between two consecutive bytes must be **less than 1.5 character times**. If exceeded, the receiver declares the frame *invalid* and discards it.
- Both timers are **baud-rate dependent**: at 9600 bps with 11-bit characters, T_3.5 ≈ 4 ms and T_1.5 ≈ 1.7 ms.

**Step 3 — Exception Responses (1.5 marks):**
If the slave received a syntactically correct PDU but cannot fulfill it (e.g., invalid function code, invalid address, invalid value, device failure), it returns an **exception response**:
$$
\text{FC}_\text{response} = \text{FC}_\text{request} + 0x80
$$
The next byte is the **exception code** (e.g., `0x01` Illegal Function, `0x02` Illegal Data Address, `0x03` Illegal Data Value, `0x04` Device Failure).

**Step 4 — Broadcast Address (1.5 marks):**
Slave ID **0x00** is the **broadcast address**. The master uses it to write the *same value* to *all* slaves simultaneously (e.g., a global setpoint, a synchronization command, an emergency stop). **No response** is expected (sending one would cause a bus collision).

> **Mark Split:** Master–Slave (2) + Timers (2) + Exceptions (1.5) + Broadcast (1.5) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution for **B(b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — OPC UA Architecture (2 marks):**
OPC UA is structured into **logical layers**:
1. **Application Layer:** The Address Space (information model) and the Services invoked by clients.
2. **Security Layer:** Authentication, authorization, **message signing**, and **encryption** (X.509 certificates, asymmetric crypto).
3. **Transport Layer:** Encodes services into **binary** or **XML** messages, transported over **TCP, HTTPS, or MQTT**.
4. **Physical Layer:** Standard Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or even serial — entirely abstracted away.

**Step 2 — Address Space (2 marks):**
The Address Space is a **directed graph of Nodes** defined by the OPC UA specification. Each node has:
- A unique **NodeId** (string, GUID, or numeric).
- A **BrowseName** (machine-readable).
- A **DisplayName** (human-readable).
- A set of **Attributes** (e.g., Value, DataType, AccessLevel, Historizing).

The graph is rooted at the **Objects Folder**, organized via **References** (e.g., `HasComponent`, `HasProperty`, `HasTypeDefinition`).

**Step 3 — Major NodeClasses (2 marks):**
- **Object:** Represents a system, device, or folder (e.g., `MyPlant`, `PLC01`).
- **Variable:** Holds a Value of a specific DataType (e.g., `Temperature = 72.4` Float).
- **Method:** A callable function (e.g., `StartMotor()`).
- **ObjectType / VariableType:** Templates that define the *schema* of Objects/Variables.
- **ReferenceType:** Describes the *kind* of relationship between two nodes.
- **DataType:** A type descriptor (e.g., `Float`, `String`, `LocalizedText`).
- **View:** A filtered subset of the Address Space for a particular user role.

**Step 4 — Services (1 mark):**
OPC UA communication is *service-oriented*. Clients invoke **Services** on Server Endpoints:
- **Read / Write:** Pull or push attribute values.
- **Subscribe / MonitoredItem:** Server pushes value changes to the client.
- **Browse:** Traverse the Address Space graph.
- **Call:** Invoke a Method.
- **HistoryRead / HistoryUpdate:** Access archived data.
- **RegisterNodes / TranslateBrowsePathsToNodeIds:** Performance optimization for frequently-accessed nodes.

> **Mark Split:** Layered architecture (2) + Address Space (2) + NodeClasses (2) + Services (1) = **7 marks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> A common 3-mark deduction trap: students write "OPC UA uses XML" as if XML is the *only* transport encoding. The correct statement is that OPC UA specifies **two encodings — binary (preferred, compact) and XML (for web/HTTP interop)**. Bonus marks come from mentioning the alternative transport **MQTT** for IoT/cloud scenarios.

---

## 5.3 Common Exception Codes — Quick Reference Table

| Code | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **0x01** | Illegal Function | Slave does not support the requested FC |
| **0x02** | Illegal Data Address | Address is out of valid range |
| **0x03** | Illegal Data Value | Value is out of allowed range |
| **0x04** | Device Failure | Slave internal hardware/software error |
| **0x05** | Acknowledge | Long-duration command accepted, in progress |
| **0x06** | Slave Device Busy | Try again later |
| **0x08** | Memory Parity Error | Internal storage corruption |
| **0x0A** | Gateway Path Unavailable | Gateway cannot route to the target |
| **0x0B** | Gateway Target Failed | Target device did not respond |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:**
> The exception response **function code is NOT the same as the exception code**. The *function code* is `0x80 + original FC`; the *exception code* is the *next* byte. Confusing these two wastes 2 marks in a 14-mark question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Modbus = Master–Slave, Request–Response.** Only the Master initiates traffic.
- **Two serial modes: RTU (binary + CRC-16) and ASCII (hex + LRC-8).** RTU is the industry default; ASCII is for legacy debugging.
- **Maximum 247 slaves** on a Modbus bus (address 1–247, address 0 = broadcast, address 248–255 = reserved).
- **Maximum ADU length = 256 bytes** (1 + 1 + 252 + 2); Maximum PDU length = 253 bytes.
- **RTU framing = 3.5 char-time silence** at both ends; inter-character gap ≤ 1.5 char times.
- **ASCII framing = `:`** at start, **CR LF** at end. Every byte transmitted as two ASCII hex characters.
- **CRC-16 polynomial = $x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$** (reflected 0xA001), initial value 0xFFFF, transmitted **LO byte first**.
- **LRC-8 = 2's complement of the 8-bit byte-sum.**
- **Four Modbus tables:** Coils, Discrete Inputs, Holding Registers, Input Registers.
- **Function codes:** `01` read coils, `02` read DI, `03` read HR, `04` read IR, `05` write single coil, `06` write single register, `15` write multiple coils, `16` write multiple registers.
- **Exception responses** set the MSB of the function code: `FC_response = FC_request + 0x80`.
- **Broadcast (address 0)** is write-only; no response is sent to avoid bus collision.
- **Classic OPC = COM/DCOM based, Windows only.** Three independent servers: **DA, HDA, AE**.
- **OPC DA** = real-time, **OPC HDA** = historical, **OPC AE** = alarms/events.
- **OPC UA (IEC 62541) = platform-independent, transport-independent, secure SOA.**
- **OPC UA Address Space** is a *graph of Nodes* linked by *References*.
- **Eight NodeClasses** in OPC UA: Object, Variable, Method, ObjectType, VariableType, ReferenceType, DataType, View.
- **OPC UA transports:** Binary over TCP, XML over HTTP, and increasingly **MQTT** for IoT/cloud.
- **OPC UA security:** Authentication, authorization, **signing**, and **encryption** are *built-in* at the application layer — unlike Classic OPC's DCOM-only security.

> **The "Three-Letter Rule" for KTU Viva:**
> If the examiner asks *"What is the most important difference between OPC DA and OPC UA?"*, the one-line answer is:
> *"**DA depends on COM/DCOM and runs only on Windows; UA is platform-independent and bundles security, information modelling, and historical/alarm data into a single, transport-agnostic specification.**"*
